         Roughly             half of FY2025E revenue is not covered by contracted backlog or a realistic pipeline yield    , and must come from sources not yet booked — including renewal of the expired PNR MSA, new task orders under existing MSAs, and unpredictable emergency-response call-outs. The         “    $74.0M visibility    ”         figure overstates true visibility because it adds the             full         pipeline without applying the win rate and ignores the PNR contract expiry. The buyer should request             customer-level and PFAS-specific backlog breakouts         (the CIM provides neither), which the sector memo also flagged as a priority.  
        8.2 Regulatory and Compliance Risk  
              August 14, 2024 Oregon DEQ enforcement action:         improper storage of contaminated soils at the Portland HQ facility; $875K settlement + $325K legal fees. The fact that the violation occurred             at the operational core         (not a remote field site) is a yellow flag for systemic compliance posture, not just an isolated incident. A full 10-year enforcement history (notices of violation, permit actions, complaints) must be obtained.  
              Recurring compliance cost reality:         as noted in Section 5, environmental services companies routinely incur $0.2–0.5M/yr of regulatory/compliance expense; treating the full $1.2M as non-recurring is not defensible QoE practice.  
              Permit currency:         all listed permits are stated         “    current,    ”         but renewal cycles and any conditions/restrictions should be verified, particularly the DEQ contractor license given the recent enforcement action.  
        8.3 Real Estate and Related-Party Risk  
      The Portland HQ (8.5 acres, purpose-built environmental operations center) is             owned personally by Mr. Oakes         and leased to CES at $1.4M/yr through 2029 — ~$0.8M above estimated market rent. This creates three intertwined issues: (i) the EBITDA add-back is conditional on a post-close negotiation with the seller (Section 5); (ii) the buyer must decide whether to acquire the property, renegotiate the lease, or relocate — but the property is customized for environmental staging (permitted wash rack, secondary containment, soil stockpiles), making relocation costly and disruptive; and (iii) Mr. Oakes, as 68% seller and landlord, has leverage on both the equity price and the rent. The process letter explicitly asks bidders to address the real estate, signaling that Greenleaf expects this to be a negotiated term. The Boise depot ($180K/yr through 2028) and Seattle office ($85K/yr through 2027) are third-party leases and present no related-party issue.  
        8.4 Key-Person and Management-Depth Risk  
              Randall Oakes (CEO, 61, 68% owner):         intends to transition out of day-to-day operations within 18–24 months post-close. He has been the sole strategic, financial, and operational decision-maker for 16 years. A replacement CEO             will         be required (which is why the owner-comp add-back is conceptually valid), but the $0.5M replacement assumption is light and the transition risk is real.  
              Janet Prewitt (VP Operations, 18-year tenure):         holds key remediation client relationships — a critical key person. Retention agreement should be a closing condition (the GreenWorks experience, where a lead PM departed within six months of close, is a direct cautionary parallel).  
              No CFO:         the Company has a Controller (Lisa Fontaine) but no CFO. For an $87M platform being integrated into a sponsor portfolio, a CFO will likely need to be installed post-close — an additional cost not reflected in the projections.  
              Professional Engineer capacity:         down to two from three (Section 6.5), constraining the growth thesis.  
        8.5 Capital Expenditure and Fleet Condition  
      CIM maintenance capex of $3.0M (3.4% of revenue) is             ~150–200 bps below the 5–7% sector floor         and below Thornfield’s actual portfolio experience (GreenWorks 6.2%, Allied 5.8%). The GreenWorks lesson — where pre-acquisition maintenance capex was understated at ~3.5% and actual post-close was ~6.2% as deferred maintenance surfaced — is directly on point. The fleet is reported at an average age of ~4.5 years with >$22M replacement value, which sounds reasonable, but a detailed fleet age-and-condition assessment is essential early diligence. If normalized maintenance capex is ~$5.5M, the ~$2.5M/yr difference directly reduces FCF (to near zero on a CIM basis) and weakens the leverage/return profile. Note also that FY2023–24 growth capex was elevated ($3.0M/$4.2M) including $0.7M/$1.2M of PFAS equipment — some         “    growth    ”         fleet additions may in fact be replacement in disguise.  
        8.6 Strategic Fit and Fund Concentration  
      The sector memo states Fund III         “    targets platforms in the $30M–$100M enterprise value range    ”         and that CES         “    fits squarely within these parameters.    ”         However, Greenleaf’s own implied EV range is             $136.8M–$171.0M         (trailing) — materially above the stated target. At a buy-side EV of ~$130–150M, less ~$13–15M net debt, the phantom-unit cash settlement, and any HQ property acquisition, the equity check is likely             ~$110–135M    , or roughly             26–32% of Fund III’s $425M         in a single platform. This is a meaningful concentration that warrants explicit IC discussion: either the         “    $30–100M    ”         target is defined on a different basis (e.g., revenue, where CES’s $87.3M does fit), or CES represents a larger-than-typical platform requiring IC approval and possibly a co-invest. The deal team should align with the IC on fund-concentration limits before the IOI.  
      
         9. Transaction Structure Considerations  
      The IOI must address the structurally sensitive items Greenleaf flagged. Recommended positions:  
              Real estate (HQ).         Propose to acquire the HQ property at appraised fair market value (eliminating the related-party lease and the add-back debate entirely)             or         negotiate a new long-term lease at market rent ($0.6M/yr) as a condition to closing. Do not assume the $0.8M add-back is free; price the EV on a basis that reflects the likely outcome.  
              Existing debt.         Refinance/repay the $17.3M Cascadia facility at close (term loan matures Aug 2026 in any event). Confirm no prepayment penalties or change-of-control triggers.  
              Phantom units.         Treat the cash settlement of all outstanding phantom units as a             debt-like closing obligation         reducing equity proceeds; obtain the current liability balance and unit agreements. Do not double-count the $0.3M annual add-back against the closing payment.  
              Oakes transition / rollover / earnout.         Accommodate the 18–24 month advisory transition. Consider a modest seller rollover (5–10%) to align Oakes on the PFAS execution and PNR renewal, and/or an earnout tied to PFAS revenue and PNR contract renewal — directly bridging the two largest value uncertainties. An earnout is well-suited here because the principal risks (PFAS ramp, PNR renewal) are measurable post-close.  
              Working capital peg.         Establish a normalized NWC peg via QoE; the DSO discrepancy (Section 4/8) and the reconciliation issues mean the peg must be set from audited data, not the CIM.  
              Tax structure.         CES is a Delaware C-corp and the process letter anticipates a stock purchase. A stock purchase of a C-corp generally provides             no tax basis step-up         absent a Section 338(h)(10)/(g) election. Buyer’s counsel (Calloway, Barrett & Sims) should evaluate 338(h)(10) feasibility and the net tax benefit versus the cost, in coordination with tax advisors, as this can materially affect after-tax returns.  
              Representations & warranties / indemnity.         Given the DEQ enforcement history, customer-contract status, and related-party lease, insist on strong reps specifically covering regulatory compliance history, material contract status/renewals, and the lease; consider R&W insurance with specific exclusions carved back to seller indemnity.  
      
        10. Recommended IOI Position and Diligence Priorities  
       10.1 Recommended IOI  
              Enterprise value range: ~$130M–$150M         on a cash-free, debt-free basis with normalized NWC, valued on trailing (FY2024A) buy-side Adjusted EBITDA of ~$15.5–16.5M (≈8.1x–9.4x). State that the range is trailing-based and contingent on confirmatory diligence.  
              Structure:         100% stock purchase; refinance existing debt at close; acquire or re-lease HQ at market; accommodate 18–24 month Oakes transition with proposed modest rollover and/or PFAS/PNR-tied earnout.  
              Financing:         sponsor equity from Fund III (indicate anticipated leverage of ~2.0–2.5x EBITDA given the thin FCF profile) plus senior debt from a lender to be identified; note that final leverage is subject to QoE and FCF confirmation.  
              Conditions / value drivers:         explicitly state that the upper end of the range is contingent on (i) confirmation of PFAS contracted backlog supporting FY2025E, (ii) PNR contract renewal/extension, and (iii) validation of the EBITDA adjustments via QoE. This signals seriousness while preserving downside protection.  
        10.2 Prioritized Diligence Requests (Phase II / VDR)  
              Reconciled, audited financial statements         with a complete EBITDA build that ties to the line items; QoE provider to resolve the ~$3.2M Excel footing gap and the CIM-vs-Excel differences.  
              PNR:         current contract status, renewal correspondence, PNR’s share of backlog, and any rebid/competitive activity.  
              PFAS:         contracted backlog vs. pipeline breakout for the PFAS practice; signed PFAS contracts supporting FY2025E; PE staffing/hiring plan.  
              Customer list:         a single reconciled top-customer list with contract copies, tenure, and segments (resolve the #6–#10 discrepancy).  
              Regulatory:         10-year enforcement/compliance history; DEQ consent agreement and any ongoing obligations; permit renewal schedule.  
              Capex/fleet:         independent fleet age-and-condition assessment; maintenance vs. growth capex detail; deferred-maintenance confirmation.  
              Real estate:         HQ appraisal, lease terms, environmental condition of the property, and Oakes’s willingness to sell/re-lease.  
              Phantom equity:         outstanding unit count, vesting, and cash settlement amount at close.  
              Management:         retention terms for Prewitt and other key personnel; CFO plan; Oakes transition/consulting terms.  
              Working capital:         monthly NWC and AR aging to set the peg and resolve the DSO discrepancy (52 days stated vs. ~71 days implied).  
        10.3 Walk-Away / Repricing Triggers  
      The IOI range should be explicitly revisitable downward if: (a) PNR is not renewed or is competitively rebid at reduced scope; (b) PFAS contracted backlog does not meaningfully support FY2025E; (c) QoE reduces reported/Adjusted EBITDA; (d) maintenance capex is confirmed materially above $3.0M; or (e) the regulatory history reveals systemic rather than isolated compliance issues. Conversely, the range could move toward/above $150M if PFAS backlog and PNR renewal are confirmed and adjustments validate.  
      
         11. Conclusion  
      CES is a high-quality environmental services platform with genuine regulatory tailwinds and a defensible regional competitive position, and it aligns with Thornfield’s sector thesis and Fund II pattern-recognition. However, the CIM package exhibits a pattern of issues that Thornfield’s own portfolio experience predicts in this sector: understated maintenance capex, regulatory costs presented as fully non-recurring, and emerging-service-line growth projected well above achievable rates — compounded here by financial statements that do not reconcile, an EBITDA bridge with conditional and overstated add-backs, a largest customer whose contract has already expired, and customer disclosures that are internally inconsistent.  
          Recommendation: submit a disciplined IOI at ~$130–150M EV, heavily conditioned on the diligence items above, with an earnout/rollover structure bridging the PFAS and PNR uncertainties.         This positions Thornfield as a credible, serious bidder while preserving the downside protection that the identified risks demand. The deal team should obtain IC alignment on the fund-concentration implication before submission. Target IC pre-read ahead of the July 14, 2025 IOI deadline.

### FILE: exhibit-summary-memo.docx
          INTERNAL MEMORANDUM — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT  
                          TO:  
          Sarah Whitfield, Esq.; File  
                FROM:  
          Jordan Reeves, Associate  
                DATE:  
          October 7, 2024  
                RE:  
          Review of Respondent’s Document Production —             Torres-Kwan v. Kwan    , Case No. 2024-DR-01847 (Cir. Ct. Fairview County, Columbia; Hon. Yoon-Callahan)  
                PRIVILEGE:  
          Attorney Work Product / Prepared in Anticipation of Litigation  
         
       I. Introduction and Scope of Review  
      This memorandum catalogs, summarizes, and evaluates the document production served by Respondent Darren Kwan through his counsel, Raymond Fossett of Hargrove Fossett & Yates LLP, in response to Petitioner Melinda Torres-Kwan’s First Request for Production of Documents, Nos. 1–22 (served September 10, 2024). The production transmittal (cover email from R. Fossett to S. Whitfield) indexes seven (7) produced document sets (PROD-001 through PROD-007) and a four-item privilege log (PRIV-001 through PRIV-004).  
      We have reviewed each produced exhibit against (a) the Respondent’s Sworn Financial Declaration (PROD-001) and (b) the internally generated figures in the supporting records.             The production reveals several material omissions and inconsistencies that, in our preliminary assessment, materially understate Respondent’s income, assets, and liabilities, and that warrant targeted follow-up discovery.         The most significant findings are (1) an entirely undisclosed investment real-property interest at 1035 Elm Street, Unit C; (2) an $85,000 wire transfer from the Crestline brokerage account to an undisclosed account at Harborstone Financial Group executed two weeks after the declared separation date; and (3) a substantial understatement of pass-through business income ($174,000 in cash distributions reported in lieu of $367,200 in K-1 ordinary business income).  
      This memorandum is work product. It is intended to guide discovery strategy and should not be produced or disclosed.  
      
        II. Exhibit Catalog  
      The following table catalogs each produced exhibit, its Bates range, statement period, custodian/issuer, and the Request for Production numbers to which it responds (per the cover letter).  
                             Prod. No.  
          Document / File  
          Bates Range  
          Period Covered  
          Issuer / Custodian  
          Responsive RFP Nos.  
            PROD-001  
          Respondent’s Sworn Financial Declaration  
          — (filed with Court)  
          Filed 9/20/2024 (per declaration); cover letter states         “    on or about 10/4/2024    ”  
          Prepared by R. Fossett, Esq.; sworn by D. Kwan  
          1, 2, 3  
            PROD-002  
          2023 Form 1040 excerpts (Schedule E, K-1, W-2s, entity summary)  
          DK-PROD-0045 – 0058  
          Tax Year 2023  
          Archer & Linden CPAs; IRS filing  
          4, 5, 8  
            PROD-003  
          Crestline Wealth Advisors brokerage statements (acct. ending 9956)  
          KWAN_PROD_00142 – 00158  
          Jan 1, 2024 – Aug 1, 2024  
          Crestline Wealth Advisors (Nathan Greer, CFP)  
          6, 7  
            PROD-004  
          Fairview Community Bank individual statements (checking 7703; savings 7704)  
          KWAN_PROD_0401 – 0424  
          Mar 1, 2024 – Aug 1, 2024  
          Fairview Community Bank  
          6, 7  
            PROD-005  
          Meridian Home Lending mortgage statements (1035 Elm St., Unit C)  
          —  
          Jun, Jul, Aug 2024  
          Meridian Home Lending  
          9, 10  
            PROD-006  
          Platinum Visa credit card statements (acct. ending 8316)  
          —  
          May 1 – Aug 31, 2024  
          Fairview Community Bank  
          6, 7, 11  
            PROD-007  
          NovaBridge Solutions LLC expense reimbursement reports (D. Kwan)  
          KWAN_PROD_00187 – 00193  
          Q1–Q2 2024 (Jan 1 – Jun 30, 2024)  
          NovaBridge Solutions LLC  
          5, 8, 12  
            PRIV-001–004  
          Privileged communications (Kwan↔Fossett, Aug 5 – Sep 8, 2024)  
          Withheld  
          Aug 5 – Sep 8, 2024  
          Withheld (privilege log forthcoming)  
          13, 14, 15, 18  
             Notable gaps in the production         (addressed in Part V): No statements were produced for the 401(k) account (ending 3310, Pinnacle Retirement Services), the joint checking/savings accounts (ending 4821/4822), or the marital-residence mortgage (418 Birchwood Lane). Entity-level NovaBridge records responsive to RFP Nos. 16–22 were not produced and are expressly reserved for supplementation.  
      
        III. Summary of Key Financial Figures  
       A. Income (as declared vs. as documented)  
                           Component  
          Per Financial Declaration (PROD-001)  
          Per Tax Return / K-1 (PROD-002)  
          Variance  
            W-2 salary (NovaBridge)  
          $185,000  
          $185,000 (W-2 Box 1)  
          None  
            K-1 / pass-through business income  
              $174,000         (characterized as         “    K-1 Income / Distributions    ”    )  
              $367,200         (K-1 Box 1 ordinary business income; Schedule E Part II)  
              +$193,200 understated  
            Net rental income (1035 Elm St.)  
              None disclosed         (    “    no other sources of income… rental income… None    ”    )  
          $2,940 (Schedule E, Part I)  
          Omitted  
            Taxable interest  
          None disclosed  
          $1,840 (Form 1040 Line 2b)  
          Omitted  
            Ordinary dividends  
          None disclosed  
          $6,310 (Line 3b)  
          Omitted  
            Capital gains  
          None disclosed  
          $8,450 (Line 7)  
          Omitted  
                Respondent total income (declared)  
              $359,000  
          —  
          —  
                Joint return total income / AGI  
          —  
          $638,940 / $612,960  
          —  
         The $174,000 figure is the             cash distribution         (K-1 Box 19a), not the             taxable pass-through income         (K-1 Box 1 = $367,200). The $193,200 difference equals Respondent’s 60% share of the $322,000 retained at the entity level, which increased his NovaBridge capital account from $482,600 to $675,800. Reporting distributions in lieu of allocable income materially understates income for support purposes.  
        B. Assets (as declared)  
                          Asset  
          Declared Value  
          Notes / Documentation Status  
            Marital residence — 418 Birchwood Lane  
          $715,000 (est.); equity $496,600  
          Online estimate; no appraisal; mortgage balance $218,400 unverified (no statement produced)  
            Joint checking (4821)  
          $12,340  
          No statement produced — unverified  
            Joint savings (4822)  
          $28,750  
          No statement produced — unverified  
            Individual checking (7703)  
          $8,215  
          Matches FCB statement as of 8/1/2024  
            Individual savings (7704)  
          $42,680  
          Matches FCB statement as of 8/1/2024  
            Crestline brokerage (9956)  
          $387,450  
          Matches 8/1/2024 valuation — but this is             post    -$85,000-wire value; 6/1/2024 value was ~$468,695  
            401(k) (3310)  
          $412,800  
          No statement produced — unverified  
            Petitioner 403(b) (5587)  
          $89,200  
          Petitioner’s account; not in Respondent’s custody  
            NovaBridge 60% interest  
          “    Not formally appraised    ”  
          K-1 ending capital account $675,800; entity retained $322,000 in 2023  
            2022 BMW X5  
          $48,500; equity $26,400  
          —  
            2020 Honda CR-V  
          $24,800; equity $24,800  
          Petitioner’s vehicle  
            Household personal property  
          ~$30,000  
          —  
                Undisclosed: 1035 Elm St., Unit C  
              Not listed  
          Titled solely in D. Kwan; mortgage ~$241,281; rental income $2,100/mo  
                Undisclosed: Harborstone Fin. Group acct. 2291  
              Not listed  
          Received $85,000 wire on 6/15/2024  
           C. Liabilities (as declared vs. documented)  
                           Liability  
          Per Declaration  
          Per Records  
          Variance  
            Mortgage — 418 Birchwood Lane  
          $218,400  
          No statement produced  
          Unverified  
            Mortgage — 1035 Elm St., Unit C  
              Not disclosed  
          ~$241,281 (Meridian stmt., 8/1/2024)  
              Omitted (~$241,281)  
            Auto loan — 2022 BMW X5  
          $22,100  
          —  
          No payment visible in checking  
            Credit card — Platinum Visa  
          $8,500 (    “    First National Card Services    ”    )  
          Card is with             Fairview Community Bank    , acct. ending 8316; balances ranged $6,418–$12,487  
          Issuer misidentified; balance inconsistent  
                Total liabilities (declared)  
              $249,000  
          —  
          Understated by ~$241,281 (Elm St. mortgage)  
           D. Monthly Expenses (as declared)  
      The declaration reports total personal monthly expenses of             $8,885    , plus             $3,500         voluntary contribution to the marital household, for a total monthly outlay of             $12,385    . The produced bank and credit-card records show materially higher actual monthly outflows (see Part IV.G–H).  
      
         IV. Flagged Inconsistencies and Areas of Concern  
      Findings are ordered by materiality.  
       A. Undisclosed Investment Real Property — 1035 Elm Street, Unit C  
      The financial declaration states, under penalty of perjury, that         “    Respondent does not hold any ownership interest, whether sole or joint, in any other real property    ”         (¶ 23) beyond 418 Birchwood Lane. This is directly contradicted by three produced exhibits:  
              Tax Return (PROD-002), Schedule E, Part I:         reports a rental condominium at 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, Fairview, Columbia 20147,         “    titled in the name of Darren Kwan,    ”         with $25,200 in rents received and $2,940 net rental income for 2023.  
              Mortgage Statements (PROD-005):         Meridian Home Lending loan No. 7842-0163-ELM, borrower Darren Kwan (sole; no co-borrower), original loan $279,000 dated October 8, 2019, current principal balance             $241,281.19         as of 8/1/2024, monthly PITI             $1,680.00    .  
              Bank Statements (PROD-004):         recurring         “    ACH Deposit — Tenant Rent Payment    ”         of             $2,100/month         (April–July 2024) and a recurring         “    ACH Payment — Meridian Home Lending    ”         of             $1,680/month         (the Elm Street mortgage), distinct from the $1,842 Birchwood Lane mortgage payment.  
          Impact:         The Elm Street property is a marital asset (acquired during the marriage, October 2019) that is wholly omitted from the asset schedule, and its ~$241,281 mortgage is omitted from the liability schedule. The $2,100/month rental income ($25,200/year) and the $1,680/month mortgage expense are omitted from the income and expense schedules, respectively. The declaration’s affirmative representation that no other real property is owned is false on its face. This is the single largest omission in the production.  
        B. $85,000 Wire Transfer to an Undisclosed Account  
      The Crestline brokerage statements (PROD-003) show an             outgoing wire transfer of $85,000.00 on June 15, 2024         (Ref. WT-2024-061524-0087) from account ending 9956 to             Harborstone Financial Group, account ending 2291    , authorized by the account holder via recorded telephone call.  
          The recipient account (Harborstone Financial Group, acct. 2291) is             not disclosed anywhere         in the financial declaration, which affirmatively states Respondent         “    does not maintain any other financial accounts    ”         (¶ 33).  
          The transfer occurred             two weeks after the declared separation date of June 1, 2024    .  
          The brokerage account value fell from ~$468,695 (6/1/2024 opening) to $385,125 (6/30/2024 closing), principally because of this wire. The declaration reports the             post-transfer         balance of $387,450 (as of 8/1/2024) while representing that all balances are         “    as of the date of separation… on or about June 1, 2024    ”         (¶ 56).  
          Impact:         This is either an undisclosed account (a material omission) or a transfer to a third party (potential dissipation/concealment of marital assets). Either way, the marital brokerage asset is understated by approximately $81,000, and the destination and current balance of the $85,000 must be traced.  
        C. Understatement of Pass-Through Business Income  
      As detailed in Part III.A, the declaration reports $174,000 in         “    K-1 Income / Distributions    ”         rather than the $367,200 in K-1 ordinary business income reported on the tax return (Schedule K-1 Box 1; Schedule E Part II). The $193,200 difference was retained at the entity level and increased Respondent’s capital account to $675,800. For child-support and alimony calculations, taxable pass-through income—not cash distributions—is the relevant measure. The declaration also omits rental, interest, dividend, and capital-gains income despite affirmatively representing         “    None    ”         (¶ 19).  
        D. Date-of-Separation Discrepancy  
              Financial Declaration (PROD-001), ¶ 5:         separation         “    on or about             June 1, 2024    .    ”         (Internal cross-references are consistent: the Harbor Point lease commenced June 1, 2024 (¶ 2); voluntary household contributions commenced         “    in approximately June 2024    ”         (¶ 51); ¶ 56 fixes all valuations         “    as of the date of separation… on or about June 1, 2024.    ”    )  
              Cover Letter / Privilege Log:         PRIV-001 describes an August 5, 2024 email         “    following the parties’ separation on             August 1, 2024    ”    ; PRIV-002 references         “    post-separation August 1, 2024.    ”  
          Impact:         Respondent’s own counsel’s privilege log contradicts the sworn declaration by two months. The separation date is the presumptive cutoff for marital-property accumulation and the anchor for any dissipation analysis. If separation was August 1 (not June 1), then the $85,000 wire (6/15), the brokerage decline, the luxury credit-card spending, and the condo lease all occurred             during         the marriage, materially strengthening dissipation claims. This internal inconsistency should be pinned down under oath.  
        E. Valuation-Date Misrepresentation  
      The declaration represents (¶ 56) that all account balances are         “    approximate as of the date of separation… on or about June 1, 2024.    ”         The produced statements show the reported balances are actually as of             August 1, 2024    :  
                           Account  
          Declared (stated as of 6/1/2024)  
          Actual 6/1/2024 value  
          Actual 8/1/2024 value (reported)  
            Crestline brokerage (9956)  
          $387,450  
          ~$468,695  
          $387,450  
            Individual checking (7703)  
          $8,215  
          $26,869.56  
          $8,215.00  
            Individual savings (7704)  
          $42,680  
          $40,547.96  
          $42,680.00  
         Reporting post-separation balances as separation-date values folds post-separation dissipation and spending into the marital-asset figures and conceals the $85,000 transfer (see Part IV.B).  
        F. Credit-Card Issuer Misidentified; Spending Pattern Suggests Dissipation  
              Issuer:         The declaration (¶ 46) identifies the card as a         “    Platinum Visa… with             First National Card Services    .    ”         The produced statements (PROD-006) show the card is issued by             Fairview Community Bank    , account ending             8316    . No         “    First National Card Services    ”         statement was produced, and no payment to that issuer appears in the bank records. Either the issuer was misidentified or a second, undisclosed card exists.  
              Balance/payment characterization:         The declaration states an ~$8,500 revolving balance with payments of         “    $500–$1,000 per month.    ”         The statements show Respondent             pays the balance in full each month         and charges             $6,418–$12,487/month         (May $7,038; June $8,124; July $12,487; August $6,419). The declared figures bear no resemblance to actual usage.  
              Luxury / lifestyle spending (May–Aug 2024, ~$34,068 total):         Monarch Fine Clothiers $7,300; Fairview Country Club dues $1,700; Aura Salon & Spa $1,440; Sapphire Sky Travel $6,200 (July); Pinnacle Electronics         “    Personal Devices    ”         $2,149.50 (May); Harbor Point Luxury Furnishings $3,236; and substantial high-end dining. These charges dwarf the corresponding declared expense lines (clothing $200/mo; personal care $100/mo; dining/entertainment $600/mo) and include categories (country club) absent from the declaration entirely.  
        G. Recurring Transfers to Unidentified Third Party (    “    Kiera M.    ”    )  
      The individual checking account (PROD-004) shows recurring             Venmo payments to                 “        Kiera M.        ”         totaling             $14,600         over March–July 2024:  
                        Date  
          Amount  
            03/15/2024  
          $2,800.00  
            04/22/2024  
          $3,100.00  
            05/10/2024  
          $2,500.00  
            06/18/2024  
          $3,200.00  
            07/14/2024  
          $3,000.00  
                Total  
              $14,600.00  
         These payments (~$2,920/month average) are unexplained, recur through the declared separation period, and are not disclosed in the declaration. The identity of         “    Kiera M.    ”         and the purpose of the transfers must be established; the pattern is consistent with support of a third party, repayment of an undisclosed obligation, or asset transfer.  
        H. Unexplained Retirement-Account Activity; 401(k) Statement Not Produced  
          The checking account shows recurring             “        ACH — Pinnacle Retirement Services 401(k)        ”         debits of             $1,437.50/month         (March–July 2024) and a             lump-sum $14,644.60 on August 1, 2024    . The 2023 W-2 already reflects $22,500 in elective 401(k) deferrals (deducted from payroll), so the checking-funded ACHs appear to be a             separate         stream (additional voluntary contributions, Roth, or a 401(k)             loan repayment    ). The declaration discloses no 401(k) loan and omits any retirement-contribution line from monthly expenses.  
          The             $14,644.60 lump sum on 8/1/2024         (post-separation under either date theory, and one day before the cover letter’s stated separation date) is suspicious and may reflect an attempt to shelter marital funds.  
              No 401(k) statement was produced    , so the declared $412,800 balance is unverified, and the nature of the ACH debits cannot be confirmed from the production.  
        I. Expense-Reimbursement Commingling and Self-Approval (PROD-007)  
              Self-approval:         Per Section 5.3 of the NovaBridge Operating Agreement, Respondent (60% managing member)             self-approves         his own expense reimbursements without co-member (Jared Ostrowski) review. This governance structure permits personal expenses to be run through the business without independent oversight.  
              Possible personal-expense reimbursement / double-dip:  
          On             5/17/2024    , the personal credit card was charged             $2,149.50         at         “    Pinnacle Electronics — Personal Devices.    ”         On the same date, the NovaBridge expense report lists             $2,150.00         to         “    TechVault Solutions — Technology Equipment — Personal Devices,    ”         reimbursed via direct deposit on 5/20/2024. Same date, near-identical amount, both labeled         “    personal devices.    ”  
              Sapphire Sky Travel         appears as a $3,400 reimbursed business expense (4/3/2024,         “    client appreciation event planning    ”    )             and         as a             $6,200 personal credit-card charge         (7/8/2024). Whether the July charge was also submitted for Q3 reimbursement is unknown.  
              Untraced reimbursements:         The H1 2024 expense reports total             $12,662    , but only             $5,550         in reimbursement deposits appear in the produced checking account ($3,400 on 4/10; $2,150 on 5/20). The remaining             ~$7,112         is not traceable to the produced account and may have been deposited elsewhere (tying back to the undisclosed-accounts concern).  
        J. Missing and Unverified Productions  
                          Item  
          Status  
          Concern  
            401(k) statement (acct. 3310)  
          Not produced  
          $412,800 balance unverified; loan/contribution activity unknown  
            Joint checking (4821) / savings (4822)  
          Not produced  
          $41,090 combined balance unverified; Respondent has access  
            418 Birchwood mortgage statements  
          Not produced  
          $218,400 balance and payment history unverified  
            2021–2022 tax returns  
          Not produced  
          Only 2023 excerpts produced; prior-year income/rental history unknown  
            NovaBridge entity records (RFP 16–22)  
          Reserved / not produced  
          Operating agreement, Form 1065, financials, bank statements, full K-1 package outstanding  
            Formal privilege log  
          Promised within 10 business days  
          Not yet received  
           K. Additional Minor Discrepancies  
              Filing date of the declaration:         The declaration states         “    Filed: September 20, 2024    ”         (verification and certificate of service both dated 9/20/2024); the cover letter describes it as         “    filed with the Court on or about October 4, 2024.    ”         Two-week discrepancy.  
              Case number on bank production stamp:         PROD-004 bears         “    Case No. 2024-DR-        00847    ”         (transposed digits); the correct number is 2024-DR-        01847    . Clerical, but should be corrected.  
              Auto insurance:         Declaration lists $210/month; bank records show $312/month to Progressive (may cover both vehicles).  
              Auto-loan payment:         Declaration lists a $425/month auto-loan payment to         “    Fairview Community Bank Auto Finance    ”    ; no such debit appears in the produced checking statements. Payment source/balance should be verified.  
              Cover-email date:         The production transmittal email contains no date in the Date field.  
      
         V. Recommended Follow-Up Discovery  
       1. Subpoena Harborstone Financial Group (Priority 1)  
      Serve a subpoena duces tecum on Harborstone Financial Group for all records of account ending 2291 and any other accounts in Darren Kwan’s name (or held for his benefit), January 2023–present, to trace the $85,000 wire of 6/15/2024 and identify any further transfers.  
        2. Pin Down the Date of Separation  
      Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to state, under oath, the precise date of separation and the factual basis, and to reconcile the June 1, 2024 date in the declaration with the August 1, 2024 date in his counsel’s privilege log. Consider a Request for Admission that separation occurred on or after August 1, 2024.  
        3. Compel Missing Account Statements  
      Move to compel (or demand supplementation for) the following, all of which Respondent can produce:         - 401(k) account ending 3310 (Pinnacle Retirement Services): full statements 2023–present, loan documents (if any), and contribution/withdrawal records — with particular attention to the $14,644.60 lump sum of 8/1/2024 and the $1,437.50/month ACHs.         - Joint checking (4821) and joint savings (4822): statements 2023–present.         - 418 Birchwood Lane mortgage statements: 2023–present (to verify the $218,400 balance and payment history).         - All credit-card accounts in Respondent’s name (including any         “    First National Card Services    ”         card), 2023–present.  
        4. Supplemental Production re: 1035 Elm Street, Unit C  
      Demand the deed/title, purchase and loan documents (origination 10/8/2019), all mortgage statements (2019–present), the tenant lease and rent ledger, Schedule E for tax years 2020–2022, and an explanation for the property’s omission from the financial declaration. Request a current market valuation or appraisal.  
        5. Entity-Level NovaBridge Discovery (RFP Nos. 16–22)  
      Demand the supplemented production the cover letter reserves: the NovaBridge Operating Agreement (including Section 5.3), complete Form 1065 returns (2021–2023), entity financial statements and bank statements, the full K-1 package, member capital-account ledgers, and distribution records. Also demand             Jared Ostrowski’s expense reports         for the same period to test whether the self-approval/personal-expense pattern is unique to Respondent, and             Q3–Q4 2024         expense reports for Respondent (to test whether the July Sapphire Sky Travel charge was reimbursed).  
        6. Identify         “    Kiera M.    ”         and the Venmo Transfers  
      Serve an interrogatory and RFP regarding the identity of         “    Kiera M.,    ”         the purpose of the ~$14,600 in transfers, and any agreement or obligation underlying them. Consider a subpoena to Venmo/PayPal for the transaction history.  
        7. Request for Admissions  
      Serve targeted RFAs to lock down the omissions: (a) admit Respondent owns 1035 Elm Street, Unit C; (b) admit the Harborstone Financial Group account ending 2291 exists; (c) admit the $85,000 wire transfer of 6/15/2024; (d) admit 2023 K-1 ordinary business income was $367,200; (e) admit the credit card is issued by Fairview Community Bank (acct. 8316); (f) admit/deny each omitted income source (rental, interest, dividends, capital gains).  
        8. Amended / Supplemental Sworn Financial Declaration  
      Demand a corrected sworn financial declaration reflecting the Elm Street property and mortgage, the Harborstone account, the $367,200 K-1 income (with reconciliation to distributions), the rental/investment income, and accurate valuation dates. Given the materiality of the omissions, consider a motion to compel and, if warranted, a request for sanctions or an adverse-inference instruction.  
        9. Forensic Accountant and Business Valuator  
      Retain a forensic accountant to (a) reconstruct Respondent’s actual income using pass-through figures; (b) trace the $85,000 wire and the Venmo transfers; (c) quantify post-separation dissipation (luxury credit-card spending, country-club dues, travel, furnishings); (d) normalize NovaBridge expenses for personal items routed through the business; and (e) value the 60% NovaBridge interest (capital account $675,800 plus retained earnings, subject to operating-agreement restrictions). Retain a business valuator for the NovaBridge interest and an appraiser for both real properties.  
        10. Privilege-Log Scrutiny  
      Confirm receipt of the formal privilege log (promised within 10 business days) and scrutinize PRIV-001–004. PRIV-003 (    “    Preliminary analysis of the marital estate,    ”         8/20/2024) may bear on what Respondent knew about his assets and when. Evaluate whether the crime-fraud exception applies to any communication reflecting asset concealment or dissipation, given the timing of the $85,000 wire and the omissions identified above.  
        11. Reciprocal Production Reminder  
      Respondent’s First RFP served on Petitioner 9/18/2024 remains outstanding. Calendar the deadline and prepare Petitioner’s reciprocal production, ensuring our client’s records are consistent and complete (particularly the joint accounts and the 403(b)).  
      
         VI. Conclusion and Recommended Next Steps  
      The Respondent’s production, while facially responsive, contains material omissions and inconsistencies that, taken together, suggest the Sworn Financial Declaration understates Respondent’s income, assets, and liabilities and may reflect post-separation dissipation or concealment of marital assets. The three most urgent items are: (1) the undisclosed 1035 Elm Street property; (2) the $85,000 wire to the undisclosed Harborstone Financial Group account; and (3) the $193,200 understatement of pass-through business income.  
          Recommended immediate actions:  
          Authorize the Harborstone Financial Group subpoena and the         “    Kiera M.    ”    /Venmo inquiry this week.  
          Draft and serve the interrogatories, RFAs, and the motion to compel for the missing account statements (401(k), joint accounts, Birchwood mortgage) and the Elm Street documents.  
          Calendar the entity-level NovaBridge supplementation (RFP 16–22) and follow up on the promised privilege log.  
          Engage the forensic accountant and business valuator; provide them with this memorandum and the produced exhibits.  
          Prepare a short letter to opposing counsel identifying the Elm Street omission and the $85,000 transfer and demanding supplementation, creating a record of the discrepancies before motion practice.  
      We will circulate a discovery plan and proposed schedule upon your review of this memorandum.  
      
          Prepared by Jordan Reeves. This memorandum is attorney work product prepared in anticipation of litigation and is privileged and confidential. Do not produce or disclose.
