            For a period of           eighteen (18) months           twelve (12) months           following the Separation Date, Delano shall not, directly or indirectly, whether on his own behalf or on behalf of any other person or entity:  
             (a) solicit, recruit, hire, engage, or encourage, or attempt to solicit, recruit, hire, engage, or encourage, any employee, independent contractor, or consultant of Greenleaf to leave the Company's employ or engagement, or to accept employment or engagement with any other person or entity;  
             (b) solicit, divert, or take away, or attempt to solicit, divert, or take away, the business or patronage of any customer, supplier, vendor, distributor, or business partner of Greenleaf with whom Delano had material contact or about whom Delano obtained confidential information during the last two (2) years of his employment with Greenleaf.  
            For the avoidance of doubt, this non-solicitation provision shall not prohibit Delano from making general solicitations of employment through public advertisements or employment postings not specifically directed at Greenleaf employees. In the event of a breach, Greenleaf shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief and all other available legal and equitable remedies.  
              Section 11. Employment References  
            Upon request from any prospective employer or other third party, Greenleaf agrees to provide           a positive, neutral, or at minimum non-negative employment reference           a neutral employment reference           for Delano. Such reference shall confirm Delano's dates of employment (June 12, 2017, through March 14, 2025), his final title of Vice President of Supply Chain Operations,           and shall include a statement that Delano was a valued member of the Greenleaf team who made significant contributions to the Company's supply chain operations during his tenure.           and that Delano is eligible for rehire.           All reference inquiries regarding Delano shall be directed to Leanne Foss, Human Resources Director, or her designee. Greenleaf shall instruct Sandra Wojcik, Tyler Huang, Patricia Engel, Leanne Foss, and all other current employees who may receive reference inquiries concerning Delano to refrain from providing any information beyond the reference described above and shall not, under any circumstances, provide any information suggesting that Delano was terminated for cause, for performance-related reasons, or in connection with any internal complaint or investigation. Greenleaf shall issue written instructions to the foregoing individuals within seven (7) calendar days of the Effective Date implementing the requirements of this Section 11.  
              Section 12. Mutual Release by Greenleaf  
            Greenleaf, on behalf of itself and each of the Releasees, hereby irrevocably and unconditionally releases, acquits, and forever discharges Delano from any and all claims, demands, actions, causes of action, suits, damages, losses, expenses, and liabilities of every kind and nature, whether known or unknown, suspected or unsuspected, that Greenleaf has, has ever had, or may hereafter have against Delano, arising out of, related to, or in any way connected with Delano's employment with Greenleaf or the termination thereof, from the beginning of time through the Effective Date, except for claims arising from: (i) Delano's fraud, embezzlement, or criminal conduct discovered after the Effective Date; (ii) Delano's breach of any provision of this Agreement; or (iii) any obligation of Delano that survives termination of employment under any prior written agreement between the Parties, to the extent that such obligation is not superseded by this Agreement.  
    
              Section 13. No Admission of Liability  
            This Agreement is a compromise and settlement of disputed claims. Nothing contained in this Agreement, including the payment of the Settlement Payment, shall be construed as an admission of liability, wrongdoing, or violation of any federal, state, or local law, regulation, or ordinance by either Party. Greenleaf specifically denies that it engaged in any unlawful retaliation, age discrimination, or other wrongful or discriminatory conduct toward Delano or any other individual. Delano specifically denies any wrongdoing or misconduct in connection with his employment with Greenleaf. The Parties enter into this Agreement solely for the purpose of avoiding the costs, burdens, and uncertainties of litigation and to achieve a full and final resolution of all disputes between them.  
              Section 14. Re-Employment Eligibility  
            Delano shall not be barred from future employment with Greenleaf and shall be considered for open positions for which he is qualified, on the same basis as any other external applicant, without prejudice or adverse consideration arising from the circumstances of his separation from employment or the claims asserted in connection with this Agreement. Greenleaf shall not maintain any internal notation, flag, code, or designation in its human resources records, personnel files, applicant tracking systems, or any other employment-related database or record that would indicate Delano is ineligible for rehire or that would otherwise adversely affect Delano's candidacy for future employment with Greenleaf.  
              Section 15. Representations and Warranties  
             (a)          Delano represents and warrants that:  
             (i) He has not assigned, transferred, conveyed, or otherwise disposed of any claim, demand, right, or cause of action released herein to any person or entity;  
             (ii) He has not filed any lawsuit, complaint, charge, or other proceeding against Greenleaf or any of the Releasees with any court, tribunal, or governmental agency, other than the Oregon OSHA complaint referenced in the Recitals above (Complaint No. OR-OSHA-2024-11872), and he agrees not to file any such lawsuit, complaint, charge, or proceeding in the future with respect to any claim released herein, except as may be required by law or to enforce the terms of this Agreement;  
             (iii) He has the full legal capacity and authority to execute this Agreement and to perform his obligations hereunder, and his execution and performance of this Agreement does not conflict with or violate any other agreement to which he is a party.  
             (b)          Greenleaf represents and warrants that:  
             (i) It has the full corporate power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder, and the execution, delivery, and performance of this Agreement has been duly authorized by all necessary corporate action;  
             (ii) The person executing this Agreement on behalf of Greenleaf is duly authorized to do so and to bind Greenleaf to the terms and conditions contained herein.  
                 (iii) The accelerated vesting of Restricted Stock Units contemplated by Section 3(d) has been duly approved by the Compensation Committee of the Board of Directors of Greenleaf in accordance with the terms of the Greenleaf 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, and Greenleaf has full power and authority to cause such accelerated vesting to occur.   
              Section 16. Execution and Consideration Period  
            Delano shall have           fourteen (14) calendar days from the date of receipt           twenty-one (21) calendar days from the date of receipt           of this Agreement to review, consider, and execute it. If Delano does not execute and return a signed copy of this Agreement to Engel & Associates, P.C., or to Greenleaf's General Counsel           within the fourteen (14) calendar day period           within the twenty-one (21) calendar day period          , this offer of settlement shall automatically expire and be of no further force or effect, and neither Party shall have any obligation hereunder.           Following Delano's execution of this Agreement, Delano shall have seven (7) calendar days to revoke this Agreement as provided in Section 5(g). This Agreement shall become effective and enforceable only upon the expiration of such seven (7) day revocation period without revocation.   
            This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original and all of which, when taken together, shall constitute one and the same instrument. Facsimile signatures and electronic (PDF) signatures transmitted by email or other electronic means shall be deemed original signatures for all purposes of this Agreement and shall be sufficient to bind each Party hereto.  
    
              Section 17. Severability  
            If any provision of this Agreement, or the application thereof to any person, entity, or circumstance, is held to be invalid, illegal, or unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, such invalidity, illegality, or unenforceability shall not affect any other provision of this Agreement, and the remaining provisions shall continue in full force and effect as if such invalid, illegal, or unenforceable provision had not been included herein. The Parties agree that in the event any provision is determined to be invalid, illegal, or unenforceable, such provision shall be modified to the minimum extent necessary to make it valid, legal, and enforceable while preserving the Parties' original intent to the greatest extent possible.  
               Section 18. Entire Agreement         
            This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous negotiations, representations, agreements, promises, and understandings, whether written or oral, relating to such subject matter, including but not limited to any prior offer letters, employment agreements, memoranda of understanding, or           term sheets exchanged between the Parties in connection with the resolution of the disputes addressed herein.           term sheets exchanged between the Parties in connection with the resolution of the disputes addressed herein; provided, however, that this Agreement shall not supersede or modify the Greenleaf 2021 Equity Incentive Plan or the RSU Grant Agreement dated July 1, 2022, each of which shall remain in full force and effect in accordance with its terms to the extent not expressly modified by this Agreement.           No amendment, modification, supplement, or waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall be effective unless made in writing and signed by both Parties. No waiver of any breach or default under this Agreement shall constitute a waiver of any subsequent breach or default.  
                  Section 19. Governing Law; Dispute Resolution   
                This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Oregon, without regard to its conflict-of-laws principles.   
                Except as provided in subsection (c) below, any dispute, claim, or controversy arising out of or relating to this Agreement, or the breach, enforcement, interpretation, or validity thereof, shall be finally resolved by binding arbitration administered by JAMS in accordance with its Employment Arbitration Rules and Procedures then in effect, before a single arbitrator, in Portland, Oregon. The arbitrator shall have authority to award any remedy available under applicable law, and judgment on the award may be entered in any court of competent jurisdiction. Each Party shall bear its own fees and costs in such arbitration, except that the prevailing Party in any arbitration or action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs from the non-prevailing Party.   
                Notwithstanding the foregoing, either Party may apply to any court of competent jurisdiction located in Multnomah County, Oregon, for injunctive or other equitable relief to enforce Sections 6, 7, 9, or 10 of this Agreement, or to preserve the status quo pending arbitration, without first submitting to arbitration, and each Party consents to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of such courts for such purposes.   
                Nothing in this Section 19 shall prevent either Party from filing a charge or complaint with, or participating in any investigation or proceeding conducted by, any governmental agency, to the extent permitted by law.   
                BY EXECUTING THIS AGREEMENT, THE PARTIES ACKNOWLEDGE AND AGREE THAT THEY ARE WAIVING THEIR RIGHT TO A JURY TRIAL AND THEIR RIGHT TO LITIGATE ANY COVERED DISPUTE IN A COURT OF LAW, EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY PROVIDED IN SUBSECTION (c).   
             IN WITNESS WHEREOF         , the Parties have executed this Settlement Agreement and General Release of All Claims as of the date first set forth above.  
             GREENLEAF ORGANIC FOODS, INC.  
            By:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
            Name (printed):           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
            Title:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
            Date:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
             MARCUS R. DELANO  
            By:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
            Name (printed): Marcus R. Delano  
            Date:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
             Approved as to Form:  
             ENGEL & ASSOCIATES, P.C.  
            By:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
            Rachel Engel, Esq. Oregon Bar No. 091247 Counsel for Marcus R. Delano  
            Date:           _          _          _          _          _          _          _          _  
            Prepared by Engel & Associates, P.C. | Confidential Settlement Document | May 5, 2025

### FILE: invoice-review-memo.docx
          INVOICE REVIEW MEMORANDUM  
          PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF BILLING DISPUTE RESOLUTION  
                          To:  
          Rachel Dominguez, Associate General Counsel (Designated In-House Counsel)  
                Cc:  
          Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel  
                From:  
          Legal Billing / Invoice Review  
                Date:  
          June 12, 2025  
                Re:  
          Review of Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN — Hargrove & Linden LLP —             Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC    , Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.)  
                Billing Period:  
          May 1 – May 31, 2025  
                Invoice Date / Total Claimed:  
          June 5, 2025 /             $201,822.50  
         
       I. Executive Summary and Recommendation  
      Hargrove & Linden LLP (    “    the Firm    ”         or         “    H&L    ”    ) has submitted Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN seeking             $201,822.50         for the May 2025 billing period ($193,372.50 in attorney fees and $8,450.00 in expenses). After review against the Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan (Feb. 10, 2025), the Pinnacle Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (Jan. 1, 2023) (the         “    Billing Guidelines    ”    ), the April 28 / May 1, 2025 staffing correspondence, and the Firm’s own June 1, 2025 Case Status Report, this review identifies             multiple material compliance failures         and recommends a substantial reduction.  
          Headline recommendation:  
                     Invoice total as submitted  
          $201,822.50  
                Recommended payable  
              $149,471.25  
                Recommended reduction  
              $52,351.25 (25.9%)  
         The recommended payable comprises             $143,121.25         in guideline-compliant attorney fees and             $6,350.00         in reimbursable expenses. In addition, the invoice contains a             footing defect    : the 117 line-item time entries actually total             $215,680.00         in fees (483.5 hours), which is             $22,307.50 more         than the $193,372.50 stated on the Summary page and Time Detail subtotal. The invoice does not foot and should be returned for correction before payment.  
      The principal findings driving the reduction are:  
              Two unauthorized timekeepers.         Nina Barros (a second mid-level associate) and Priya Sengupta (a second junior associate) billed a combined             $33,400.00         without the prior written approval required by Billing Guidelines §4.2 and the express terms of the Approved Staffing Plan. The April 28 request to add Ms. Barros was             expressly deferred         by Ms. Dominguez on May 1 (    “    let me review this with Sandra and get back to you    ”    ); no approval ever issued. No request to add Ms. Sengupta was made at all.  
              Contract-attorney rate overcharge.         Marcus Tate was billed at $210/hr, exceeding both the $200/hr §5.1 cap and the $185/hr pass-through rate approved in the Staffing Plan — a             $3,862.50         overcharge.  
              Pervasive block billing.         Forty-three (43) entries combine two or more discrete tasks in a single line item, triggering the automatic 25% reduction under §5.4 —             $26,693.75    .  
              Task-to-timekeeper mismatches.         Partner time was spent on document review and cite-checking, and a junior associate performed routine first-level document review, contrary to §6.1 —             $5,290.00    .  
              Over-staffed meetings and deposition.         Five timekeepers billed a single internal strategy call (§4.3 cap: 3) and three attorneys billed the Dr. Liu deposition without the advance approval §4.3 requires for more than two —             $3,312.50    .  
              Non-reimbursable travel.         $2,100.00 in         “    travel    ”         for two Atlanta-based attorneys to attend a deposition held at the Firm’s own Atlanta office is local travel/commuting plus meals, which is non-reimbursable under §§6.4 and 8.2.  
              Budget overage without pre-approval and missing reconciliation.         Attorney fees exceeded the §7.3 pre-approval threshold ($189,750) with no advance notice, and the invoice omits the §7.2/§9.1(g) budget-to-actual reconciliation.  
      The specific entry-level reductions below bring the compliant fee to $143,121.25, which is             below         the §7.3 budget ceiling of $189,750; accordingly the budget ceiling does not further reduce the payable, although the overage itself remains a compliance violation that must be cured prospectively.  
      
        II. Documents Reviewed  
              Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN         (Hargrove & Linden LLP), with Summary, Time Detail (117 entries), Expense Detail, and Rate Schedule tabs.  
              Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan    , Hargrove & Linden LLP to Pinnacle, dated February 10, 2025 (signed/approved by R. Dominguez and S. Whitmore).  
              Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines    , effective January 1, 2023.  
              Staffing correspondence    : K. Cho to R. Dominguez (April 28, 2025) requesting addition of Nina Barros; R. Dominguez to K. Cho (May 1, 2025) deferring the request.  
              Monthly Case Status Report — May 2025    , Hargrove & Linden LLP to Pinnacle, dated June 1, 2025.  
      
        III. Invoice as Submitted  
                           Timekeeper  
          Role  
          Rate  
          Hours (Summary)  
          Fees (Summary)  
            David Hargrove  
          Lead Partner  
          $985  
          38.5  
          $37,922.50  
            Karen Cho  
          Senior Associate  
          $625  
          74.0  
          $46,250.00  
            James Pettersson  
          Mid-Level Associate  
          $475  
          82.5  
          $39,187.50  
            Nina Barros  
          Mid-Level Associate  
          $475  
          45.0  
          $21,375.00  
            Tyler Webb  
          Junior Associate  
          $375  
          35.0  
          $13,125.00  
            Priya Sengupta  
          Junior Associate  
          $375  
          27.5  
          $10,312.50  
            Marcus Tate  
          Contract Attorney  
          $210  
          120.0  
          $25,200.00  
                Total  
       
       
              422.5  
              $193,372.50  
         Plus expenses of $8,450.00 =             $201,822.50 total claimed.  
      
        IV. Findings and Reduction Calculations  
      The reductions below are computed against the             117 line-item time entries as submitted    , which total             $215,680.00         (483.5 hours). Entry-level compliance cannot be assessed from the Summary page alone; the line items are the operative record. The Summary’s understatement of that record is addressed separately in Finding A.  
       Finding A — Invoice Integrity: The Time Detail Does Not Foot to the Summary  
      The 117 time entries sum to             $215,680.00 / 483.5 hours    , but the Summary page and the Time Detail         “    SUBTOTAL    ”         row each state             $193,372.50 / 422.5 hours         — a discrepancy of             $22,307.50 / 61.0 hours    . The variance is concentrated in several timekeepers:  
                             Timekeeper  
          Line-Item Hours  
          Line-Item Fees  
          Summary Hours  
          Summary Fees  
          Variance (Fees)  
            Karen Cho  
          89.5  
          $55,937.50  
          74.0  
          $46,250.00  
          +$9,687.50  
            Marcus Tate  
          154.5  
          $32,445.00  
          120.0  
          $25,200.00  
          +$7,245.00  
            James Pettersson  
          91.0  
          $43,225.00  
          82.5  
          $39,187.50  
          +$4,037.50  
            Nina Barros  
          49.0  
          $23,275.00  
          45.0  
          $21,375.00  
          +$1,900.00  
            Priya Sengupta  
          27.0  
          $10,125.00  
          27.5  
          $10,312.50  
          −$187.50  
            Tyler Webb  
          34.0  
          $12,750.00  
          35.0  
          $13,125.00  
          −$375.00  
            David Hargrove  
          38.5  
          $37,922.50  
          38.5  
          $37,922.50  
          $0.00  
                Total  
              483.5  
              $215,680.00  
              422.5  
              $193,372.50  
              +$22,307.50  
             Disposition.         An invoice whose supporting detail does not reconcile to its stated total cannot be reliably processed or audited. This is a threshold billing-integrity defect.             Recommendation: return the invoice to the Firm with directions to correct the footing (the Summary and subtotal must equal the sum of the line items) and to confirm which figure is intended.         The substantive reductions in Findings B–G are computed from the line items because they are the only record that permits entry-level review; the recommended payable ($143,121.25 in fees) falls below both the line-item total and the stated Summary total, so it is a safe ceiling regardless of how the footing is corrected.  
        Finding B — Unauthorized Timekeepers (Billing Guidelines §4.2; Staffing Plan §§2.3, 2.4, 4) — $33,400.00  
      Section 4.2 requires that any addition to or substitution on the Approved Staffing Plan be             pre-approved in writing         by the Designated In-House Counsel             before         the new timekeeper performs billable work, and provides that after-the-fact changes         “    are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle’s sole discretion.    ”         The Staffing Plan reinforces this: it states that James Pettersson is the             only         mid-level associate on the roster (    “    No other mid-level associate is included    ”    ), and that             only one junior associate         may bill at any given time, with any second junior associate constituting a staffing change requiring prior written approval. The Guidelines further specify that         “    the absence of a response shall not constitute approval.    ”  
          B.1 — Nina Barros (mid-level associate): $23,275.00 (49.0 hours).         Ms. Barros is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Firm’s own Rate Schedule tab marks her status as         “    Pending Approval — Added May 2025.    ”         The documentary record confirms that approval was             never granted    :  
          On             April 28, 2025    , Karen Cho emailed Ms. Dominguez requesting that Ms. Barros be added, estimating 40–50 hours/month.  
          On             May 1, 2025    , Ms. Dominguez replied:             “        Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you. I also want to check where things stand against the current budget and staffing plan on our end. I’ll try to circle back with you early next week.        ”  
      This is an             express deferral    , not an approval — and no subsequent written approval appears anywhere in the record. The Firm nonetheless billed Ms. Barros for 49.0 hours beginning May 1. No exigent circumstances were claimed, no 24-hour notice was given, and no retroactive approval was sought. The Firm’s June 1 Case Status Report acknowledges that Ms. Barros’s involvement was         “    consistent with the             request         submitted to Ms. Dominguez on April 28, 2025    ”         — a request is not an approval.             Recommendation: reject all fees attributable to Ms. Barros — $23,275.00.  
          B.2 — Priya Sengupta (second junior associate): $10,125.00 (27.0 hours).         The Staffing Plan designates Tyler Webb as the assigned junior associate and expressly provides that         “    only one junior associate will be assigned to and authorized to bill on this matter at any given time,    ”         warning that assigning a second junior concurrently         “    would constitute a staffing change requiring prior written approval under Section 4.2.    ”         Ms. Sengupta (a 1st-year associate) billed concurrently with Mr. Webb throughout May — the two overlap on at least seven dates (May 13, 16, 22, 27, 28, 29, and 30). The Firm’s Rate Schedule tab marks her status merely as         “    Added May 2025    ”         (not         “    Approved    ”    ), and             no written request or approval for Ms. Sengupta exists in the record at all.             Recommendation: reject all fees attributable to Ms. Sengupta — $10,125.00.  
          Combined unauthorized-timekeeper reduction: $33,400.00.         Pinnacle retains discretion under §4.2 to grant retroactive approval if it elects; absent such election, these fees are not payable.  
        Finding C — Contract Attorney Rate Overcharge (§5.1; Staffing Plan §§2.5, 6) — $3,862.50  
      Marcus Tate (contract attorney, sourced through Redwood Document Solutions) was billed at             $210/hr    . This rate exceeds (a) the             $200/hr         maximum for contract/staff attorneys under §5.1, and (b) the             $185/hr         pass-through rate expressly approved for this role in the Staffing Plan (§2.5:         “    pass-through at vendor rates with no firm markup    ”    ). Sections 4.4 and 8.4 require contract-attorney and vendor costs to be passed through at actual cost with no markup. The lower of the two governing figures — the             $185/hr         approved pass-through rate — controls.  
          Line-item hours: 154.5. Overcharge per hour: $210 − $185 = $25.  
              Reduction: 154.5 × $25 = $3,862.50         (Tate’s fees reduced from $32,445.00 to $28,582.50).  
      Note: even if only the §5.1 $200 cap were applied, the reduction would be $1,545.00 (154.5 × $10). The $185 approved rate is recommended because it is the rate Pinnacle actually approved and because §4.4/§8.4 prohibit any markup. The Firm should also be required to substantiate that $185 remains the vendor’s actual cost.  
        Finding D — Task-to-Timekeeper Appropriateness (§6.1) — $5,290.00  
      Section 6.1 requires each task to be staffed to the lowest-cost timekeeper capable of performing it, and lists tasks         “    presumptively inappropriate    ”         for senior timekeepers. Four entries violate this rule:  
                              Entry  
          Date  
          Timekeeper  
          Task  
          Billed  
          Appropriate Rate  
          Reduction  
            9  
          05/05  
          D. Hargrove (Partner, $985)  
          Review/analyze third-production documents; prepare findings memo  
          $4,432.50 (4.5h)  
          Junior associate $375  
          $2,745.00  
            43  
          05/14  
          D. Hargrove (Partner, $985)  
          Review/revise cite-check memo; finalize citations  
          $1,970.00 (2.0h)  
          Junior associate $375  
          $1,220.00  
            79  
          05/22  
          T. Webb (Junior, $375)  
          Routine first-level document review for relevance/privilege  
          $1,312.50 (3.5h)  
          Contract attorney $200  
          $612.50  
            15  
          05/06  
          J. Pettersson (Mid-Level, $475)  
          Schedule depositions; coordinate court reporter; confirm locations  
          $712.50 (1.5h)  
          Administrative — write off  
          $712.50  
         
       
       
       
       
              Total  
              $5,290.00  
                 Entry 9         — Partner document review is presumptively inappropriate under §6.1(a). The Case Status Report characterizes Mr. Hargrove’s review as strategic (calibrating the review protocol, identifying key documents), which supports reduction to the             junior-associate         rate (exercise of some judgment) rather than the contract-attorney rate. The entry is also block-billed (see Finding E); the task-appropriateness reduction is applied as the primary remedy.  
              Entry 43         — Cite-checking by a partner is presumptively inappropriate under §6.1(b) (appropriate timekeeper: junior associate or paralegal); reduced to the junior-associate rate.  
              Entry 79         — Routine first-level review for relevance/privilege by a junior associate is reduced to the contract-attorney rate under §6.1 (document review by a junior associate that constitutes routine first-level review → contract attorney rate). The narrative (    “    Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege    ”    ) confirms this is routine review, not privilege-judgment work.  
              Entry 15         — Scheduling depositions, coordinating court reporters, and confirming locations are Administrative Tasks under §§2 and 6.1(g)–(h) and are non-compensable overhead. Written off in full.  
        Finding E — Block Billing (§5.4) — $26,693.75 (43 entries)  
      Section 5.4 prohibits combining two or more discrete tasks in a single line item and mandates an             automatic 25% reduction         to each block-billed entry. Block billing is pervasive in this invoice: 43 of the entries by authorized timekeepers combine distinct, independently timeable activities — most commonly         “    draft X; research Y,    ”         “    review/revise X; conference with Z,    ”         and entries stacking three or more tasks. (Examples: Entry 2 —         “    Draft responses to Interrogatories Nos. 12–18; research Georgia Trade Secrets Act preemption…    ”    ; Entry 20 —         “    Review and revise draft motion to compel; analyze MedCore privilege log…; confer with D. Hargrove…    ”    ; Entry 92 —         “    Prepare for and conduct strategy session…; draft outline for expert discovery plan. Review and edit interrogatory responses…    ”    .)  
      The 25% reduction is applied to each block-billed entry’s fee (after any rate adjustment). Entries already fully rejected under Findings B, D, F, or G are excluded to avoid double-counting. The full schedule of 43 entries and the per-entry 25% amounts is set forth in             Appendix A    ; the aggregate reduction is             $26,693.75    .  
      Under §5.4, the Firm may avoid this reduction by resubmitting each block-billed entry as separate, individually-timed line items within 15 days of notice. Pinnacle should so advise the Firm.  
        Finding F — Excess Conference Attendance (§4.3) — $937.50  
      Section 4.3 caps billing timekeepers for any single internal meeting or conference call at             three (3)    , even for Substantive Case-Strategy Sessions, and requires advance designation when more than three are expected. On             May 8, 2025    , five timekeepers billed the same team strategy call (Entries 22–26: Hargrove, Cho, Pettersson, Barros, and Webb). No advance designation was made.  
      After removing Ms. Barros (fully rejected under Finding B), four authorized timekeepers remain — one over the cap. The three timekeepers most central to a discovery-strategy session (Hargrove, Cho, and Pettersson) are retained; the fourth, junior associate Tyler Webb (Entry 26, 2.5h), is the excess.             Reduction: 2.5 × $375 = $937.50         (Entry 26 written off in full).  
        Finding G — Excess Deposition Staffing (§4.3) — $2,375.00  
      Section 4.3 provides that depositions are             not         Court Appearances and that more than             two (2)         billing timekeepers at a deposition requires advance approval requested no later than five business days before the deposition; absent approval,         “    Pinnacle reserves the right to reduce the time of the additional timekeepers in their entirety.    ”  
      On             May 19, 2025    , three attorneys billed the Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition (Entries 60, 61, 62: Hargrove, Cho, and Pettersson), and the Case Status Report confirms all three were         “    present for the full duration.    ”         No advance approval for a third attorney was sought. The two justified billing attorneys are Karen Cho (who conducted the direct examination) and David Hargrove (who provided real-time strategic guidance and credibility assessment on the case’s most critical witness). James Pettersson’s role — managing exhibit presentation, taking notes, and preparing a testimony summary — is supportive/clerical and constitutes the unapproved third timekeeper.             Reduction: Entry 62 written off in full — 5.0 × $475 = $2,375.00.  
      (Note: the Case Status Report states all three attorneys were present for the deposition’s full ~6.5-hour duration, yet Mr. Hargrove billed only 4.0 hours and Mr. Pettersson 5.0 hours for that day. This narrative/hours inconsistency should be resolved when the Firm corrects the invoice, but it does not increase the payable.)  
        Finding H — Budget Overage Without Pre-Approval; Missing Reconciliation (§§7.2, 7.3, 9.1(g))  
      The Approved Staffing Plan establishes a monthly attorney-fee range of             $95,000–$165,000    . Under §7.3, fees exceeding             115% of the high end — $189,750         — require prior written pre-approval, and Pinnacle may reduce any unapproved excess to that ceiling. Attorney fees here exceed $189,750             under either measure         of the invoice ($193,372.50 as stated; $215,680.00 per the line items), and             no pre-approval was sought.         The April 28/May 1 correspondence shows Ms. Dominguez specifically wanted to         “    check where things stand against the current budget    ”         before approving any staffing increase — yet the Firm proceeded without that review.  
      Because the entry-level reductions in Findings B–G bring the compliant fee to             $143,121.25    , which is             below         the $189,750 ceiling, the budget cap does not further reduce the payable. The overage nonetheless remains a             standalone compliance violation    : the Firm exceeded the pre-approval threshold without notice and must obtain pre-approval before any future month that is projected to exceed $189,750.  
      In addition, the invoice             omits the budget-to-actual reconciliation         required by §7.2 and §9.1(g) (approved range, actual fees/expenses, cumulative figures, percentage of phase budget consumed, and narrative explanation of any variance >10% from the midpoint). This must be supplied before payment.  
        Finding I — Expenses (§§8.1, 8.2, 8.3, 8.4) — $2,100.00 Disallowed  
                             #  
          Date  
          Category  
          Vendor  
          Amount  
          Disposition  
            1  
          05/19  
          Court Reporting  
          Grayson Court Reporting Services  
          $3,200.00  
              Allow         (approved vendor; one transcript + one copy; expedited). Subject to vendor receipt and confirmation that expedited service was necessary.  
            2  
          05/18  
          Travel  
          Various  
          $2,100.00  
              Disallow         — see below.  
            3  
          05/31  
          E-Discovery Hosting  
          Tripoint Litigation Support LLC  
          $2,650.00  
              Allow         (approved vendor; within contemplated expense budget). Subject to vendor receipt and confirmation of §8.1 pre-approval.  
            4  
          05/15  
          Courier/Delivery  
          Various  
          $500.00  
              Allow         (time-sensitive service of discovery/filing under §8.1). Subject to receipts; $500 is high for a single month and should be itemized.  
         
       
       
       
              $8,450.00  
              Recommended: $6,350.00  
             Expense #2 — Travel ($2,100.00): disallowed in full.         The Case Status Report states that the Dr. Liu deposition was         “    taken at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia.    ”         Both Mr. Hargrove and Ms. Cho are based at the Firm’s Atlanta office. Travel by Atlanta-based attorneys to their own Atlanta office is             local travel/commuting    , which is non-reimbursable: §6.4 provides that         “    [l]ocal travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable under any circumstances,    ”         and §8.2 treats meals as non-reimbursable except during approved out-of-town travel. No out-of-town travel occurred. The $2,100.00 (mileage, parking, and meals for local travel to the Firm’s own office) is overhead and is disallowed. (The $2,100 aggregate also exceeds the §8.3 $1,000 pre-approval trigger, which was not obtained.)  
      All allowed expenses are             contingent on receipt/vendor-invoice documentation         as required by §8.1; none is indicated as attached to the invoice.  
        Finding J — Other Observations (no separate dollar reduction)  
              Expected-hour-range variances (Staffing Plan §2).         Per the line items, four approved timekeepers exceeded their expected monthly ranges: Hargrove 38.5h (range 15–25), Cho 89.5h (50–80), Pettersson 91.0h (60–90), and Tate 154.5h (80–150). These variances corroborate the budget-overage and over-staffing concerns. The Case Status Report anticipates that partner hours will         “    return to more typical levels    ”         in June/July; Pinnacle should hold the Firm to that.  
              Half-hour rounding.         Every entry is recorded in 0.5-hour increments. This is not a §5.5 violation (0.5 is a multiple of 0.1 and is not a prohibited 0.25 increment), but the uniform half-hour rounding reduces timing precision and should be flagged for future invoices.  
              Redundant senior review (§6.2).         Several work products received both senior-associate review and partner review (e.g., the motion to compel and the Liu deposition outline). A single supervisory review round is presumptively reasonable under §6.2; the Firm should avoid stacking multiple senior reviews on the same deliverable.  
              Invoice-preparation time.         Entry 111 (Hargrove, 1.5h) includes         “    prepare cover memorandum for May 2025 invoice    ”         and Entry 114 (Cho) includes         “    review and compile billing summaries.    ”         Invoice preparation and billing compilation are administrative/overhead and should not be billed at attorney rates; these entries are reduced through the block-billing reduction (Finding E) and are flagged for elimination going forward.

If Pinnacle elects to ratify either addition prospectively, it should do so in writing and only for future months; ratification would not revive the May fees absent an express decision to do so.

### FILE: gap-analysis-memo.docx
 
                HARTWELL & CABOT LLP  
              Attorneys at Law  
              One Gateway Center, Suite 2400, Newark, NJ 07102  •  225 Franklin Street, 18th Floor, Boston, MA 02110  
       
              PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL  —  ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION  —  ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT  
              MEMORANDUM  
              Deliverable 2 of 2 — Gap Analysis & Remediation Memorandum  
                                          TO:  
                       Rebecca Thorsen, SVP & General Counsel, Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.  
            cc: Marcus Delgado, Director of Human Resources  
                          FROM:  
                       Catherine Ng, Partner  /  Daniel Ikeda, Senior Associate, Hartwell & Cabot LLP  
                          DATE:  
                       May 19, 2025  
                          RE:  
                       Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review — Gap Analysis, Risk Assessment, and Prioritized Remediation Plan (NJ, NY, CT, MA, CO, OR, WA)  
                          MATTER:  
                       Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. — Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review (engagement dated April 14, 2025)  
       
                I.  Executive Summary  
            This memorandum sets forth Hartwell & Cabot LLP’s (“H&C” or the “Firm”) gap analysis and risk assessment of Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.’s (“Vantage” or the “Company”) paid leave policies and practices across seven jurisdictions—New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut (current operations) and Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, and Washington (planned expansion). It accompanies the Multi-State Compliance Matrix (Deliverable 1) and should be read together with that workbook, which contains the dimension-by-dimension comparison and color-coded gap indicators underlying the findings below.  
            Our review identifies material compliance deficiencies in every jurisdiction. The Company’s Employee Handbook (Rev. January 15, 2022) was drafted primarily for New Jersey and New York and has not been substantively updated in over three years; it does not address the requirements of any of the four expansion states and is non-compliant in several respects even in the current three-state footprint. The most serious findings are structural rather than incremental: (i) the Company’s self-funded paid family leave benefit is below the statutory benefit level in all seven states and no private-plan application has been filed in any jurisdiction; (ii) the Company provides no employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee’s own serious health condition, and HR staff have been directing employees to an employee-paid voluntary short-term disability plan instead of the state programs those employees are funding through payroll withholdings; (iii) the Company has not registered with the paid leave insurance program in any of the four expansion states, with Massachusetts and Colorado on or past the critical path; and (iv) the paid sick leave policy is non-compliant on accrual rate, caps, carryover, covered uses, and family-member definition in most or all states—deficiencies that are the subject of the pending New Jersey Department of Labor complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117, Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.).  
            We have categorized each deficiency as Critical, High, or Moderate in accordance with the severity framework in the engagement letter. The summary counts are as follows:  
                                      Severity  
                         Definition  
                         Count  
                         Posture  
                           CRITICAL  
                    Active legal exposure, imminent regulatory deadline at risk, or deficiency already resulting in / likely to result in regulatory action, penalties, or litigation.  
                       5  
                       Immediate action required  
                           HIGH  
                    Non-compliance likely to result in enforcement action, monetary penalties, or employee claims if not remediated before operations commence or in the near term.  
                       10  
                       Remediate by July 1, 2025 / each opening  
                           MODERATE  
                    Technical non-compliance, best-practice gaps, or policy ambiguities that may not trigger immediate enforcement but expose the Company over time or on audit.  
                       6  
                       Remediate on handbook revision cycle  
       
            The three most urgent priorities, each of which is detailed in the body of this memorandum, are: (1) immediate registration with the Massachusetts and Colorado paid leave programs; (2) a structural decision on the self-funded paid family leave plan and the paid medical leave gap; and (3) revision of the paid sick leave policy to cure the deficiencies at issue in the Muñoz complaint and to satisfy the requirements of all seven states before the revised Handbook target date of July 1, 2025.  
                II.  Scope, Methodology, and Limitations  
            Scope. This engagement encompasses a compliance review of Vantage’s paid leave policies and practices across the seven jurisdictions identified in the engagement letter, covering paid sick leave, paid family leave, paid medical leave, employer obligations (registration, contributions, withholding, recordkeeping, posting), and PTO/vacation payout on separation. The review addresses both current-state gaps (NJ, NY, CT) and prospective gaps for the four expansion states (MA, CO, OR, WA).  
            Methodology. We compared the Company’s written policies (Employee Handbook, Rev. January 15, 2022, Section 6: Leave Policies) and documented practices against the statutes, regulations, and agency guidance of each jurisdiction. We also reviewed the PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. payroll withholding summary (data as of March 31, 2025), the HR expansion plan memorandum (March 28, 2025), the internal HR email thread (March 10–17, 2025), the NJ DOL complaint correspondence (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117), and a representative sample of the Company’s leave-tracking spreadsheets. Where practice diverges from written policy, we have assessed the practice as well.  
         New Jersey FLI/TDI benefit parameters (duration, maximum weekly benefit) are not detailed in the PinnacleHR withholding report, which addresses NJ contribution rates; those parameters are drawn from the applicable statutes (N.J.S.A. 43:21-25 et seq.) per the engagement's legal-research scope and should be confirmed against current NJDOL guidance.  
            Limitations. Our analysis is based on the laws, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of the date of this memorandum and on the accuracy and completeness of the information and representations provided by the Company. State paid leave law is a rapidly evolving area; legislative or regulatory changes may occur after delivery. This memorandum (i) is outside scope with respect to representation in the pending NJ DOL complaint, which continues under the separate engagement dated February 15, 2025; (ii) does not constitute a federal FMLA compliance review; (iii) does not address local or municipal paid leave ordinances; and (iv) does not include drafting of the revised Handbook, which would be a separate engagement. This memorandum is a compliance diagnostic and does not constitute legal advice as to any individual employee claim.  
                III.  Current-State Gap Analysis (New Jersey, New York, Connecticut)  
             A.  Paid Sick Leave — Cross-Cutting Deficiencies (NJ, NY, CT)  
            The Company’s paid sick leave policy (Handbook §6.2) is non-compliant in all three current states on multiple dimensions. These deficiencies are not state-specific drafting errors; they reflect a policy designed to a single, simplified standard that falls below the requirements of every jurisdiction in which the Company operates.  
              Accrual rate.           The policy accrues sick leave at one hour per 40 hours worked. New Jersey and New York require one hour per 30 hours worked; Connecticut requires one hour per 40 hours (compliant on rate only). The under-accrual is systemic and quantifiable; the leave-tracking sample confirms it (e.g., the complainant in the Muñoz matter accrued 13.0 hours on 520 hours worked, a 1/40 rate).  
              Annual cap.           The policy caps full-time employees at 40 hours and part-time employees at 24 hours per year. New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut each require 40 hours (New York requires 56 hours for employers with 100 or more employees, a threshold Vantage exceeds with 680 New York employees) and none permits a reduced part-time cap. The part-time cap directly underpays part-time employees and is confirmed in the tracking sample.  
              Waiting period.           The policy imposes a uniform 120-day waiting period before accrued sick leave may be used. New Jersey permits a 120-day waiting period (compliant). New York permits no waiting period—employees may use leave as it accrues—so the 120-day rule is non-compliant there. Connecticut’s eligibility is hours-based rather than a flat calendar waiting period, making the 120-day rule at risk. The tracking sample shows employees denied use during the waiting period in both New York and New Jersey.  
              Carryover.           The policy provides no carryover of unused sick leave (balances reset to zero on January 1) and no year-end payout. All three states require carryover (New Jersey permits carryover of up to 40 hours or, alternatively, year-end payout plus front-loading). The tracking sample confirms year-end forfeitures (e.g., 12 hours and 8 hours forfeited for New Jersey employees).  
              Covered uses.           The policy limits permitted uses to the employee’s own illness, injury, or medical appointment. All three states additionally require leave for care of a family member, absences related to domestic or sexual violence (“safe leave”), public-health-emergency and school/place-of-care closures, and (in New Jersey) school-related meetings and functions. The omission of these uses is a central allegation in the Muñoz complaint.  
            Bereavement (as a sick leave use).        Bereavement is not a required paid sick leave use in any of the seven jurisdictions, so the policy's omission of bereavement from the sick leave policy is not a sick-leave compliance gap.  
              Family-member definition.           The policy defines “family member” as spouse, child, or parent only. Each state’s definition is materially broader. New Jersey’s definition (N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1) expressly includes a child of a domestic partner, domestic and civil-union partners, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, any blood relative, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship. The narrow definition is the direct cause of the denial at issue in the Muñoz complaint and is non-compliant in all three states.  
              Documentation threshold.           The policy requires a doctor’s note after two consecutive scheduled workdays. Each state permits documentation to be required only after three or more consecutive days (or, in New York, on reasonable suspicion of abuse). The two-day threshold is more stringent than the statutes permit and creates a denial risk if applied rigidly.  
              Posting and notice.           The Handbook is silent on the state-specific workplace postings and individual hire notices that each state requires. We could not confirm that the required postings are displayed at the Company’s facilities. Posting violations are independently citable.  
             B.  New Jersey — Specific Considerations  
            The New Jersey deficiencies are compounded by the pending NJ DOL complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117). The complaint alleges that the Company denied Carla Muñoz, a warehouse associate at the Edison facility, the right to use accrued earned sick leave to care for her domestic partner’s child—a covered family member under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1—and that the policy’s enumeration of covered uses omits categories required by N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3. The Company’s February 21, 2025 response acknowledged that the policy language “raises legitimate questions” and committed to a policy review by July 1, 2025. The NJ DOL has reserved the right to conduct a broader investigation of the Company’s sick leave practices, policies, and records. Because the deficiencies identified in the complaint (family-member definition and covered uses) are present in the policy as applied to all New Jersey employees, the exposure is not limited to the individual complainant; a broader investigation could surface the accrual-rate, cap, and carryover deficiencies as well. Penalties are available under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-7.  
             C.  New York — Specific Considerations  
            New York presents the most acute paid sick leave exposure among the current states because the Company exceeds the 100-employee threshold, triggering the 56-hour annual entitlement (versus the policy’s 40-hour cap), and because New York permits no waiting period before use of accrued leave (versus the policy’s 120-day waiting period). The tracking sample shows New York employees denied use during the waiting period and balances forfeited at year-end. The combination of a sub-statutory cap, an impermissible waiting period, and no carryover produces a cumulative shortfall for New York employees that is readily quantifiable from payroll and time records.  
             D.  Connecticut — Specific Considerations  
            Connecticut is the only current state in which the Company’s sick leave accrual rate (1/40) matches the statute. However, the part-time cap (24 hours) is non-compliant (Connecticut applies 40 hours regardless of full-time/part-time status), there is no carryover, and the covered-uses and family-member definitions are narrower than required. Connecticut is also the focal point of the paid medical leave gap discussed in Section V.B below: at least four Bridgeport employees have inquired about paid leave for their own serious health conditions and were directed to the employee-paid voluntary short-term disability plan rather than the Connecticut Paid Leave program they are funding through payroll withholdings.  
                IV.  Prospective Gap Analysis (Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, Washington)  
            The four expansion states each impose paid sick leave and paid family/medical leave requirements that the current Handbook does not address. In addition, the Company has not registered with the paid leave insurance program in any of the four states, and no payroll withholding has been configured. The following summarizes the principal prospective gaps; the Compliance Matrix contains the dimension-by-dimension comparison.  
             A.  Massachusetts (facility opens August 1, 2025)  
            Massachusetts is on the critical path. The Massachusetts Paid Family and Medical Leave (MA PFML) program requires employer registration and contribution remittance from the first day any covered employee works in the state. PinnacleHR has advised that it cannot configure withholding until it receives the Company’s registration confirmation, and that its configuration lead time is approximately six to eight weeks. To have withholding operational by the August 1, 2025 opening, registration with the Massachusetts Department of Family and Medical Leave (DFML) should be completed no later than approximately June 1, 2025. As of the date of this memorandum, the Company has not registered.  
            Separately, the paid sick leave policy is non-compliant in Massachusetts: the accrual rate (1/40 versus the required 1/30), the part-time cap (24 hours versus 40 hours), the waiting period (120 days versus the 90-day statutory threshold), the absence of carryover, and the narrow covered-uses and family-member definitions all require correction before the opening. Massachusetts also requires payout of accrued unused vacation upon separation under the Wage Act, M.G.L. c. 149 § 148; the Company’s no-payout policy is non-compliant and exposes the Company to treble damages and attorney’s fees. The 5-day PTO carryover cap is high-risk in Massachusetts for the same reason. The revised Handbook (target July 1, 2025) must address all of these items before the first Massachusetts employee is hired.  
             B.  Colorado (facility opens November 1, 2025; 3 remote hires April 2025)  
            Colorado is the most urgent registration item. Three Colorado-based salaried employees are being hired in April 2025 to work remotely in advance of the Aurora facility opening. If the Colorado FAMLI registration obligation is triggered by the first date a Colorado-based employee performs work—a position the Colorado Division of Family and Medical Leave Insurance generally takes—then the Company’s registration may already be overdue, or will be within days. Even under the more favorable interpretation that registration is tied to the November 1, 2025 facility opening, the six-to-eight-week PinnacleHR lead time requires registration no later than approximately mid-August 2025. We recommend immediate registration and a conservative reading of the trigger date.  
            Colorado is also the only expansion state in which the Company cannot self-fund in lieu of state program participation: the FAMLI private-plan opt-out is limited to local-government employers. The Company must register with and participate in CO FAMLI. The paid sick leave policy is non-compliant in Colorado on the accrual rate (1/40 versus 1/30), the cap (40/24 hours versus 48 hours), the waiting period (120 days versus immediate use—Colorado permits no waiting period), the absence of carryover, and the narrow covered-uses and family-member definitions. Colorado also requires supplemental public-health-emergency leave of up to 80 hours under the Healthy Families and Workplaces Act. Finally, Colorado requires payout of all accrued unused vacation upon separation (C.R.S. § 8-4-101 et seq.); the no-payout policy and the 5-day carryover cap are both non-compliant. A Colorado candidate has already raised the payout issue with the Company’s recruiter.  
             C.  Oregon (facility opens February 1, 2026)  
            Oregon provides more runway but remains on the critical path. Registration with Paid Leave Oregon (administered by the Oregon Employment Department) should be completed no later than approximately December 1, 2025 to allow PinnacleHR configuration before the February 1, 2026 opening. Paid Leave Oregon covers family leave, medical leave (the employee’s own serious health condition), and safe leave (domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, and harassment); the Company’s self-funded plan covers none of the medical or safe-leave components and is below the statutory benefit on rate, cap, and duration. A private-plan option is available in Oregon but only if the private plan exceeds Paid Leave Oregon benefits. The paid sick leave policy is non-compliant in Oregon on the accrual rate, the part-time cap, the waiting period (120 days versus 90 days), the absence of carryover, and the covered-uses and family-member definitions. Oregon also requires payout of accrued unused vacation upon separation (ORS 652.140), with penalty wages of up to 30 days available under ORS 652.150 for late or withheld payment; the no-payout policy and the 5-day carryover cap are non-compliant.  
             D.  Washington (facility opens May 1, 2026)  
            Washington is the furthest out but requires the same advance planning. Registration with the Washington Paid Family and Medical Leave program (administered by the Washington Employment Security Department) should be completed no later than approximately March 1, 2026. Washington PFML covers family leave, medical leave (the employee’s own serious health condition), and qualifying military exigency; the Company’s self-funded plan omits the medical and military-exigency components and is below the statutory benefit on rate, cap, and duration. A voluntary-plan option is available but only if the voluntary plan meets or exceeds Washington PFML benefits. The paid sick leave policy is non-compliant in Washington on the accrual cap (Washington prohibits accrual caps, though a use cap may be permissible), the waiting period (120 days versus 90 days), the absence of carryover (Washington requires full year-to-year carryover), and the covered-uses and family-member definitions. The accrual rate (1/40) does match Washington’s requirement. Washington, like New Jersey and New York, does not require payout of unused vacation if a clear written forfeit policy is in place, so the no-payout policy is permissible there subject to consistent application.  
                V.  Structural Assessment: Self-Funded Paid Family Leave Plan and the Paid Medical Leave Gap  
             A.  Self-Funded Paid Family Leave Plan  
            The Company self-funds a paid family leave benefit providing 50% of the employee’s average weekly wage, capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks per qualifying event (Handbook §6.3). The Company does not participate as the contributing employer in any state family leave insurance program; PinnacleHR processes only the mandatory employee-side withholdings in New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut. The Company has not filed a private-plan application in any jurisdiction. Our assessment is that the self-funded plan is below the statutory benefit level in all seven states and does not qualify as a compliant private-plan substitute in any of them.

            The self-funded plan is below the statutory benefit on the replacement rate, the weekly cap, and the duration in every state. In Colorado, a private-plan substitute is not available to private employers at all. In the remaining six states, a private plan is permitted only if it meets or exceeds (in Oregon, exceeds) the state program in all respects; the current plan fails that test in every state, and no application has been filed. The practical consequence is that employees in New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut are paying state program contributions through payroll withholdings while receiving an inferior self-funded benefit—a dual-coverage structure that creates both overpayment/underpayment confusion and a basis for employee complaints. The Muñoz complaint demonstrates that the Company’s employees are willing to escalate to state agencies when they perceive a gap between what they are paying into and what they are receiving.  
            Recommended structural options. The Company should select one of the following approaches, in consultation with H&C, before the July 1, 2025 handbook revision:  
             Option 1 —            participate in the state-administered program in each jurisdiction and treat the self-funded benefit as a supplemental, top-up benefit (or discontinue it to avoid employee confusion and dual-coverage issues);  
             Option 2 —            file private-plan (or voluntary-plan) approvals in the six states that permit them (NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR, WA), with enhanced benefits that meet or exceed each state’s statutory minimum, and participate in CO FAMLI (no opt-out available); or  
             Option 3 —            discontinue the self-funded plan and rely entirely on the state programs, redirecting employees to file claims through the state programs (with the Company administering job protection and any supplemental top-up).  
            H&C’s preliminary recommendation is Option 1 or Option 3. A private-plan approval (Option 2) is administratively burdensome across six states, requires the Company to assume the insurance risk, and must be re-approved as benefits change; it is generally attractive only where an employer can offer benefits meaningfully above the state floor at lower cost, which is not the case here. Whichever option is selected, the Company must register with and begin remitting contributions to the four expansion-state programs regardless, because registration is a prerequisite to either participation or a private-plan filing.  
             B.  Paid Medical Leave and the Voluntary STD Misdirection  
            The Company provides no employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee’s own serious health condition beyond accrued sick leave (capped at 40 hours) and PTO (Handbook §6.4). The voluntary short-term disability plan through Keystone Benefit Administrators is employee-paid and optional; fewer than 30% of Connecticut hourly employees are enrolled. Every state in which the Company operates or will operate provides a state-mandated benefit for the employee’s own serious health condition: New Jersey TDI (up to 26 weeks), New York DBL (up to 26 weeks), Connecticut Paid Leave (up to 12 weeks), and the MA PFML, CO FAMLI, OR PLO, and WA PFML medical-leave components.  
            The more serious concern is operational. The internal email thread (March 10–17, 2025) establishes that HR staff in Connecticut and New Jersey have been directing employees who inquire about paid leave for their own conditions to the employee-paid voluntary STD plan rather than to the state programs (NJ TDI, NY DBL, CT PL) that those employees are funding through payroll withholdings. At least seven such inquiries have been identified across the current footprint (four in Bridgeport, two in Edison, one in Queens). This pattern of misdirection creates two distinct exposures: (i) employees may have retroactive claims for state benefits they were discouraged from filing; and (ii) the pattern itself is evidence of a systemic administration failure that a state agency investigating a complaint (such as the Muñoz matter) could expand to examine. The General Counsel’s March 17, 2025 interim guidance—directing HR staff not to turn away employees who ask about paid medical leave for their own conditions without checking whether a state program applies, and to escalate uncertain cases—is an appropriate stopgap but must be formalized in the revised Handbook and reinforced with training.  
                VI.  Operational Risk Assessment: Manual Leave Tracking  
            The Company tracks sick leave accruals, usage, and balances through manual Microsoft Excel workbooks maintained by two HR coordinators, one workbook per state. Paid family leave is tracked through a separate paper-based process; PTO is tracked within WorkBridge Pro but is not connected to the sick leave spreadsheets. The spreadsheets are not integrated with WorkBridge Pro (HRIS) or ClockSync Enterprise (time and attendance). This approach has been adequate for three states but presents material compliance and recordkeeping risk as the Company scales to seven states and approximately 2,560 employees.  
            Specific deficiencies identified in the HR expansion plan memorandum and confirmed by the tracking sample include:  
              No state-specific rule differentiation.            The spreadsheets apply a single company-wide policy and do not differentiate state-specific accrual rates, caps, waiting periods, or carryover rules. As documented above, the states differ significantly on each of these dimensions.  
              No automated alerts.            There are no automated notifications for accrual caps, waiting-period eligibility, or carryover deadlines. Coordinators must manually review each employee record. At 2,560 employees this is not sustainable.  
              No audit trail.            Changes to the spreadsheets are not logged. There is no record of original entries, correction dates, or reasons for adjustments. In an audit or complaint proceeding, the Company could not demonstrate how a particular balance was determined.  
              No system integration.            Hours-worked data is manually extracted from ClockSync and re-entered; demographic data is manually cross-referenced against WorkBridge Pro. This creates transcription-error risk and data inconsistency between systems.  
              Limited scope.            The spreadsheets track only earned sick leave. Paid family leave, PTO, and state program claims are tracked elsewhere or not at all. There is no single view of an employee’s total leave entitlements and usage.  
            Recent errors. Internal audits have identified a part-time New Jersey employee whose sick leave balance was incorrectly capped at 24 hours due to application of the wrong accrual rate; a full-time New York employee who was told he could not use accrued sick leave until 120 days after hire (a waiting period whose legality HR could not confirm); and negative balances in the tracking sample (a New Jersey employee at –2.5 hours and a New York employee at –0.75 hours), indicating data-entry or calculation errors. Several states impose specific recordkeeping requirements regarding leave accruals and usage; the manual system’s lack of an audit trail and state-specific logic creates a recordkeeping deficiency independent of the substantive policy gaps.  
            Recommendation. We concur with the Director of Human Resources’ recommendation that the Company invest in a dedicated leave management solution that integrates with WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync Enterprise and can be configured with state-specific rules for all seven jurisdictions. The estimated cost ($40,000–$75,000) falls within the $175,000 compliance remediation budget but would consume a material portion of it; the Company should confirm budget allocation priorities with Finance. The system should be selected by July 1, 2025 and live by Q1 2026 to support the Oregon and Washington openings. In the interim, the manual process must be supplemented with documented state-specific calculation rules, a change log, and a reconciliation procedure against WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync data.  
                VII.  Severity-Ranked Findings  
            The following consolidates the material deficiencies by severity. The Compliance Matrix (Deliverable 1) and the “Gap Summary by Severity” sheet therein contain the complete dimension-by-dimension listing with recommended owners and deadlines.  
             A.  Critical  
             C-1. Massachusetts PFML registration.           The Company has not registered with the MA DFML. With the Worcester facility opening August 1, 2025 and PinnacleHR’s 6–8 week configuration lead time, registration must be completed by approximately June 1, 2025. Any delay cascades into operating in Massachusetts without proper withholding on day one. Estimated annual contributions approximately $68,640.  
             C-2. Colorado FAMLI registration and early remote hires.           The Company has not registered with the CO FAMLI Division. Three Colorado-based employees were hired in April 2025; if registration is triggered by the first Colorado-based hire, registration may already be overdue. Colorado permits no private-employer opt-out, so the Company must participate. Registration no later than approximately September 1, 2025 under the facility-opening interpretation; immediate under the first-hire interpretation. Estimated annual contributions approximately $45,900.  
             C-3. Self-funded paid family leave plan fails equivalency in all seven states; no private-plan application filed.           The plan (50% AWW / $850 cap / 8 weeks) is below the statutory benefit on rate, cap, and duration in every state and does not qualify as a compliant private-plan substitute. Employees in NJ, NY, and CT are paying state contributions while receiving an inferior benefit. A structural decision is required before the July 1, 2025 handbook revision.  
             C-4. No employer-funded paid medical leave; HR misdirection to voluntary STD.           The Company provides no paid medical leave for an employee’s own serious health condition, and HR staff have directed employees to the employee-paid voluntary STD plan instead of the state TDI/DBL/PL programs those employees are funding. At least seven inquiries identified. Creates retroactive-claim and expanded-investigation exposure. Immediate interim guidance in place; structural fix required by July 1, 2025.  
             C-5. PTO/vacation payout non-compliant in MA, CO, and OR.           Massachusetts (Wage Act, treble damages), Colorado, and Oregon each require payout of accrued unused vacation at separation. The no-payout policy and the 5-day carryover cap are non-compliant in these states. Must be corrected before the first MA/CO/OR employees are hired; a Colorado candidate has already flagged the issue.  
             B.  High  
             H-1. Sick leave accrual rate below statutory minimum.           1/40 accrual is below the 1/30 minimum in NJ, NY, MA, CO, and OR. Systemic under-accrual. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-2. Sick leave caps below statutory requirements.           Part-time 24-hour cap non-compliant in NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR (40 hours required regardless of FT/PT); full-time 40-hour cap below NY’s 56 hours and CO’s 48 hours. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-3. 120-day sick leave waiting period exceeds statutory thresholds.           Non-compliant in NY (no waiting period permitted), MA, OR, WA (90 days), and CO (immediate use). Remediate by July 1, 2025 and before each opening.  
             H-4. No sick leave carryover.           All seven states require carryover (or, in NJ, carryover or year-end payout). Policy provides neither. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-5. Sick leave covered uses omit family care, safe leave, PHE, and school-related uses.           Required by all seven states; central allegation in the Muñoz complaint. CO also requires supplemental PHE leave up to 80 hours. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-6. Sick leave family-member definition narrower than all seven states.           Direct cause of the Muñoz denial. Must be expanded to each state’s statutory definition. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-7. Paid family leave qualifying reasons omit military exigency and safe leave.           Six states cover military exigency; three cover safe leave. Policy covers only bonding and caregiving. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-8. Paid family leave eligibility more restrictive than state programs.           12-month/1,000-hour test may exclude employees the state programs cover. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-9. Paid family leave notice requirement does not address unforeseeable leave.           30-day flat notice creates denial risk; HR has inconsistently applied interim guidance, creating discrimination exposure. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-10. Oregon and Washington PFML registration.           OR by approximately December 1, 2025; WA by approximately March 1, 2026. Less urgent than MA/CO but on the critical path given PinnacleHR lead time.  
             C.  Moderate  
             M-1. Sick leave documentation threshold more stringent than statute.           2-day threshold versus 3-day statutory threshold in all seven states. Align to 3-day threshold by July 1, 2025.  
             M-2. State-specific postings and individual notices not verified/implemented.           Posting violations are independently citable. Verify current states immediately; implement expansion states before each opening.  
             M-3. Manual leave-tracking system does not scale to seven states.           No state-specific logic, alerts, audit trail, or integration. Recent errors confirmed. Select system by July 1, 2025; live by Q1 2026.  
             M-4. Connecticut PTO carryover cap at risk.           5-day cap with year-end forfeiture may forfeit “earned” wages in CT. Review by July 1, 2025.  
             M-5. HRIS and time-and-attendance not configured for expansion states.           WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync require configuration/deployment per state, sequenced to openings. Initiate MA procurement immediately.  
             M-6. Connecticut sick leave waiting-period interaction requires verification.           CT PSL eligibility is hours-based; the 120-day calendar rule may be more or less restrictive. Verify against current CT PSL regulations by July 1, 2025.
