                        OR facility go-live: PLO withholding operational; OR sick leave (40 hrs, 90-day use, carryover, safe leave), PTO payout, and posting policies in effect.  
                        HR + IT + PinnacleHR  
                        OR opening (H-10, C-5)  
                          By March 1, 2026  
                        Complete WA PFML registration; provide confirmation to PinnacleHR.  
                        HR + Legal  
                        WA opening 5/1/26 (H-10)  
                          By May 1, 2026  
                        WA facility go-live: WA PFML withholding operational; WA sick leave (no accrual cap, 90-day use, full carryover), PTO payout, and posting policies in effect.  
                        HR + IT + PinnacleHR  
                        WA opening (H-10, C-5)  
                          Within 30 days of WA opening  
                        Post-launch audit of all systems and compliance processes across all seven states.  
                        HR + Legal  
                        All  
                          Ongoing (2025–2026)  
                        Train all supervisors and HR staff on the revised policies, state-specific family-member definitions, covered uses, unforeseeable-leave notice, and the paid medical leave / state-program claims process.  
                        HR (Delgado)  
                        All; supports NJ DOL response  
       
                IX.  NJ DOL Complaint — Resolution Strategy Framework  
            Representation in the pending NJ DOL complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117, Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.) is outside the scope of this engagement and continues under the separate engagement dated February 15, 2025. To the extent the compliance review findings inform the Company’s response posture in that proceeding, we offer the following framework for the Company’s consideration with the litigation team:  
              Remediation as good faith.            The Company’s February 21, 2025 response acknowledged that the policy language “raises legitimate questions” and committed to a policy review by July 1, 2025. The compliance review confirms that the family-member definition and covered-uses deficiencies identified in the complaint are present in the policy as applied to all New Jersey employees. A revised policy adopted before the July 1, 2025 target will support the Company’s good-faith remediation posture.  
              Individual make-whole.            The complaint seeks payment for the unpaid time off or restoration of PTO hours used by Ms. Muñoz. Making the complainant whole for the specific incident is a low-cost, high-value step that demonstrates good faith and may support a cooperative resolution with the Department.  
              Anticipate expanded scope.            The Department has reserved the right to conduct a broader investigation. Because the accrual-rate, cap, and carryover deficiencies affect all New Jersey employees, the Company should be prepared for the possibility that the investigation expands beyond the individual complainant. Completing the policy revision and supervisor training before any expanded inquiry materially improves the Company’s position.  
              Cooperative resolution.            The Company should request that the Department allow a reasonable period—through the July 1, 2025 handbook revision—for completion of the review, policy update, and training before any enforcement action, consistent with the position taken in the February 21 response.  
              Record preservation.            The Company should preserve all leave records, payroll records, and communications relevant to the complaint and to the broader sick leave practices, given the Department’s reserved right to investigate and the recordkeeping deficiencies identified in Section VI.  
                X.  Budget Considerations  
            The Company has allocated a compliance remediation budget of $175,000 encompassing legal fees, system updates, and policy redrafting. H&C estimates professional fees for this engagement in the range of $28,000–$42,000. The principal competing budget demand is the dedicated leave management software ($40,000–$75,000) recommended in Section VI. Additional HR headcount (two generalists, estimated $120,000–$140,000 annualized) is recommended by the Director of Human Resources but would be pursued through the normal budgeting process rather than the remediation budget. We recommend that the Company confirm budget allocation priorities with Finance, and consider whether the software investment should be pursued through a separate capital expenditure request so that the remediation budget can be reserved primarily for legal fees and policy work. The registration and policy-revision workstreams are time-critical and should not be delayed by budget deliberation; the software selection can proceed on the July 1, 2025 RFP timeline with implementation funding confirmed thereafter.  
                XI.  Conclusion  
            Vantage’s paid leave policies and practices require substantial remediation before the multi-state expansion proceeds and to cure existing deficiencies in the current three-state footprint. The good news is that the great majority of the deficiencies are curable through a single, comprehensive revision of the Employee Handbook (target July 1, 2025) combined with timely state program registrations and a structural decision on the self-funded plan. The most time-critical items—Massachusetts and Colorado registration—require immediate action independent of the handbook revision. We are available to discuss these findings with you and the HR team at your convenience, to assist in preparing non-privileged communications for PinnacleHR and Keystone Benefit Administrators where appropriate, and to scope any additional engagement the Company may wish to pursue, including assistance with the state program registrations and drafting of the revised Handbook.  
            We will provide an interim status update on or about May 2, 2025. Given the registration urgency, the Company should anticipate that we may provide an early-action notification regarding Massachusetts and Colorado registration requirements in advance of that update.  
            Respectfully submitted,  
    
             HARTWELL & CABOT LLP  
            Catherine Ng, Partner  
            Daniel Ikeda, Senior Associate  
                This memorandum is privileged and confidential, constitutes attorney work product, and is provided for the purpose of obtaining and implementing legal advice. It is a compliance diagnostic based on the laws, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of May 19, 2025, and on the information and representations provided by the Company; it is not legal advice as to any individual claim and does not guarantee any particular outcome. State paid leave law is rapidly evolving and periodic re-review is recommended. Distribution should be limited to authorized Vantage personnel; sharing with third-party vendors should be limited to non-privileged summaries or operational directives.

### FILE: buy-side-cim-analysis-memo.docx
          CONFIDENTIAL — INTERNAL USE ONLY  
        Buy-Side CIM Analysis Memorandum  
       Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. (    “    CES    ”         or the         “    Company    ”    ) — Project Cascade  
                          To:  
          Marcus Yuen, Partner (Deal Lead); Diane Holbrook, Managing Partner  
                From:  
          David Koh, Associate  
                CC:  
          Sarah Lindgren, Vice President  
                Date:  
          June 25, 2025  
                Re:  
          Buy-side analysis of the CES Confidential Information Memorandum and supporting materials — financial inconsistencies, risk factors, valuation, and IOI recommendations  
             Prepared in connection with the Confidential Information Memorandum and financial exhibits distributed by Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC on June 9, 2025. IOI deadline: July 14, 2025. This memorandum is intended solely for internal use by Thornfield Capital Partners investment professionals and incorporates the sector benchmarks developed in Ms. Lindgren’s June 12, 2025 sector memo. It should not be shared with any external party.  
      
        1. Executive Summary  
          Recommendation: Proceed to a disciplined Indication of Interest, but anchor valuation on independently adjusted trailing EBITDA and use a PFAS-linked earnout to bridge the gap between the seller’s growth narrative and a defensible underwriting case.         CES is a genuinely attractive lower-middle-market environmental services platform that fits Fund III’s sector and geographic focus, and the core business (excluding the disputed PFAS ramp) exhibits real, durable growth. However, the CIM and supporting exhibits contain a pattern of financial inconsistencies, aggressive PFAS projections, and EBITDA adjustments that are likely overstated — issues that are consistent with the pattern observed in our GreenWorks Remediation and Allied Waste Systems investments and that materially affect both earnings quality and valuation.  
          Key findings:  
             The financial exhibits do not reconcile to the CIM, and contain internal inconsistencies.         The balance sheet in the financial exhibits shows total assets of $50.0M versus $46.1M in the CIM (a $3.9M variance), equity of $18.8M versus $17.1M, and cash of $4.1M versus $2.3M. The income statement exhibits report operating income of $11.1M that does not tie to the stated Reported EBITDA of $12.1M (a $3.2M internal gap). Segment splits, the customer list, and geographic mix also differ between documents. These are not rounding items; they must be reconciled in QoE before any number in the CIM can be relied upon.  
             The stated DSO of 52 days does not reconcile to the balance sheet.         Year-end accounts receivable of $16.9M against FY2024 revenue of $87.3M implies DSO of approximately 71 days (82 days if unbilled receivables are included) — roughly 19–30 days longer than represented. This suggests working capital is heavier than presented and that the NWC peg and cash-conversion profile need independent verification.  
             PFAS projections are aggressive and underpin the growth story.         The CIM projects PFAS revenue growing 125.8% in FY2025E (to $14.0M) and 57.1% in FY2026E (to $22.0M), with PFAS driving ~64% of incremental revenue through FY2026. Sector data and our GreenWorks experience indicate 30–50% annual growth is the realistic ceiling once a practice exceeds $5M. The August 2024 departure of one of three Professional Engineers (PE headcount now two) creates a near-term capacity bottleneck for the PE-oversight-intensive PFAS work.  
             Maintenance capex is materially understated versus sector norms.         CIM maintenance capex of $3.0M (3.4% of revenue) is 150–200 bps below the 5–7% sector range ($4.4M–$6.1M). Our GreenWorks experience — where pre-close maintenance capex was understated at ~3.5% and ran at 6.2% post-close — is directly on point. A $2.5M annual understatement reduces free cash flow and the implied return.  
             The $1.2M regulatory/legal add-back is likely overstated.         The August 2024 Oregon DEQ enforcement action (improper storage of contaminated soils at CES’s own Portland facility) is presented as fully non-recurring. Sector run-rates of $200K–$500K and our portfolio experience (GreenWorks $275K/yr; Allied $310K/yr) indicate only the amount above a normalized run-rate is defensible — a $300K–$500K haircut, or $2.4M–$5.0M of enterprise value at the guided multiple.  
             Customer concentration is high and the largest contract has already expired.         PNR represents 21.4% of revenue; the top 5 represent 52.5%; the top 10 represent 71.0%. The PNR master service agreement expired March 31, 2025 — before the CIM was distributed — and renewal status is not disclosed. This is the single most important commercial diligence item.  
             Real estate and related-party arrangements require structural resolution.         The Portland HQ is owned personally by the founder/CEO and leased to CES at $1.4M/yr versus an estimated $0.6M market rate. The $0.8M normalization is defensible only if the buyer either acquires the property at fair market value or executes a new arm’s-length lease; the structure of this resolution affects both EBITDA quality and transaction mechanics.  
          Preliminary valuation view.         On a buyer-adjusted trailing basis, FY2024 EBITDA is approximately $16.4M (versus the CIM’s $17.1M), implying an enterprise value of $131M–$164M at 8.0x–10.0x. On a forward basis with a haircut PFAS case, FY2025E EBITDA is approximately $17.2M (versus the CIM’s $19.7M), implying $138M–$172M. We recommend an IOI enterprise value range of             $145M–$165M    , structured as approximately             $140M–$150M of cash consideration at close         plus a             contingent earnout of up to ~$15M–$20M (total consideration ~$140M–$170M depending on earnout achievement)         tied to FY2025–FY2026 PFAS revenue and adjusted EBITDA thresholds. This bridges the seller’s ~$171M+ expectation to a defensible underwriting case while preserving upside if the PFAS ramp is realized.  
      
        2. Transaction Overview and Process  
      Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC (Managing Director Philip Tran) is running a competitive sale of 100% of the equity of CES on behalf of founder/CEO Randall Oakes (68% holder) and the other equity holders. Counsel to the Company is Wexford, Mallory & Chen LLP; auditors are Ridgeline Accounting Group, PC. The process is three-phased:  
              Phase I — IOIs due 5:00 PM PT, Monday, July 14, 2025.         The process letter requires each IOI to specify (i) an enterprise value range on a cash-free, debt-free basis with normalized NWC, identifying trailing vs. projected metrics and the implied EBITDA multiple; (ii) transaction structure, including treatment of existing indebtedness, the founder-owned HQ real estate, and any rollover/earnout; (iii) sources of financing; (iv) remaining diligence items; (v) post-closing management structure and accommodation of Mr. Oakes’s 18–24 month transition; (vi) approvals and timeline to close; (vii) relevant sector experience; and (viii) contacts.  
              Phase II — Management presentations (August 2025) and confirmatory diligence         for selected bidders, with VDR access and site visits to Portland (and Boise/Seattle if requested).  
              Phase III — Final bids and definitive documentation    , with a draft stock purchase agreement distributed for markup.             Target signing/closing: Q4 2025.  
      Existing indebtedness with Cascadia Commercial Bank (senior term loan SOFR + 3.75%, maturing August 31, 2026, plus a $8.0M revolver) is expected to be refinanced or repaid at closing. The seller seeks a partner to support continued growth (particularly PFAS) while providing liquidity; Mr. Oakes intends to exit day-to-day operations within 18–24 months post-close and is willing to serve in a transitional advisory capacity.  
      
        3. Company Overview and Investment Thesis  
      CES is a Delaware C-corporation founded in 2009 and headquartered in Portland, Oregon, with additional facilities in Seattle, WA and Boise, ID. The Company operates an integrated environmental services platform across a four-state Pacific Northwest territory (Oregon, Washington, Idaho, Northern California) through three segments: Environmental Remediation (FY2024 revenue $48.2M, 55.2%), Industrial Cleaning ($27.8M, 31.8%), and Emergency Response ($11.3M, 12.9%). Total FY2024 revenue was $87.3M (12.5% CAGR FY2021–FY2024). The Company employs 412 people, holds hazardous waste transporter permits in all four states plus an Oregon DEQ contractor license, and reports an EMR of 0.87.  
          Genuinely attractive attributes (independently assessed):  
              Integrated, regulated platform with a real moat.         Four-state hazmat transporter permits, the Oregon DEQ contractor license, and PE-stamped remediation capabilities take years to replicate and create meaningful barriers to entry. The integrated remediation/cleaning/emergency-response model drives cross-selling and share-of-wallet expansion.  
              Durable core growth, even excluding PFAS.         Traditional remediation grew from $34.8M (FY2021) to $42.0M (FY2024) and industrial cleaning from $19.2M to $27.8M over the same period — respectable organic growth in a market that generally grows 5–10% annually.  
              Regulatory tailwinds are real.         EPA’s 2024 PFAS drinking water rule, CERCLA designation activity, and active Oregon/Washington state enforcement underpin multi-year demand for investigation, remediation, and compliance work.  
              Revenue visibility.         $42.6M contracted backlog plus $31.4M pipeline ($74.0M total) as of 12/31/2024; ~77.5% of backlog expected to convert within twelve months. Reported 90% MSA renewal rate and no top-10 customer losses in five years.  
              Recurring revenue characteristics.         MSA-based industrial cleaning on scheduled turnaround cycles, multi-year remediation lifecycles, and standby emergency-response retainers provide a predictable base.  
              Platform for consolidation.         A fragmented Pacific Northwest market with 5–7 identified tuck-in targets supports a buy-and-build thesis consistent with Fund III’s strategy.  
              Strong safety record.         EMR of 0.87 is a meaningful commercial qualifier for access to refining and industrial facilities.  
      These attributes support proceeding. The remainder of this memo focuses on the issues that affect price, structure, and diligence — not on whether the asset is worth pursuing.  
      
        4. Financial Analysis  
       4.1 Historical Performance  
      Revenue grew from $61.4M (FY2021) to $87.3M (FY2024), a 12.5% CAGR, with growth accelerating to 14.7% in FY2024. Reported EBITDA grew from $7.4M to $12.1M (12.1% → 13.9% margin), and Adjusted EBITDA from $9.7M to $17.1M (15.8% → 19.6% margin). The reported EBITDA margin of 13.9% is within the 10–15% sector range for companies of this size and service mix, which is reassuring and suggests the core operation is reasonably well managed. The Adjusted EBITDA margin of 19.6% is at the upper boundary of plausibility for the sector (sector adjusted margins typically 15–18%) and is driven entirely by the $5.0M of add-backs analyzed in Section 4.3.  
        4.2 Segment Analysis — PFAS Is the Story  
      The financial exhibits break out PFAS within Environmental Remediation. The trajectory and the projections:  
                              PFAS Revenue ($M)  
          FY2022A  
          FY2023A  
          FY2024A  
          FY2025E  
          FY2026E  
          FY2027E  
            PFAS Remediation  
          1.1  
          3.4  
          6.2  
          14.0  
          22.0  
          27.0  
            YoY growth  
          —  
          209.1%  
          82.4%  
          125.8%  
          57.1%  
          22.7%  
            PFAS % of total revenue  
          1.6%  
          4.5%  
          7.1%  
          14.2%  
          19.6%  
          21.8%  
         The CIM states PFAS will drive ~64% of incremental revenue from FY2024 to FY2026 ($15.8M of $24.7M). Traditional remediation is projected roughly flat ($42.0M → $44.0M), meaning the entire growth narrative — and the projected margin expansion from 19.6% to 21.0%+ — rests on a service line that is two years old and generated $6.2M last year. This concentration of the growth thesis in a single, nascent service line is the central risk in the deal and is addressed in detail in Section 6.1.  
        4.3 Adjusted EBITDA Bridge — Line-by-Line Critique  
      The CIM adds back $5.0M to bridge from $12.1M reported EBITDA to $17.1M Adjusted EBITDA. Each adjustment is assessed below.  
                           Adjustment  
          CIM ($M)  
          Assessment  
          Buyer view  
            Owner compensation normalization  
          1.9  
          Oakes total comp $2.4M less $0.5M replacement. The $2.4M explicitly includes         “    personal expenses allocated to the Company.    ”         The personal-expense component is not quantified and may include legitimate business costs a replacement CEO would also incur.  
          Largely defensible, but haircut ~$0.3M pending verification of the personal-expense component.  
            Related-party lease normalization  
          0.8  
          HQ leased from Oakes at $1.4M vs. $0.6M market. Defensible as a normalization             only if         the buyer pays market rent going forward (via new arm’s-length lease or property acquisition).  
          Defensible, conditional on real-estate resolution (Section 6.6).  
            One-time legal/regulatory costs  
          1.2  
          DEQ enforcement settlement ($875K) + legal fees ($325K) for improper storage of contaminated soils at CES’s             own         Portland facility. Sector run-rate $200K–$500K; our portfolio experience (GreenWorks $275K/yr; Allied $310K/yr) indicates a baseline of regulatory cost is inherent.  
          Overstated; defensible add-back ~$0.7M–$0.9M (haircut $0.3M–$0.5M).  
            Non-recurring equipment relocation  
          0.4  
          Boise depot relocation/expansion, completed FY2024.  
          Defensible as one-time.  
            Stock-based comp (phantom units)  
          0.3  
          Non-cash. All phantom units settle in             cash at closing         — a separate use of funds, not captured here.  
          Defensible as EBITDA add-back, but the cash settlement liability must be quantified for sources & uses.  
            One-time IT system implementation  
          0.1  
          ERP upgrade, completed FY2024.  
          Defensible as one-time.  
             Buyer-adjusted FY2024 EBITDA: $17.1M − $0.4M (regulatory) − $0.3M (owner comp) ≈ $16.4M         (range $16.1M–$16.8M). The related-party lease, relocation, IT, and phantom add-backs are retained as defensible. The two principal haircuts (regulatory and owner comp) reduce Adjusted EBITDA by ~$0.7M, which at 8.0x–10.0x equates to ~$5.6M–$7.0M of enterprise value before considering the larger PFAS and capex issues.  
        4.4 Free Cash Flow and Capital Intensity  
      Reported free cash flow is thin relative to EBITDA and is the area where the capex and working-capital issues compound:  
                           ($M)  
          FY2021  
          FY2022  
          FY2023  
          FY2024  
            Cash from operations (CIM)  
          5.8  
          6.4  
          7.8  
          9.2  
            Capital expenditures  
          (3.6)  
          (4.1)  
          (5.8)  
          (7.2)  
            Free cash flow (CIM)  
          2.2  
          2.3  
          2.0  
          2.0  
            Reported EBITDA  
          7.4  
          8.6  
          10.3  
          12.1  
            FCF / Reported EBITDA  
          30%  
          27%  
          19%  
          17%  
         FCF conversion has declined from ~30% to ~17% of reported EBITDA as capex (notably growth capex for PFAS equipment and the Boise expansion) and working-capital build (accounts receivable grew from $10.8M to $16.9M) have absorbed cash. Two implications: (i) the business is more capital- and working-capital-intensive than the EBITDA headline suggests, which constrains leverage capacity and the equity check; and (ii) if maintenance capex is understated by ~$2.5M (Section 6.2), true FCF is even lower — roughly breakeven on a normalized basis in FY2024 — which is a material underwriting concern that must be stress-tested.  
        4.5 Balance Sheet and Net Debt  
      The CIM reports total debt of $17.3M (term loan $14.2M, of which $2.3M current; revolver $3.1M drawn against an $8.0M commitment) and cash of $2.3M, implying net debt of ~$15.0M. The term loan matures August 31, 2026 — near-term and to be refinanced at closing per the process letter. Net working capital of $11.8M (13.5% of revenue) is within the sector range of 10–14%, though the DSO discrepancy discussed in Section 5.2 suggests the quality of that working capital warrants scrutiny. The financial exhibits’ balance sheet differs materially from the CIM’s (Section 5.1), including $2.5M of         “    Goodwill & Intangible Assets    ”         that is not mentioned or explained anywhere in the CIM despite no acquisitions being described — an item that requires explanation.  
      
         5. Data Integrity and Reconciliation Issues  
      A buy-side analysis must first establish whether the numbers can be relied upon. Across the four documents (CIM, financial exhibits, customer/backlog report, and process letter), there are numerous non-reconciling figures. The most material are summarized below; a full reconciliation table is in the Appendix.  
       5.1 CIM vs. Financial Exhibits — Balance Sheet (FY2024)  
                          Line item ($M)  
          CIM  
          Exhibits  
          Variance  
            Cash & equivalents  
          2.3  
          4.1  
          (1.8)  
            Accounts receivable, net  
          16.9  
          16.9  
          0.0  
            Other current assets  
          4.8  
          4.1  
          0.7  
            Total current assets  
          24.0  
          25.1  
          (1.1)  
            PP&E, net  
          18.7  
          21.3  
          (2.6)  
            Other non-current assets  
          3.4  
          3.6  
          (0.2)  
                Total assets  
              46.1  
              50.0  
              (3.9)  
            Accounts payable  
          5.4  
          6.1  
          (0.7)  
            Total current liabilities  
          12.2  
          13.3  
          (1.1)  
            Total liabilities  
          29.0  
          31.2  
          (2.2)  
                Stockholders’ equity  
              17.1  
              18.8  
              (1.7)  
         The exhibits also carry $2.5M of         “    Goodwill & Intangible Assets    ”         with no corresponding disclosure in the CIM (no acquisitions are described). Total assets differ by $3.9M and equity by $1.7M — these are not rounding items.  
        5.2 CIM vs. Financial Exhibits — Income Statement (FY2024) and Internal Inconsistency  
                          Line item ($M)  
          CIM  
          Exhibits  
          Variance  
            Revenue  
          87.3  
          87.3  
          0.0  
            Gross profit  
          29.5  
          28.8  
          0.7  
            Gross margin  
          33.8%  
          33.0%  
          0.8 pp  
            SG&A  
          (14.0)  
          (12.1)  
          (1.9)  
            Other operating expenses  
          (3.4)  
          (1.8)  
          (1.6)  
            Reported EBITDA  
          12.1  
          12.1  
          0.0  
            D&A  
          (4.3)  
          (4.2)  
          (0.1)  
            Operating income  
          7.8  
          11.1  
          (3.3)  
            Pre-tax income  
          6.7  
          9.0  
          (2.3)  
            Net income  
          5.0  
          6.7  
          (1.7)  
             Internal inconsistency within the exhibits:         the income-statement sheet reports         “    Operating Income (EBIT)    ”         of $11.1M and D&A of $4.2M, yet states         “    Reported EBITDA    ”         of $12.1M. EBIT plus D&A equals $15.3M, not $12.1M — a $3.2M internal non-reconciliation on the same sheet. The CIM’s figures (EBITDA $12.1M; operating income $7.8M) are internally consistent and tie to the EBITDA bridge, which suggests the exhibits’ EBIT line is mislabeled or erroneous. Either way, the exhibits as delivered cannot be relied upon without reconciliation.  
          DSO does not reconcile.         The CIM states DSO of 52 days. Year-end accounts receivable of $16.9M against FY2024 revenue of $87.3M implies DSO of             ~71 days         (and ~82 days if the exhibits’ $2.7M of unbilled receivables are included). To support a 52-day DSO, receivables would need to be ~$12.4M, not $16.9M. This ~19–30 day discrepancy is significant: it implies working capital is heavier than represented, cash conversion is slower, and the NWC peg for the transaction should be set on verified, not stated, metrics.  
        5.3 CIM vs. Customer Backlog Report — Customer List and Other Items  
      The top 5 customers are identical in name and revenue across both documents, but             customers #6–#10 are entirely different entities         despite identical revenue amounts and an identical top-10 total ($62.0M, 71.0%):  
                           Rank  
          CIM name  
          Backlog report name  
          Revenue ($M)  
            6  
          Cascade Terminals, Inc.  
          Clearwater Municipal Utility District  
          3.8  
            7  
          Puget Sound Utilities Group  
          Northshore Industrial Park  
          3.6  
            8  
          City of Portland — BES  
          Summit Materials Processing  
          3.2  
            9  
          Northern Pacific Rail Systems  
          Timberline Construction Group  
          2.9  
            10  
          Tidewater Chemical Corp.  
          Harborview Port Authority  
          2.7  
         Additional cross-document inconsistencies:  
              Segment labels differ         for several top-5 customers (e.g., PNR: CIM         “    Industrial Cleaning, Remediation    ”         vs. report         “    IC, ER    ”    ; Meridian: CIM         “    Industrial Cleaning    ”         vs. report         “    IC, Rem    ”    ; Willamette: CIM         “    Remediation, IC    ”         vs. report         “    IC    ”    ).  
              PNR relationship start date:         CIM states 2011; backlog report states 2012.  
              Geographic mix:         CIM states         “    approximately 75%    ”         of revenue in Oregon + Washington; the backlog report’s state breakdown shows 82.3% (Oregon 54.5% + Washington 27.8%) — a ~7 percentage-point variance.  
              Segment revenue, FY2021:         CIM Remediation $34.1M / Industrial Cleaning $20.1M / Emergency Response $7.2M; exhibits $34.8M / $19.2M / $7.4M (totals match at $61.4M, but the split differs).  
              Segment revenue, FY2025E:         CIM Remediation $56.0M / Industrial Cleaning $29.5M / Emergency Response $13.0M; exhibits $56.5M / $30.2M / $11.8M (totals match at $98.5M, but the split differs — notably Emergency Response is $1.2M lower in the exhibits).  
              Cash flow, FY2024:         CIM operating cash $9.2M / FCF $2.0M; exhibits $10.0M / $2.8M.  
              PFAS % of remediation, FY2023:         CIM 8.2%; exhibits 8.0%.  
          Interpretation.         The pattern — identical totals with differing components, identical revenue amounts attached to different customer names, and a balance sheet that differs by $3.9M — is consistent with the materials having been assembled from different drafts or data sources without a final reconciliation pass. It does not by itself indicate fraud, but it means             no figure in the CIM should be treated as reliable until independently verified in QoE    , and the customer-identity discrepancy in particular must be resolved before IOI submission so we know whose contracts we are actually underwriting.  
      
         6. Key Risk Factors and Diligence Priorities  
      The following are prioritized by impact on valuation and deal certainty. Each maps to a specific diligence request in Section 10.  
       6.1 PFAS Revenue Projections (HIGH)  
      The CIM projects PFAS revenue of $14.0M in FY2025E (125.8% growth) and $22.0M in FY2026E (57.1% growth), with PFAS providing ~64% of incremental revenue and underpinning the projected margin expansion to 21.0%+. Sector analysts project 25–35% growth for the overall PFAS market, but company-level growth is constrained by (a) licensed-engineer and trained-technician availability, (b) specialized treatment-equipment capital, (c) competitive entry by larger national firms, and (d) the pace of enforcement that actually triggers             funded         remediation (as opposed to investigation/assessment).  
      Our GreenWorks experience is directly analogous: an emerging service line (vapor intrusion mitigation) projected at 80%+ growth in years two and three moderated to ~35% once pent-up demand was worked through. Applying a 30–50% company-level growth assumption to CES’s $6.2M FY2024 PFAS base yields FY2025E PFAS of ~$8.1M–$9.3M — roughly $5M–$6M below the CIM’s $14.0M. If PFAS carries above-average margins (as the CIM implies), this shortfall could reduce FY2025E EBITDA by ~$1.5M–$2.0M and undermine the projected margin expansion.  
          PE capacity constraint.         PE headcount fell from three to two in January 2025 (one retired, unreplaced). PFAS site assessments and remediation designs require PE oversight. Sustaining the projected ramp likely requires hiring two to three additional PEs; environmental PE recruitment typically runs six to twelve months. Management’s PE hiring plan — and whether the loss of one-third of PE capacity creates a near-term execution bottleneck — is a critical diligence question.  
        6.2 Maintenance Capex (HIGH)  
      CIM maintenance capex of $3.0M (3.4% of revenue) is 150–200 bps below the 5–7% sector range ($4.4M–$6.1M for CES’s revenue). Our portfolio data confirms the range: GreenWorks ran at 6.2% post-close (after a pre-close understatement to ~3.5%), and Allied at 5.8%. If true maintenance capex is ~$5.5M (sector midpoint), the ~$2.5M annual difference directly reduces free cash flow — and at CES’s already-thin FCF (Section 4.4), this could push normalized FCF near breakeven. A detailed fleet age and condition assessment (the fleet is ~4.5 years old, replacement value >$22M) is essential early diligence. This is the single largest potential swing factor in the underwriting model after PFAS.  
        6.3 Regulatory Cost Add-Back (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
      The $1.2M add-back for the August 2024 DEQ enforcement action is presented as fully non-recurring. Two concerns: (i) the action concerned             improper storage of contaminated soils at CES’s own Portland facility         — an operational compliance failure at the Company’s primary site, not a client-site issue — which raises the question of whether it reflects systemic internal compliance weakness rather than an isolated event; and (ii) sector and portfolio data show a baseline of $200K–$500K/yr in regulatory and compliance cost is inherent to the business model. Best-practice QoE normalizes to an ongoing run-rate and adds back only the excess. At a $300K run-rate, the defensible add-back is ~$0.9M (a $0.3M haircut); at $500K, ~$0.7M (a $0.5M haircut). The full 10-year regulatory enforcement history must be reviewed to assess whether prior actions suggest a pattern.  
        6.4 Customer Concentration and PNR Contract Renewal (HIGH)  
      Top-customer concentration (PNR 21.4%; top 5 52.5%; top 10 71.0%) is typical for project-based environmental services but is a real risk.             The PNR master service agreement expired March 31, 2025 — before the CIM was distributed on June 9, 2025.         The CIM and backlog report describe the PNR relationship as         “    deeply embedded    ”         with on-site personnel and preferred-provider status, but neither document confirms renewal. Whether PNR has renewed (and on what terms), the renewal cadence of the other top-10 contracts (several expire in 2025–2026), and the realistic revenue-replacement capacity if PNR scope is reduced are the most important commercial diligence items. The concentration trend is favorable (top-10 share declining from 74.3% to 71.0% over FY2021–FY2024), but the absolute level remains high and the largest contract is in renewal at the moment of sale.  
        6.5 Founder Transition and Key-Person Risk (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
      Mr. Oakes (age 61, 68% owner, 30+ years’ experience) intends to exit within 18–24 months post-close. He is the central strategic, customer, and regulatory-relationship figure. The GreenWorks experience — where a key project manager departed within six months of close — is a cautionary precedent. Janet Prewitt (VP Operations, 18-year tenure, holds key remediation client relationships) is the critical retention target and should be covered by a retention agreement as a closing condition. The broader management bench (VP Sales since 2014; Controller since 2016; Safety Director since 2012) is reasonably tenured but has not operated the company without Mr. Oakes. A credible CEO succession plan — including the $0.5M replacement-compensation assumption underlying the owner-comp add-back — must be validated.  
        6.6 Real Estate and Related-Party Lease (MEDIUM)  
      The Portland HQ (8.5 acres, purpose-built environmental operations center) is owned personally by Mr. Oakes and leased to CES at $1.4M/yr through 2029, versus an estimated $0.6M market rate. The $0.8M EBITDA normalization is defensible only if the buyer will in fact pay market rent going forward. The buyer must either (a) acquire the property from Mr. Oakes at independently appraised fair market value (adding to uses of funds but eliminating rent and the related-party conflict), or (b) execute a new arm’s-length lease at market rate (preserving the normalization but leaving a related-party landlord in place for the lease term). The CIM notes industrial yard space in Portland leases at $8–$12/SF and that         “    specialized environmental staging facilities    ”         can command $1.0M–$1.2M/yr — which, if accurate, suggests the $0.6M         “    market    ”         estimate may itself be low and that the property’s specialized improvements carry premium value. The real-estate resolution is both a valuation and a transaction-structure issue and should be addressed explicitly in the IOI.  
        6.7 Adjusted EBITDA Adjustment Defensibility (MEDIUM)  
      Beyond the regulatory and owner-comp items already discussed, the phantom equity plan warrants attention. The $0.3M annual non-cash add-back is appropriate for EBITDA, but             all outstanding phantom units settle in cash at closing    . The total cash settlement liability is not quantified in the CIM and must be estimated for sources & uses; based on the annual expense run-rate it could be a low-single-digit-million obligation. This is a use of funds at closing, not an EBITDA item, and reduces net equity proceeds to the seller.  
        6.8 Fund Fit (CONFIRM)  
      The sector memo characterizes CES as fitting         “    squarely within    ”         Fund III’s parameters. Fund III ($425M, closed 2023) is described as targeting platforms in the $30M–$100M enterprise value range. CES’s implied enterprise value of $137M–$171M (CIM) — and our recommended $145M–$165M IOI range — is             above         that stated ceiling. The deal team should confirm Fund III’s actual EV/equity-check limits, concentration limits, and whether a platform of this size requires LPAC approval or a fund-limit exception. At a ~$150M EV with ~40% leverage, the equity check is ~$90M (~21% of fund), which is a meaningful but supportable platform concentration if within mandate.  
      
         7. Valuation Analysis  
       7.1 CIM Implied Valuation  
      The CIM cites comparable environmental services transactions at 8.0x–10.0x Adjusted EBITDA and implies:  
          FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA $17.1M × 8.0x–10.0x =             $136.8M–$171.0M         enterprise value  
          FY2025E Adjusted EBITDA $19.7M × 8.0x–10.0x =             $157.6M–$197.0M         enterprise value  
      The CIM argues CES’s growth, PFAS opportunity, and platform characteristics support the higher end and the forward basis.  
        7.2 Buyer-Adjusted EBITDA  
      Applying the haircuts in Section 4.3:  
              FY2024 buyer-adjusted EBITDA ≈ $16.4M         ($17.1M less ~$0.4M regulatory and ~$0.3M owner-comp; range $16.1M–$16.8M).  
              FY2025E buyer-adjusted EBITDA (PFAS haircut case) ≈ $17.2M         ($19.7M less ~$1.8M PFAS shortfall impact, ~$0.4M regulatory, ~$0.3M owner-comp; range approximately $17.0M–$17.5M). This assumes PFAS grows ~35% to ~$8.4M rather than 125.8% to $14.0M, with other segments at CIM/exhibit projections.  
        7.3 Valuation Sensitivity  
                              EBITDA basis  
          $M  
          8.0x  
          8.5x  
          9.0x  
          9.5x  
          10.0x  
            FY2024 Reported (CIM)  
          12.1  
          97  
          103  
          109  
          115  
          121  
            FY2024 Adjusted (CIM)  
          17.1  
          137  
          145  
          154  
          162  
          171  
                FY2024 Buyer-Adjusted  
              16.4  
              131  
              139  
              148  
              156  
              164  
            FY2025E Adjusted (CIM)  
          19.7  
          158  
          167  
          177  
          187  
          197  
                FY2025E Buyer-Adjusted (PFAS haircut)  
              17.2  
              138  
              146  
              155  
              163  
              172  
         (EV in $M, rounded.)  
      The defensible underwriting zone, blending the buyer-adjusted trailing case at 8.5x–9.5x and the forward PFAS-haircut case at 8.5x–9.5x, clusters around             $140M–$165M    . The CIM’s $171M trailing ceiling and $197M forward ceiling are achievable only if (a) the full $5.0M of add-backs is accepted, (b) PFAS hits the projected ramp, and (c) a buyer is willing to pay a forward multiple on management’s projections — none of which we would underwrite without confirmation.  
        7.4 Equity Value Bridge (Illustrative, at $150M EV midpoint)  
                         Item  
          $M  
            Enterprise value (midpoint)  
          150.0  
            Less: total debt (term loan + revolver)  
          (17.3)  
            Plus: cash (per CIM)  
          2.3  
                Equity value (cash-free / debt-free, pre-adjustments)  
              135.0  
            Less: phantom equity cash settlement at closing (to be quantified)  
          (TBD)  
            Less: NWC adjustment vs. peg (to be determined)  
          TBD  
            Plus/less: HQ real estate (acquire at FMV or lease at market)  
          TBD  
                Indicative equity to sellers  
              ~$130M–$135M  
         The real-estate treatment is shown as TBD because it is a structural choice: acquiring the property adds to uses of funds (and to the buyer’s investment) but eliminates rent and the related-party conflict; leasing at market preserves the EBITDA normalization. The phantom settlement and any NWC deficiency reduce net seller proceeds and should be modeled once quantified.  
      
         8. IOI Recommendations  
       8.1 Indication of Value  
      Submit an enterprise value range of             $145M–$165M         on a cash-free, debt-free basis with normalized net working capital, structured as:  
              ~$140M–$150M cash at close         (implying ~8.5x–9.1x buyer-adjusted FY2024 EBITDA of ~$16.4M), plus  
              A contingent earnout of up to ~$15M–$20M (total consideration ~$140M–$170M depending on earnout achievement)    , payable based on FY2025 and FY2026 PFAS revenue and/or consolidated Adjusted EBITDA thresholds (e.g., PFAS revenue ≥ $12M in FY2025 and ≥ $18M in FY2026; or Adjusted EBITDA ≥ $19M in FY2025 and ≥ $22M in FY2026).  
      This structure pays the seller for the growth story if it materializes, protects our downside if it does not, and directly addresses the principal valuation dispute (PFAS) without requiring us to underwrite the full ramp at close. State the valuation on both a trailing (FY2024 buyer-adjusted) and forward (FY2025E) basis, and identify the implied multiple range (approximately 8.5x–9.1x trailing buyer-adjusted EBITDA on the cash-at-close component, scaling to ~10x trailing if the full earnout is earned).  
        8.2 Transaction Structure  
              Stock purchase         (consistent with the process letter’s expected structure).  
              Existing indebtedness         refinanced/repaid at closing; transaction valued cash-free/debt-free with a normalized NWC peg to be set on verified (QoE) metrics, not the CIM’s stated DSO.  
              HQ real estate:         propose to acquire the Portland property from Mr. Oakes at independently appraised fair market value (preferred — eliminates related-party conflict and rent), or alternatively execute a new long-term arm’s-length lease at market rate. Either way, the $0.8M lease normalization is preserved in EBITDA only against market-rate occupancy.  
              Rollover equity:         given Mr. Oakes’s stated desire to exit, a meaningful rollover is unlikely to be required; consider a modest rollover (e.g., 5–10%) for key management (e.g., Ms. Prewitt) as a retention/alignment mechanism rather than for the founder.  
              Earnout         as described above, with clear, auditable metrics and a 12–24 month measurement period.  
        8.3 Sources of Financing  
              Equity:         committed from Fund III. Indicate the equity check (~$85M–$95M at ~40% leverage) and that Fund III has capacity.  
              Debt:         ~40–45% of EV, anticipated as a senior debt / unitranche facility from a lender with environmental-services experience; name candidate lenders if appropriate. Note the capital-intensive, working-capital-heavy profile supports moderate (not aggressive) leverage.  
          Indicate financing is subject only to customary conditions (definitive docs, QoE, final diligence).  
        8.4 Diligence Priorities (Summary)  
      The prioritized diligence list is in Section 10. In the IOI, summarize the key remaining items: (i) PFAS backlog breakout (signed vs. pipeline) and PE hiring plan; (ii) PNR renewal status and top-10 contract renewals; (iii) QoE and reconciliation of the CIM/exhibits discrepancies; (iv) fleet age/condition and true maintenance capex; (v) 10-year regulatory enforcement history; (vi) real-estate resolution; (vii) phantom equity liability; (viii) management retention. Indicate a 6–8 week confirmatory diligence timeline consistent with an August management presentation and Q4 close.  
        8.5 Management and Operations  
          Accommodate Mr. Oakes’s 18–24 month transition in an advisory/transition role.  
          Commit to retaining the existing management team and field workforce; propose retention agreements for Ms. Prewitt (VP Operations) and other key personnel as a closing condition.  
          Plan to recruit a replacement CEO at the ~$0.5M compensation level assumed in the owner-comp normalization, with environmental-services operating experience.  
          No anticipated near-term operational changes beyond continued investment in PFAS capacity and fleet.  
        8.6 Approvals, Timeline, and Relevant Experience  
          Internal approvals: Thornfield investment committee; confirm LPAC requirements given EV size relative to fund (Section 6.8).  
          Regulatory: customary HSR if applicable; state environmental permit transfers (hazardous waste transporter permits in four states; Oregon DEQ contractor license) — confirm transferability and timing.  
          Timeline: target definitive agreement Q4 2025, closing 30–60 days thereafter subject to permit transfers.  
          Relevant experience: cite GreenWorks Remediation (Fund II, 2019) and Allied Waste Systems (Fund II, 2021) as directly comparable environmental-services platform investments, with operational and regulatory-compliance lessons applicable to CES.  
      
         9. Recommendation and Next Steps  
          Recommendation: Proceed to IOI at $145M–$165M enterprise value, structured as ~$140M–$150M cash at close plus a PFAS/EBITDA-linked earnout of up to ~$15M–$20M.         The asset is attractive and strategically on-target for Fund III; the risks are identifiable, quantifiable, and largely addressable through structure (earnout, retention conditions, real-estate resolution) and diligence (QoE, fleet assessment, regulatory history, PNR renewal). The pattern of financial inconsistencies and aggressive adjustments is consistent with seller-CIM behavior we have observed in this sector and does not, on present information, warrant passing — but it does warrant a disciplined valuation anchored on independently adjusted trailing EBITDA rather than management’s forward projections.  
          Immediate next steps (target completion ahead of July 14 IOI):  
          Resolve the customer-identity discrepancy (CIM vs. backlog report) with Greenleaf so we know whose contracts we are underwriting; confirm PNR renewal status.  
          Engage a QoE provider (per the sector memo, to be selected by the deal team) and transmit the reconciliation items in the Appendix as the opening QoE request.  
          Request the PFAS backlog breakout (signed contracts vs. pipeline) and management’s PE hiring plan.  
          Confirm Fund III mandate/LPAC requirements for a platform of this EV size.  
          Circulate this memo to the deal team for discussion; schedule an IC pre-read ahead of IOI submission.  
          Prepare the IOI letter per the Section 8 framework, with buyer’s counsel (Calloway, Barrett & Sims LLP) reviewing structure and conditions language.  
      
        10. Diligence Request List (Prioritized)  
          A. Commercial / PFAS  
          PFAS backlog breakout as of 6/30/2025: signed contracts vs. awarded vs. pipeline, by customer and expected conversion period.  
          PFAS pipeline detail: opportunity names, scope, expected award dates, win-rate assumptions supporting $14.0M FY2025E.  
          Management’s PE hiring plan: open requisitions, recruitment timeline, compensation, and impact on PFAS capacity.  
          PFAS unit economics: gross margin, project economics, equipment utilization, and competitive positioning vs. national entrants.  
          B. Customers / Contracts  
          PNR: current MSA status (post March 31, 2025), renewal terms, scope, pricing, and any change-order or volume trends; on-site personnel arrangement.  
          Reconciliation of the top-10 customer list between the CIM and the backlog report — confirm correct customer identities, segments served, and contract expirations.  
          Top-10 contract renewals: status of all 2025–2026 expirations (Meridian 12/31/25; Cascade Terminals/Clearwater 6/30/26; Puget Sound/Northshore 9/30/26; Tidewater/Harborview 3/31/27; etc.).  
          Customer retention and win/loss history for the past five years; any lost top-10 customers.  
          C. Financial / QoE  
          Reconciliation of all discrepancies between the CIM, financial exhibits, and backlog report (see Appendix) — balance sheet, income statement, segment splits, cash flow, geographic mix.  
          Explanation of the $2.5M         “    Goodwill & Intangible Assets    ”         on the exhibits’ balance sheet (no acquisitions described in the CIM).  
          DSO reconciliation: stated 52 days vs. ~71 days implied by AR/revenue; receivables aging and unbilled-revenue detail.  
          Quality of EBITDA adjustments: owner-compensation detail (salary, bonus, benefits, and the unquantified         “    personal expenses allocated to the Company    ”    ); related-party lease documentation and market-rate support; phantom equity plan terms and total cash settlement liability at closing.  
          Maintenance vs. growth capex detail for FY2022–FY2024; fleet age, condition, and replacement schedule; deferred-maintenance assessment.  
          D. Regulatory / Compliance  
          Complete 10-year regulatory enforcement history (all agencies, all facilities); details of the August 2024 DEQ action and corrective measures.  
          Current status and transferability of all permits (four-state hazmat transporter permits; Oregon DEQ contractor license; EPA Generator ID); renewal calendar.  
          Environmental compliance status of CES’s own facilities (Portland HQ wash rack, soil stockpile/staging areas) post-DEQ action.  
          E. Real Estate / Facilities  
          HQ property: appraisal, environmental site assessment, title, and Mr. Oakes’s willingness to sell vs. lease at market.  
          Boise and Seattle lease assignments and any change-of-control provisions.  
          F. Management / Employees  
          Management retention risk assessment; willingness of Ms. Prewitt and key PMs to commit to retention agreements.  
          Phantom equity plan: participant list, vesting, and total cash settlement obligation at closing.  
          CEO succession plan and replacement-compensation benchmarking supporting the $0.5M assumption.  
      
        Appendix: Reconciliation of Key Discrepancies  
       A. Balance Sheet — CIM vs. Financial Exhibits (FY2024, $M)

           B. Income Statement — CIM vs. Financial Exhibits (FY2024, $M)

        C. Segment Revenue — CIM vs. Financial Exhibits ($M)

No figures herein have been independently verified. All adjustments and projections are preliminary and subject to confirmatory due diligence and quality-of-earnings analysis. For internal use only; do not distribute externally.

                SECTION I  —  SUMMARY OF TRANSACTION  
                                                 Description  
                             Buyer Debit  
                             Seller Credit  
                             Notes  
                             Purchase Price  
                           3,900,000.00  
                           3,900,000.00  
                            Per PSA §3.1  
                             New Loan Proceeds — Tidewater Savings Bank  
                           —

                           $730.42  
                             Total settlement charges — Buyer column  
                           $67,074.00  
                             Total settlement charges — Seller column (incl. payoffs & escrow)  
                           $958,223.89  
                             Aggregate lien payoffs (1st mortgage + HELOC + mechanic's lien)  
                           $866,631.89  
                             Repair escrow holdback (from Seller's proceeds)  
                           $45,000.00  
                             Delinquent property tax + interest (paid to City)  
                           $28,602.80  
                             Water/sewer (WPCA) paid to City  
                           $1,847.60  
                             Security deposits transferred to Buyer  
                           $32,400.00  
                             Net collected-rent proration (credit to Buyer)  
                           $12,941.94  
                             Next-FY property tax proration (Seller's 14-day share)  
                           $2,012.93  
                             Heating oil reimbursement (credit to Seller)  
                           $693.00  
                              CASH REQUIRED FROM BUYER AT CLOSING  
                            $1,084,681.71  
                              NET PROCEEDS TO SELLER  
                            $2,864,663.84  
                             Total sources = total uses (reconciled)  
                           $3,920,412.13  
                 The settlement statement reconciles exactly: total sources of funds ($3,920,412.13) equal total uses of funds ($3,920,412.13). See Section IV of the Settlement Statement for the full sources-and-uses schedule.  
                3.  DISCREPANCIES FLAGGED  
             The following inconsistencies were identified among the closing documents. None is believed to affect the monetary reconciliation, but each should be reconciled or corrected on or before Closing.  
            Title commitment number and date.         PSA §5.1 references a title commitment "dated April 30, 2025, Commitment No. PCT-2025-08814." The title commitment actually delivered is Commitment No. TC-2025-07182, effective June 18, 2025 (issued by Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC for Continental Fidelity/Hartleigh Title Insurance Company). The file number PCT-2025-08814 is used on the settlement statement as the file reference. Confirm that the June 18 commitment (TC-2025-07182) is the operative commitment and that the PSA reference is simply an earlier-filed or placeholder number.  
            Title underwriter name.         The title commitment refers to the underwriter as both "Continental Fidelity Title Insurance Company" (header) and "Continental Hartleigh Title Insurance Company" (body and Conditions & Stipulations). These appear to be two different names for the same entity. Confirm the correct legal name of the underwriter so that the owner's and lender's policies are issued in the proper name.  
            Closing-agent address.         The PSA and title commitment list Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC at 88 Field Point Road, Greenwich, CT 06830. The lien payoff cover letter is addressed to the closing officer at 3100 Main Street, Suite 204, Bridgeport, CT 06604, and the tax-lien statement is addressed to 1200 Post Road East, Suite 214, Westport, CT 06880. Confirm the correct office and, critically, verify all wire instructions by independent verbal callback before disbursing (wire-fraud best practice).  
            Letters Testamentary date.         The Fiduciary's Certificate states Letters Testamentary were issued on October 11, 2024; the title commitment states "on or about October 14, 2024." Reconcile the exact issuance date. The certificate is dated June 25, 2025, which is within the Lender's 60-day and the Title Company's 60-day recency requirements for the July 15, 2025 Closing.  
            Commercial lease remaining term.         PSA Exhibit C states the remaining term as of Closing (7/15/2025) is "approximately 5 years, 5 months, and 16 days" (lease expires 12/31/2030). The Lender Commitment and the rent-roll Notes column both state "approximately four and one-half years remaining." From July 15, 2025 to December 31, 2030 is approximately 5 years, 5.5 months — not 4.5 years. The "4.5 years" figures appear to be errors. Confirm the correct remaining term, as it is relevant to the Lender's underwriting and the required Subordination, Non-Disturbance, and Attornment Agreement (SNDA).  
            Lender loan-to-value (LTV) figure.         The Lender Commitment states LTV is "approximately 67.7%" and then computes $2,640,000 ÷ $3,920,000 (appraised value) = 67.35%, "rounded to approximately 67.7%." However, 67.35% does not round to 67.7%; the 67.7% figure corresponds to $2,640,000 ÷ $3,900,000 (purchase price) = 67.69%. The commitment conflates the purchase-price-based and appraised-value-based LTV. Cosmetic, but confirm the Lender's intended LTV basis.  
            Payoff-letter date references.         Title Commitment Requirements 4 and 5 reference Harborstone payoff statements "dated June 10, 2025." The payoff letters actually enclosed (and used on the settlement statement) are dated July 8, 2025 and supersede any earlier statement. Confirm the July 8 figures ($687,412.33 and $148,219.56, good through 7/20/2025) are the operative payoffs.  
            Financing-contingency cross-reference.         The Lender Commitment states the financing contingency was waived "pursuant to Section 5.2 of the Purchase Agreement." In the PSA, §5.2 is "Permitted Exceptions"; the financing waiver is addressed in PSA §3.3 and §10.2. Minor cross-reference error in the commitment letter; the waiver itself is documented and effective as of June 10, 2025.  
            Occupied vs. collected rent distinction.         The rent roll reports "Total Monthly Rent (Occupied Units) $25,200.00" (which includes Unit 2E, occupied but delinquent) and "Total Collected July 2025 Rent $23,600.00" (which excludes 2E). The $1,600.00 difference equals Unit 2E's delinquent July rent. The settlement statement prorates only the $23,600.00 collected, consistent with PSA §7.3 (no credit for uncollected rents). No error, but the distinction should be understood by all parties.  
                4.  ASSUMPTIONS MADE  
             The following assumptions were used where figures were unavailable, not yet determined, or required estimation. Each is subject to adjustment, reproration, or confirmation as noted.  
            Earnest-money accrued interest (estimated $730.42).         No interest figure was provided in the delivered documents. The settlement statement estimates $730.42 assuming a 2.00% APY escrow rate: First Deposit $100,000 × 82 days (4/24/2025–7/15/2025) = $449.32; Second Deposit $95,000 × 54 days (5/22/2025–7/15/2025) = $281.10. The actual interest credit will be per the Escrow Agent's final accounting and will adjust Buyer's cash-to-close (and the sources/uses) by any difference. PSA §3.2(e) requires the interest be credited to Buyer.  
            Connecticut Real Estate Conveyance Tax (estimated $44,750.00 total; $22,375.00 per party).         Taken from the Title Commitment Schedule C estimate, split 50/50 per PSA §12.1 and §6.4. The Title Commitment notes this is an estimate using "the tiered rate structure applicable to the City of Bridgeport" and that the actual amount "will be confirmed upon preparation and filing of Form OP-236." The mixed-use character of the property (commercial ground floor + residential upper floors) may affect the rate computation. Subject to adjustment at Closing.  
            Next-fiscal-year (2025–2026) property tax (estimated at the current-year levy of $52,480.00).         The new mill rate has not been set and no bill has been issued (Tax Lien Statement §3). Per PSA §7.1(b), the current-year amount is used as the estimate. Seller is charged for 14 days (7/1–7/14) = $2,012.93; Buyer is credited and will pay the bill when issued. Subject to reproration under PSA §7.7 within 90 days after Closing.  
            Security-deposit accrued interest (excluded).         The $32,400.00 transferred reflects principal only (per the security-deposit schedule). Connecticut General Statutes §47a-21 may require interest on residential security deposits held in escrow. Any accrued interest is not quantified in the delivered records and is excluded from the settlement statement. To be confirmed with Bayshore Management Co. and added to the credit to Buyer if applicable.  
            Gas and electric utilities (no proration reflected).         No final bills, meter readings, or account balances were provided for gas or electric service. Per PSA §7.5(d), utility deposits are retained by Seller and Buyer establishes new accounts as of Closing. The settlement statement reflects no gas/electric proration. If final landlord-paid gas/electric charges for Seller's period are later determined, they will be handled by reproration under PSA §7.7. (The commercial space is served by a separate HVAC system; residential/common areas use the oil-fired boiler; residential electric is likely tenant-metered.)  
            Water/sewer treatment — PSA §7.5(a) interpretation.         PSA §7.5(a) states that where a utility billing period ends on or before the Closing Date, "the full unpaid balance shall be credited to Buyer on the settlement statement." The WPCA account statement and Tax Lien Statement, however, require that the $1,847.60 water/sewer balance "be satisfied at or before closing." The settlement statement treats the $1,847.60 as a charge to Seller paid from Seller's proceeds directly to the City of Bridgeport at Closing (consistent with the WPCA's closing requirement and the Tax Lien Statement payoff summary). This is economically equivalent to the PSA's allocation (Seller bears the full cost); the difference is only mechanical (direct disbursement vs. credit to Buyer). If the parties prefer to follow the PSA's "credit to Buyer" language literally, the entry would be adjusted to Debit Seller / Credit Buyer $1,847.60, with Buyer remitting payment to the WPCA by the 8/1/2025 due date — but this would conflict with the WPCA's closing-satisfaction requirement.  
            Repair Escrow treatment.         Per PSA §8.4(c), the $45,000.00 Repair Escrow is a third-party holdback funded solely from Seller's proceeds. It is NOT a credit to Buyer and NOT part of the Purchase Price. It appears as a charge to Seller (disbursement to the Escrow Agent) and is held in a segregated, interest-bearing account. The pre-closing inspection (7/14/2025) confirms the roof deficiencies remain unrepaired; the escrow is therefore required at Closing. Any unused balance plus interest reverts to Seller after 12 months (PSA §8.4(e)).  
            Estate tax / FIRPTA.         The Estate of Gerald T. Whitford is below the Connecticut estate-tax threshold; CT estate tax due is $0.00, and the CT DRS estate-tax lien requires only a recorded release (no payoff). The settlement statement reflects $0.00 for the DRS lien; the $73.00 release-recording fee is a Seller charge. The PSA requires a FIRPTA affidavit or estate-equivalent certification; no federal or state withholding is anticipated.  
            Appraisal fee — Paid Outside of Closing (POC).         The $4,500.00 appraisal fee was paid by Buyer directly to Ashford Valuation Associates LLC on 5/5/2025, outside of Closing. Per the Lender Commitment's explicit and repeated instructions, it is disclosed on the settlement statement as POC with $0.00 impact on Buyer's cash-to-close and is NOT included in any debit/credit computation.  
            Payoff good-through dates.         All lien payoffs (Harborstone first mortgage $687,412.33; HELOC $148,219.56) and the delinquent-tax payoff ($28,602.80) are good through July 20, 2025. Closing on July 15, 2025 falls within the good-through period, so no per-diem interest adjustment is required. Assumption: Closing occurs on or before July 20, 2025. If delayed, per-diem interest applies ($112.67/day first mortgage; ~$27.93/day HELOC; $13.12/day delinquent tax) and updated payoff statements must be obtained.  
                5.  ITEMS REQUIRING CONFIRMATION OR COMPLETION BEFORE CLOSING  
             The following should be confirmed or delivered on or before the Closing Date. Monetary items may adjust the final cash-to-close and net-proceeds figures.  
           Confirm exact earnest-money accrued interest with the Escrow Agent (Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC).  
           Confirm final Connecticut Conveyance Tax on Form OP-236 (filed with the Bridgeport Town Clerk at recording).  
           Confirm with Bayshore Management Co. whether any interest has accrued on residential escrow deposits under CGS §47a-21; add to the security-deposit credit if applicable.  
           Obtain final gas/electric readings or bills for any landlord-paid accounts; otherwise confirm accounts are tenant-metered or to be established by Buyer.  
           Reconcile the title-commitment number/date (PSA: PCT-2025-08814, 4/30/2025 vs. delivered: TC-2025-07182, 6/18/2025); confirm the operative commitment.  
           Confirm the correct legal name of the title underwriter (Continental Fidelity vs. Continental Hartleigh) for policy issuance.  
           Verify all wire instructions by independent verbal callback to a known number before disbursing (wire-fraud prevention).  
           Confirm the correct remaining term of the Commercial Lease (~5.5 years to 12/31/2030) for the SNDA and Lender records.  
           Execute the Subordination, Non-Disturbance, and Attornment Agreement (SNDA) among Lender, Buyer, and Coastal Provisions Market LLC (Lender Condition 7).  
           Deliver evidence of property/casualty insurance ($3,900,000 replacement cost) naming Tidewater Savings Bank as mortgagee/loss payee (Lender Condition 5).  
           Deliver the ALTA/NSPS land title survey or survey endorsement (Title Requirement 12; Lender Condition 4).  
           Deliver the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment (ASTM E1527-21) (Lender Condition 6).  
           Execute the personal guaranty by Elaine R. Matsuda (Lender Condition 10).  
           Deliver Buyer entity documentation: Certificate of Good Standing, Operating Agreement, and member resolution (dated within 30 days of Closing) (Lender Condition 11; Title Requirement 11).  
           Deliver the certified Fiduciary's Certificate (dated within 60 days of Closing; issued 6/25/2025 — within requirement) (Title Requirement 2; Lender Condition 12).  
           Deliver the CT DRS estate-tax lien release (Release Certificate No. ETL-2025-08834) for recording (Title Requirement 7).  
           Deliver the Northbridge Construction Co. mechanic's-lien release in recordable form, in exchange for the $31,000.00 settlement disbursement (Title Requirement 6).  
           Deliver the Assignment and Assumption of Leases (commercial and residential) executed by both parties (Title Requirement 9; PSA §6.2(c), §6.3(f)).  
           Deliver the Executor's Warranty Deed and the Connecticut Conveyance Tax Return (Form OP-236) for recording (Title Requirement 1, 3).  
           Confirm the Repair Escrow Agreement is executed and names Tidewater Savings Bank as an interested party (Lender Condition 13; PSA §8.4(c)).  
                6.  SURVIVAL, REPRORATION, AND POST-CLOSING ADJUSTMENTS  
             Pursuant to PSA §7.7 and §15.1, the proration and adjustment obligations survive the Closing for 90 days. Any proration that could not be finally determined at Closing — most notably the next-fiscal-year property tax (estimated at the current-year levy), any gas/electric utility charges, and any security-deposit interest — is to be readjusted between the parties when final figures become available. Any amount owed as a result of readjustment is payable within 10 business days following demand, accompanied by reasonable supporting documentation. The Repair Escrow survives for 12 months (PSA §8.4(d)–(e)); any unused balance plus accrued interest reverts to the Estate.  
                7.  SOURCES REVIEWED  
             This memo and the accompanying settlement statement were prepared from the following closing documents:  
           Purchase and Sale Agreement dated April 22, 2025 (with Exhibits A–D)  
           Rent Roll and Security Deposit Schedule (Bayshore Management Co., dated July 1, 2025)  
           Title Commitment No. TC-2025-07182 (Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC, effective June 18, 2025)  
           Lender Commitment Letter, Loan No. TSB-2025-CRE-04183 (Tidewater Savings Bank, dated June 24, 2025)  
           Existing Lien Payoff Letters (Ashford, Clement & Paige LLP compilation, dated July 10, 2025): Harborstone FCU first mortgage, Harborstone FCU HELOC, Northbridge Construction Co. mechanic's lien  
           Municipal Tax Status Certificate and Payoff Statement No. TLC-2025-04892 (City of Bridgeport Tax Collector, dated July 10, 2025)  
           WPCA Account Statement / Final Read (City of Bridgeport Water Pollution Control Authority, dated July 7, 2025)  
           Pre-Closing Inspection Report and Heating Oil Tank Reading (Soundview Property Inspections LLC, dated July 14, 2025)  
           Property Management Agreement and Notice of Termination (Estate and Bayshore Management Co., dated October 15, 2024 / June 13, 2025)  
           Fiduciary's Certificate and Estate Summary (Fairfield County Probate Court, Docket No. 2024-PR-04417; Ashford, Clement & Paige LLP, dated July 10, 2025)  
             
              Prepared by the Closing Agent for the transaction. This memorandum is provided for the convenience of the parties and their counsel and is not legal advice. In the event of any conflict between this memo and the PSA, the PSA controls.  
              Distribution:  Seller's Counsel (Ashford, Clement & Paige LLP)  •  Buyer's Counsel (Ridgeline Law Group PLLC)  •  Lender (Tidewater Savings Bank)  •  Closing File

### FILE: exhibit-summary-memo.docx
          INTERNAL MEMORANDUM  
          PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT  
                          TO:  
          Sarah Whitfield, Esq.; Jordan Reeves  
                FROM:  
          Case Team, Calloway & Birch LLP  
                DATE:  
          October 7, 2024  
                RE:  
          Review of Respondent Darren Kwan’s Document Production —             Torres-Kwan v. Kwan    , Case No. 2024-DR-01847 (Cir. Ct. Fairview County, Columbia; Hon. Patricia Yoon-Callahan)  
                PRODUCTION REVIEWED:  
          Respondent’s production of documents in response to Petitioner’s First Request for Production, Nos. 1–22, transmitted by cover letter from Raymond Fossett, Esq. (Hargrove Fossett & Yates LLP)  
         
       I. Executive Summary  
      Respondent Darren Kwan produced seven (7) document sets (PROD-001 through PROD-007) in response to Petitioner’s First Request for Production, Nos. 1–22, together with a cover letter identifying four (4) documents withheld on privilege (PRIV-001 through PRIV-004). The production is facially responsive but, on cross-referencing the exhibits against one another and against the Sworn Financial Declaration (PROD-001), reveals             material omissions and internal inconsistencies         that, in our preliminary assessment, materially understate Respondent’s income, assets, and liabilities, and that raise concerns about post-separation dissipation and incomplete disclosure.  
      The most significant findings are:  
             Undisclosed real property.         The 2023 tax return (PROD-002) and the produced mortgage statements (PROD-005) both reflect a rental condominium at             1035 Elm Street, Unit C    , titled in Respondent’s name alone, carrying a mortgage of approximately             $241,281         and generating             $2,100/month         in rent. This property, its mortgage, and its rental income are             entirely omitted         from the Sworn Financial Declaration, which affirmatively states that Respondent         “    does not hold any ownership interest … in any other real property    ”         and that he has         “    no … rental income.    ”  
             Income understatement.         The Declaration reports 2023         “    K-1 income    ”         of             $174,000    , which is the             cash distribution         figure. The K-1 (PROD-002) reports             ordinary business income         of             $367,200         — a difference of             $193,200    . The Declaration also omits interest, dividend, and capital-gain income reflected on the joint return, and omits the rental income noted above.  
             $85,000 post-separation wire to an undisclosed account.         On             June 15, 2024         — two weeks after the Declaration’s stated separation date of June 1, 2024 — Respondent wired             $85,000         from the Crestline brokerage account (PROD-003) to             Harborstone Financial Group, account ending 2291    , an account that appears             nowhere         in the Declaration.  
             Recurring transfers to an unidentified third party.         The bank statements (PROD-004) show             $14,600         in Venmo transfers to         “    Kiera M.    ”         over March–July 2024 (~$2,920/month), none of which is disclosed or explained.  
             Separation-date conflict.         The Declaration fixes separation at             June 1, 2024    , while Respondent’s own cover-letter privilege log repeatedly references a separation date of             August 1, 2024    . The date of separation is dispositive to the marital-estate cutoff and to characterization of the June 15 wire and other transactions.  
             Valuation-date misrepresentation.         The Declaration represents all balances as of the June 1, 2024 separation date, but the figures reported for the individual checking, savings, and brokerage accounts in fact match the             August 1, 2024         statement values — obscuring the post-separation decline in the brokerage account (approximately             $81,245    , driven largely by the $85,000 wire).  
      A detailed exhibit catalog, figure summary, itemized inconsistency log, and recommended follow-up discovery follow.  
      
        II. Exhibit Catalog  
      The production consists of the cover letter and seven document sets. Bates numbering is inconsistent across sets (see Part IV.M).  
                             Prod. No.  
          Document  
          Source / Custodian  
          Bates Range  
          Period Covered  
          Responsive RFP Nos.  
            Cover Ltr.  
          Transmittal letter & privilege log (PRIV-001–004)  
          R. Fossett / Hargrove Fossett & Yates LLP  
          n/a  
          undated (transmittal)  
          1–22 (index); 13, 14, 15, 18 (withheld)  
            PROD-001  
          Respondent’s Sworn Financial Declaration  
          Darren Kwan (prep. by counsel)  
          n/a  
          filed Sept. 20, 2024 (per declaration)  
          1, 2, 3  
            PROD-002  
          2023 Form 1040 excerpts (Sched. E, K-1, W-2s, entity summary)  
          Archer & Linden CPAs; joint return  
          DK-PROD-0045 – 0058  
          Tax Year 2023  
          4, 5, 8  
            PROD-003  
          Crestline Wealth Advisors brokerage statements (acct. 9956)

          KWAN_PROD_00142 – 00158  
          Jan. 1 – Aug. 1, 2024  
          6, 7  
            PROD-004  
          Fairview Community Bank individual statements (acct. 7703, 7704)  
          Fairview Community Bank  
          KWAN        PROD    0401 – 0424  
          Mar. 1 – Aug. 1, 2024  
          6, 7

          Meridian Home Lending mortgage statements (1035 Elm St., Unit C)

          n/a (loan 7842-0163-ELM)
