          Platinum Visa credit card statements (acct. ending 8316)  
          Fairview Community Bank  
          none stamped  
          May–Aug. 2024  
          6, 7, 11  
            PROD-007  
          NovaBridge Solutions LLC expense reimbursement reports (D. Kwan)  
          NovaBridge Solutions LLC  
          KWAN        PROD    00187 – 00193  
          Q1–Q2 2024  
          5, 8, 12  
             Documents withheld (privilege log):         PRIV-001 (email, Kwan→Fossett, Aug. 5, 2024, re: financial obligations post-separation); PRIV-002 (email chain, Aug. 12–14, 2024, re: document preservation/disclosure post-separation); PRIV-003 (memorandum, Fossett→Kwan, Aug. 20, 2024, re:         “    Preliminary analysis of marital estate    ”    ); PRIV-004 (email, Kwan→Fossett, Sept. 8, 2024, re:         “    Response to petition    ”    ). A formal privilege log is promised within ten (10) business days.  
          Documents not yet produced (noted in cover letter):         Entity-level NovaBridge records, the Operating Agreement, and documents responsive to RFP Nos. 16–22 are being         “    coordinated    ”         with NovaBridge and co-member Jared Ostrowski and will be         “    supplemented.    ”         Respondent’s own First RFP to Petitioner (served Sept. 18, 2024) remains outstanding.  
      
        III. Summary of Key Figures (As Declared vs. As Documented)  
       A. Income (Calendar Year 2023)  
                           Component  
          Declared (PROD-001)  
          Per Tax Return / K-1 (PROD-002)  
          Variance  
            W-2 salary (NovaBridge)  
          $185,000  
          $185,000 (W-2 Box 1)  
          none  
            K-1 pass-through income  
          $174,000 (labeled         “    K-1 Income / Distributions    ”    )  
          $367,200 (K-1 Box 1, ordinary business income)  
              +$193,200  
            K-1 cash distributions  
          (reported as income, above)  
          $174,000 (K-1 Box 19a)  
          n/a — distribution, not income  
            Net rental income (1035 Elm St.)  
          $0 (declared         “    None    ”    )  
          $2,940 (Sched. E)  
              +$2,940  
            Interest income (joint)  
          $0 (declared         “    None    ”    )  
          $1,840 (Form 1040, Line 2b)  
              +$1,840  
            Ordinary dividends (joint)  
          $0 (declared         “    None    ”    )  
          $6,310 (Line 3b)  
              +$6,310  
            Capital gains (joint)  
          $0 (declared         “    None    ”    )  
          $8,450 (Line 7)  
              +$8,450  
            Vehicle allowance (W-2 Box 14)  
          not disclosed  
          $9,600 ($800/mo.)  
              +$9,600  
                Respondent-attributable total  
              $359,000  
              ≥ $555,140         (W-2 + K-1 income + rental) plus share of joint investment income  
              understated by ≥ $196,140  
         The Declaration’s ¶19 affirmatively states that Respondent has         “    no other sources of income, including but not limited to interest income, dividend income, rental income, royalty income, trust income, or income from any other business venture or investment.    ”         The tax return contradicts this on four of those categories. The $193,200 gap between K-1             income         ($367,200) and the             distribution         ($174,000) equals Respondent’s 60% share of NovaBridge’s 2023 entity-level retained earnings ($322,000 × 60% = $193,200), i.e., marital equity retained inside the business and not captured by the distribution figure.  
        B. Assets (As Declared)  
                          Asset  
          Declared Value  
          Notes / Cross-Reference  
            Marital residence — 418 Birchwood Lane  
          $715,000 (value); $218,400 mortgage; $496,600 equity  
          Online estimate; no appraisal; no mortgage statement produced for this property  
            Joint checking (FCB 4821)  
          $12,340  
          No statement produced  
            Joint savings (FCB 4822)  
          $28,750  
          No statement produced  
            Individual checking (FCB 7703)  
          $8,215  
          Matches             Aug. 1, 2024         balance, not June 1  
            Individual savings (FCB 7704)  
          $42,680  
          Matches             Aug. 1, 2024         balance, not June 1  
            Brokerage (Crestline 9956)  
          $387,450  
          Matches             Aug. 1, 2024         valuation; was ~$468,695 on June 1  
            401(k) (Pinnacle 3310)  
          $412,800  
          No statement produced  
            403(b) — Petitioner (Pinnacle 5587)  
          $89,200  
          Petitioner’s; not in Respondent’s control  
            2022 BMW X5  
          $48,500 value; $22,100 loan; $26,400 equity  
          Titled to Respondent  
            2020 Honda CR-V  
          $24,800 value; $0 loan; $24,800 equity  
          Titled to Petitioner  
            Household/personal property  
          $30,000  
          Estimate  
            NovaBridge 60% membership interest  
              unvalued  
          K-1 ending capital account $675,800 (tax basis); no appraisal  
                Total financial accounts (declared)  
              $981,435  
          includes Petitioner’s 403(b)  
           C. Liabilities (As Declared)  
                          Liability  
          Declared Balance  
          Notes  
            Mortgage — 418 Birchwood Lane (Meridian)  
          $218,400  
          No statement produced  
            Auto loan — 2022 BMW X5 (FCB Auto Finance)  
          $22,100  
          $425/mo.; payment not visible in produced checking records  
            Credit card — Platinum Visa  
          $8,500  
          Creditor misstated; see Part IV.G  
                Total declared liabilities  
              $249,000  
       
                Undisclosed: mortgage — 1035 Elm St. (Meridian)  
              ~$241,281  
          Per PROD-005 (Aug. 1, 2024)  
                Adjusted total liabilities  
              ~$490,281  
          understated by ~$241,281  
           D. Monthly Expenses (As Declared, PROD-001 §V)  
      Declared total personal monthly expenses:             $8,885    ; plus declared voluntary contribution of             $3,500         to the marital household =             $12,385/month         total outlay. The bank records show materially higher actual monthly outflows (see Part IV.I).  
        E. Business Interest — NovaBridge Solutions LLC (60%)  
                          Metric  
          2023  
          2022  
            Gross revenue  
          $4,280,000  
          $3,915,000  
            Ordinary business income (Form 1065)  
          $612,000  
          $548,000  
            Total partner distributions  
          $290,000 (Kwan $174,000 / Ostrowski $116,000)  
          $260,000  
            Entity-level retained earnings  
          $322,000  
          n/a  
            Respondent’s K-1 ordinary income  
          $367,200 (60%)  
          n/a  
            Respondent’s ending capital account  
          $675,800  
          n/a  
            Respondent’s share of recourse liabilities  
          $36,400  
          n/a  
         No business valuation has been performed. The Operating Agreement (containing transfer restrictions, buyout provisions, and the self-approval provision discussed in Part IV.J) has not been produced.  
      
         IV. Flagged Inconsistencies and Red Flags  
       A. Undisclosed Rental Property — 1035 Elm Street, Unit C (HIGH)  
      The Sworn Financial Declaration (¶20–23) lists only the marital residence at 418 Birchwood Lane and states that Respondent         “    does not hold any ownership interest, whether sole or joint, in any other real property.    ”         Three other exhibits contradict this:  
              PROD-002 (tax return), Schedule E, Part I:         reports a rental condominium at 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, Fairview, Columbia 20147,         “    Property titled in the name of Darren Kwan,    ”         rented 365 days, generating             $25,200         in 2023 rent ($2,100/month) and             $2,940         net rental income, with             $11,280         in mortgage interest paid to Meridian Home Lending.  
              PROD-005 (mortgage statements):         three monthly statements for loan 7842-0163-ELM, borrower Darren Kwan (sole, no co-borrower), property 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, original loan $279,000 dated Oct. 8, 2019, current principal balance             $241,281.19         as of Aug. 1, 2024, PITI             $1,680/month    .  
              PROD-004 (bank statements):         recurring         “    ACH Deposit — Tenant Rent Payment    ”         of             $2,100/month         (April 5, May 3, June 5, July 3, 2024) and a recurring         “    ACH Payment — Meridian Home Lending    ”         of             $1,680/month         (the Elm Street mortgage), distinct from the $1,842 Birchwood mortgage payment.  
          Impact:         The Declaration omits (i) a marital asset (the Elm Street condominium, acquired and financed during the marriage in October 2019), (ii) a liability of approximately             $241,281    , and (iii) rental income of             $2,100/month    . The property was acquired during the marriage and is presumptively marital notwithstanding sole title. Notably, Respondent produced mortgage statements for the             undisclosed         Elm Street property but produced             no         mortgage statements for the             disclosed         Birchwood marital residence.  
        B. Income Understatement (HIGH)  
      As detailed in Part III.A, the Declaration reports $174,000 of         “    K-1 Income / Distributions,    ”         conflating the cash distribution (K-1 Box 19a) with taxable pass-through income (K-1 Box 1 = $367,200). It also affirmatively denies interest, dividend, rental, and capital-gain income that the joint return reports. The understatement of Respondent-attributable 2023 income is at least             $196,140         and is directly material to child-support and alimony calculations.  
        C. $85,000 Wire Transfer to Undisclosed Account (HIGH — Dissipation Concern)  
      PROD-003 (Crestline, June 2024 statement) shows an outgoing wire transfer of             $85,000         on             June 15, 2024    , from account 9956 to             Harborstone Financial Group, account ending 2291         (reference WT-2024-061524-0087), initiated by the account holder with verbal telephone authorization. The Harborstone account is not listed anywhere in the Declaration’s account schedule (¶24–33). The wire occurred two weeks after the Declaration’s stated June 1, 2024 separation date. The Crestline year-to-date summary confirms total withdrawals of $97,300 through Aug. 1, 2024, of which $85,000 is this single wire (the remainder being advisory fees and a $10,550 IRS estimated-tax payment). This transfer is a prime candidate for a dissipation-of-marital-assets claim and for a tracing/subpoena of the Harborstone account.  
        D. Recurring Venmo Transfers to         “    Kiera M.    ”         (HIGH)  
      PROD-004 (checking 7703) shows recurring Venmo payments to         “    Kiera M.    ”    :  
                        Date  
          Amount  
            Mar. 15, 2024  
          $2,800  
            Apr. 22, 2024  
          $3,100  
            May 10, 2024  
          $2,500  
            Jun. 18, 2024  
          $3,200  
            Jul. 14, 2024  
          $3,000  
                Total (5 months)  
              $14,600  
         These transfers (~$2,920/month) are not disclosed or explained anywhere in the production. The identity of         “    Kiera M.    ”         and the purpose of the payments are unknown. The pattern is consistent with support of a third party and should be explored by interrogatory and at deposition.  
        E. Separation-Date Conflict (HIGH)  
              PROD-001 (Declaration, ¶5):         “    The parties effectively separated on or about             June 1, 2024    .    ”  
              Cover letter, privilege log:         PRIV-001 references         “    the parties’ separation on             August 1, 2024    ”    ; PRIV-002 is captioned         “    post-separation August 1, 2024.    ”  
      A two-month discrepancy in the date of separation is material: it determines the marital-estate cutoff and the characterization of the June 15, 2024 $85,000 wire, the June–July Venmo transfers, the brokerage decline, and the commencement of separate living expenses. If separation was August 1 (as Respondent’s counsel’s own privilege log states), then the June 15 wire and related transactions occurred             during         the marriage and the June 1         “    separation-date    ”         valuations in the Declaration are premature. This conflict must be pinned down.  
        F. Valuation-Date Misrepresentation (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
      The Declaration (¶56) represents that         “    [a]ll asset values and account balances reflected herein are approximate as of the date of separation, which Respondent identifies as on or about June 1, 2024.    ”         The produced statements show the reported balances actually match             August 1, 2024         values:  
                            Account  
          Declared (claimed 6/1/24)  
          Actual 6/1/24 value  
          Actual 8/1/24 value  
          Match  
            Checking 7703  
          $8,215  
          ~$26,870  
          $8,215.00  
          Aug. 1  
            Savings 7704  
          $42,680  
          ~$40,548  
          $42,680.00  
          Aug. 1  
            Brokerage 9956  
          $387,450  
          ~$468,695  
          $387,450.00  
          Aug. 1  
         The brokerage account declined approximately             $81,245         between June 1 and August 1, 2024, driven principally by the $85,000 wire (Part IV.C). Reporting the post-transfer August 1 value while labeling it a June 1 separation-date value obscures the transfer and the account’s higher marital value.  
        G. Credit-Card Discrepancies (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
              Creditor misidentified.         The Declaration (¶46) states the card is held with         “    First National Card Services.    ”         The produced statements (PROD-006) and the bank ACH payee (PROD-004) both identify the creditor as             Fairview Community Bank         (account ending 8316, $50,000 credit limit).  
              Balance/payment characterization.         The Declaration describes an         “    approximate revolving balance    ”         of $8,500 and states Respondent         “    typically pays between $500 and $1,000 per month.    ”         The statements show the card is             paid in full each month         (payment equals prior balance), with monthly charges of $7,038.45 / $8,124.30 / $12,486.75 / $6,418.50 (May–Aug. 2024), totaling             $34,068         in four months (~$8,517/month average). No statement reflects an $8,500 revolving balance, and actual payments far exceed $500–$1,000/month.  
              Possible second credit account.         The checking account shows monthly ACH payments to         “    Fairview Community Bank Visa    ”         of $1,450 / $2,230 / $3,875 / $4,120 / $5,480 (March–July 2024). These amounts             do not reconcile         with the pay-in-full payments shown on the Platinum Visa statements (ending 8316), which post on the 1st of each month in different amounts. This suggests either a second card/line of credit at Fairview Community Bank or misapplied payments — either way, an obligation not fully disclosed.  
        H. Mischaracterization of         “    Voluntary    ”         Support (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
      The Declaration (¶51) states Respondent         “    voluntarily    ”         contributes             $3,500/month         “    toward the marital home mortgage payment and household expenses for the benefit of Petitioner and the minor children.    ”         The bank records (PROD-004) show two monthly Meridian Home Lending ACH payments totaling $3,522:             $1,842         (the Birchwood marital mortgage) and             $1,680         (the Elm Street rental mortgage). The $1,680 payment services Respondent’s own undisclosed investment property and confers no benefit on Petitioner or the children. Characterizing the combined $3,522 as support         “    for the benefit of Petitioner and the minor children    ”         overstates the support provided to the household by approximately             $1,680/month         and is relevant to any temporary-support motion or credit claim.  
        I. Expense Declaration Understates Actual Spending (MEDIUM)  
      The declared monthly budget ($8,885 personal; $12,385 with voluntary contribution) omits several substantial recurring outflows visible in the bank records, including: the Venmo transfers to         “    Kiera M.    ”         (~$2,920/month), the Elm Street mortgage ($1,680/month), the full credit-card payments ($1,450–$5,480/month), and a recurring $1,437.50/month ACH to Pinnacle Retirement Services 401(k) (see Part IV.L). As an illustration, June 2024 checking outflows totaled             $15,450.08         — exceeding the declared $12,385 total outlay even before accounting for the $85,000 brokerage wire. Specific line-item variances include auto insurance (declared $210/month vs. actual Progressive ACH $312/month) and clothing/dining (declared $200 + $600 = $800/month vs. credit-card charges routinely exceeding $3,000/month in those categories alone, e.g., Monarch Fine Clothiers $1,420–$2,340/month; country-club dues $425/month).  
        J. NovaBridge Expense Reimbursements — Self-Approval and Personal-as-Business (MEDIUM)  
      PROD-007 discloses that, pursuant to             Section 5.3 of the NovaBridge Operating Agreement    , Respondent — as the 60% managing member —             self-approves his own expense reimbursements         without any co-member review (Ostrowski’s review is marked         “    N/A — not required    ”    ). Reimbursements totaled             $12,662         in H1 2024 (Q1 $3,373; Q2 $9,289) and were deposited directly into Respondent’s personal checking (PROD-004: $3,400 on Apr. 10; $2,150 on May 20). Two concerns:  
              Governance.         With no independent oversight of the majority member’s reimbursements, the reimbursement channel can function as disguised, discretionary compensation — relevant to both income and business-valuation analyses.  
              Personal items reimbursed as business.         A Q2 entry reimburses         “    TechVault Solutions — Technology Equipment —             Personal Devices         — Tablet, smartphone, and accessories — $2,150    ”         (05/17/2024); the credit-card statements show a same-day         “    Pinnacle Electronics — Personal Devices — $2,149.50    ”         charge (05/17/2024). The         “    Personal Devices    ”         labeling on both is a red flag that personal electronics may have been charged to the company. The Q2 report also includes a $3,400         “    Sapphire Sky Travel — client appreciation event planning    ”         charge, while the credit card shows a separate $6,200         “    Sapphire Sky Travel    ”         charge in July 2024 (Q3, not produced).  
      The Operating Agreement itself has             not         been produced (RFP Nos. 16–22 outstanding).  
        K. Vehicle Allowance Not Disclosed as Income (LOW-MEDIUM)  
      The 2023 W-2 (PROD-002) Box 14 reports a             $9,600 vehicle allowance         ($800/month) paid by NovaBridge. This benefit is not reflected anywhere in the Declaration’s income section (¶12–19) or expense section (where auto costs are listed gross, with no offsetting allowance). Separately, the declared $425/month BMW auto-loan payment is not visible as a distinct ACH in the produced checking records — its payment source is unclear (possibly the vehicle allowance or a separate account).  
        L. 401(k) ACH Anomaly — Possible Undisclosed Loan (MEDIUM)  
      PROD-004 shows a recurring             $1,437.50/month         ACH to         “    Pinnacle Retirement Services 401(k)    ”         (March 28, April 28, May 28, June 28, July 28, 2024),             in addition to         the payroll-deferred 401(k) contributions already reflected in the net direct deposit. On             August 1, 2024    , a single ACH of             $14,644.60         to Pinnacle 401(k) (    “    Early Month    ”    ) appears. A separate monthly ACH to the 401(k) custodian, on top of payroll deferrals, is consistent with a             401(k) loan repayment         — which would be an undisclosed liability. No 401(k) statements have been produced to confirm the nature of these transfers or the $412,800 declared balance.  
        M. Production Metadata / Authentication Inconsistencies (LOW-MEDIUM)  
              Case number.         Most documents bear Case No.         2024-DR-01847    . The Fairview Community Bank production legend (PROD-004) cites             2024-DR-00847         and captions the matter         “        In re Marriage of Kwan    ”         (vs.     “        Torres-Kwan v. Kwan    ”    ) in the         “    Circuit Court of Columbia, Fairview County    ”         (vs.     “    Circuit Court of Fairview County, Columbia    ”    ). These clerical discrepancies should be corrected to avoid authentication issues.  
              Bates numbering.         Prefixes and formats are inconsistent:         “    DK-PROD-    ”         (PROD-002),         “    KWAN_PROD_    ”         (PROD-003),         “    KWAN        PROD    ”         (PROD-004, PROD-007). PROD-006 (credit card) bears             no Bates stamp at all    . The ranges also overlap oddly (Crestline 00142–00158; expense reports 00187–00193; FCB 0401–0424). A unified, consecutive Bates scheme should be demanded.  
              Crestline account tax-ID anomaly.         The Crestline statements list the account Tax ID as             ●●●-●●-8341    , whose last four digits match the SSN of the parties’ minor child             Ethan Kwan (XXX-XX-8341)         rather than Respondent’s (XXX-XX-4718). This may be a redaction artifact, but it warrants verification of the account’s registered owner/tax ID (via the account application or W-9), as it could bear on whether the account is properly Respondent’s individual asset.  
        N. Filing-Date and Counsel-Contact Discrepancies (LOW)  
              Filing date.         The cover letter describes PROD-001 as         “    filed with the Court on or about             October 4, 2024    ,    ”         while the Declaration itself states         “    Filed:             September 20, 2024    ”         and bears a September 20, 2024 notarization and certificate of service.  
              Counsel contact.         The cover letter lists Respondent’s counsel as tel. (571) 555-4200, email rfossett@hfy-law.com. The Declaration’s prepared-by and certificate-of-service blocks list tel. (571) 555-0184, email rfossett@hfylaw.com (different number and email domain).  
      
         V. Recommended Follow-Up Discovery  
      The following follow-up is recommended, organized by objective. Priorities are marked             [P1]         (highest) through             [P3]    .  
       A. Real Property and the Marital Residence  
              [P1]         Serve a targeted interrogatory demanding that Respondent identify             every         real property interest held at any time during the marriage, including 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, and to explain its omission from the Declaration.  
              [P1]         Request/produce mortgage statements and the note for             418 Birchwood Lane         (the disclosed marital residence), which were             not         produced, to verify the declared $218,400 balance and $1,842 PITI.  
              [P1]         Request the deed, settlement statement, and current mortgage statement for             1035 Elm Street, Unit C    , plus all lease/rental records and tenant information, and an appraisal or market analysis of its fair market value.  
              [P2]         Request Schedule E and rental records for tax years 2020–2022 to establish the Elm Street acquisition and rental history.  
        B. Income and Tax  
              [P1]         Request the             complete         2023 Form 1040 (all 47 pages), including Schedule A, Schedule B, Schedule D, Schedule SE, and Form 8995, plus the complete 2021 and 2022 returns.  
              [P1]         Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to reconcile the $174,000         “    K-1 income    ”         figure in the Declaration with the $367,200 K-1 ordinary business income on the tax return, and to state his total 2023 gross income per the return.  
              [P1]         Request             2024 pay stubs         (year-to-date) to reconcile the $7,115.38 bi-monthly net direct deposit ($170,769 annualized) against the declared $185,000 gross salary — the implied total withholding (~7.7%) is inconsistent with the 2023 W-2 withholding pattern and suggests either higher 2024 gross pay or sharply reduced withholding/deferral.  
              [P2]         Request all K-1s and distribution records for 2021–2024 and any 2024 estimated-tax payment records.  
        C. The $85,000 Wire and the Harborstone Account  
              [P1]         Serve a             subpoena duces tecum on Harborstone Financial Group         for all records of account ending 2291 (and any other accounts in Respondent’s name), including opening documents, signature cards, statements, and wire/transfer records from January 2024 to present.  
              [P1]         Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to identify the Harborstone account, its purpose, all deposits and withdrawals, and the disposition of the $85,000 wired on June 15, 2024.  
              [P1]         Request the Crestline wire-transfer authorization file, including the recorded telephone authorization referenced in the June 2024 statement.  
        D.         “    Kiera M.    ”         and Other Unexplained Transfers  
              [P1]         Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to identify         “    Kiera M.,    ”         the nature of the relationship, and the purpose of each Venmo transfer (totaling $14,600 March–July 2024), and to produce any related correspondence or agreements.  
              [P2]         Request Venmo/PayPal/Zelle and other P2P payment records for Respondent for 2022–2024, and bank records for any account used to fund or receive such transfers.  
        E. Separation Date and Valuation  
              [P1]         Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to state under oath the precise date of separation and to reconcile the Declaration’s June 1, 2024 date with the August 1, 2024 date in his counsel’s privilege log.  
              [P1]         Request that Respondent re-state all account balances and asset values as of the agreed/court-determined valuation date, with monthly statement balances for each account from January 2024 to present, to expose post-separation changes (especially the brokerage decline).  
        F. Credit Cards and Other Debts  
              [P1]         Request             all         credit-card, charge-card, and line-of-credit statements for Respondent (and joint) for 2022–2024 from Fairview Community Bank and any other issuer, to resolve the reconciliation gap between the checking-account         “    FCB Visa    ”         ACH payments and the Platinum Visa (8316) statements, and to confirm whether a second credit account exists.  
              [P2]         Request the auto-loan account statements for the 2022 BMW X5 and confirmation of the payment source (the $425/month payment is not visible in the produced checking records).  
        G. Retirement Accounts  
              [P1]         Request             401(k) statements         (Pinnacle, account 3310) for 2022–2024, including any loan account records, to determine whether the $1,437.50/month ACH and the $14,644.60 August 1 transfer constitute loan repayments (an undisclosed liability) and to verify the $412,800 balance.  
              [P2]         Request the 403(b) records for Petitioner’s account (5587) through Petitioner’s own production.  
        H. NovaBridge and the Business Interest  
              [P1]         Press for production of the             NovaBridge Operating Agreement         (all versions) and all entity-level financial records responsive to RFP Nos. 16–22, which Respondent has deferred.  
              [P1]         Request Form 1065 and financial statements for NovaBridge for 2021–2023, and year-to-date 2024, plus the K-1 package and capital-account ledgers.  
              [P1]         Request the             underlying receipts         for the PROD-007 expense reimbursements, with particular attention to the         “    Personal Devices    ”         entries and the Sapphire Sky Travel charges, and an interrogatory on whether any personal expenses were reimbursed as business expenses.  
              [P2]         Retain a             business valuator         to value Respondent’s 60% membership interest, accounting for the self-approval/compensation discretion in Section 5.3, transfer restrictions, buyout provisions, and the $36,400 recourse-liability exposure.  
              [P2]         Consider a deposition of co-member             Jared Ostrowski         (or a 30(b)(6)/entity deposition of NovaBridge) on compensation, distributions, reimbursements, and any post-separation changes.  
        I. Joint Accounts and Petitioner’s Reciprocal Production  
              [P1]         Request statements for the             joint checking (4821) and joint savings (4822)         accounts, which were not produced despite being listed in the Declaration; Respondent’s objection to producing         “    Petitioner’s solely-held accounts    ”         does not extend to joint accounts.  
              [P2]         Pursue Petitioner’s             reciprocal production         in response to Respondent’s First RFP (served Sept. 18, 2024), and gather Petitioner’s 403(b), pay, and bank records to complete the estate picture.  
        J. Privilege and Withheld Documents  
              [P2]         Hold Respondent to the promised             formal privilege log         within 10 business days. Scrutinize             PRIV-003         (the Aug. 20, 2024         “    Preliminary analysis of marital estate    ”         memorandum) — to the extent it contains factual financial information or communications that are not legal advice, consider a privilege challenge. The privilege-log entries referencing         “    financial obligations and asset disclosures    ”         (PRIV-001) and         “    document preservation and disclosure obligations    ”         (PRIV-002) may also contain non-privileged factual content.  
              [P3]         Consider a motion to compel if the Operating Agreement and entity-level records (RFP Nos. 16–22) are not produced promptly.  
        K. Authentication / Production Hygiene  
              [P3]         Request that Respondent correct the case-number and caption discrepancies in the FCB production (2024-DR-00847 vs. 01847) and adopt a single, consecutive Bates-numbering scheme, and that PROD-006 (credit card) be properly Bates-stamped.  
      
         VI. Preliminary Assessment and Next Steps  
      The production, while voluminous, is incomplete and internally inconsistent in ways that uniformly favor Respondent: income is understated (K-1 distribution reported instead of pass-through income; investment and rental income denied); an entire real-property asset and its mortgage are omitted; a large post-separation transfer to an undisclosed account is not explained; recurring transfers to an unidentified third party are not disclosed; and the declared account balances use a later valuation date than represented, masking the transfer. The separation date itself is contradicted by Respondent’s own privilege log.  
          Recommended immediate actions:  
          Calendar the 10-business-day deadline for the formal privilege log and the supplementation deadline for RFP Nos. 16–22.  
          Prepare and serve the             targeted interrogatories         (Part V.A.1, V.B.6, V.C.10, V.D.12, V.E.14) and the             subpoena to Harborstone Financial Group         (Part V.C.9) — these are the highest-value, fastest-moving items.  
          Draft a             meet-and-confer letter         to opposing counsel identifying the Elm Street omission, the income understatement, the $85,000 wire, the         “    Kiera M.    ”         transfers, and the separation-date conflict, and demanding supplementation of the Declaration and production of the missing mortgage, joint-account, 401(k), and Operating Agreement records.  
          Begin scoping a             forensic accounting/tracing         engagement focused on (i) the $85,000 wire and the Harborstone account, (ii) the         “    Kiera M.    ”         transfers, and (iii) the Elm Street rental cash flow.  
          Evaluate a             motion to compel         and/or a             dissipation claim         contingent on the supplementation and subpoena returns.  
      We are available to draft the interrogatories, subpoena, and meet-and-confer letter upon your direction.  
      
          Prepared by the case team for internal use. This memorandum constitutes attorney work product and is privileged and confidential. All figures are drawn from the produced exhibits cited; final figures should be confirmed against source documents and any supplementation.

### FILE: gap-analysis-memo.docx
         HARTWELL & CABOT LLP  
      Attorneys at Law — One Gateway Center, Suite 2400, Newark, New Jersey 07102  
       
          PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT  
          May 19, 2025  
      VIA ELECTRONIC DELIVERY  
      Rebecca Thorsen, Esq.         Senior Vice President & General Counsel         Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.         400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300         Edison, New Jersey 08817  
          Re: Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review — Gap Analysis and Remediation Memorandum  
      Dear Rebecca:  
      This memorandum, together with the accompanying Multi-State Compliance Matrix (the         “    Matrix    ”    ), constitutes Hartwell & Cabot LLP’s (    “    H&C    ”    ) deliverables in connection with the multi-state paid leave compliance review of Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. (    “    Vantage    ”         or the         “    Company    ”    ) undertaken pursuant to our engagement letter dated April 14, 2025. The Matrix provides the dimension-by-dimension, state-by-state comparison of the Company’s current paid leave policies and practices against the statutory and regulatory requirements of all seven jurisdictions. This memorandum identifies the material compliance deficiencies, categorizes them by severity, explains the associated legal risk, and sets forth a prioritized remediation plan with action items, responsible parties, and recommended deadlines calibrated to the Company’s facility opening schedule and the July 1, 2025 target date for the revised Employee Handbook.  
       I. Executive Summary  
      Vantage operates paid leave programs that are materially out of compliance with the laws of the three states in which it currently operates and are not yet configured for the four states into which it plans to expand. The Company’s Employee Handbook leave policies (Section 6, revised January 15, 2022) were drafted primarily for New Jersey and New York, with only informal consideration of Connecticut, and contain no provisions addressing Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, or Washington. The Company’s self-funded paid family leave benefit — 50% of average weekly wage (    “    AWW    ”    ), capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks — falls below the mandated benefit level in all seven jurisdictions, no private-plan approval has been filed in any state, and the benefit does not cover the employee’s own serious health condition in any state. A pending New Jersey Department of Labor (    “    NJ DOL    ”    ) complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117,             Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.    ) has already resulted in regulatory action and confirms that the Company’s narrow family-member definition and limited covered-uses language are non-compliant under the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act. Separately, the Company has not registered with the state paid leave insurance programs in any of the four expansion states, creating imminent registration deadlines — most urgently for Massachusetts (facility opening August 1, 2025) and Colorado (three remote employees hired in April 2025).  
      H&C has identified four Critical deficiencies, eight High deficiencies, and seven Moderate deficiencies, detailed in Part III below. The most urgent actions are: (1) register with the Colorado FAMLI program immediately and the Massachusetts PFML program no later than early June 2025; (2) remediate the New Jersey sick leave policy to resolve the active NJ DOL complaint and align with N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1             et seq.    ; (3) make a structural decision on the self-funded paid family leave plan — participate in each state program, seek approved private-plan status with enhanced benefits, or discontinue the self-funded plan; and (4) redraft the Employee Handbook leave policies for all seven jurisdictions by the July 1, 2025 target date.  
        II. Scope, Methodology, and Foundational Facts  
          Scope.         Pursuant to Engagement Letter §2, this review covers paid sick leave, paid family leave, paid medical leave, employer obligations (registration, contributions, withholding, recordkeeping, posting), and PTO/vacation payout on separation across seven jurisdictions: New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut (current operations) and Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, and Washington (planned expansion). Federal FMLA compliance, local/municipal ordinances, representation in the pending NJ DOL complaint, drafting of the revised Handbook, and vendor contract negotiation are expressly outside scope (Engagement Letter §2.4).  
          Methodology.         H&C reviewed the Employee Handbook §6 (Rev. January 15, 2022); the PinnacleHR Solutions payroll withholding summary (data as of March 31, 2025); the NJ DOL complaint correspondence (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117); the HR Expansion Plan Memorandum (March 28, 2025); the internal email thread on leave-policy concerns (March 10–17, 2025); and the current leave-tracking spreadsheet sample. State requirements reflect statutes, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of May 19, 2025. The Matrix employs color-coded gap indicators: red (non-compliant), yellow (partially compliant or at risk), and green (compliant).  
          Foundational facts.         The Company is a Delaware corporation headquartered in Edison, New Jersey. Current operations comprise 2,140 employees and $112.4 million in annual payroll across three states: New Jersey (890 employees; $46.7 million), New York (680 employees; $36.8 million), and Connecticut (570 employees; $28.9 million). The planned expansion will add 420 employees and $23.6 million in annual payroll across four states: Massachusetts (130 employees; $7.8 million; facility opening August 1, 2025 at 150 Barber Avenue, Worcester), Colorado (95 employees; $5.1 million; opening November 1, 2025 at 2800 Tower Road, Aurora), Oregon (110 employees; $6.2 million; opening February 1, 2026 at 9500 SW Sandburg Street, Tigard), and Washington (85 employees; $4.5 million; opening May 1, 2026 at 6401 South 228th Street, Kent). Total projected headcount is 2,560 employees and total projected annual payroll is $136.0 million. The Employee Handbook was last revised January 15, 2022; the target date for a revised Handbook is July 1, 2025. The Company’s self-funded paid family leave benefit provides 50% of AWW, capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks. Payroll is administered by PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc., whose lead time for configuring withholding in a new state is approximately six to eight weeks following receipt of the employer’s registration confirmation. The Company’s compliance remediation budget is $175,000.  
        III. Gap Analysis by Severity  
      H&C has categorized each deficiency using the severity framework set forth in Engagement Letter §2.2:             Critical         (active legal exposure, imminent regulatory deadline at risk, or regulatory action already commenced or likely imminent);             High         (non-compliance likely to result in enforcement, penalties, or employee claims if not remediated before operations commence or in the near term); and             Moderate         (technical non-compliance, best-practice gaps, or policy ambiguities that may not trigger immediate enforcement but expose the Company over time or upon complaint or audit).  
       A. Critical Deficiencies  
       Critical 1 — New Jersey Earned Sick Leave non-compliance and active NJ DOL complaint  
          Source.         NJ DOL Complaint Summary (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117, received February 3, 2025); H&C response letter dated February 21, 2025; Employee Handbook §6.2; PinnacleHR withholding summary (NJ Earned Sick Leave compliance note).  
          Deficiency.         The Company’s paid sick leave policy is non-compliant with the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1             et seq.    , on multiple dimensions, and the non-compliance has already resulted in a formal NJ DOL complaint filed by warehouse associate Carla Muñoz. The complaint alleges that Ms. Muñoz was denied the use of accrued sick leave to care for her domestic partner’s child. The policy’s family-member definition — limited to         “    spouse, child, or parent    ”         — is narrower than the statutory definition, which includes a child of a domestic partner, a domestic partner or civil union partner, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, any blood relative, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship (N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1). The policy’s enumeration of covered uses — limited to the employee’s own illness, injury, or medical appointment — omits several statutorily required categories under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3, including care for a family member during illness, preventive medical care, absences related to domestic or sexual violence, absences due to public health emergency closures, and attendance at school-related functions.  
      Beyond the issues raised in the complaint, H&C’s review identifies additional New Jersey sick leave gaps confirmed by the PinnacleHR withholding summary and the Company’s own leave-tracking spreadsheet: (a) the accrual rate of one hour per 40 hours worked is below the statutory rate of one hour per 30 hours worked; (b) the part-time accrual cap of 24 hours per year is non-compliant, as New Jersey requires 40 hours for all employees regardless of full-time or part-time status (the spreadsheet itself notes         “    NJ law requires 40 hrs for all employees    ”    ); and (c) the no-carryover policy is non-compliant, as New Jersey requires carryover of up to 40 hours of unused sick leave to the following benefit year.  
          Why it matters.         This is the only deficiency that has already resulted in regulatory action. The NJ DOL has reserved the right to conduct a broader investigation of the Company’s sick leave practices, policies, and records, and violations may subject the Company to fines, administrative penalties, and orders of restitution under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-7. The complaint also signals that Vantage employees are willing to escalate to state agencies, increasing the likelihood of additional complaints if the underlying policy defects remain unremediated.  
          Recommendation.         Redraft the paid sick leave policy to (i) adopt the full statutory family-member definition; (ii) enumerate all covered uses required by N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3; (iii) increase the accrual rate to one hour per 30 hours worked; (iv) apply a 40-hour annual cap to all employees regardless of classification; and (v) provide carryover of up to 40 hours. Make Ms. Muñoz whole for lost wages or expended PTO. Complete supervisor and HR training on covered family members and permissible leave purposes. Target completion: July 1, 2025 (revised Handbook), with the Muñoz remediation addressed in coordination with the separate NJ DOL representation matter.  
        Critical 2 — Colorado FAMLI non-registration with April 2025 remote hires  
          Source.         HR Expansion Plan Memo §3.2, §4.2; PinnacleHR withholding summary (Expansion States tab); internal email thread (March 10, 2025).  
          Deficiency.         The Company has not registered with the Colorado Family and Medical Leave Insurance (    “    FAMLI    ”    ) program (C.R.S. § 8-13.3-501             et seq.    ). FAMLI is mandatory for employers with 10 or more employees, and — critically — Colorado does not permit private employers to opt out in favor of a self-funded plan (the limited opt-out provision is available only to local government employers). The Company is hiring three remote Colorado-based employees in April 2025 (a regional operations manager and two logistics coordinators), approximately seven months before the Aurora facility opens on November 1, 2025. If the FAMLI registration obligation is triggered by the first date a Colorado-based employee performs work — which H&C views as the better-supported interpretation — the Company’s registration is already overdue or will be within days. Even under the more favorable interpretation that registration is tied to the November 1 facility opening, the PinnacleHR six-to-eight-week configuration lead time requires registration to be completed no later than approximately mid-August 2025.  
          Why it matters.         Operating with Colorado employees without FAMLI registration and premium remittance exposes the Company to back-premium liability, penalties, and interest, and the inability to self-fund means there is no compliant alternative to participation. The early hires convert this from a prospective risk into a present, active exposure.  
          Recommendation.         Register with the Colorado FAMLI Division immediately and provide registration confirmation to PinnacleHR. Confirm with H&C the applicable registration trigger date and assess whether back-premium remediation is required for the April 2025 hires. Issue the three Colorado offer letters with PTO-payout language referencing Colorado law (per Rebecca Thorsen’s March 12, 2025 guidance), and do not include blanket         “    no payout    ”         language. Target completion: registration initiated immediately; completed no later than September 1, 2025.  
        Critical 3 — Massachusetts PFML non-registration (August 1, 2025 opening)  
          Source.         HR Expansion Plan Memo §3.1, §4.2; PinnacleHR withholding summary (Expansion States tab); Engagement Letter §3.  
          Deficiency.         The Company has not registered with the Massachusetts Paid Family and Medical Leave (    “    MA PFML    ”    ) program (M.G.L. c. 175M). MA PFML requires employer registration and contribution remittance from the first day any covered employee works in Massachusetts. The Worcester facility opens August 1, 2025. Factoring in PinnacleHR’s six-to-eight-week configuration lead time, registration must be completed and confirmation provided to PinnacleHR no later than approximately early June 2025 to ensure withholding is operational by the opening date. As of the PinnacleHR report dated April 10, 2025, no registration has been filed and no withholding is configured.  
          Why it matters.         Any delay in registration will cascade into a delay in PinnacleHR configuration, risking the Company operating in Massachusetts without proper withholding on day one — a regulatory violation from the first payroll cycle. The window is approximately seven weeks from the report date.  
          Recommendation.         Register with the Massachusetts Department of Family and Medical Leave (    “    DFML    ”    ) immediately and provide registration confirmation to PinnacleHR no later than June 1, 2025. Determine whether to participate in the state program or apply for an approved private plan (noting the self-funded benefit does not currently meet the equivalency threshold — see Part IV). Target completion: June 1, 2025.  
        Critical 4 — Self-funded paid family leave plan below statutory minimums in all seven states; no private-plan approval filed; HR misdirection of state-program claimants  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6.3, §6.4; PinnacleHR withholding summary (all tabs and Summary & Notes); internal email thread (March 10–17, 2025); HR Expansion Plan Memo §6.3.  
          Deficiency.         The Company’s self-funded paid family leave benefit (50% of AWW, capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks, covering bonding and family caregiving only) falls below the mandated benefit level in all seven jurisdictions on the dimensions of wage replacement rate, weekly benefit cap, and duration, and it does not cover the employee’s own serious health condition in any state. No private-plan application or approval has been filed in any jurisdiction. The self-funded plan therefore does not qualify as a compliant private-plan substitute in any state.          Compounding this structural gap, PinnacleHR is withholding employee contributions for NJ FLI, NJ TDI, NY PFL, NY DBL, and CT PL — meaning employees are paying into state programs that cover their own medical conditions and family leave — but HR staff at multiple facilities have been directing employees who inquire about paid medical leave for their own conditions to the voluntary, employee-paid short-term disability plan through Keystone Benefit Administrators rather than to the applicable state program. The internal email thread identifies at least seven such inquiries in the past 12 months (four in Bridgeport, CT; two in Edison, NJ; one in Queens, NY), and fewer than 30% of Connecticut hourly staff are enrolled in the voluntary STD plan. Rebecca Thorsen acknowledged in her March 12, 2025 email that the self-funded plan         “    was supposed to supplement, not replace, the state programs    ”         but that she is         “    no longer confident that’s how it was implemented.    ”  
          Why it matters.         In the current states, employees are paying state-program contributions but are being misdirected away from the state benefits they are funding, creating exposure to retroactive claims, restitution, and additional NJ DOL-style complaints (the Muñoz matter demonstrates employee willingness to escalate). In the expansion states, the self-funded plan cannot lawfully substitute for the state programs — and in Colorado it cannot substitute at all. The dual-coverage structure (self-funded benefit running alongside unforgone state entitlements) creates employee confusion and overpayment/underpayment risk.  
          Recommendation.         Make a structural decision among three options: (a) participate in each state program and treat the self-funded benefit as a supplemental, non-substitutive benefit; (b) seek private-plan/voluntary-plan approvals in the states that permit them (NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR, WA) with enhanced benefits meeting or exceeding statutory minimums; or (c) discontinue the self-funded plan to eliminate employee confusion and dual-coverage issues. In all events, immediately direct HR staff to route employees to the applicable state program (NJ TDI, NY DBL, CT PL) for their own medical conditions and cease misdirection to the voluntary Keystone STD plan. Compile the full 12-month inquiry log (per Rebecca Thorsen’s March 12 action item) and assess retroactive exposure. Target completion: structural decision by July 1, 2025; HR routing correction immediately.  
         B. High Deficiencies  
       High 1 — New York Paid Sick Leave non-compliance  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6.2; PinnacleHR withholding summary (NY Paid Sick Leave compliance note); leave-tracking spreadsheet (NY tab).  
          Deficiency.         Vantage has 680 employees in New York, exceeding the 100-employee threshold under NY Labor Law § 196-b, which requires 56 hours of paid sick leave per year. The Company’s policy provides 40 hours (full-time) and 24 hours (part-time). Additional gaps: the accrual rate (one hour per 40 hours worked) is below the statutory one hour per 30 hours worked; the 120-day waiting period is non-compliant because New York permits employees to use sick leave as it is accrued (no waiting period); and the no-carryover policy is non-compliant because New York requires carryover of unused sick leave. The covered-uses and family-member definitions are also narrower than New York law requires.  
          Why it matters.         Non-compliance with the New York sick leave law exposes the Company to NY Department of Labor enforcement, penalties, and employee claims, and the 56-hour shortfall affects all 680 New York employees.  
          Recommendation.         Increase the New York sick leave entitlement to 56 hours, adopt the one-hour-per-30 accrual rate, eliminate the waiting period for New York employees, and provide carryover. Target completion: July 1, 2025 (revised Handbook).  
        High 2 — Connecticut Paid Sick Leave non-compliance  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6.2; PinnacleHR withholding summary (CT Paid Sick Leave compliance note).  
          Deficiency.         Connecticut (Conn. Gen. Stat. § 31-57s             et seq.    ) requires 40 hours of paid sick leave per year for covered employers without distinguishing between full-time and part-time employees for accrual and cap purposes. The Company’s 24-hour part-time cap is non-compliant. The no-carryover policy is non-compliant (Connecticut requires carryover). The covered-uses and family-member definitions are narrower than Connecticut law. The 120-day waiting period is misaligned with Connecticut’s hours-based threshold (use after 680 hours worked), particularly for part-time employees who may not reach 680 hours within 120 days.  
          Why it matters.         Affects the 570 Connecticut employees; exposes the Company to CT Department of Labor enforcement and claims.  
          Recommendation.         Apply a 40-hour cap to all Connecticut employees, provide carryover, align the waiting period with the 680-hour standard, and expand covered uses and family-member definition. Target completion: July 1, 2025.  
        High 3 — Connecticut Paid Leave (medical) gap and HR misdirection  
          Source.         Internal email thread (March 10, 2025); PinnacleHR withholding summary (CT PL).  
          Deficiency.         The Connecticut Paid Leave program covers the employee’s own serious health condition (up to 12 weeks, plus two weeks for pregnancy-related incapacity, at a 95%/60% tiered replacement up to approximately $941 per week). The Company’s self-funded plan does not cover the employee’s own condition, and the Company provides no employer-funded paid medical leave. Four Bridgeport employees have inquired about paid medical leave for their own conditions (including a hip replacement and a chronic autoimmune condition) and were directed to the voluntary, employee-paid Keystone STD plan. Employees are paying CT PL contributions via PinnacleHR but are not being directed to file state claims.  
          Why it matters.         Active exposure across 570 Connecticut employees; employees are funding a state benefit they are being denied access to.  
          Recommendation.         Direct HR to route Connecticut employees to the CT PL program for their own medical conditions; assess retroactive claims exposure for the four identified inquiries and any others in the 12-month log. Target completion: HR routing correction immediately; exposure assessment by July 1, 2025.  
        High 4 — New Jersey TDI and New York DBL claim-routing failure  
          Source.         Internal email thread (March 14, 2025); PinnacleHR withholding summary (NJ TDI, NY DBL).  
          Deficiency.         Employees in New Jersey and New York are paying into NJ Temporary Disability Insurance and NY Disability Benefits Law, respectively, both of which cover the employee’s own non-work-related disability (NJ TDI: up to 26 weeks; NY DBL: up to $170 per week for up to 26 weeks). HR staff have been directing employees to the voluntary Keystone STD plan instead of the state programs. Marcus Delgado’s March 14 email confirms that NJ HR staff         “    don’t even know that NJ TDI exists as a separate benefit from our self-funded plan.    ”  
          Why it matters.         Employees are paying for state benefits they cannot access due to HR misdirection; retroactive claim and restitution exposure across New Jersey (890 employees) and New York (680 employees).  
          Recommendation.         Train HR staff on NJ TDI and NY DBL claim procedures; route employees to the state programs; assess retroactive exposure. Target completion: HR training and routing correction by July 1, 2025.  
        High 5 — Massachusetts Earned Sick Time and PTO payout gaps (before August 1, 2025 opening)  
          Source.         PinnacleHR withholding summary (MA Earned Sick Time, MA PFML notes); Employee Handbook §6.2, §6.5.  
          Deficiency.         Massachusetts Earned Sick Time (M.G.L. c. 149 § 148C) requires one hour per 30 hours worked, a 40-hour cap for all employees (no full-time/part-time split), use after 90 days (the Company’s 120-day waiting period is non-compliant), and carryover of up to 40 hours. Separately, the Massachusetts Wage Act (M.G.L. c. 149 § 148) requires payment of accrued, unused vacation upon separation — the Company’s no-payout policy is non-compliant in Massachusetts, and the Wage Act imposes treble damages and attorney’s fees for non-payment of earned wages.  
          Why it matters.         Must be remediated before the first Massachusetts employee starts (August 1, 2025); Wage Act treble damages create significant monetary exposure.  
          Recommendation.         Configure Massachusetts sick leave (one hour per 30, 40-hour cap, 90-day use, carryover) and adopt a Massachusetts PTO-payout provision. Target completion: July 1, 2025 (revised Handbook), before August 1, 2025 opening.  
        High 6 — Colorado HFWA paid sick leave and PTO payout gaps (before November 1, 2025 opening)  
          Source.         PinnacleHR withholding summary (CO HFWA, CO FAMLI notes); Employee Handbook §6.2, §6.5.  
          Deficiency.         The Colorado Healthy Families and Workplaces Act (C.R.S. § 8-13.3-401             et seq.    ) requires all employers to provide paid sick leave at one hour per 30 hours worked, up to 48 hours per year, available for use immediately (no waiting period — the Company’s 120-day waiting period is non-compliant), with carryover, plus supplemental public health emergency leave of up to 80 hours. Colorado also requires payout of all accrued, unused vacation upon separation (C.R.S. § 8-4-101             et seq.    ) regardless of employer policy, and disallows         “    use-it-or-lose-it    ”         vacation forfeiture — rendering the Company’s no-payout policy and five-day carryover cap with forfeiture non-compliant.  
          Why it matters.         Must be remediated before the November 1, 2025 opening; the April 2025 remote hires are already exposed to the sick leave and PTO-payout requirements.  
          Recommendation.         Configure Colorado sick leave (48 hours, one hour per 30, no waiting period, carryover, supplemental PHE leave) and adopt a Colorado PTO-payout provision with no forfeiture. Target completion: revised Handbook by July 1, 2025; full compliance before November 1, 2025.  
        High 7 — Oregon Paid Leave Oregon and Sick Time gaps (before February 1, 2026 opening)  
          Source.         PinnacleHR withholding summary (PLO, OR Sick Time notes).  
          Deficiency.         The Company has not registered with Paid Leave Oregon (ORS 657B); registration should be completed by approximately December 1, 2025 to allow PinnacleHR configuration before the February 1, 2026 opening. PLO covers family leave, medical leave (own condition), and safe leave (domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, harassment) — none of which the Company’s self-funded plan covers at the required level. Oregon Sick Time (ORS 653.601) requires one hour per 30 hours worked, a 40-hour cap (10+ employees), use after 90 days (the Company’s 120-day waiting period is non-compliant), and carryover of up to 40 hours.  
          Why it matters.         Registration deadline and policy gaps must be resolved before the February 1, 2026 opening; safe-leave coverage is entirely absent from the Company’s policy.  
          Recommendation.         Register with the Oregon Employment Department by December 1, 2025; configure Oregon sick time (one hour per 30, 40-hour cap, 90-day use, carryover); add safe-leave coverage. Target completion: registration by December 1, 2025; policy by July 1, 2025 (revised Handbook).  
        High 8 — Washington PFML and Paid Sick Leave gaps (before May 1, 2026 opening)  
          Source.         PinnacleHR withholding summary (WA PFML, WA Paid Sick Leave notes).  
          Deficiency.         The Company has not registered with the Washington Paid Family and Medical Leave program (RCW 50A); registration should be completed by approximately March 1, 2026. WA PFML covers family leave, medical leave (own condition), and qualifying military exigency. Washington Paid Sick Leave (RCW 49.46.200) requires one hour per 40 hours worked (which the Company’s rate matches), but permits no cap on accrual (the Company’s 40/24-hour accrual cap is non-compliant), requires use after 90 days (the Company’s 120-day waiting period is non-compliant), and requires carryover of all unused sick leave.  
          Why it matters.         Registration deadline and policy gaps must be resolved before the May 1, 2026 opening.  
          Recommendation.         Register with the Washington Employment Security Department by March 1, 2026; remove the accrual cap for Washington employees, reduce the waiting period to 90 days, and provide carryover. Target completion: registration by March 1, 2026; policy by July 1, 2025 (revised Handbook).  
         C. Moderate Deficiencies  
       Moderate 1 — Paid family leave notice requirement lacks unforeseeable-leave carve-out  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6.3.6; internal email thread (March 10, 2025).  
          Deficiency.         The Handbook requires 30 calendar days’ advance written notice for all paid family leave and does not distinguish between foreseeable and unforeseeable leave. A Connecticut employee recently gave 10 days’ notice before starting leave to care for a parent after an unexpected hospitalization; HR initially pushed back citing the 30-day requirement. The FMLA and state programs require 30 days only for foreseeable leave and         “    as soon as practicable    ”         for unforeseeable leave. The policy’s silence could be read as requiring 30 days in all cases, which is unreasonable and likely non-compliant. Inconsistent application across sites (some strict, some flexible) creates disparate-treatment risk.  
          Recommendation.         Rewrite the notice provision to address foreseeable vs. unforeseeable leave for each state. Interim guidance (per Rebecca Thorsen, March 12, 2025) directing HR not to deny or delay unforeseeable leave should remain in effect until the policy is revised. Target completion: July 1, 2025.  
        Moderate 2 — PTO carryover cap and forfeiture at risk in wage-protection states  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6.5.3; internal email thread (March 14, 2025).  
          Deficiency.         The PTO policy caps carryover at five days, forfeits excess at year-end, and forfeits unused carryover not used by March 31. In states that treat accrued vacation as wages (Massachusetts, Colorado), this forfeiture is non-compliant (addressed in High 5 and High 6). In Connecticut and Oregon, the carryover cap and forfeiture are at risk of challenge. Marcus Delgado raised this concern for Colorado in his March 14 email.  
          Recommendation.         Eliminate vacation forfeiture in Massachusetts and Colorado; review the carryover cap for Connecticut and Oregon. Target completion: July 1, 2025.  
        Moderate 3 — Manual spreadsheet leave tracking and recordkeeping  
          Source.         HR Expansion Plan Memo §5.1; leave-tracking spreadsheet sample.  
          Deficiency.         Leave accruals, usage, and balances are tracked via manual Excel spreadsheets (one per state) maintained by two HR coordinators, with no integration with the HRIS (WorkBridge Pro) or time-and-attendance system (ClockSync), no automated alerts, no audit trail, and coverage limited to sick leave (paid family leave is tracked on paper; PTO in WorkBridge). Recent internal audits found errors, including a part-time employee’s balance incorrectly capped at 24 hours due to a wrong accrual rate, inconsistent application of the 120-day waiting period, and at least two negative balances indicating data-entry errors. Several expansion states impose specific recordkeeping requirements. Scaling to seven states with distinct rules is not sustainable on the current system.  
          Recommendation.         Implement a dedicated leave management system configured with state-specific rules for all seven jurisdictions, integrated with WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync. Estimated cost $40,000–$75,000 and three-to-six months to implement. Target completion: system selected by July 1, 2025; live for all seven states by Q1 2026 (before Oregon and Washington openings).  
        Moderate 4 — Workplace posting and employee-notice obligations  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6 (silent); PinnacleHR withholding summary (posting notes).  
          Deficiency.         The Handbook does not address state-required workplace postings or individual employee notices for any of the seven jurisdictions. In the current states, postings may or may not be displayed (unverified). In the expansion states, postings and notices must be in place from the first day of operations.  
          Recommendation.         Audit current-state postings; procure and display all required postings in each expansion facility before its opening date. Target completion: current-state audit by July 1, 2025; expansion-state postings before each opening.  
        Moderate 5 — Medical documentation thresholds not state-aligned  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6.2.8.  
          Deficiency.         The uniform two-consecutive-workday documentation threshold may not align with each state’s documentation rules (e.g., New Jersey permits documentation for absences of three or more consecutive days). The policy is more aggressive than some states require, which is generally permissible but should be verified against each state’s abuse-suspicion standards.  
          Recommendation.         Align documentation thresholds with each state’s rules in the revised Handbook. Target completion: July 1, 2025.  
        Moderate 6 — Oregon PTO payout on separation (verification needed)  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6.5.4; PinnacleHR withholding summary (OR notes).  
          Deficiency.         Oregon generally treats accrued, vested vacation as wages payable on separation (ORS 652.140), though         “    use-it-or-lose-it    ”         is permitted if clearly communicated. The Company’s no-payout policy and five-day carryover cap are at risk in Oregon and require verification.
