        Moderate 7 — Employee Handbook staleness and absence of expansion-state provisions  
          Source.         Employee Handbook §6 (Rev. January 15, 2022); HR Expansion Plan Memo §6.2.  
          Deficiency.         The Handbook was last revised January 15, 2022, and contains no provisions addressing Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, or Washington. The policies were designed for New Jersey and New York with only informal consideration of Connecticut. The intervening three-plus years have seen legislative and regulatory changes in all current states.  
          Recommendation.         Comprehensive redraft of Section 6 for all seven jurisdictions by the July 1, 2025 target date, with state-specific addenda where uniform policies cannot satisfy divergent requirements. Target completion: July 1, 2025.  
          IV. Self-Funded Paid Family Leave — Private-Plan Equivalency Analysis  
      Pursuant to Engagement Letter §2.2, H&C has specifically assessed the Company’s self-funded paid family leave benefit against each state’s mandated benefit levels, contribution structures, and eligibility criteria, and has evaluated whether the self-funded plan qualifies as a compliant private-plan alternative.  
          Benefit equivalency.         As detailed in Critical 4 and the accompanying Matrix (Sheet 6), the self-funded benefit (50% of AWW, $850 weekly cap, eight weeks, family leave only) is below the statutory benefit in all seven jurisdictions on rate, cap, and duration, and does not cover the employee’s own serious health condition. It does not qualify as a compliant private-plan substitute in any state.  
          Private-plan availability.         Six of the seven jurisdictions (NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR, WA) permit a private-plan or voluntary-plan alternative, but only if the private plan provides benefits equal to or greater than the state program in all respects (wage replacement, weekly cap, duration, covered leave types, family-member definition, eligibility). No private-plan application has been filed in any of these states. Colorado is the exception: FAMLI does not permit private-employer opt-out (the provision is limited to local government employers), so the Company must participate in the state program for Colorado employees and cannot self-fund in lieu of FAMLI.  
          Contribution structure.         In the current states, PinnacleHR is already withholding employee contributions for NJ FLI, NY PFL, and CT PL, and employee-plus-employer contributions for NJ TDI and NY DBL. Employees are therefore paying into state programs that provide benefits exceeding the self-funded plan. The self-funded plan is thus both redundant and sub-standard in the current states. In the expansion states, no contributions are being remitted because no registration has occurred.  
          Eligibility.         The self-funded plan’s eligibility threshold (12 months of employment plus 1,000 hours worked) is more restrictive than the eligibility criteria of every state program, which are generally wage- or hours-based without a 12-month tenure requirement.  
          Recommended structural approach.         H&C recommends that the Company select one of the following approaches and implement it consistently across all seven jurisdictions:  
             Participate in each state program; treat the self-funded benefit as supplemental.         This is the lowest-risk approach. The Company would participate in NJ FLI/TDI, NY PFL/DBL, CT PL, MA PFML, CO FAMLI, PLO, and WA PFML, and employees would access the full state benefit. The self-funded benefit could be retained as a supplemental top-up (e.g., to bring total replacement closer to 100% of AWW), clearly documented as non-substitutive. This eliminates equivalency risk and the misdirection problem.  
             Seek private-plan/voluntary-plan approvals with enhanced benefits.         In the six states that permit it (not Colorado), the Company could file private-plan applications with an enhanced benefit meeting or exceeding each state’s minimums. This requires enhancing the benefit (higher rate, higher cap, longer duration, own-condition coverage, broader family definition, military exigency/safe leave) and obtaining agency approval in each state. This is more complex and carries approval-timing risk.  
             Discontinue the self-funded plan.         The Company could discontinue the self-funded benefit and rely entirely on the state programs, eliminating employee confusion and dual-coverage issues. This is the simplest approach but reduces the Company’s total leave offering unless replaced with a supplemental program.  
      In all cases, Colorado requires mandatory FAMLI participation. H&C recommends Option 1 (participate and supplement) as the most efficient and lowest-risk path, particularly given the compressed expansion timeline.  
        V. Operational Risks — Manual Leave Tracking  
      The Company’s manual spreadsheet-based leave tracking system (described in HR Expansion Plan Memo §5.1) presents operational and compliance risks that will compound as the Company scales to seven jurisdictions. The system tracks only sick leave (paid family leave is tracked on paper; PTO in WorkBridge Pro), has no integration with the HRIS or time-and-attendance system, provides no automated alerts for accrual caps, waiting-period eligibility, or carryover deadlines, and maintains no audit trail of balance corrections. Recent audits identified miscalculated part-time balances, inconsistent waiting-period application, and negative balances from data-entry errors. Each expansion state imposes distinct accrual rates, caps, waiting periods, carryover provisions, and recordkeeping requirements that the current system cannot reliably administer. The lack of an audit trail is particularly significant in light of the NJ DOL’s reserved right to investigate the Company’s sick leave records. H&C concurs with Marcus Delgado’s recommendation to implement a dedicated leave management system (estimated $40,000–$75,000; three-to-six months) and notes that this expenditure must be weighed against the $175,000 compliance remediation budget (see Part VIII).  
        VI. NJ DOL Complaint Context  
      The pending NJ DOL complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117,             Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.    ) is the subject of a separate representation matter under the engagement letter dated February 15, 2025, and is outside the scope of this engagement (Engagement Letter §2.4(a)). However, the complaint informs the Company’s remediation posture. The complaint confirms that the family-member definition and covered-uses language in the paid sick leave policy are non-compliant under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 and 34:11D-3, and the NJ DOL has reserved the right to broaden its investigation. The remediation steps identified in Critical 1 (policy redraft, Muñoz remediation, supervisor training) are consistent with the remedial steps the Company represented to the NJ DOL it would undertake in H&C’s February 21, 2025 response letter, and the July 1, 2025 revised Handbook target aligns with the timeline the Company represented to the Department. H&C’s findings in this memorandum may inform the Company’s response posture in that proceeding, but the Company should coordinate any communications with the NJ DOL through the separate representation matter.  
        VII. Prioritized Remediation Plan  
      The following remediation plan sets forth action items, responsible parties, and recommended deadlines calibrated to the facility opening schedule and the July 1, 2025 revised Handbook target. Items are ordered by urgency.  
       Phase 1 — Immediate (May–June 2025)  
                           #  
          Action item  
          Owner  
          Deadline  
            1  
          Register with Colorado FAMLI Division; confirm registration trigger date for April 2025 remote hires; assess back-premium exposure  
          Legal (Rebecca Thorsen) + HR (Marcus Delgado)  
          Immediately; no later than Sept 1, 2025  
            2  
          Register with Massachusetts DFML; provide registration confirmation to PinnacleHR  
          HR + Legal, with PinnacleHR (Priya Samuelson)  
          June 1, 2025  
            3  
          Issue Colorado offer letters with PTO-payout language referencing Colorado law (no blanket         “    no payout    ”    ); legal review before issuance  
          HR + Legal  
          Upon registration  
            4  
          Direct HR staff at all sites to route employees to state programs (NJ TDI, NY DBL, CT PL) for own medical conditions; cease misdirection to Keystone STD  
          Marcus Delgado  
          Immediately  
            5  
          Maintain interim guidance: do not deny/delay unforeseeable leave; do not turn away own-condition medical-leave inquiries without checking state program  
          Marcus Delgado  
          Ongoing until Handbook revision  
            6  
          Compile 12-month log of own-condition medical-leave inquiries (CT/NJ/NY) with dates, names, HR responses  
          Marcus Delgado  
          May 30, 2025  
           Phase 2 — By July 1, 2025 (Revised Employee Handbook)  
                           #  
          Action item  
          Owner  
          Deadline  
            7  
          Redraft paid sick leave policy for all seven states: statutory family-member definitions; all covered uses; one-hour-per-30 accrual (NJ/NY/MA/CO/OR); 40-hr (NJ/CT/MA/OR) / 48-hr (CO) / 56-hr (NY) / no-accrual-cap (WA); 90-day (MA/OR/WA) / no-waiting (NY/CO) / 680-hr (CT) use thresholds; carryover in all states  
          Legal + HR  
          July 1, 2025  
            8  
          Make structural decision on self-funded PFL (participate-and-supplement recommended); file private-plan applications only if Option 2 selected  
          Legal + HR + Finance  
          July 1, 2025  
            9  
          Redraft PFL notice provision for foreseeable vs. unforeseeable leave by state  
          Legal + HR  
          July 1, 2025  
            10  
          Adopt PTO-payout provisions for MA and CO; eliminate vacation forfeiture in MA/CO; review CT/OR carryover cap  
          Legal + HR  
          July 1, 2025  
            11  
          Add qualifying military exigency (NJ/NY/CT/MA/CO/WA) and safe leave (NJ/CO/OR) coverage to PFL policy  
          Legal + HR  
          July 1, 2025  
            12  
          Audit and display all required workplace postings in current states; align medical-documentation thresholds by state  
          HR  
          July 1, 2025  
            13  
          Issue RFP / select dedicated leave management software  
          Marcus Delgado + IT  
          July 1, 2025  
            14  
          Conduct supervisor and HR training on covered family members, permissible uses, and state-program claim routing  
          HR  
          July 1, 2025  
           Phase 3 — Facility openings (August 2025 – May 2026)  
                           #  
          Action item  
          Owner  
          Deadline  
            15  
          Massachusetts facility live (Aug 1, 2025): MA PFML withholding operational; sick leave, PTO payout, postings compliant  
          HR + PinnacleHR  
          Aug 1, 2025  
            16  
          Register with Paid Leave Oregon (Oregon Employment Department); provide to PinnacleHR  
          HR + Legal  
          Dec 1, 2025  
            17  
          Colorado facility live (Nov 1, 2025): FAMLI withholding operational; sick leave (48 hr/no waiting), PTO payout, postings compliant  
          HR + PinnacleHR  
          Nov 1, 2025  
            18  
          Confirm Oregon PTO-payout obligations; update policy  
          Legal  
          Oct 1, 2025  
            19  
          Oregon facility live (Feb 1, 2026): PLO withholding operational; sick time, safe leave, postings compliant  
          HR + PinnacleHR  
          Feb 1, 2026  
            20  
          Register with Washington PFML (Employment Security Department); provide to PinnacleHR  
          HR + Legal  
          March 1, 2026  
            21  
          Washington facility live (May 1, 2026): WA PFML withholding operational; sick leave (no accrual cap, 90-day, carryover), postings compliant  
          HR + PinnacleHR  
          May 1, 2026  
            22  
          Implement leave management software live for all seven states  
          Marcus Delgado + IT  
          Q1 2026  
            23  
          Post-launch audit of all systems and compliance processes  
          HR + Legal  
          Within 30 days of WA opening  
            VIII. Budget Considerations  
      The Company has allocated a compliance remediation budget of $175,000 covering legal fees, system updates, and policy redrafting. H&C’s estimated professional fees for this engagement are $28,000–$42,000 (Engagement Letter §6). The recommended leave management software is estimated at $40,000–$75,000, and WorkBridge Pro / ClockSync deployment at $15,000–$25,000. Marcus Delgado has flagged that the budget may be tight if a significant software investment is made simultaneously with the outside-counsel engagement, and has recommended that the Company confirm budget allocation priorities with Finance, potentially pursuing the software investment through a separate capital expenditure request. H&C concurs with that recommendation and further notes that the registration and policy-redraft workstreams (the highest-risk items) should be prioritized within the existing budget, with the software investment sequenced to be live before the Oregon and Washington openings (Q1 2026). Two additional HR generalists (estimated $120,000–$140,000 combined annual salary) are recommended by HR but would be pursued through the normal headcount budget, not the compliance remediation budget.  
        IX. Limitations and Next Steps  
      This analysis is based on the laws, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of May 19, 2025, and on the accuracy and completeness of the information and representations provided by the Company. State paid leave law is a rapidly evolving area; legislative or regulatory changes after delivery may affect the analysis, and H&C recommends periodic re-review, particularly as each expansion phase is completed. This engagement does not constitute a guarantee of any particular outcome with respect to the pending NJ DOL complaint or any future regulatory action, audit, or employee claim; the ultimate compliance outcome will depend on the Company’s implementation of the recommended remediation steps.  
      H&C is available to participate in a working session with the Company’s legal and HR teams to discuss these findings and to assist with any additional scope the Company may wish to pursue, including drafting of the revised Handbook leave policies (a separate engagement), assistance with state program registrations, and preparation of non-privileged communications for PinnacleHR and Keystone Benefit Administrators. Given the registration urgency for Massachusetts and Colorado, H&C recommends that the Company initiate those registrations immediately and not await further deliverables.  
      Respectfully submitted,  
      HARTWELL & CABOT LLP  
      Catherine Ng, Partner  
      Daniel Ikeda, Senior Associate  
      cc: Marcus Delgado, Director of Human Resources, Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.  
          Enclosure: Multi-State Compliance Matrix (multi-state-compliance-matrix.xlsx)

### FILE: buy-side-cim-analysis-memo.docx
          BUY-SIDE CIM ANALYSIS MEMORANDUM  
          CONFIDENTIAL — INTERNAL USE ONLY — PREPARED FOR THORNFIELD CAPITAL PARTNERS  
                          To:  
          Marcus Yuen (Partner, Deal Lead); Diane Holbrook (Managing Partner); Sarah Lindgren (Vice President) — Fund III Investment Committee  
                From:  
          David Koh, Associate  
                Date:  
          June 25, 2025  
                Re:  
          Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. (    “    CES    ”         or the         “    Company    ”    ) — Buy-Side CIM Analysis and IOI Recommendation (Project Cascade)  
                Sources reviewed:  
          Confidential Information Memorandum (    “    CIM    ”    ) dated June 9, 2025 (Greenleaf Advisory Group); Financial Exhibits workbook (ces-financial-exhibits.xlsx); Customer Summary and Backlog Report (    “    Customer Report    ”    ); Process Letter dated June 9, 2025; Sector Overview Memo dated June 12, 2025 (S. Lindgren)  
                Process deadline:  
          Indications of Interest due July 14, 2025; target close Q4 2025  
         
       1. Executive Summary and Recommendation  
          Recommendation: Proceed to submit a non-binding Indication of Interest by the July 14, 2025 deadline, at a disciplined enterprise value of approximately $135–$150 million, contingent on confirmatory due diligence and a quality-of-earnings (        “        QoE        ”        ) review.         The asset is an attractive, on-strategy Pacific Northwest environmental services platform with genuine regulatory tailwinds and platform-for-consolidation characteristics, but the sell-side materials contain material financial inconsistencies and aggressive assumptions that must be normalized before any binding bid.  
          Why we like it.         CES is a 16-year-old, founder-built, integrated environmental services platform (remediation, industrial cleaning, emergency response) across a four-state territory with hard-to-replicate permits and licensed Professional Engineer (    “    PE    ”    ) credentials. FY2024 revenue of $87.3M grew at a 12.5% CAGR (FY2021–FY2024); reported EBITDA margin of 13.9% is within the sector range; the PFAS practice is a real, early-mover growth vector backed by federal/state regulatory mandates; and the fragmented market supports a buy-and-build thesis consistent with Fund III’s sector focus.  
          Why we must be disciplined.         Five categories of issue reduce confidence in the headline sell-side figures:  
              The FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA bridge does not foot.         The six listed adjustments sum to $4.7M, which applied to reported EBITDA of $12.1M yields $16.8M — not the $17.1M stated. The $0.3M overstatement flows directly into the valuation the seller is marketing ($2.4M–$3.0M of enterprise value at 8–10x). FY2021–FY2023 foot correctly; only FY2024 does not.  
              The CIM and the Financial Exhibits do not reconcile.         The balance sheets differ by $3.9M in total assets, $1.7M in equity, $1.8M in cash, and $1.8M in total debt; the income statements differ by $1.7M in net income; and the Exhibits contain an internal inconsistency in which operating income ($11.1M) plus D&A ($4.2M) does not equal EBITDA ($12.1M).  
              Several EBITDA add-backs are overstated or indefensible.         The $1.2M DEQ legal/regulatory add-back appears to include an $875K settlement that the Exhibits classify             below         the EBITDA line (in Other Income/Expense), meaning it was never deducted from EBITDA; and regulatory/compliance costs are recurring in this sector (CES itself recorded $0.2M of         “    one-time    ”         legal/regulatory costs in FY2023 as well). The owner-compensation add-back assumes a $500K replacement CEO, which is low for an $87M+ revenue, 412-employee platform.  
              Stated DSO of 52 days does not reconcile to the balance sheet.         Year-end accounts receivable of $16.9M implies ~71 days (or ~82 days including $2.7M of unbilled revenue), not 52.  
              The growth story is almost entirely PFAS, which is aggressively projected and capacity-constrained.         Traditional remediation (the largest sub-segment) is projected to grow only ~1–6% per year; PFAS is projected to more than double in FY2025 (+125.8%) despite the Company having lost one of three PEs in January 2025 and providing no contracted PFAS backlog breakout.  
          Headline figures (as presented vs. as normalized).  
                          Metric  
          CIM / Sell-side  
          Buyer-normalized (this memo)  
            FY2024 Revenue  
          $87.3M  
          $87.3M (unchanged)  
            FY2024 Reported EBITDA / margin  
          $12.1M / 13.9%  
          $12.1M / 13.9% (unchanged)  
            FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA / margin  
          $17.1M / 19.6%  
          ~$15.1–$15.9M / ~17.3–18.2%  
            Implied EV range (8–10x LTM Adj. EBITDA)  
          $136.8M–$171.0M  
          ~$121M–$159M  
            Recommended IOI EV range  
          —  
              ~$135M–$150M  
            Net debt (12/31/24)  
          $15.0M (CIM)  
          $15.0M (coincidentally equal; gross components differ)  
             Recommended IOI posture.         Submit a $135–$150M enterprise value indication (cash-free, debt-free, normalized NWC), framed on a buyer-normalized LTM Adjusted EBITDA of ~$15.5M with explicit upside to FY2025E contingent on verification of contracted PFAS backlog. Bridge the PFAS projection gap with a structured earnout tied to FY2025–FY2026 PFAS revenue/EBITDA. Make the IOI conditional on (i) QoE, (ii) confirmation of Pacific Northwest Refining Co. (    “    PNR    ”    ) contract renewal, (iii) a contracted PFAS backlog breakout, (iv) a fleet condition assessment, and (v) a 10-year regulatory enforcement history. Address the headquarters real estate (owned by the founder), the cash settlement of phantom units, and key-manager retention explicitly in the structure section of the IOI.  
      
        2. Transaction and Process Overview  
          Seller and advisor.         Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC (Philip Tran, Managing Director) is running a competitive sale of 100% of the equity of CES on behalf of founder/CEO Randall Oakes (68% equity holder) and the remaining 32% (early investors and key employees; no institutional investors). Company counsel is Wexford, Mallory & Chen LLP; auditor is Ridgeline Accounting Group, PC. (CIM, Sections I, II.B; Process Letter, Sections I, VI.)  
          Structure.         Sale of 100% equity, expected to be structured as a stock purchase agreement. Bids are requested on a cash-free, debt-free basis with a normalized level of net working capital. Existing indebtedness with Cascadia Commercial Bank (senior term loan + revolver) is expected to be refinanced or repaid at closing. The Process Letter specifically asks bidders to address (a) the headquarters real estate owned by Mr. Oakes and leased to the Company, (b) any rollover equity or earnout, and (c) Mr. Oakes’s desired 18–24 month transition period. (Process Letter, Sections I, III.)  
          Timeline.         CIM distributed June 9, 2025; IOIs due 5:00 PM Pacific, July 14, 2025; management presentations targeted August 2025 (Portland HQ, with optional Boise/Seattle site visits); final bids and definitive-document markups in Phase III; target signing/closing Q4 2025. (CIM, Section X.A; Process Letter, Section II.)  
          Deal-structure items the IOI must address (and our preliminary views).  
              Real estate.         The Portland HQ/operations yard (8.5 acres, purpose-built) is owned personally by Mr. Oakes and leased to CES at $1.4M/year through 2029 — roughly 2.3x the $0.6M market estimate. We should propose either (i) an option to purchase the property at independently appraised fair market value at/close to closing, or (ii) a new market-rate lease with Mr. Oakes. Either path removes the above-market rent but creates a continuing related-party dependency if leased.  
              Phantom equity.         Outstanding phantom units are expected to be settled in             cash at closing         (CIM, Section II.B; VI.C). The CIM discloses only the $0.3M annual non-cash expense, not the total accrued liability. This is a debt-like closing cost that must be quantified and deducted from equity proceeds; it is             not         captured by the EBITDA add-back.  
              Debt.         Refinance/repay Cascadia facilities at closing (see Section 4.4 for the debt-balance discrepancy).  
              Transition.         Accommodate Mr. Oakes’s 18–24 month advisory transition, but pair it with a professional CEO search and retention agreements for the operating leadership (see Section 8).  
      
        3. Company Overview  
          Profile.         Delaware C-corporation, founded 2009, headquartered at 4200 Industrial Way, Portland, OR 97218. 412 full-time employees across three facilities (Portland HQ/yard, Seattle office, Boise depot). Four-state service territory (Oregon, Washington, Idaho, Northern California) with hazardous waste transporter permits in all four states and Oregon DEQ Contractor License ENV-2009-04821. (CIM, Sections I, II.A, II.C; Appendix D.)  
          Segments (FY2024).  
                           Segment  
          FY2024 Revenue  
          % of Total  
          Key services  
            Environmental Remediation  
          $48.2M  
          55.2%  
          Soil/groundwater remediation, Phase I/II ESAs, UST removal, brownfield, PFAS investigation & treatment  
            Industrial Cleaning  
          $27.8M  
          31.8%  
          Tank cleaning, hydroblasting (to 40,000 PSI), vacuum trucks, confined space  
            Emergency Response  
          $11.3M  
          12.9%  
          24/7 hazmat spill response, transportation/marine/industrial  
                Total  
              $87.3M  
              100.0%  
       
         (CIM, Section I; Section III; Section VI.B.)  
          Ownership and management.         Randall Oakes (age 61, founder, CEO/Chairman, 68% equity) oversees         “    all strategic, financial, and operational decisions    ”         and intends to transition out of day-to-day operations within 18–24 months post-close. Operating leadership: Janet Prewitt (VP Operations, 18-year tenure, founding employee, manages the largest segment and key client relationships); Derek Cahill (VP Sales/BD, since 2014); Lisa Fontaine (Controller, since 2016); Mark Stovall (Safety Director, since 2012; EMR 0.87). (CIM, Sections II.B, VII.B.)  
          Competitive strengths (genuinely attractive).         Integrated three-service platform enabling cross-sell; 16-year operating history and deep state-agency relationships (OR DEQ, WA Ecology, ID DEQ); 24/7 emergency response across four states; early-mover PFAS capability; diversified end markets; hard permits/PE credentials that constitute real barriers to entry. (CIM, Section II.D; Sector Memo, Section 2.1.)  
      
        4. Financial Analysis  
       4.1 Historical performance (as reported)  
                           ($M)  
          FY2021  
          FY2022  
          FY2023  
          FY2024  
            Revenue  
          61.4  
          68.9  
          76.1  
          87.3  
            YoY growth  
          —  
          12.2%  
          10.4%  
          14.7%  
            Reported EBITDA  
          7.4  
          8.6  
          10.3  
          12.1  
            Reported EBITDA margin  
          12.1%  
          12.5%  
          13.5%  
          13.9%  
            Adjusted EBITDA (as stated)  
          9.7  
          11.1  
          13.4  
          17.1  
            Adjusted EBITDA margin (as stated)  
          15.8%  
          16.1%  
          17.6%  
          19.6%  
         Revenue CAGR FY2021–FY2024 = 12.5% (verified: (87.3/61.4)^(1/3)−1 = 12.45%). Reported EBITDA margin expansion from 12.1% to 13.9% is credible and within the sector range (Sector Memo, Section 4.1: 10–15% reported is normal). The Adjusted EBITDA figures are where scrutiny is required (Sections 4.3 and 9). (CIM, Sections VI.A, VI.C; Appendix B.)  
        4.2 Segment analysis — the growth story is almost entirely PFAS  
      The Exhibits disaggregate remediation into PFAS and         “    traditional    ”         remediation. Traditional remediation — the Company’s largest sub-segment — is projected to grow only modestly, while PFAS is expected to carry the platform.  
                           ($M)  
          FY2024A  
          FY2025E  
          FY2026E  
          FY2027E  
            Environmental Remediation (total)  
          48.2  
          56.5  
          66.0  
          73.5  
            — of which PFAS  
          6.2  
          14.0  
          22.0  
          27.0  
            — of which Traditional remediation  
          42.0  
          42.5  
          44.0  
          46.5  
            Traditional remediation YoY growth  
          —  
          1.2%  
          3.5%  
          5.7%  
            Industrial Cleaning  
          27.8  
          30.2  
          33.0  
          35.5  
            Emergency Response  
          11.3  
          11.8  
          13.0  
          15.0  
                Total revenue  
              87.3  
              98.5  
              112.0  
              124.0  
         (Exhibits,         “    Revenue by Segment    ”         sheet.)  
          Implication.         PFAS drives ~64% of incremental revenue from FY2024 to FY2026E ($15.8M of $24.7M — verified). Traditional remediation, at ~48% of current revenue ex-PFAS, is effectively a low-single-digit grower. Industrial cleaning and emergency response grow mid-single-digits.             The entire equity story above sector-average growth rests on a two-year-old service line projected to grow 125.8% in FY2025 and 57.1% in FY2026         — see Section 7.  
          Note on segment-history inconsistency.         The CIM (Section VI.B) and the Exhibits disagree on the FY2021–FY2023 segment split (e.g., FY2023 remediation: CIM $41.5M vs. Exhibits $42.5M; FY2021 industrial cleaning: CIM $20.1M vs. Exhibits $19.2M), even though both foot to the same annual totals and agree on FY2024. The historical segment composition is not pinned down and should be reconciled in QoE.  
        4.3 The FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA bridge does not foot — and several add-backs are weak  
          The arithmetic error.         The CIM (Section VI.C) and the Exhibits (    “    EBITDA Bridge    ”         sheet) both present the following FY2024 bridge:  
                        Item  
          Amount ($M)  
            Reported EBITDA  
          12.1  
            (+) Owner compensation normalization  
          1.9  
            (+) Related-party lease normalization  
          0.8  
            (+) One-time legal/regulatory costs (DEQ)  
          1.2  
            (+) Non-recurring equipment relocation (Boise)  
          0.4  
            (+) Stock-based compensation (phantom units)  
          0.3  
            (+) One-time IT system implementation  
          0.1  
                Sum of listed adjustments  
              4.7  
                Adjusted EBITDA (computed)  
              16.8  
                Adjusted EBITDA (stated by CIM/Exhibits)  
              17.1  
                Non-footing gap  
              0.3 (overstated)  
         The six listed adjustments total $4.7M; $12.1M + $4.7M =             $16.8M, not $17.1M    . The Exhibits even print         “    Total Adjustments 4,700    ”         directly above         “    Adjusted EBITDA 17,100,    ”         making the non-footing self-evident. Notably, the FY2021–FY2023 reconciliations in Appendix B             do         foot (9.7, 11.1, 13.4); only FY2024 is broken. The $0.3M gap would close only if the owner-compensation add-back were $2.2M rather than the stated $1.9M — i.e., if the $500K replacement-CEO assumption were not actually netted — which would itself contradict the normalization methodology described.             The QoE must establish whether Adjusted EBITDA is $16.8M (per the listed adjustments) or $17.1M (per the stated total), and why.         At 8–10x, the $0.3M equals $2.4M–$3.0M of enterprise value.  
          Substantive add-back concerns (detailed in Section 9).         Beyond the arithmetic error:  
              DEQ legal/regulatory ($1.2M) is likely substantially overstated.         The Exhibits income statement shows         “    Other Income / (Expense)    ”         of $(0.875)M in FY2024 — exactly the DEQ settlement amount — classified             below         EBITDA. If correct, the $875K settlement was never in EBITDA and should not be added back; only the $325K of legal fees (above the line) is a candidate add-back, and even that is subject to a recurring regulatory run-rate (CES recorded $0.2M of         “    one-time    ”         legal/regulatory costs in FY2023 as well — Appendix B — confirming these costs recur).  
              Owner compensation ($1.9M) assumes a $500K replacement CEO.         A professional CEO for an $87M+ revenue, 412-employee, four-state environmental services platform will likely cost meaningfully more than $500K all-in; a $750K–$1.0M assumption is more defensible, reducing the add-back by $0.25M–$0.5M.  
              Related-party lease ($0.8M)         is defensible             only if         the buyer can achieve market rent (via renegotiation or property purchase); it creates a continuing related-party dependency if leased from Mr. Oakes.  
              Phantom-unit SBC ($0.3M)         is a standard non-cash add-back, but the             cash settlement of phantom units at closing         is a separate, unquantified debt-like item not captured here.  
        4.4 The CIM balance sheet and the Exhibits balance sheet do not reconcile

              Fund III fit — flag for the deal lead.         The Sector Memo (Section 1) states Fund III         “    targets platforms in the $30M–$100M enterprise value range    ”         and asserts CES         “    fits squarely within these parameters.    ”         The CIM’s implied EV of $136.8M–$171.0M (LTM) / $157.6M–$197.0M (forward) is             materially above a literal $100M EV ceiling    , and even our disciplined $135–$150M range implies an equity check of ~23–30% of the fund — at or above typical single-deal concentration limits.             The deal team should confirm Fund III’s actual platform-size mandate (equity check vs. enterprise value) and single-investment concentration limits before the IC endorses an IOI    , and should consider whether a co-invest or LP advisory committee waiver is required. (This appears to be an inconsistency in the Sector Memo’s own framing that the IC should resolve.)  
        5.5 PFAS downside sensitivity  
      If PFAS reaches ~$9–10M in FY2025 (the Sector Memo’s realistic range, Section 3.2) rather than the projected $14.0M, FY2025E revenue falls by ~$4–5M and, at the above-average margins ascribed to PFAS, FY2025E Adjusted EBITDA falls by roughly $0.8–$1.0M (to ~$18.7M vs. $19.7M). The EBITDA impact is modest in absolute terms, but the             growth narrative and the margin-expansion story (19.6% → 21.5% by FY2027E) weaken materially    , because PFAS is presented as the higher-margin mix-shift engine. This argues for an earnout (Section 10) rather than paying upfront for the full projection.  
      
         6. Customer and Backlog Analysis  
       6.1 Concentration  
      Top 5 = $45.8M (52.5%); Top 10 = $62.0M (71.0%); all other = $25.3M (29.0%). Concentration has been declining modestly (Top 10: 74.3% in FY2021 → 71.0% in FY2024), a favorable trend. (CIM, Section V.B; Customer Report, Section 3.3.) The Sector Memo (Section 2.2) views this concentration as consistent with sector norms but a risk factor requiring diligence into contract terms and replacement capacity.  
        6.2 PNR contract expires before close — critical  
          PNR is 21.4% of FY2024 revenue ($18.7M) and its master service agreement expires March 31, 2025 — approximately six to nine months before the expected Q4 2025 closing.         (CIM, Section V.B Top 10 table; Customer Report, Section 3.2.) The CIM and Customer Report describe PNR as deeply embedded (on-site personnel,         “    preferred provider    ”         status, relationship since 2011/2012), but the             formal MSA will have lapsed by closing    . Meridian Lumber & Paper (6.6%, $5.8M) similarly has an MSA expiring December 31, 2025.             Renewal status of the PNR MSA (and Meridian) is a gating diligence item and should be a condition to a binding bid.         The Customer Report’s claim that CES         “    has not lost a top 10 customer in the past five years    ”         (Section 7.2) is reassuring but does not substitute for confirmation that the largest contract has been renewed on comparable terms.

      Contracted backlog $42.6M (Remediation 62.9%, Industrial Cleaning 26.8%, Emergency Response 10.3%); proposal pipeline $31.4M; total visibility $74.0M. Backlog conversion: 77.5% ($33.0M) expected within FY2025 (H1 $18.2M, H2 $14.8M), 22.5% ($9.6M) in 2026+. Pipeline win rate 35–40% implies $11.0M–$12.6M of incremental contracted work. (Customer Report, Sections 5, 6.) These figures foot and are reasonable in aggregate.  
          Critical data gap — PFAS backlog.         Neither the CIM nor the Customer Report breaks out             contracted PFAS backlog         versus pipeline. The $14.0M FY2025E PFAS projection (more than double FY2024’s $6.2M) is the single most important growth assumption, and it is not tied to disclosed signed contracts. The Sector Memo (Section 3.2) identifies this as the top diligence question.             We must obtain a PFAS backlog breakout (signed vs. pipeline vs. assumption) before crediting the FY2025E trajectory in our model.  
      
         7. PFAS Growth Assessment  
          The projection is aggressive relative to achievable company-level growth.         PFAS revenue: FY2022 $1.1M → FY2023 $3.4M (+209.1%) → FY2024 $6.2M (+82.4%) → FY2025E $14.0M (+125.8%) → FY2026E $22.0M (+57.1%) → FY2027E $27.0M (+22.7%). The early trajectory is encouraging but from a small base. The Sector Memo (Section 3.2) benchmarks             company-level         PFAS growth at 30–50% annually once a practice exceeds ~$5M, based on Thornfield’s direct GreenWorks experience (vapor intrusion mitigation projected at 80%+ in years two/three; actual ~35% as pent-up demand was worked through and competition intensified).             The CIM’s 125.8% FY2025E growth is roughly three times the achievable company-level rate.         Sector-wide PFAS market growth of 25–35% should not be conflated with any single company’s capture rate.  
          Capacity constraint — PE headcount.         CES’s licensed PE headcount declined from three to two in January 2025 (one retired, not yet replaced) (CIM, Section II.D, item 6). PFAS site assessments and remediation designs require PE oversight. Growth at the projected pace would likely require hiring two to three additional PEs, and the environmental PE market is tight (6–12 month recruitment timelines per the Sector Memo, Section 3.2).             The loss of one-third of PE capacity is a near-term execution bottleneck that directly threatens the PFAS projection.         Management’s PE hiring plan must be vetted.  
          Regulatory tailwind is real but timing-dependent.         The EPA’s April 2024 national drinking water MCLs for six PFAS compounds and Oregon/Washington state initiatives are genuine demand drivers (CIM, Section IV.B; Sector Memo, Section 3.1). However, funded remediation work (vs. investigation/assessment) lags regulatory mandates, and larger national firms are entering the PFAS market with greater resources. The tailwind supports a             directional         PFAS thesis but not the specific FY2025E magnitude.  
          Net.         We credit PFAS as a real, attractive growth vector but model it at a moderated trajectory (~30–50% company-level growth) and bridge the gap to the seller’s projection with an earnout rather than paying for it upfront.  
      
        8. Key Risks and Diligence Priorities  
      The following are prioritized by severity (impact on value and/or deal certainty). Each is cited to source, explained, and paired with a recommendation.  
          1. PNR contract renewal (Severity: High).         PNR is 21.4% of revenue ($18.7M) and its MSA expires March 31, 2025, before the Q4 2025 close (CIM Section V.B; Customer Report Section 3.2). Loss or material repricing of PNR would be value-destructive.             Recommendation:         Confirm renewal status and terms as a gating diligence item and a condition to a binding bid; obtain the current MSA and any renewal correspondence in the VDR; model a downside case with PNR revenue at risk.  
          2. Adjusted EBITDA quality and the non-footing bridge (Severity: High).         The $17.1M does not foot to the listed adjustments ($16.8M), and the DEQ, owner-comp, and lease add-backs are overstated or conditional (Sections 4.3, 9). The buyer-normalized figure is ~$15.1–$15.9M.             Recommendation:         Engage an independent QoE provider; re-derive every add-back; recompute Adjusted EBITDA and the implied multiple; base the IOI on the normalized figure.  
          3. PFAS projection aggressiveness and capacity (Severity: High).         FY2025E PFAS growth of 125.8% is ~3x the achievable company-level rate; PE capacity just dropped 33%; no contracted PFAS backlog is disclosed (Sections 6.5, 7).             Recommendation:         Obtain PFAS backlog breakout (signed vs. pipeline); vet the PE hiring plan; model PFAS at 30–50% growth; use an earnout to bridge to the seller’s projection.  
          4. Maintenance capex understatement / fleet condition (Severity: High).         Stated 3.4% of revenue vs. 5–7% sector benchmark; declining capex % as fleet ages; GreenWorks precedent of deferred maintenance surfacing post-close (Sections 4.7; Sector Memo 4.2). A $2.5M/yr FCF haircut is plausible.             Recommendation:         Commission an independent fleet age/condition assessment; re-underwrite FCF at ~5.5–6.0% maintenance capex; reflect in the return model and purchase price.  
          5. Balance-sheet and income-statement non-reconciliation (Severity: High).         CIM vs. Exhibits differ by $3.9M assets, $1.7M equity, $1.8M cash, $1.8M debt; Exhibits internally inconsistent on EBIT/EBITDA; CIM omits the $875K settlement from its income statement (Sections 4.4, 4.5).             Recommendation:         QoE to establish a single, audited, reconciled set of financials; confirm cash, debt, and the DEQ settlement classification before closing.  
          6. DSO / working-capital integrity (Severity: Medium-High).         Stated 52 days vs. ~71–82 days balance-sheet-implied (Section 4.6).             Recommendation:         Independently compute DSO and set the NWC peg on verified, balance-sheet-consistent figures; investigate unbilled-revenue recognition.  
          7. Key-person and transition risk (Severity: Medium-High).         Founder-CEO (61, 68% owner, runs         “    all strategic, financial, and operational decisions    ”    ) exits in 18–24 months; VP Operations Janet Prewitt (18-year tenure, key client relationships) is a single point of failure; GreenWorks lost its lead PM within six months of close (Sector Memo 6.1).             Recommendation:         Retention agreements for Prewitt, Cahill, Fontaine, and Stovall as a closing condition; structured Oakes transition/consulting agreement; begin CEO search in parallel with diligence.  
          8. Regulatory/enforcement history and the DEQ matter (Severity: Medium-High).         The August 2024 DEQ enforcement action (improper storage of contaminated soils at the Portland facility) and the FY2023 $0.2M legal/regulatory cost indicate compliance costs recur (Sections 4.3, 9; Sector Memo 5).             Recommendation:         Obtain a 10-year regulatory enforcement history; establish an ongoing regulatory run-rate ($200K–$500K/yr) and add back only excess; environmental compliance diligence on the Portland facility.  
          9. Related-party real estate and phantom units (Severity: Medium).         HQ owned by the seller at 2.3x market rent; phantom units settle in cash at closing (unquantified) (Sections 2, 9).             Recommendation:         Independent appraisal of the HQ; propose purchase option or market-rate lease; quantify and treat the phantom liability as debt-like.  
          10. Fund III fit / concentration (Severity: Medium).         Implied EV and equity check may exceed Fund III’s stated platform size and single-deal concentration limits (Section 5.4).             Recommendation:         Deal lead to confirm mandate and concentration limits; evaluate co-invest if required.  
          11. Cyclical end-market exposure (Severity: Medium).         ~42%+ of revenue from cyclical refining/manufacturing/forest-products/metals (Section 6.4).             Recommendation:         Stress-test revenue and margins in a downturn scenario.  
          12. Customer-data integrity (Severity: Low-Medium).         Conflicting customer #6–#10 identities and relationship-since years across documents (Section 6.3).             Recommendation:         Obtain a reconciled customer master file; verify retention/renewal claims independently.

          Defensible only if market rent is achieved via renegotiation or property purchase. Independent appraisal required. If the buyer purchases the HQ, there is no lease expense but there is a capital outlay and ownership cost (taxes, maintenance, building capex) not captured by the add-back.  
            One-time legal/regulatory (DEQ)  
          $1.2M  
          $0–$0.3M  
          The $875K settlement appears below the EBITDA line in the Exhibits (Other Income/Expense) and so was likely never in EBITDA — adding it back is inappropriate. Only the $325K legal fees (above the line) is a candidate, and it should be normalized against a $200K–$500K recurring regulatory run-rate (CES recorded $0.2M of         “    one-time    ”         legal/regulatory costs in FY2023 too). Defensible add-back ≈ $0–$0.3M, not $1.2M.  
            Non-recurring Boise relocation  
          $0.4M  
          $0.4M  
          Plausibly one-time; confirm it does not recur with further depot expansion.  
            Stock-based comp (phantom units)  
          $0.3M  
          $0.3M (EBITDA)  
          Standard non-cash add-back for EBITDA.             However, the cash settlement of outstanding phantom units at closing is a separate debt-like item         that must be quantified and deducted from equity proceeds — not captured here.  
            One-time IT implementation  
          $0.1M  
          $0.1M  
          Small; confirm one-time.  
                Arithmetic correction  
          —  
          −$0.3M  
          The CIM’s listed adjustments total $4.7M, yielding $16.8M, not $17.1M.  
                Total adjustments  
              $5.0M (implied)  
              ~$3.0–$3.8M  
       
                Adjusted EBITDA  
              $17.1M (stated) / $16.8M (as listed)  
              ~$15.1–$15.9M  
       
             Debt-like / closing items to quantify (not EBITDA adjustments):         (i) total accrued phantom-unit liability (cash at close); (ii) the debt balance to be refinanced (resolve the $17.3M vs. $19.1M discrepancy); (iii) any deferred maintenance/capex liabilities; (iv) the NWC peg (set on verified figures).             NWC peg:         target ~$11.8M (13.5% of revenue) on a verified, balance-sheet-consistent basis, with seasonal analysis.  
      
        10. Recommendation and IOI Strategy  
          Recommendation: Proceed.         CES is an on-strategy, genuinely attractive environmental services platform with real regulatory tailwinds and consolidation optionality. The issues identified are diligence and valuation issues — not disqualifiers — and are precisely the kind of pattern (understated maintenance capex,         “    one-time    ”         recurring regulatory costs, aggressive emerging-service-line growth, owner-operator add-backs) that Thornfield’s portfolio experience predicts and that disciplined diligence can price.  
          Proposed IOI terms.  
              Enterprise value:         ~$135M–$150M, cash-free, debt-free, normalized NWC (~$11.8M). Stated as ~7.9x–8.8x CIM Adjusted EBITDA / ~8.7x–9.7x buyer-normalized LTM Adjusted EBITDA, with explicit upside to FY2025E metrics contingent on verification of contracted PFAS backlog.  
              Structure:         Stock purchase. Refinance/repay Cascadia debt at closing. Treat phantom-unit cash settlement as a debt-like deduction. Address HQ real estate via purchase option at appraised FMV or a new market-rate lease.  
              Earnout:         Propose ~$10–$15M of contingent consideration tied to FY2025–FY2026 PFAS revenue/EBITDA milestones, to bridge the seller’s projection to a defensible base case and align incentives through the founder’s transition.  
              Management/transition:         Accommodate Mr. Oakes’s 18–24 month advisory transition; commit to retaining the existing operating team; make retention agreements for Prewitt, Cahill, Fontaine, and Stovall a closing condition; initiate a parallel CEO search.  
              Financing:         Indicate committed equity from Fund III (subject to IC approval) and anticipated leverage of ~2.0x–3.0x EBITDA; name prospective lenders subject to confirmatory diligence. Flag the Fund III concentration question for IC sign-off.  
              Conditions:         IOI non-binding and subject to (i) QoE, (ii) confirmatory legal/environmental diligence including a 10-year regulatory history, (iii) confirmation of PNR (and Meridian) contract renewal, (iv) a contracted PFAS backlog breakout, (v) a fleet condition assessment, (vi) IC and any required LPAC approval, and (vii) execution of a definitive purchase agreement.  
          Next steps (to hit the June 25 internal target and the July 14 IOI deadline).  
          Circulate this memo to the deal team and schedule an IC pre-read (target: this week).  
          Engage QoE provider and buyer’s counsel (Calloway, Barrett & Sims LLP per Sector Memo, Section 8) on a parallel track.  
          Submit the VDR access request and a first-round diligence demand list (Appendix E) to Greenleaf immediately upon IC go-ahead.  
          Prepare the IOI letter for IC approval no later than July 10, 2025.

        Appendix E — First-Round Diligence Request List  
          Financial / QoE.         (1) Reconciled, audited FY2021–FY2024 financial statements (resolve CIM vs. Exhibits balance-sheet and income-statement differences); (2) monthly P&L and trial balances; (3) detailed Adjusted EBITDA bridge with support for each add-back and the $0.3M non-footing; (4) AR aging and DSO computation methodology; (5) unbilled-revenue recognition policy; (6) capex detail and fleet register (age, condition, replacement cost, maintenance history); (7) debt schedules and phantom-unit liability reconciliation; (8) NWC monthly trend and seasonality.  
          Commercial.         (9) Reconciled customer master file (resolve #6–#10 identity and relationship-since discrepancies); (10) PNR and Meridian MSA renewal status and correspondence; (11)             contracted PFAS backlog breakout (signed vs. pipeline vs. assumption)    ; (12) top-customer contract files; (13) win/loss records on the $31.4M pipeline.  
          Regulatory / Environmental.         (14) 10-year regulatory enforcement history (all jurisdictions); (15) DEQ consent agreement and remediation status for the Portland facility; (16) all permits/licenses and renewal calendar; (17) insurance loss runs and EMR history.  
          Legal / Real Estate / HR.         (18) HQ lease and property documentation (for appraisal/purchase analysis); (19) phantom-unit plan documents and accrued liability; (20) organizational chart, key-employee agreements, and retention-risk assessment; (21) PE staffing plan; (22) any litigation, claims, or environmental liabilities.  
      
          Prepared by David Koh, Associate, for internal use by Thornfield Capital Partners. This memorandum is based solely on the sell-side materials listed above, which have not been independently verified. All figures should be confirmed through confirmatory due diligence and an independent quality-of-earnings review. Do not distribute outside Thornfield Capital Partners.

                            2,640,000.00  
                            
                              Earnest money on deposit — principal (PSA §3.2)  
                            195,000.00

                            TBD

                            2,012.93

                                CASH REQUIRED FROM BUYER AT CLOSING  (Total debits − Total credits)

                  E.  RECONCILIATION SUMMARY  
             Funding sources must equal funding uses. Internal items (purchase price, heating oil, and all prorations) appear on both parties' statements at offsetting amounts and cancel out of the overall reconciliation, leaving only true third-party disbursements.  
             E.1  Sources of Funds (Cash In)

             E.2  Uses of Funds (Cash Out)

           Reconciliation check:  Total sources $3,920,412.12  =  Total uses $3,920,412.12  —  BALANCED (variance $0.00).  
               F.  PAID OUTSIDE OF CLOSING (POC) — DISCLOSURE ONLY  
             The following item was paid by Buyer prior to Closing and is disclosed here for transparency only. Per PSA §3.3 and the Lender's closing instructions, it is NOT included in any computation of Buyer's cash required at Closing and carries a $0.00 impact on the settlement statement.  
                                                POC Item

                  G.  DISBURSEMENT SUMMARY (WIRES / CHECKS AT CLOSING)

                Note: The CT DRS estate tax lien release (Cert. ETL-2025-08834) requires no disbursement ($0.00); only its $73.00 recording fee is paid (included in Seller recording fees). All payoffs are good through 7/20/2025; closing on 7/15/2025 requires no per-diem adjustment.  
               H.  POST-CLOSING PRORATION RECONCILIATION (PSA §7.7)  
             The following items are estimated at Closing and shall be readjusted between the parties within ninety (90) days after Closing when final figures become available, with any balance due paid within ten (10) business days of demand (PSA §7.7). These obligations survive Closing.  
           Next fiscal year (2025–2026) property taxes — reprorate when the actual tax bill is issued (estimate used: $52,480.00).  
           Gas and electric utility charges — no final bills/readings provided; reprorate if any straddle the Closing Date.  
           Accrued interest on residential security deposits (CT §47a-21) and on earnest money — to be confirmed and credited to Buyer.  
           Any delinquent rents (e.g., Unit 2E) collected by Buyer after Closing — applied first to current rent, excess remitted to Seller within 30 days (PSA §7.3(b)).  
               ACKNOWLEDGMENT AND APPROVAL OF SETTLEMENT STATEMENT  
             The parties acknowledge that they have reviewed this Combined Settlement Statement and agree that the credits, debits, prorations, and adjustments set forth herein accurately reflect the terms of the Purchase and Sale Agreement dated April 22, 2025, subject to the post-closing reproration items identified in Section H and the discrepancies/assumptions noted in the accompanying Settlement Statement Notes Memo.  
                                    SELLER:  
          Estate of Gerald T. Whitford  
          
          By: ____________________________  
          Claudia Whitford-Barnes, Executrix  
          Date: ____________________  
                  BUYER:  
          Meridian Cove Properties LLC  
          
          By: ____________________________  
          Elaine R. Matsuda, Manager  
          Date: ____________________  
                              
                            
                    CLOSING / ESCROW AGENT:  
          Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC  
          
          By: ____________________________  
          Lorraine M. Grasso, Closing Officer  
          Date: ____________________

          Tidewater Savings Bank  
          
          By: ____________________________  
          Marcus J. Pellegrino, Loan Officer  
          Date: ____________________  
                              
                            
                
           — End of Combined Settlement Statement —  
           Prepared by Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC  |  This statement is subject to the discrepancies and assumptions detailed in the accompanying Settlement Statement Notes Memo.  
        

### FILE: settlement-statement-notes.docx
 
              SETTLEMENT STATEMENT — NOTES MEMO  
           Discrepancies, Assumptions, and Data Gaps  
                                               TO:  
                            Lorraine M. Grasso, Closing Officer — Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC; Nora F. Kapadia, Esq. (Buyer's Counsel); David H. Clement, Esq. (Seller's Counsel); Marcus J. Pellegrino, Tidewater Savings Bank  
                               FROM:  
                            Closing File — Settlement Statement Preparation  
                               RE:  
                            Closing of 4280 Harborview Boulevard, Bridgeport, CT 06604 — Estate of Gerald T. Whitford (Seller) v. Meridian Cove Properties LLC (Buyer)  
                               DATE:  
                            July 14, 2025 (prepared in advance of the July 15, 2025 closing)  
                               RE:  
                            Companion to the Combined Settlement Statement — please read both together  
                
               1.  PURPOSE AND SCOPE  
             This memo accompanies the Combined Settlement Statement for the above-referenced commercial property closing. It documents (a) the methodology and assumptions used to compute the prorations and adjustments, (b) discrepancies identified among the closing documents, (c) data gaps where required amounts were not quantified in the file, and (d) items that survive Closing for post-closing reproration under Section 7.7 of the Purchase and Sale Agreement ("PSA"). All figures on the settlement statement were computed (not estimated) and the statement reconciles to a $0.00 variance. Items marked "TBD" on the statement are explained below.  
               2.  HEADLINE FIGURES (AS PREPARED)  
                                                Item  
                              Amount  
                              Purchase Price  
                            $3,900,000.00  
                              Mortgage loan proceeds (Tidewater Savings Bank)  
                            $2,640,000.00  
                              Earnest money on deposit (principal)  
                            $195,000.00  
                               Net proceeds to Seller  
                             $2,864,663.83  
                               Cash required from Buyer at Closing  
                             $1,085,412.12  
                              Total lien payoffs (excl. $0 DRS estate tax lien)  
                            $866,631.89  
                              Total paid to City of Bridgeport (taxes + water/sewer)  
                            $30,450.40  
                              Repair escrow funded (roof — held by Escrow Agent)  
                            $45,000.00  
                               Reconciliation (sources − uses)  
                             $0.00  (balanced)  
                  3.  PRORATION METHODOLOGY AND KEY ASSUMPTIONS  
             All prorations are made as of 12:01 a.m. on July 15, 2025, with the Closing Date allocated to Buyer, using a 365-day year on a per-diem basis unless otherwise specified (PSA Art. 7). Specific conventions applied:  
            Property taxes (next FY):         Computed as $52,480.00 ÷ 365 = $143.7808/day. Seller's 14-day share (7/1–7/14) = $2,012.93 (debit Seller / credit Buyer); Buyer's 351-day share = $50,467.07. The estimate uses the current-year levy because the FY2025–26 mill rate has not been set (Tax Cert. §3).  
            Rents:         Collected July 2025 rents prorated over 31 days (Seller 14 days, Buyer 17 days), computed separately per lease with fractional cents rounded to the nearest cent and any remainder allocated to Buyer (PSA §7.2(c)–(d)). Total collected = $23,600.00; Buyer's share = $12,941.95.  
            Uncollected rents:         Per PSA §7.3, delinquent/uncollected rents receive no credit to either party. Unit 2E ($1,600.00, delinquent) and vacant Unit 3C ($0.00) are excluded from the rent proration entirely.  
            Security deposits:         $32,400.00 principal (11 residential @ $18,000 + commercial @ $14,400) transferred as a debit to Seller / credit to Buyer (PSA §7.4). Accrued interest on residential deposits is not quantified in the file (see §5.2).  
            Utilities:         The WPCA billing period (5/15–7/14/2025) ends before the Closing Date, so the full $1,847.60 is Seller's responsibility (PSA §7.5(a)). It is paid to the City of Bridgeport at Closing from Seller's proceeds (see discrepancy §4.3).  
            Heating oil:         180 gallons × $3.85/gal (Seller's last delivered price, Shoreline Fuel & Oil Co., 6/2/2025) = $693.00, per the 7/14/2025 pre-closing inspection (PSA §7.6). Credit Seller / debit Buyer.  
            Repair escrow:         $45,000.00 is funded from Seller's proceeds and held by the Escrow Agent in a segregated interest-bearing account. Per PSA §8.4(c), it is NOT a credit to Buyer and is NOT part of the Purchase Price; it appears only as a deduction from Seller's net proceeds.  
            Conveyance tax:         Estimated at $44,750.00 total per the title commitment Schedule C, split 50/50 ($22,375.00 each) per PSA Art. 12 (see discrepancy §4.2).  
               4.  DISCREPANCIES IDENTIFIED IN THE CLOSING FILE  
             The following discrepancies were identified by comparing the PSA, title commitment, lender commitment, payoff letters, municipal tax certificate, and related documents. None prevents closing, but each should be resolved or acknowledged on the record at or before Closing.  
            4.1.            Delinquent property-tax interest — accrual period appears mislabeled.  
            The Bridgeport Tax Collector's municipal tax status certificate (No. TLC-2025-04892, §2) states interest of $2,362.80 "accrued through July 15, 2025," computed at 1.5% per month under CT Gen. Stat. §12-146 from the January 2, 2025 delinquency date, with a per diem of $13.12/day. However, $2,362.80 ÷ $13.12/day = 180 days, which from January 2, 2025 lands on approximately July 1, 2025 — not July 15. A true accrual through July 15 (194 days) would be approximately $2,545.28, and a strict "1.5% per month or fraction thereof" reading (7 fractions: Jan–Jul) would yield $2,755.20 (10.5% × $26,240.00). The statement uses the certificate's stated $2,362.80 (it is the operative municipal payoff figure and the certificate is valid through July 20, 2025), but the Closing Agent should confirm the exact interest with the Tax Collector at disbursement, because interest continues to accrue at $13.12/day and the certificate is only valid through July 20, 2025. Impact if understated: up to ~$392.40 of additional Seller-side interest.  
            4.2.            Connecticut conveyance tax — estimate may be understated by ~$100.  
            The title commitment Schedule C estimates total conveyance tax at $44,750.00 (split $22,375.00 each). Applying the standard published Connecticut rates for this non-residential, non-owner-occupied mixed-use property in Bridgeport (a "specified municipality") — 1.00% state rate (CGS §12-494(a), the 0.75% base plus 0.25% additional for non-residential property) plus 0.15% municipal rate (CGS §12-494(b)) = 1.15% — produces $3,900,000.00 × 1.15% = $44,850.00, a $100.00 variance from the title commitment estimate. The statement uses the title commitment's $44,750.00 figure (the documented estimate; PSA Art. 12 delegates final determination to the Closing Agent). The Closing Agent should confirm the exact amount on Form OP-236 at recording; if $44,850.00 is correct, each party owes an additional $50.00.  
            4.3.            Water/sewer — tension between PSA §7.5(a) and the municipal tax certificate.  
            PSA §7.5(a) directs that where a utility billing period ends on or before the Closing Date, "the full unpaid balance shall be credited to Buyer on the settlement statement." The WPCA billing period (5/15–7/14/2025) ends July 14, one day before Closing, so the full $1,847.60 is Seller's responsibility. However, the Bridgeport Tax Collector's certificate (§5–§6) requires this balance be "satisfied at or before closing" and provides wire instructions to the City, and the WPCA statement confirms the account will NOT transfer to Buyer (Buyer must establish a new account). Accordingly, the settlement statement pays the $1,847.60 directly to the City of Bridgeport from Seller's proceeds (grouped with the delinquent taxes as the $30,450.40 City payoff) rather than showing a credit to Buyer. This deviates from the literal "credit to Buyer" language of PSA §7.5(a). Net cost to Seller is identical under either treatment; the only difference is whether Buyer receives a $1,847.60 credit (and then pays the City) or the City is paid directly at Closing. Recommend the parties confirm this treatment; paying the City directly is recommended to clear the municipal lien at Closing and protect Buyer's title.  
            4.4.            Lien recording references conflict between the title commitment and the payoff letters.  
            The recording references (volume/page) for three liens differ between the title commitment (Schedule B-I) and the payoff letters/summary prepared by Seller's counsel. Because recordable releases/satisfactions must cite the correct instrument, the Closing Agent should reconcile these before recording:  
           First mortgage — title commitment Req. 4: Vol. 1087, Pg. 445; Harborstone payoff letter (Letter 1) and lien summary: Vol. 312, Pg. 88. Note that Vol. 312, Pg. 88 is the recording reference for the property deed/plat (Exhibit A / title commitment §A-4), not the mortgage — the payoff letter appears to have copied the property's legal-description reference.  
           HELOC — title commitment Req. 5: Vol. 1204, Pg. 218; Harborstone payoff letter (Letter 2) and lien summary: Vol. 487, Pg. 112.  
           Mechanic's lien — title commitment Req. 6: Vol. 1398, Pg. 77; Northbridge settlement letter (Letter 3) and lien summary: Vol. 1064, Pg. 517.  
             The title commitment references derive from the title search and are generally authoritative for preparing releases, but the discrepancy should be confirmed against the Bridgeport Land Records before the releases are recorded. This does not affect any dollar amount on the settlement statement.  
            4.5.            Title commitment number and date differ from the PSA citation.  
            PSA §5.1 cites the title commitment as "Commitment No. PCT-2025-08814," dated April 30, 2025. The operative title commitment in the file is No. TC-2025-07182, effective June 18, 2025 (title search effective June 16, 2025), issued by Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC. The June 18 commitment appears to supersede the April 30 commitment referenced in the PSA. The settlement statement and title charges reflect the June 18 commitment. Confirm that the June 18 commitment is the final operative commitment and that no requirements/exceptions were added that affect Closing.  
            4.6.            Title insurer name used inconsistently within the title commitment.  
            The title commitment header and countersignature block name the underwriter "Continental Fidelity Title Insurance Company," while the body (and the Conditions and Stipulations, §1) refer to "Continental Hartleigh Title Insurance Company." Confirm the correct legal name of the underwriting company so that the issued Owner's and Lender's policies name the correct insurer. No dollar impact.  
            4.7.            Lender commitment — loan-to-value ratio is conflated.  
            The Tidewater Savings Bank commitment letter (§2) states the LTV is "approximately 67.7%," then says $2,640,000.00 ÷ $3,920,000.00 (appraised value) = 67.35%, "rounded to approximately 67.7%." In fact, 67.35% rounds to 67.4%, and 67.7% is actually the loan-to-purchase-price ratio ($2,640,000.00 ÷ $3,900,000.00 = 67.69%). The letter conflates the appraised-value LTV (67.35%) with the purchase-price LTV (67.69%). This is informational only and does not affect the loan amount or any settlement figure, but the commitment should be corrected for accuracy. The appraisal ($3,920,000.00) exceeds the purchase price ($3,900,000.00), so the loan is supported either way.  
            4.8.            Closing-agent and counsel addresses appear in multiple inconsistent forms.  
            Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC's address appears as "88 Field Point Road, Greenwich, CT 06830" (PSA / title commitment), "3100 Main Street, Suite 204, Bridgeport" (lien-payoff cover letter), and "1200 Post Road East, Suite 214, Westport" (tax-certificate request). Buyer's counsel appears as "915 Chapel Street, Suite 400, New Haven" (PSA / lender commitment) and "55 Railroad Avenue, Suite 300, Greenwich" (lien-payoff cover letter). Given that all funds move by wire transfer, the parties should confirm the Closing Agent's correct address and wire instructions through a verified, independent channel to mitigate wire-fraud risk. No dollar impact.  
               5.  DATA GAPS — AMOUNTS NOT QUANTIFIED IN THE FILE (SHOWN AS "TBD")  
             The following required amounts are not stated in any closing document and are shown as "TBD" on the settlement statement. Each is a credit to Buyer (reducing Buyer's cash to close) and, where it derives from Seller-held funds, a corresponding debit to Seller. The statement reconciles with these at $0.00; if and when confirmed, they will reduce Buyer's cash required at Closing (and, for deposit interest, Seller's net proceeds).  
            Earnest money accrued interest:         PSA §3.2(d) requires that all interest earned on the $195,000.00 earnest money (held in an interest-bearing account by the Escrow Agent) be credited to Buyer at Closing. No interest amount or rate is stated in the file. To be confirmed by Pinnacle and added as a credit to Buyer.  
            Residential security-deposit interest:         PSA §7.4(a) and CT Gen. Stat. §47a-21 require accrued interest on the 11 residential security deposits ($18,000.00 principal, held by Bayshore Management Co. at Harborstone FCU) to be transferred/credited to Buyer. The rent-roll security-deposit schedule states "principal amounts only"; no interest figure is provided. The commercial deposit ($14,400.00) is non-interest-bearing per the management agreement §6.2, so no interest is due on it. To be confirmed and added as a debit to Seller / credit to Buyer.  
            Commercial NNN additional rent:         The commercial lease is triple-net (NNN): the tenant pays a pro-rata share of taxes, insurance, and CAM (Lease Summary, GF row). The rent roll shows only the $7,200.00 monthly base rent; any NNN additional rent collected for July 2025 is not separately stated. If NNN reimbursements were collected for July, they should be included in the rent proration under PSA §7.2(d). Confirm with Bayshore Management Co. whether any July NNN/CAM was collected.  
            Gas and electric utilities:         PSA §7.5 covers water, sewer, gas, electric, and other municipal services, but the only utility account provided is the Bridgeport WPCA water/sewer statement. No gas or electric account statements or final meter readings were delivered (PSA §7.5(c) requires final readings two business days before Closing). If any gas/electric billing period straddles the Closing Date, those charges are to be estimated at Closing and reprorated under PSA §7.7 within 90 days. (Note: the commercial space is served by a separate rooftop HVAC unit, and residential/common-area heat is oil-fired, so gas/electric exposure may be limited — but this must be confirmed.)  
               6.  ASSUMPTIONS MADE IN PREPARING THE STATEMENT  
           All payoff figures are used as stated in the respective payoff letters and are good through July 20, 2025; because Closing occurs July 15, 2025, no per-diem interest adjustment is applied to the Harborstone mortgage, Harborstone HELOC, or the municipal tax payoff.  
           The CT DRS estate tax lien requires $0.00 payoff (Release Cert. ETL-2025-08834); only its $73.00 recording fee is charged to Seller (included in Seller recording fees).  
           The FY2025–26 property tax is estimated at the current-year levy of $52,480.00 (Tax Cert. §3; PSA §7.1(b)); the actual bill will trigger reproration under PSA §7.7.  
           Collected July 2025 rents are taken from the Bayshore rent roll dated July 1, 2025; Seller represents it will deliver an updated rent roll three business days before Closing (PSA §9.7). If the updated rent roll changes collected amounts, the rent proration must be recomputed.  
           Security-deposit and earnest-money interest are assumed $0.00 pending confirmation (shown as TBD); the statement is balanced on that basis.  
           The appraisal fee of $4,500.00 is POC (paid 5/5/2025) and is excluded from Buyer's cash to close per PSA §3.3 and the lender's explicit closing instructions; it is disclosed in Section F only.  
           No brokerage commission is due (PSA §15.7 — each party represents no broker was involved).  
           The Repair Escrow is treated solely as a Seller-side holdback (PSA §8.4(c)) and is not netted against the Purchase Price or credited to Buyer.  
               7.  ITEMS SURVIVING CLOSING FOR POST-CLOSING REPRORATION (PSA §7.7)  
             The proration and adjustment obligations survive Closing for 90 days for purposes of reproration and final settlement (PSA §15.1). Items to be readjusted, with any balance paid within 10 business days of demand:  
           FY2025–26 property taxes — reprorate when the actual tax bill issues (estimate: $52,480.00).  
           Gas/electric utility charges — reprorate when final bills/readings are available.  
           Accrued interest on residential security deposits and on earnest money — confirm and credit to Buyer.  
           Delinquent rents (e.g., Unit 2E, $1,600.00) — if collected by Buyer after Closing, applied first to current rent; any excess remitted to Seller within 30 days (PSA §7.3(b)).  
               8.  OBSERVATIONS AND RISK NOTES (INFORMATIONAL)  
            Lease rollover risk:         Unit 3A's lease expires 7/31/2025 (16 days after Closing), and Units 2B, 2C, 2E, 3B, 3D, and 3F are on month-to-month holdover. Buyer assumes meaningful near-term lease-rollover and re-letting risk; the rent proration reflects only currently collected rents and does not guarantee future occupancy.  
            Roof / repair escrow adequacy:         The 7/14/2025 pre-closing inspection confirms the roof deficiencies (blistering, failed flashing, ponding, EPDM seam separation) remain unrepaired. The inspector recommends Buyer obtain competitive bids promptly after Closing to confirm the $45,000.00 escrow is sufficient; the escrow is held for 12 months, with any unused balance returned to Seller (PSA §8.4(d)–(e)).  
            Unit 2E delinquency:         Unit 2E is occupied but delinquent on July 2025 rent ($1,600.00); prior months are current. No credit is given at Closing (PSA §7.3). Buyer should evaluate collection/eviction strategy post-Closing; Seller may not commence or continue eviction/collection after Closing without Buyer's consent (PSA §7.3(c)).  
            Lender conditions:         The lender requires an SNDA with Coastal Provisions Market LLC and a personal guaranty from Elaine R. Matsuda (Lender commitment §5). Confirm these are executed at Closing as conditions to funding.  
               9.  RECOMMENDED ACTION ITEMS BEFORE / AT CLOSING  
              1.          Confirm exact delinquent-tax interest with the Bridgeport Tax Collector (discrepancy §4.1); obtain an updated payoff if Closing slips past July 20, 2025.  
              2.          Confirm final conveyance tax on Form OP-236 (discrepancy §4.2); adjust the 50/50 split if the amount differs from $44,750.00.  
              3.          Obtain party confirmation of the water/sewer treatment (discrepancy §4.3); pay the City directly from Seller's proceeds.  
              4.          Reconcile the three lien recording references against the Bridgeport Land Records before recording releases (discrepancy §4.4).  
              5.          Confirm the operative title commitment (No. TC-2025-07182, 6/18/2025) and the correct underwriter name (discrepancy §4.5–§4.6).  
              6.          Obtain the accrued interest figures for the earnest money and residential security deposits (data gaps §5.1–§5.2) and update the statement.  
              7.          Confirm with Bayshore whether any July NNN/CAM was collected for the commercial tenant (data gap §5.3).  
              8.          Deliver/obtain final gas and electric readings or estimates (data gap §5.4).  
              9.          Verify the Closing Agent's wire instructions via independent callback before transmitting any funds (discrepancy §4.8).  
              10.          Confirm the updated rent roll (PSA §9.7) and recompute the rent proration if collected rents changed.  
             
           — End of Settlement Statement Notes Memo —  
           This memo is prepared for closing-file purposes and does not constitute legal, tax, or accounting advice. Figures are derived from the documents listed herein.
