### FILE: exhibit-summary-memo.docx
          PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT  
        MEMORANDUM  
                          TO:  
          Sarah Whitfield, Esq. (Lead Counsel); Jordan Reeves, Esq.  
                FROM:  
          Associate, Calloway & Birch LLP  
                DATE:  
          October 15, 2024  
                RE:  
          Review of Respondent’s Document Production —             Torres-Kwan v. Kwan    , Case No. 2024-DR-01847 (Cir. Ct. Fairview County, Columbia, Fam. Div.; Hon. Patricia Yoon-Callahan)  
                PRIVILEGE:  
          Prepared in anticipation of litigation; attorney work product.  
         
       I. Introduction and Scope  
      This memorandum reviews the document production served by Respondent Darren Kwan through his counsel, Raymond Fossett of Hargrove Fossett & Yates LLP, in response to Petitioner Melinda Torres-Kwan’s First Request for Production of Documents, Nos. 1–22. The production consists of a cover/transmittal letter and seven (7) produced document sets (PROD-001 through PROD-007), together with notice of four (4) documents withheld on privilege (PRIV-001 through PRIV-004) and a stated intent to supplement entity-level NovaBridge records responsive to Request Nos. 16–22.  
      The purpose of this review is to (a) catalog the exhibits, (b) summarize the financial figures reported, (c) flag inconsistencies, omissions, and red flags between and within the produced documents, and (d) recommend follow-up discovery. All figures below were recomputed and footed against the source documents; internal arithmetic in the Financial Declaration, the 2023 tax return, the Schedule K-1, the Crestline statements, the Elm Street mortgage statements, and the NovaBridge expense reports was verified and ties, except as noted. Three of the four credit-card statements do not foot (see Issue No. 17).  
          Bottom line.         The production, on its face, materially understates Respondent’s income and liabilities and omits at least one real property, one mortgage, one financial account, and recurring transfers to an unidentified third party. The most serious items are: (1) reporting $174,000 of cash distributions as         “    K-1 income    ”         when the Schedule K-1 reports $367,200 of ordinary business income (a $193,200 understatement); (2) the complete omission of a solely-owned rental property at 1035 Elm Street, Unit C and its ~$241,281 mortgage; (3) an $85,000 outgoing wire on June 15, 2024 from the Crestline brokerage account to an undisclosed account at Harborstone Financial Group; and (4) a conflict in the date of separation (June 1, 2024 in the sworn declaration vs. August 1, 2024 in counsel’s own privilege log). These warrant targeted supplemental demands, third-party subpoenas, and a forensic accounting trace before any support or equitable-distribution numbers are relied upon.  
      
        II. Exhibit Catalog

          Excerpts only (full return is 47 pp.); SSNs/EINs redacted per Protective Order entered 10/3/2024.

          Covers 1/1–6/30/2024 only; Q3–Q4 not produced.  
            PRIV-001

            PRIV-003

             Production-level observations.         (i) The cover letter is undated. (ii) Bates prefixes are inconsistent (    “    DK-PROD-    ”         for the tax excerpts vs.     “    KWAN_PROD_    ”         for the brokerage, bank, and expense records). (iii) The Fairview Community Bank production footer misstates the case number as             2024-DR-00847         (correct: 2024-DR-01847) and the caption as         “        In re Marriage of Kwan    ,    ”         “    Circuit Court of Columbia, Fairview County.    ”         (iv) A formal privilege log is promised         “    within ten (10) business days    ”         but was not served with the production. (v) Entity-level NovaBridge records (Operating Agreement, Form 1065, financials) responsive to Nos. 16–22 are withheld pending         “    coordination    ”         with co-member Jared Ostrowski.  
      
        III. Summary of Reported Figures  
       A. Parties and procedural posture  
              Petitioner:         Melinda Torres-Kwan — art teacher, Fairview Regional High School; 2023 W-2 wages             $67,200         (PROD-002, W-2 #2); 403(b) acct 5587 (~$89,200 per declaration).  
              Respondent:         Darren Kwan — Co-Founder/CTO, NovaBridge Solutions LLC; 60% member; 2023 W-2 wages             $185,000         (PROD-002, W-2 #1).  
              Marriage:         June 14, 2009.             Separation:         disputed —             June 1, 2024         (Declaration ¶5) vs.         August 1, 2024         (cover letter, PRIV-001/002).             Petition filed:         September 3, 2024.             Children:         Ethan (b. 3/2/2011) and Sofia (b. 11/18/2014), residing with Petitioner at 418 Birchwood Lane.  
        B. 2023 income (as reported in the Financial Declaration, PROD-001 ¶16)  
                          Source  
          Amount (Declaration)  
          Amount (Tax Return / K-1)  
            W-2 salary (NovaBridge)  
          $185,000.00  
          $185,000.00 (W-2 Box 1) ✓  
            “    K-1 Income / Distributions    ”  
          $174,000.00  
              $367,200.00         K-1 ordinary business income (Box 1); $174,000 is the             cash distribution         (Box 19a)  
                Total annual income (declared)  
              $359,000.00  
          W-2 + K-1 OBI =             $552,200.00  
            Rental income (1035 Elm St)  
              None disclosed  
          $25,200 gross / $2,940 net (Schedule E)  
            Interest / dividends / capital gains  
              None disclosed  
          $1,840 / $6,310 / $8,450 (Form 1040)

                Joint return total income  
          —  
              $638,940         (Form 1040, Line 9)  
         The declaration’s $359,000 understates Respondent’s W-2 + pass-through business income by             $193,200    , and omits rental, interest, dividend, capital-gain, and vehicle-allowance income entirely (see Issue Nos. 1–3).  
        C. Assets (as reported, PROD-001)  
          Real property — only the marital residence is listed:

          $1,842.00  
            Estimated equity  
          $496,600.00 (verified: 715,000 − 218,400)  
            Title  
          Joint tenancy (both parties)  
         The declaration states Respondent         “    does not hold any ownership interest … in any other real property    ”         (¶23). This is contradicted by PROD-002 (Schedule E) and PROD-005 (see Issue No. 5).  
          Financial accounts (¶32, verified total $981,435.00):  
                            Account

                Total  
       
       
       
              $981,435.00  
             Vehicles (¶38, verified):         2022 BMW X5 (value $48,500; loan $22,100; equity $26,400) and 2020 Honda CR-V (value $24,800; loan $0; equity $24,800). Totals: value $73,300; loan $22,100; equity $51,200.  
          Other personal property:         ~$30,000 (household contents, ¶41).  
          Business interest:         60% membership in NovaBridge Solutions LLC (Delaware LLC, formed 1/15/2016); no formal valuation (¶37).  
        D. Liabilities (¶43, declared total $249,000 — verified)  
                          Liability  
          Creditor (as declared)  
          Balance  
            Mortgage — 418 Birchwood Lane  
          Meridian Home Lending  
          $218,400.00  
            Auto loan — 2022 BMW X5  
          Fairview Community Bank Auto Finance  
          $22,100.00  
            Credit card — Platinum Visa  
          “    First National Card Services    ”  
          ~$8,500.00  
                Total (declared)  
       
              $249,000.00  
         The declared total omits the Elm Street mortgage (~$241,281.19 per PROD-005). Including it, total mortgage/consumer debt is approximately             $490,281         — roughly double the declared figure (see Issue Nos. 5–6, 18).  
        E. Monthly expenses (¶49, verified personal total $8,885; plus $3,500 voluntary = $12,385)  
      Rent $4,750; utilities $350; groceries $800; dining/entertainment $600; auto loan $425; auto insurance $210; gas $300; health insurance $650; cell $150; clothing $200; personal care $100; professional dues $150; misc $200. Plus         “    voluntary contributions    ”         of $3,500/mo toward the marital household (¶51). The credit-card records show actual spending well above several of these line items (see Issue No. 23).  
        F. NovaBridge Solutions LLC — entity summary (PROD-002)  
                          Metric  
          FY2023

            Gross receipts  
          $4,280,000  
          $3,915,000  
            Cost of services  
          $2,614,000  
          —  
            Gross profit  
          $1,666,000  
          —  
            Total deductions (incl. owner W-2: Kwan $185,000 + Ostrowski $165,000 = $350,000)  
          $1,054,000  
          —  
            Ordinary business income (Form 1065, Line 22)  
          $612,000  
          $548,000  
            Total distributions to partners  
          $290,000 (Kwan $174,000 / Ostrowski $116,000)  
          $260,000  
            Retained at entity level  
          $322,000 (Kwan 60% share = $193,200)  
          —  
         K-1 capital account (Darren Kwan): beginning $482,600 + income $367,200 − distributions $174,000 =             ending $675,800         (verified). K-1 also reports recourse liabilities of             $36,400         (not addressed in the declaration).  
      
         IV. Inconsistencies, Omissions, and Red Flags  
      Issues are grouped by theme and tagged by severity:             [HIGH]         bears directly on income, asset/liability disclosure, or dissipation;             [MODERATE]         requires explanation or corroboration;             [LOW]         is a production/housekeeping error.  
       A. Income reporting  
          1. [HIGH] K-1 cash distributions reported as                 “        income        ”                 instead of K-1 ordinary business income — $193,200 understatement.         The declaration (¶15–16) reports         “    $174,000    ”         as         “    K-1 Income / Distributions    ”         and totals income at $359,000. The Schedule K-1 (PROD-002, DK-PROD-0053) reports             Box 1 ordinary business income of $367,200    ; the $174,000 is             Box 19a cash distributions    . These are different concepts: the $367,200 is Respondent’s taxable share of NovaBridge’s 2023 profit; the $174,000 is the cash he withdrew. W-2 ($185,000) + K-1 ordinary business income ($367,200) =             $552,200    , not $359,000. For child-support and alimony purposes the pass-through income figure (subject to the court’s add-back/deduction analysis), not the cash distribution, is the relevant measure.             Cite:         Declaration ¶¶15–16; K-1 Box 1 vs. Box 19a.  
          2. [HIGH] Undisclosed rental income from 1035 Elm Street, Unit C.         Schedule E (PROD-002, DK-PROD-0050) reports $25,200 gross rents and $2,940 net rental income for 2023, with mortgage interest of $11,280 paid to Meridian. The FCB checking statements (PROD-004) show         “    ACH Deposit — Tenant Rent Payment    ”         of             $2,100/month         in April, May, June, and July 2024 (continuing the same $2,100/mo stream). The declaration nonetheless states Respondent has         “    no other sources of income, including … rental income …             None    ”         (¶19).             Cite:         Declaration ¶19; Schedule E; FCB statements 4/5, 5/3, 6/5, 7/3/2024.  
          3. [MODERATE] Undisclosed vehicle allowance.         W-2 #1 Box 14 reports a $9,600 vehicle allowance ($800/mo employer stipend) (PROD-002, DK-PROD-0048). This compensation is not mentioned in the declaration’s income section, even though the declaration separately claims auto-insurance and gasoline expenses.             Cite:         W-2 Box 14; Declaration ¶19.  
          4. [MODERATE] Pay frequency / deposit amount do not reconcile; no pay stubs produced.         The declaration states the $185,000 salary is         “    paid on a bi-monthly basis    ”         (¶14) — i.e., 24 semi-monthly pays, which would be $7,708.33 gross per pay. The FCB statements show direct deposits of             $7,115.38         on approximately the 1st and 15th of each month. $7,115.38 × 26 = $185,000 (bi-weekly), while $7,115.38 × 24 = $170,769.12 (not $185,000). The deposit amount corresponds to a bi-weekly gross, not a semi-monthly gross, so the stated frequency and the deposit amount are inconsistent. Pay stubs — which the declaration says         “    have been or will be produced    ”         (¶57) — were             not         in this production.             Cite:         Declaration ¶14, ¶57; FCB payroll deposits.  
        B. Undisclosed assets and liabilities  
          5. [HIGH] Undisclosed real property — 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, Fairview, Columbia 20147.         Schedule E (PROD-002) lists this condominium as rental real estate         “    titled in the name of Darren Kwan,    ”         365 rental days / 0 personal-use days. PROD-005 is a full set of Meridian mortgage statements for this property (loan 7842-0163-ELM, sole borrower Darren Kwan, original loan $279,000 dated 10/8/2019, 4.875% fixed, 30-yr). The declaration affirmatively states Respondent         “    does not hold any ownership interest … in any other real property    ”         (¶23) and lists only 418 Birchwood Lane. The Elm Street unit was acquired and mortgaged during the marriage (2019) and is generating rental income; it is a marital asset omitted from the sworn declaration.             Cite:         Declaration ¶¶20–23; Schedule E; PROD-005.  
          6. [HIGH] Undisclosed Elm Street mortgage (~$241,281) omitted from liabilities.         PROD-005 shows the Elm Street principal balance declining from $242,536.18 (5/1/2024) to             $241,281.19         (8/1/2024) (roll-forward verified). The declaration’s liability schedule (¶43) lists only the Birchwood mortgage ($218,400), the BMW loan ($22,100), and the credit card (~$8,500), totaling $249,000. Adding the Elm Street mortgage, total debt is ~        $490,281    . The FCB statements corroborate two monthly Meridian ACH payments — $1,842 (Birchwood) and             $1,680 (Elm Street)         — every month from March through August 2024.             Cite:         Declaration ¶43; PROD-005; FCB ACH         “    Meridian Home Lending    ”         $1,842 and         “    Meridian Home Lending    ”         $1,680.  
          7. [HIGH] Undisclosed account at Harborstone Financial Group (acct ending 2291) — recipient of $85,000 wire.         On 6/15/2024, an $85,000 outgoing wire transferred from the Crestline brokerage account (PROD-003, KWAN_PROD_00152) to         “    Harborstone Financial Group, Acct ending 2291,    ”         reference WT-2024-061524-0087, authorized by verbal/recorded telephone call. No Harborstone account appears in the declaration’s account schedule (¶24). This is an undisclosed account receiving a large post-separation transfer (see Issue No. 11).             Cite:         Crestline 6/15/2024 wire detail; Declaration ¶24, ¶33.  
          8. [MODERATE] Joint account statements (4821, 4822) not produced.         The declaration lists a joint checking ($12,340) and joint savings ($28,750) at Fairview Community Bank (¶¶25–26), but PROD-004 covers only the individual accounts (7703/7704). The cover letter’s objection to producing         “    accounts held solely by Petitioner    ”         does not reach joint accounts, to which Respondent has signatory access. The joint-account balances are uncorroborated.             Cite:         Cover letter         “    Additional Notations    ”    ; Declaration ¶¶25–26; PROD-004 (individual only).  
          9. [MODERATE] 401(k) statements (3310) not produced; anomalous $14,644.60 transaction.         The declaration reports a 401(k) balance of $412,800 (¶30) but no 401(k) statements were produced. The FCB statements show a recurring         “    ACH — Pinnacle Retirement Services 401(k)    ”         of             $1,437.50/month         ($17,250/yr annualized), which does not match the 2023 W-2 elective deferral of $22,500, and a single anomalous             $14,644.60         “    Early Month    ”         401(k) ACH on 8/1/2024. The nature of that transaction (loan repayment, hardship withdrawal, lump-sum contribution, or rollover) is unexplained.             Cite:         Declaration ¶30; W-2 Box 12a ($22,500); FCB 401(k) ACHs.  
          10. [MODERATE] No mortgage statement for the marital residence (Birchwood).         PROD-005 produced Elm Street statements only. The declaration’s Birchwood figures ($218,400 balance; $1,842 PITI) are corroborated only by the $1,842 ACH in the FCB records; no Meridian statement for the Birchwood loan was produced, so the $218,400 balance is uncorroborated.             Cite:         Declaration ¶¶20–22; PROD-005 (Elm Street only).  
        C. Post-separation transfers and possible dissipation  
          11. [HIGH] $85,000 outgoing wire (6/15/2024) to undisclosed Harborstone account.         Fourteen days after the declared June 1, 2024 separation, $85,000 was wired out of the Crestline account to Harborstone acct 2291. The declaration then reports the Crestline balance as             $387,450         (¶29) — which is the             post-wire 8/1/2024         valuation, not the ~$468,695 balance as of the declared 6/1/2024 separation date (Crestline 5/31/2024 close). The declaration also represents that all balances are         “    approximate as of the date of separation … June 1, 2024    ”         (¶56), so reporting the reduced post-transfer balance understates the marital brokerage asset by ~$81,000 and conceals the transfer. If separation is instead August 1, 2024 (per the privilege log), the wire occurred during the marriage. Either way the transfer and the recipient account are undisclosed.             Cite:         Crestline 6/15/2024 wire; Declaration ¶¶29, 56; cover letter PRIV-001/002 (8/1/2024 separation).  
          12. [HIGH] Recurring Venmo transfers to unidentified                 “        Kiera M.        ”                 — $14,600 (Mar–Jul 2024).         The FCB checking statements show five Venmo payments to         “    Transfer to Kiera M.    ”    : $2,800 (3/15), $3,100 (4/22), $2,500 (5/10), $3,200 (6/18), and $3,000 (7/14) — averaging ~$2,920/month.         “    Kiera M.    ”         is not identified anywhere in the production. Separately, the declaration claims $3,500/month in         “    voluntary contributions … directly to Petitioner or to the mortgage servicer    ”         (¶51), but the FCB records show only the $1,842 marital-mortgage ACH and             no         transfer to Petitioner; the recurring third-party Venmo transfers are not reflected in the declared expense schedule.             Cite:         FCB Venmo entries; Declaration ¶¶49–51.  
          13. [MODERATE] $6,200 personal travel charge (7/8/2024).         The Platinum Visa statement (PROD-006, July) shows a $6,200 charge to         “    Sapphire Sky Travel    ”         on 7/8/2024. A separate $3,400 Sapphire Sky Travel charge appears in the NovaBridge expense report as reimbursed business         “    client appreciation event planning    ”         (PROD-007, 4/3/2024). The July $6,200 charge falls outside the produced expense reports (which end 6/30/2024) and is not identified as business.             Cite:         PROD-006 (7/8/2024); PROD-007 (4/3/2024).  
        D. Date and procedural inconsistencies  
          14. [HIGH] Date of separation conflict — June 1, 2024 vs. August 1, 2024.         The sworn declaration fixes separation at         “    on or about June 1, 2024    ”         (¶5) and uses that date as the valuation reference (¶56). The cover letter’s privilege log repeatedly dates the separation             August 1, 2024         (PRIV-001:         “    following the parties’ separation on August 1, 2024    ”    ; PRIV-002:         “    post-separation August 1, 2024    ”    ). The two-month gap is material: it determines the valuation date, the marital/separate character of the $85,000 wire, the Venmo transfers, the $6,200 travel, and the accrual of post-separation income/distributions.             Cite:         Declaration ¶¶5, 56; cover letter PRIV-001, PRIV-002.  
          15. [MODERATE] Financial declaration filing date conflict.         The declaration bears         “    Filed: September 20, 2024    ”         and a Certificate of Service dated September 20, 2024. The cover letter describes PROD-001 as         “    filed with the Court on or about October 4, 2024.    ”         The two dates are inconsistent.             Cite:         Declaration filing line & Certificate of Service; cover letter Document Index item 1.  
          16. [MODERATE] Harbor Point lease commencement not corroborated by bank records.         The declaration states the Harbor Point condominium lease commenced         “    on or about June 1, 2024    ”         (¶2). The FCB records show the             first         Harbor Point rent payment on             7/30/2024         (Check #4038, $4,750) and 8/1/2024 (ACH, $4,750), with no June rent payment; the credit-card mailing address changed to Harbor Point in the             July         cycle (PROD-006). The records thus place the move in late July, not June 1 — consistent with a later separation date and inconsistent with the declaration.             Cite:         Declaration ¶2; FCB 7/30 & 8/1/2024 Harbor Point payments; PROD-006 July address change.  
        E. Internal arithmetic and production errors  
          17. [MODERATE] Three credit-card statements do not foot — each overstates                 “        Total New Charges        ”                 by exactly $1,000.         Summing the itemized transactions:             May         $7,038.45 (stated $7,038.45 — foots);             June         $7,124.30 (stated $8,124.30 —             +$1,000    );             July         $11,486.75 (stated $12,486.75 —             +$1,000    );             August         $5,418.50 (stated $6,418.50 —             +$1,000    ). The overstated June balance ($8,124.30) carries forward as the July         “    Previous Balance    ”         and is shown as paid 7/1/2024, so the error propagates. Either line items are missing or the statement totals are arithmetically wrong; the records must be reconciled before any balance is relied upon.             Cite:         PROD-006, May–Aug transaction details vs. stated totals.  
          18. [MODERATE] Credit-card creditor and balance misstated in the declaration.         The declaration identifies the card issuer as         “    First National Card Services    ”         with an         “    ~$8,500    ”         revolving balance and payments of         “    $500 to $1,000 per month    ”         (¶46). The statements show the issuer is             Fairview Community Bank         (Platinum Visa, acct 8316), the card is             paid in full each month         (payments of $3,240.18, $7,038.45, $8,124.30, $12,486.75), and the 8/31/2024 balance was             $6,418.50    , not $8,500. The FCB checking ACHs to         “    Fairview Community Bank Visa    ”         ($1,450–$5,480/mo) confirm the issuer.             Cite:         Declaration ¶46; PROD-006; FCB         “    Fairview Community Bank Visa    ”         ACHs.  
          19. [MODERATE] Crestline account tax-ID discrepancy.         Every Crestline statement lists the account tax ID as ●●●-●●-        8341    . Per the 2023 return,             8341         is the SSN of the minor child             Ethan Kwan         (dependent, DOB 3/2/2011); Respondent’s own SSN ends in             4718    . Either this is a redaction/production error or the account is registered under the child’s SSN (which would conflict with its characterization as Respondent’s individual taxable brokerage account         “    funded with marital earnings    ”         (¶29)). Clarification is required.             Cite:         PROD-003 account information blocks; PROD-002 dependents table.  
          20. [LOW] Case-number and caption errors in the FCB production.         The PROD-004 footer reads         “    Case No. 2024-DR-        00847    ”         (correct: 2024-DR-01847) and         “        In re Marriage of Kwan    ,    ”         “    Circuit Court of Columbia, Fairview County.    ”             Cite:         PROD-004 footer.  
          21. [LOW] Inconsistent Bates prefixes and undated cover letter.         Tax excerpts use         “    DK-PROD-####    ”    ; brokerage, bank, and expense records use         “    KWAN_PROD_#####    ”    . The transmittal email’s Date field is blank.             Cite:         cover letter; PROD-002/003/004/007 Bates stamps.  
        F. Expense understatement / lifestyle spending  
          22. [MODERATE] Declared monthly expenses materially understate actual spending.         The credit-card records show spending far above several declared line items:             clothing         declared $200/mo vs. Monarch Fine Clothiers charges of $1,420–$2,340/mo;             dining         declared $600/mo vs. restaurant charges (Capital Grille, Ridgeline Steakhouse, Bella Notte) frequently exceeding $1,000/mo plus $425/mo country-club dues;             personal care         declared $100/mo vs. Aura Salon & Spa charges of $295–$485/mo; plus luxury furnishings (Harbor Point Luxury Furnishings $2,280 and $956) and the $6,200 July travel. Average monthly card charges run ~$8,500, on top of rent, utilities, and groceries. The declared $8,885 personal-expense schedule (and the $12,385 total outlay) understates Respondent’s actual lifestyle and bears on both the support analysis and the surplus available for dissipation.             Cite:         Declaration ¶49; PROD-006.  
        G. Business interest and governance  
          23. [HIGH] NovaBridge Operating Agreement not produced; expense self-approval rests on unproduced Section 5.3.         The expense report (PROD-007) states that, under Section 5.3 of the Operating Agreement, a managing member holding ≥60% may approve his own expense reimbursements         “    without requirement of separate board or member approval,    ”         and that Darren Kwan is         “    the sole approving authority for his own expense reimbursements,    ”         with co-member Ostrowski’s review         “    not required.    ”         The Operating Agreement itself was not produced (cover letter: Nos. 16–22 to be supplemented). Self-approved, unreviewed reimbursements are a potential vehicle for extracting marital value from the entity and must be examined against the agreement and the entity books.             Cite:         PROD-007 §1, §5; cover letter (Nos. 16–22).  
          24. [MODERATE] Expense-reimbursement deposits do not reconcile to the reported totals.         The FCB statements show only two NovaBridge reimbursement deposits in the produced window: $3,400 (4/10/2024) and $2,150 (5/20/2024), total $5,550. The expense report claims H1 2024 reimbursements of             $12,662         (Q1 $3,373 approved 4/8; Q2 $9,289 approved 7/3). The Q2 total of $9,289 does not appear as a deposit through 8/1/2024, and the 4/10 deposit ($3,400) does not equal the Q1 total ($3,373). Reimbursements may be paid per-line or to another account; the full 2024 bank records and the company reimbursement ledger are needed.             Cite:         PROD-007 §4; FCB         “    NovaBridge Solutions LLC Expense Reimb.    ”         deposits.  
          25. [MODERATE] No business valuation; capital account, retained earnings, and recourse liabilities not addressed.         The declaration reserves the right to value the 60% interest but discloses no figure (¶37). The K-1 shows an ending tax-basis capital account of             $675,800    , 2023 retained earnings at the entity of $322,000 (Respondent’s 60% share $193,200), and recourse liabilities of             $36,400         — none of which is mentioned. Entity revenue grew from $3,915,000 (2022) to $4,280,000 (2023) and ordinary business income from $548,000 to $612,000. A formal valuation is required for equitable distribution.             Cite:         Declaration ¶37; K-1 capital account & liabilities; PROD-002 entity summary.  
          26. [MODERATE] Expense reports cover only Q1–Q2 2024.         No Q3–Q4 2024 reports were produced, so post-June reimbursements (and the relationship, if any, between the July $6,200 Sapphire Sky Travel charge and a later reimbursement) cannot be tested.             Cite:         PROD-007 (period 1/1–6/30/2024).  
      
         V. Recommended Follow-Up Discovery  
       A. Supplemental production demands on Respondent (meet-and-confer first)  
              Complete 2023 Form 1040         with all schedules and attachments (A, B, D, SE, 8995, full Schedule E, Form 8606 if applicable) — only excerpts were produced; the full 47-page return is available (PROD-002 cover sheet).  
              NovaBridge Operating Agreement         (all versions/amendments) and             Form 1065         U.S. returns and entity financial statements for             2021–2024    ; member capital-account ledgers; distribution ledger; expense-reimbursement ledger; member/manager minutes or written consents.  
              Pay stubs         for 2023 and 2024 (referenced at Declaration ¶57 but not produced) to resolve the pay-frequency/deposit discrepancy (Issue No. 4).  
              Joint account statements         (Fairview Community Bank 4821 and 4822) for 2022–present (Issue No. 8).  
              401(k) statements         (Pinnacle 3310) for 2022–present, plus loan/withdrawal records and an explanation of the             $14,644.60         transaction on 8/1/2024 (Issue No. 9).  
              Marital-residence (Birchwood) mortgage statements and loan history         from Meridian (Issue No. 10).  
              Q3–Q4 2024 NovaBridge expense reports         and the 2024 estimated K-1 / distribution records (Issue No. 26).  
              Identification of                 “        Kiera M.        ”         and production of all Venmo/Zelle/CashApp/payment-app records for 2022–present (Issue No. 12).  
              Explanation and documentation of the $85,000 wire         (6/15/2024) to Harborstone acct 2291, including the recorded authorization call (ref. WT-2024-061524-0087) and Harborstone statements (Issue Nos. 7, 11).  
              Complete, sworn privilege log         as promised (cover letter) — itemizing PRIV-001–004 with author, recipient, date, subject, and privilege basis.  
              Supplemental Sworn Financial Declaration         correcting the income figure (K-1 ordinary business income), adding the Elm Street property and mortgage, the Harborstone account, the rental and other omitted income, and reconciling the credit-card creditor/balance.  
        B. Third-party subpoenas duces tecum  
              Harborstone Financial Group         — all accounts in the name of or beneficially held for Darren Kwan (esp. acct ending 2291); statements 2022–present; account agreements, beneficiary/ownership, and all transfers in/out.  
              Meridian Home Lending         — both loans (418 Birchwood Lane and 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, loan 7842-0163-ELM); full payment and loan histories, note/mortgage, payoff statements, escrow analyses, and current status.  
              Fairview Community Bank         — all accounts held by or with Darren Kwan (joint 4821/4822, individual 7703/7704, credit card 8316, auto loan); statements 2022–present; signature cards; address-change records.  
              Crestline Wealth Advisors         — account 9956 full transaction history 2022–present; all wire-transfer authorization records (including the recorded call for WT-2024-061524-0087); clarification of the account tax-ID/registration (8341 vs. 4718).  
              Pinnacle Retirement Services         — 401(k) 3310 and 403(b) 5587; balances, contribution history, loans, withdrawals, and beneficiary designations 2022–present.  
              NovaBridge Solutions LLC         (via registered agent / Jared Ostrowski) — Operating Agreement, 2021–2024 Form 1065, financial statements, distribution and expense-reimbursement ledgers, member capital accounts, minutes/consents.  
              Venmo/PayPal         (payment processor) — transaction records for Darren Kwan, 2022–present, to identify         “    Kiera M.    ”         and all peer transfers.  
              Fairview Regional School District         — Petitioner’s employment/earnings records (to corroborate her income independent of Respondent’s representation).  
              Archer & Linden CPAs         — tax workpapers and source documents for the 2022–2023 returns (subject to a privilege/§ 7525 analysis).  
        C. Interrogatories and requests for admission  
              Interrogatory:         Identify         “    Kiera M.    ”         (full name, relationship to Respondent), the purpose of each transfer, and whether any funds were returned or re-transferred.  
              Interrogatory:         Identify every financial, brokerage, crypto, or payment-app account held individually, jointly, or in another’s name for Respondent’s benefit, 2022–present, including Harborstone acct 2291.  
              Interrogatory:         State the date of separation and all facts supporting it; explain the June 1, 2024 vs. August 1, 2024 conflict.  
              Interrogatory:         Identify the Elm Street property — purchase date, price, source of funds, title history, tenant(s), lease, rent, management, and whether marital funds were used for acquisition, mortgage, or upkeep.  
              Interrogatory:         Itemize every transfer exceeding $5,000 from any account from 1/1/2024 to present (recipient, purpose, consideration).  
              Interrogatory:         Explain the use of $174,000 (cash distributions) as         “    income    ”         given the $367,200 K-1 ordinary business income; state 2023 and 2024 K-1 income vs. distributions.  
              Interrogatory:         Explain the $14,644.60 401(k) transaction on 8/1/2024.  
              RFA:         Admit that 1035 Elm Street, Unit C is owned by Respondent and was not disclosed in the Sworn Financial Declaration.  
              RFA:         Admit that the $85,000 wire on 6/15/2024 was sent to Harborstone Financial Group acct 2291.  
              RFA:         Admit that the Elm Street mortgage balance was approximately $241,281 as of 8/1/2024 and was not disclosed.  
              RFA:         Admit that Respondent received rental income in 2023 and 2024.  
              RFA:         Admit that Respondent’s 2023 Schedule K-1 ordinary business income was $367,200.  
        D. Experts  
              Forensic accountant         — trace the $85,000 wire and the Harborstone 2291 account; reconstruct the Venmo/    “    Kiera M.    ”         transfers and the expense-reimbursement flow; reconcile actual 2023–2024 income (W-2 + K-1 OBI + rental + investment income) against the declaration; quantify any dissipation.  
              Business valuator         — value the 60% NovaBridge interest (consider the $675,800 capital account, $193,200 share of 2023 retained earnings, $4.28M revenue, $612,000 OBI, growth trend, and Operating-Agreement transfer/buyout restrictions).  
              Real-estate appraiser(s)         — appraise both 418 Birchwood Lane (declaration uses an uncorroborated online estimate) and 1035 Elm Street, Unit C.  
        E. Motion practice  
              Emergency motion / TRO         to restrain further dissipation and compel an accounting of the $85,000 wire, the Harborstone 2291 account, and any other undisclosed accounts, given post-separation transfers and the sworn-declaration omissions.  
              Motion to compel         complete production: full tax return and schedules; Operating Agreement and entity records (Nos. 16–22); joint-account statements; pay stubs; 401(k) records; Birchwood mortgage statements; Q3–Q4 expense reports.  
              Motion to compel a complete privilege log         if not served within the promised ten business days.  
              Motion to determine the date of separation         — the record (first Harbor Point rent 7/30/2024; credit-card address change in July; counsel’s 8/1/2024 references) supports a date later than June 1, 2024.  
              Sanctions motion         if the omissions prove knowing — the declaration is sworn under penalty of perjury under Columbia Family Code § 9-207 and Local Rule 12.04, and omits a real property, a mortgage, an account, rental income, and ~$193,200 of pass-through income.  
        F. Depositions  
              Darren Kwan         — financial topics (income, Elm Street, the $85,000 wire,         “    Kiera M.,    ”         expense reimbursements, date of separation).  
              Jared Ostrowski         (NovaBridge co-member/CEO) — entity finances, distributions, the Operating Agreement, and the self-approval practice.  
              Nathan Greer, CFP         (Crestline advisor) — the 6/15/2024 wire authorization and account registration, if subpoenas are insufficient.  
      
         VI. Conclusion and Recommended Next Steps  
      The production is internally coherent in its arithmetic but materially incomplete and, in several respects, inconsistent with the sworn Financial Declaration. The most consequential gaps — the $193,200 income understatement, the omitted Elm Street property and ~$241,281 mortgage, the $85,000 wire to an undisclosed Harborstone account, the recurring transfers to         “    Kiera M.,    ”         and the June 1 vs. August 1 separation-date conflict — should be addressed immediately. Recommended sequence:  
          Send a meet-and-confer letter and supplemental demand (Section V.A) this week.  
          Issue the third-party subpoenas (Section V.B), prioritizing Harborstone, Meridian (both loans), Fairview Community Bank (joint accounts and credit card), and NovaBridge.  
          Serve the interrogatories and RFAs (Section V.C).  
          Retain the forensic accountant and move for a TRO/accounting and to compel (Section V.E) if Respondent does not voluntarily supplement.  
          Calendar a date-of-separation hearing and a business-valuation track.  
      We should not rely on any income, asset, or liability figure in the current declaration for temporary support or equitable-distribution purposes until the items above are resolved.  
          Prepared by Calloway & Birch LLP in anticipation of litigation. Attorney work product — privileged and confidential.

### FILE: arbitrator-disclosure-issues-memo.docx
          MEMORANDUM  
          PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT / PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF LITIGATION  
          TO:         Arbitration Challenge Evaluation File  
          FROM:         Arbitration Counsel  
          DATE:         June 20, 2024  
          RE:         Assessment of Grounds for Challenge to the Appointment of Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.) as Sole Arbitrator — AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217,             Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. v. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC  
      
       I. Introduction and Scope  
      This memorandum assesses whether grounds exist to challenge the appointment of Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.) as Sole Arbitrator in AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217. The assessment compares the arbitrator’s Disclosure Statement dated June 17, 2024 against the other source documents in the record: (a) the Arbitration Demand and Statement of Claims filed March 15, 2024; (b) the AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules excerpts (Rules R-16, R-17, R-18, and related provisions); (c) the arbitrator appointment data workbook (case list, fee summary, and pending-matters tabs); and (d) the entity and relationship profiles memorandum dated June 19, 2024.  
          Source provenance.         The entity-relationship-profiles memorandum was prepared by Catherine M. Aldridge, an associate at Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (counsel for Respondent Whitmore Capital Partners LLC) and is labeled attorney-client privileged / work product. It is advocacy-oriented research. The underlying facts it reports are stated to be drawn from public records (SEC EDGAR/Form ADV/Form D filings, IRS Form 990 filings, AAA directories, academic publication databases, CLE archives, and public award databases) and are, in principle, independently verifiable. The arbitrator-appointment-data workbook is an unattributed data file that corroborates and, in certain respects, extends the relationship memorandum. Where the two sources disagree, or where a source’s summary figure does not match its underlying line-item data, the discrepancy is flagged below rather than papered over.  
          Bottom line.         The disclosure statement is materially incomplete. Comparison with the source documents reveals             eight         distinct disclosure concerns, of which             five         are outright non-disclosures of facts within the arbitrator’s knowledge or readily available to her,             one         is a partial disclosure that omits material detail, and             two         are aggregate/structural concerns that aggravate the others. Several of the non-disclosures implicate specific subparagraphs of AAA Rule R-17 and contradict the arbitrator’s own certification that her list of prior/pending matters is         “    complete.    ”         The issues do not all point in the same direction: some create an appearance of partiality toward the Claimant’s side (notably the arbitrator’s spouse’s undisclosed academic collaboration with the Claimant’s key expert witness), while others create an appearance of partiality toward the Respondent’s side (notably undisclosed relationships with the Respondent’s lead counsel, the Respondent’s principal, and an indirect financial interest in a fund affiliated with the Respondent). Under the governing         “    justifiable doubt    ”         standard, that bidirectional pattern is itself significant: a reasonable party on either side could question the arbitrator’s ability to render a fair determination. The challenge deadline is             June 24, 2024    .  
      
        II. Procedural Framework and Governing Standards  
          Governing rules.         The arbitration is administered by the AAA under its Commercial Arbitration Rules (as amended effective September 1, 2022), with the Large Complex Case Supplementary Procedures invoked by virtue of the amount in controversy ($46.9 million in compensatory damages alone). The seat is Minneapolis, Minnesota; the substantive law is Delaware; the Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S.C. §§ 1–16, governs the arbitration agreement.  
          The disclosure obligation (Rules R-16 and R-17).         Rule R-16 requires disclosure of         “    any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator’s impartiality or independence, including any bias or any financial or personal interest in the result of the arbitration, or any past or present relationship with the parties, their representatives, or their counsel,    ”         and states that the obligation         “    shall be construed broadly.    ”         Rule R-17(a) enumerates four non-exclusive disclosure categories:  
              R-17(a)(i):         any known direct or indirect financial or personal interest in the outcome,         “    including any interest, whether legal or beneficial, in the subject matter of the dispute, in any party, or in any entity that may be affected by the result of the proceeding    ”    ;  
              R-17(a)(ii):         any known existing or past financial, business, professional, family, or social relationship with any party, its counsel, representatives,             or witnesses         likely to affect impartiality or that might reasonably create an appearance of partiality —         “    including, without limitation, relationships arising from employment, professional association, joint ventures, board memberships,             academic collaboration    , or regular social contact    ”    ;  
              R-17(a)(iii):         any prior or pending cases in which the arbitrator has been involved         “    with any party to the arbitration, its counsel, or representatives,    ”         in any capacity, before the AAA or any other institution, neutral or non-neutral; and  
              R-17(a)(iv):         a catch-all covering         “    any other circumstances that might cause a reasonable party to question the arbitrator’s ability to render a fair and impartial determination,    ”         expressly         “    including but not limited to the arbitrator’s prior publications, public statements, or expressed views on legal or factual issues relevant to the dispute.    ”  
      Rule R-17(b) makes disclosure a             continuing obligation    ; the arbitrator         “    shall not await inquiry by a party or by the AAA before making any required supplemental disclosure.    ”         Rule R-17(d) provides that failure to make complete and timely disclosure         “    may result in the arbitrator’s removal in accordance with Rule R-18, and may constitute grounds for vacatur of any resulting award under applicable law, including but not limited to Section 10 of the Federal Arbitration Act.    ”  
          The challenge procedure (Rule R-18).         A challenge must be made by written notice to the AAA and the other party,         “    stating with specificity the grounds for the challenge,    ”         within             seven (7) calendar days         after the later of (i) the appointment being communicated to the challenging party, or (ii) the circumstances giving rise to the challenge becoming known or reasonably should have become known to the challenging party. If the other party does not agree and the arbitrator does not withdraw, the AAA decides the challenge (R-18(d)). A party that fails to challenge timely is deemed to have waived grounds then known to it,             except         grounds that         “    were not and could not reasonably have been known at the time,    ”         and nothing in the rule precludes a later evident-partiality vacatur motion under FAA § 10 for issues not previously known and subject to waiver (R-18(f)).  
          Related authorities referenced in the rules excerpts.         The AAA/ABA Code of Ethics for Arbitrators in Commercial Disputes (Canon I — integrity/fairness; Canon II — disclosure; Canon IV — fair conduct); the IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest in International Arbitration (General Standards; Non-Waivable Red List, Waivable Red List, Orange List, Green List); and FAA § 10(a)(2), which permits vacatur         “    where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators.    ”         The IBA Guidelines are not binding AAA rules, but they are expressly referenced in the rules excerpts as interpretive guidance and are widely used to evaluate disclosure adequacy. The IBA Orange List is particularly relevant here: item 3.3.3 calls for disclosure where an arbitrator has, within the past three years, received more than three appointments from the same counsel or law firm; items 3.3.5 and 3.3.6 call for disclosure of past or present relationships through shared professional-association or board membership.  
          Deadline.         The arbitrator’s disclosure is dated June 17, 2024. Seven calendar days thereafter is             June 24, 2024    , which the arbitrator herself identifies as the objection deadline. The safest course is to file any challenge on or before that date. The         “    whichever is later    ”         prong of R-18(a)(ii) may afford modest additional time for facts that first became known to a party after June 17 (e.g., facts surfaced in the June 19, 2024 research memorandum), but because most of the relevant facts are drawn from public records that         “    should reasonably have become known    ”         earlier, reliance on that extension is risky and should not be assumed.  
      
        III. Foundational Facts  
          The arbitration.             Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. (by and through its minority shareholders, derivatively and on behalf of the Company) v. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC    , AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217. The dispute arises from a controlling shareholder’s alleged self-dealing. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (    “    Whitmore    ”    ) manages three fund vehicles; Whitmore Fund III LP holds a 62% controlling stake in Cascade (acquired January 8, 2019 for $118 million), and Whitmore Fund II LP owns Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC. Whitmore, exercising control over Cascade’s board, directed Cascade to license its proprietary         “    ThermalCore    ”         technology (14 U.S. patents) to Volaris under a Technology License Agreement dated March 1, 2023, at $2.8 million per year for a seven-year term (March 1, 2023 – February 28, 2030). An independent valuation by Ridgeline Advisory Group (November 15, 2023) set fair market value at $9.5 million per year. The annual differential is $6.7 million; over seven years the claimed damages total             $46.9 million    . The claims include breach of fiduciary duty (duty of loyalty), self-dealing in violation of Section 8.7 of the Operating Agreement, unjust enrichment, and waste of corporate assets.  
          Parties and counsel.  
              Claimant:         Cascade Thermal Systems Inc., derivatively and through its minority shareholders (collectively 38%; lead minority shareholder Janet Okafor, a board member holding 11.4%). Counsel:             Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP         (Minneapolis); lead counsel             Priya Narayanan    .  
              Respondent:         Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (Boston). Counsel:             Barrow & Thistlewood LLP         (Boston); lead partner             Dominic Hargrave    ; senior partner             Ellen Wu         (head of the firm’s national arbitration practice and involved in arbitrator-selection strategy for this matter).  
              Non-party / affiliate:         Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC (Charlotte, NC), a Whitmore Fund II LP portfolio company and the direct beneficiary of the challenged license; CEO Thomas Kinnear.  
          Anticipated witnesses identified in the demand.         Janet Okafor (Claimant board member/representative);             Garrett T. Whitmore         (Respondent’s founder and Managing Partner; anticipated adverse fact witness);             Thomas Kinnear         (Volaris CEO; anticipated adverse/non-party witness);             Dr. Rita Peshkov         (Cascade VP of R&D; the demand’s designated key expert witness on the ThermalCore technology); a Ridgeline Advisory Group valuation expert; and a damages/financial expert. The arbitrator states in her disclosure that she         “    reviewed the identities of the parties, their counsel, and the individuals identified in the arbitration demand and answering statement.    ”         (The record contains the demand but no answering statement; this assessment proceeds on the demand, which on its face placed the arbitrator on notice that Dr. Peshkov and Garrett T. Whitmore were anticipated witnesses.)  
          The arbitrator.         Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.): J.D., University of Wisconsin Law School (1989); LL.M. in Intellectual Property, Franklin Pierce Center (1994); commercial litigator/IP attorney in Minneapolis; Hennepin County District Court judge 2004–2020; retired 2020; approximately 85 arbitrations since retirement; listed on the AAA Large Complex Case panel and the Technology Case Management panel; affiliated as a neutral with the Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center (750 Nicollet Mall, Suite 3200, Minneapolis); spouse Dr. Alan Fairhaven (retired professor of mechanical engineering, University of Minnesota); personal investment portfolio managed on a discretionary basis by Northbridge Wealth Advisors; serves on the advisory board of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute (since 2021).  
          Key dates.  
                         Date  
          Event  
            Jan. 8, 2019  
          Whitmore Fund III LP acquires 62% of Cascade for $118 million  
            Mar. 1, 2023  
          Volaris License Agreement executed ($2.8M/yr; 7-yr term)  
            Nov. 15, 2023  
          Ridgeline Advisory Group FMV valuation ($9.5M/yr)  
            Mar. 15, 2024  
          Arbitration demand filed  
            June 3, 2024  
          Parties select Fairhaven (Whitmore ranked her #1; Cascade ranked her #3)  
            June 10, 2024  
          AAA confirms appointment  
            June 17, 2024  
          Arbitrator disclosure statement submitted  
                June 24, 2024  
              Deadline to challenge (7 calendar days from disclosure)  
            Aug. 5, 2024  
          Preliminary hearing scheduled  
            Jan. 13 – Feb. 7, 2025  
          Evidentiary hearing (15 hearing days)  
             Fee arrangement (as disclosed and corroborated by the relationship memorandum).         $850/hour; estimated 400 hours (120 hearing + 280 pre/post); estimated total fees ~$340,000; ~$170,000 per party at 50/50 allocation. (Arithmetic checks: 400 × $850 = $340,000; $340,000 ÷ 2 = $170,000.)  
      
        IV. Assessment of Specific Disclosure Issues  
       Issue 1 — Undisclosed academic collaboration between the arbitrator’s spouse and the Claimant’s key expert witness (Dr. Rita Peshkov)  
          The fact (source: relationship memorandum §§III.2, 1.2; demand §VIII.B.2).         Dr. Alan Fairhaven, the arbitrator’s husband, co-authored a peer-reviewed paper with Dr. Rita S. Peshkov, Cascade’s Vice President of R&D and the demand’s designated key expert witness for the Claimant:  
          Title:         “    Optimization of Phase-Change Heat Exchangers in High-Load Industrial Applications    ”  
          Journal:             Journal of Advanced Thermal Engineering    , Vol. 42, Issue 3 (September 2022)  
          Authors: Alan R. Fairhaven, Ph.D.; Rita S. Peshkov, Ph.D.  
      The paper’s subject — optimization of phase-change heat exchangers in high-load industrial applications — is directly on point with the ThermalCore technology at the center of this arbitration (described in the demand as a phase-change heat-exchange system for heavy industrial environments). Co-authorship of a peer-reviewed article in a specialized field ordinarily reflects a sustained professional collaboration (joint research design, data collection, drafting, peer-review response) extending over months, with a relationship that may continue beyond publication.  
          What the disclosure said (Section V).         The arbitrator identifies her husband and his former University of Minnesota faculty position, then states that he         “    does not currently serve in any consulting or advisory capacity to any party,    ”         is         “    not currently engaged in any active research collaborations with industrial partners,    ”         and,         “    [t]o the best of my knowledge, he has no financial or professional relationship with Cascade Thermal Systems Inc., Whitmore Capital Partners LLC, or any of their officers, directors, or employees.    ”         The disclosure does not mention Dr. Peshkov, does not mention the co-authored paper, and does not address any connection between the spouse and any witness.  
          Obligation implicated.         R-17(a)(ii) squarely requires disclosure of         “    professional … relationships with any party … its counsel, representatives,             or witnesses    ,    ”         expressly including         “        academic collaboration    .    ”         R-17(a)(iv) (catch-all) is also implicated. The AAA/ABA Code Canon II imposes a corresponding disclosure duty. Under the IBA Guidelines, the arbitrator’s family members’ relationships with witnesses fall within the General Standard 3 duty to disclose facts that might give rise to doubts.  
          Why it matters.         This is one of the strongest grounds in the record. Three features compound it. First, the relationship is with a             witness         — and not a peripheral witness but the Claimant’s designated             key expert witness         on the central technical issue (the nature, uniqueness, and value of the ThermalCore technology). Second, the collaboration is on the             exact subject matter         of the dispute (phase-change heat exchangers for high-load industrial use), so the spouse’s expertise and the witness’s expertise overlap precisely with the technical questions the arbitrator must decide. Third, the arbitrator was on notice: the demand (which she says she reviewed) names Dr. Peshkov as a key expert witness, and the arbitrator’s own husband is a retired mechanical engineering professor in the same field. A diligent arbitrator in those circumstances should have inquired whether her spouse had any professional relationship with the identified witness. The disclosure’s affirmative statement that the spouse         “    has no … professional relationship with Cascade … or any of their officers, directors, or employees    ”         is, if the co-authorship is accurate,             inaccurate         (Dr. Peshkov is a Cascade employee), or at minimum reflects a failure of the         “    diligent inquiry    ”         the arbitrator certifies she performed. The         “    to the best of my knowledge    ”         qualifier does not cure a failure to inquire into an obvious and checkable connection.  
          Severity: HIGH.             Favors a challenge by the Respondent         (it suggests potential favorability toward the Claimant’s key expert), though it is also relevant to the Claimant to the extent it bears on the arbitrator’s ability to evaluate Dr. Peshkov’s testimony independently of her husband’s collaboration.  
          Recommendation.         This issue likely supports a successful challenge on its own. The challenging party should (a) verify the co-authorship via the cited journal citation, (b) demand that the arbitrator supplement her disclosure to address the spouse–Peshkov relationship and the scope/timing of the collaboration, and (c) if the co-authorship is confirmed, seek the arbitrator’s withdrawal or AAA disqualification under R-18(d). The relationship between the arbitrator’s spouse and a central expert witness on the precise subject matter of the dispute creates justifiable doubt that supplementation alone may not cure.  
        Issue 2 — Undisclosed potential indirect financial interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (via the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund)  
          The fact (source: relationship memorandum §§III.4, 4.3).         The arbitrator’s personal investment portfolio is managed on a discretionary basis by Northbridge Wealth Advisors. Northbridge also manages a fund-of-funds product, the             Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund    , which per its most recent public filing (March 2024) holds a             2.3% limited partner interest in Whitmore Fund II LP    . Whitmore Fund II LP owns Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC, the direct beneficiary of the challenged below-market license. The economic outcome of this arbitration — any award rescinding, renegotiating, repricing, or imposing damages with respect to the Volaris license — could affect the value of Whitmore Fund II LP’s portfolio and, by extension, the value of the LP interest held by the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund. The chain of potential indirect interest is:  
      Arbitrator’s portfolio (managed by Northbridge Wealth Advisors) → possible allocation to the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund (managed by the same firm) → 2.3% LP interest in Whitmore Fund II LP → owns Volaris → beneficiary of the disputed $2.8M/yr license (FMV $9.5M/yr).  
          What the disclosure said (Section IV).         The arbitrator states she holds         “    no direct equity positions in any party to this arbitration,    ”         that she has         “    not undertaken a position-by-position review of each fund or sub-investment held within my managed accounts,    ”         that she relies on Northbridge to manage the portfolio within her investment policy statement, and that she is         “    not aware of any … investment that would constitute a material financial interest in the outcome of this proceeding.    ”  
          Obligation implicated.         R-17(a)(i) requires disclosure of         “    any known             direct or indirect         financial or personal interest in the outcome … including any interest, whether legal or beneficial, in the subject matter of the dispute, in any party,             or in any entity that may be affected by the result of the proceeding    .    ”         Whitmore Fund II LP is an         “    entity that may be affected by the result    ”         — it owns Volaris, the beneficiary of the license whose rescission/repricing is the core remedy sought. FAA § 10(a)(2) (evident partiality) is implicated to the extent an indirect financial interest is confirmed.  
          Why it matters.         The disclosure’s         “    no direct equity positions    ”         framing is technically accurate but does not address the             indirect         interest that R-17(a)(i) expressly reaches. Critically, the arbitrator admits she has not reviewed the underlying holdings of her managed accounts, so she cannot in fact certify the absence of an indirect interest — she can only say she is         “    not aware    ”         of one. Where her own investment adviser manages a fund-of-funds that holds a direct LP interest in a fund affiliated with the Respondent and directly affected by the remedy, the         “    not aware    ”         statement is not a disclosure; it is an acknowledgment of an unexamined risk. The interest, if held, is aligned with the             Respondent         (a Claimant victory that rescinds or reprices the license reduces Volaris’s benefit and, derivatively, Whitmore Fund II LP’s portfolio value).  
          Important caveat.         The public record does             not         confirm that the arbitrator’s personal portfolio includes an allocation to the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund. The interest is therefore             potential         rather than established. The point nonetheless stands as a disclosure deficiency: the arbitrator should have made inquiry of Northbridge (or disclosed the limits of her knowledge and the specific exposure pathway) rather than relying on a         “    no direct equity    ”         formulation that sidesteps the indirect-interest obligation.  
          Severity: MEDIUM-HIGH as a disclosure deficiency (potentially HIGH if the allocation is confirmed).             Favors a challenge by the Claimant.  
          Recommendation.         Demand that the arbitrator make inquiry of Northbridge Wealth Advisors and supplement her disclosure to state whether her portfolio (or any pooled vehicle in which she participates) holds the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund or any other vehicle with exposure to Whitmore Fund II LP, Whitmore Fund III LP, or any Whitmore-managed fund or portfolio company. If any such exposure is confirmed, disqualification is warranted under R-17(a)(i) and R-18. Even if no exposure is confirmed, the arbitrator’s initial framing was inadequate and the supplementation should be on the record.  
        Issue 3 — Undisclosed CLE co-panelist relationship with Dominic Hargrave (Respondent’s lead counsel) on a directly germane topic  
          The fact (source: relationship memorandum §§III.6, 4.1).         On October 14, 2023, the Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center hosted a CLE panel titled             “        Arbitrating Complex IP Licensing Disputes,        ”         at Lakeview’s offices (750 Nicollet Mall, Suite 3200, Minneapolis). The listed panelists were Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.);             Dominic Hargrave         (lead partner for Whitmore/Respondent in this arbitration); Professor Naomi J. Strickland (University of Minnesota Law School); and Marcus DelVecchio (Henning & Burwell P.A.). The panel addressed valuation methodologies, arbitrator selection in technology-intensive cases, and the management of expert testimony — topics directly germane to this arbitration. The event occurred roughly five months before the demand was filed and eight months before the arbitrator’s appointment was confirmed.  
          What the disclosure said.         The disclosure statement does not mention the CLE, does not identify the arbitrator and Mr. Hargrave as co-panelists, and does not otherwise disclose any professional relationship with Mr. Hargrave beyond the arbitration matters in which Barrow & Thistlewood has appeared as counsel.  
          Obligation implicated.         R-17(a)(ii) (professional relationships with counsel, including         “    professional association    ”    ); R-17(a)(iv) (the catch-all expressly reaches         “    public statements, or expressed views on legal or factual issues relevant to the dispute    ”         — a CLE panel on arbitrating IP licensing disputes qualifies). IBA Orange List items 3.3.5/3.3.6 (shared professional-association activity) are interpretively relevant. AAA/ABA Code Canon II.  
          Why it matters.         Co-paneling a single CLE is not per se disqualifying, and the arbitrator did disclose her repeat arbitration appointments with Barrow & Thistlewood. But four aggravating features make this non-disclosure material: (a) the co-panelist was the             lead counsel for the Respondent specifically         (not merely a member of the firm); (b) the topic was             directly germane         to this arbitration (IP licensing disputes, valuation, expert testimony); (c) the event was hosted at             Lakeview    , the arbitrator’s own affiliated institution, tying the CLE to her institutional affiliation (Issue 8); and (d) the arbitrator disclosed other, less direct professional relationships (e.g., Ellen Wu’s advisory-board service) but omitted this one. The combination creates an appearance of professional familiarity with the Respondent’s lead counsel on the very subject matter of the dispute.  
          Severity: MEDIUM-HIGH.             Favors a challenge by the Claimant.  
          Recommendation.         Disclosable and should have been disclosed. Standing alone it may not mandate disqualification, but combined with the Lakeview affiliation (Issue 8) and the repeat-player pattern (Issue 7) it meaningfully supports a challenge. Demand supplementation; the arbitrator should disclose the event, the co-panelists, the topic, and any substantive interaction with Mr. Hargrave.  
        Issue 4 — Undisclosed shared organizational service with Garrett T. Whitmore (Respondent’s principal and anticipated witness) at the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute  
          The fact (source: relationship memorandum §§1.1, III.3, 4.2).         Per IRS Form 990 filings for the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute (fiscal year 2023),             Garrett T. Whitmore         — Whitmore Capital Partners’ founder and Managing Partner, and an anticipated adverse fact witness in this arbitration — serves on the             Board of Trustees         of the Institute. The Board of Trustees is the Institute’s governing body, responsible for strategic direction, budgetary oversight, and appointments to leadership positions, including the advisory board. The arbitrator serves on the             advisory board         (since 2021). The Institute is described as a small nonprofit; its bylaws and posted minutes indicate that both bodies attend annual meetings and hold periodic joint sessions, increasing the likelihood of direct interaction between the arbitrator and Mr. Whitmore.  
          What the disclosure said (Section VI).         The arbitrator disclosed her own advisory-board service and disclosed that             Ellen Wu         (Barrow & Thistlewood) also serves on the advisory board. The disclosure does             not         mention Garrett T. Whitmore’s service on the Board of Trustees, and does not address whether any party or party principal serves the Institute in any capacity.  
          Obligation implicated.         R-17(a)(ii) (relationships with parties, representatives,             or witnesses    , including         “    board memberships    ”    ); R-17(a)(iv). IBA Orange List 3.3.6 (shared board/professional-association membership). AAA/ABA Code Canon II.  
          Why it matters.         Three features elevate this above ordinary shared-board service. First, Mr. Whitmore is not merely a party principal; he is also an             anticipated witness         (the demand identifies him as an adverse fact witness on the central conduct allegations). Second, the relationship is             hierarchical    , not merely peer service: the Board of Trustees governs the Institute and appoints/oversees the advisory board on which the arbitrator sits, so the Respondent’s principal sits on the body that appoints and can remove the arbitrator from her advisory-board role. Third, the disclosure is             asymmetric    : the arbitrator disclosed the less significant connection (Ellen Wu, a Barrow & Thistlewood partner who is not lead counsel and who serves on the same advisory board as the arbitrator) while omitting the more significant one (the Respondent’s principal and witness, on the governing board). That asymmetry itself creates an appearance of selective disclosure.  
          Severity: MEDIUM-HIGH.             Favors a challenge by the Claimant.  
          Recommendation.         Disclosable and should have been disclosed. Demand supplementation identifying all parties, counsel, witnesses, and their principals who serve the Institute in any capacity, and describing the governance relationship between the Board of Trustees and the advisory board and any interaction between the arbitrator and Mr. Whitmore in that context.  
        Issue 5 — Omission of a pending arbitration (        Halcyon Biotech v. Stonewall Partners    ) in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP appears as counsel  
          The fact (source: relationship memorandum §III.5; appointment-data workbook,                 “        Pending Matters        ”                 tab).         The arbitrator is currently serving as sole arbitrator in             Halcyon Biotech v. Stonewall Partners    , AAA Case No. 01-24-0003105, in which Barrow & Thistlewood LLP represents a party (respondent; claimant’s counsel Kirkridge Poole LLP). The arbitrator was appointed April 22, 2024; the matter is in the pre-hearing phase; estimated remaining fees are $175,000. This matter does not appear in the disclosure statement.

The disclosure lists seven prior/pending matters involving the parties’ counsel, of which two are pending matters involving Barrow & Thistlewood: the instant case and             Orion Fabrication v. Mountain West Supply Co.         (item 6). The arbitrator certifies that         “    the foregoing represents a complete list of arbitrations in which I have served alongside counsel from either Barrow & Thistlewood LLP or Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP.    ”  
          Obligation implicated.         R-17(a)(iii) (prior or pending cases with counsel, in any capacity, before any institution). The arbitrator’s own certification of completeness. AAA/ABA Code Canon II.  
          Why it matters.         This is a direct, unambiguous disclosure failure. The arbitrator has a             third concurrent pending matter         with the Respondent’s counsel — one worth approximately $175,000 in remaining fees — that she did not disclose, despite certifying her list complete. The omission is material in two respects: (a) it understates the extent of the arbitrator’s ongoing concurrent engagement with Barrow & Thistlewood, which bears directly on the economic-dependence/structural-bias concern (Issue 7); and (b) it falsifies the completeness certification on which the parties are entitled to rely in deciding whether to object. Whether the omission was inadvertent or deliberate, the result is the same: the parties were deprived of information they were entitled to receive within the objection window.  
          Severity: HIGH (as a disclosure violation).             Favors a challenge by the Claimant.  
          Recommendation.         A clear basis to challenge. The challenging party should (a) cite the specific case number and appointment date, (b) note the contradiction with the completeness certification, and (c) request that the AAA direct a complete, verified supplemental list of all pending matters involving either firm. The omission itself is grounds to question the arbitrator’s diligence and the reliability of her disclosure.

Whether the omissions reflect inadequate record-keeping or selective disclosure, they violate R-17(a)(iii) and the certification, and they deprived the parties of data relevant to evaluating repeat-player concerns within the objection window.

        Issue 7 — Repeat-player concentration and economic dependence on Barrow & Thistlewood LLP (aggregate/structural)  
          The facts (source: appointment-data workbook                 “        Case List,        ”                 “        Fee Summary,        ”                 and                 “        Pending Matters        ”                 tabs; relationship memorandum §III.5).         Over the arbitrator’s last 20 completed arbitrations (2021–2024):  
          Barrow & Thistlewood appeared as counsel in             9 of 20         completed matters (        45%    ).  
          Fielding Marsh appeared as counsel in             5 of 20         completed matters (        25%    ) — one of which also involved Barrow & Thistlewood.  
          The Barrow & Thistlewood client prevailed in             7 of 9         matters, a             77.8%         prevailing rate (per the workbook’s summary tab and the relationship memorandum).  
          Estimated aggregate fees from Barrow & Thistlewood matters:              $1,020,000           , stated to be                    48.0%         of the arbitrator’s total estimated arbitration income over the period.  
          Estimated aggregate fees from Fielding Marsh matters:              $410,000** (        19.3% of total); the Fielding Marsh client prevailed in             1 of 4** adjudicated matters (        25.0%    ), with one matter settled.  
      On the pending docket (5 matters), Barrow & Thistlewood appears in             3 of 5         pending matters (the instant case,             Orion Fabrication    , and the undisclosed             Halcyon Biotech    ), accounting for             $560,000 of $835,000         in estimated remaining fees —             67.1%         of the arbitrator’s pending fee base. The arbitrator was ranked             first         by Whitmore (the Respondent) in the selection process and third by Cascade.  
          Data-integrity note (discrepancies flagged).         Two figures in the source data do not fully foot and should be verified before being relied upon in a challenge filing:  
              B&T prevailing rate.         The workbook’s summary tab and the relationship memorandum both report 7 of 9 (77.8%). A row-by-row re-derivation from the workbook’s own case-list tab, however, yields             8 of 9 (88.9%)    : the case-list tab shows the Barrow & Thistlewood client prevailing in eight matters (NorthPoint, Meridian, Braddock, Crestfield, Ashford, Westbrook, Redstone, Warwick) and losing in one (Trident). The case-list tab thus appears internally inconsistent with its own summary on this point. Either figure reflects a pronounced asymmetry well above 50%; the precise rate should be confirmed.  
              Total income denominator.         The workbook’s         “    Fee Summary    ”         reports a total of $2,125,000 across 20 matters, but the sum of the 20 line-item fees is             $1,974,000         (a $151,000 difference). The relationship memorandum describes the $2,125,000 figure as covering         “    all 20 completed matters plus estimated income from pending matters,    ”         which would explain the gap, but the workbook labels the $2,125,000 as the 20-matter total. Using the line-item sum, Barrow & Thistlewood’s share is ~51.7% rather than 48.0%. The B&T fee total itself ($1,020,000) does foot against the line items.  
          Obligation implicated.         R-17(a)(ii) and (a)(iv) (relationships and circumstances creating an appearance of partiality); IBA Orange List 3.3.3 (disclosure where an arbitrator has received more than three appointments from the same counsel/firm within three years — here far exceeded, at nine); FAA § 10(a)(2) (evident partiality) as a post-award backstop.  
          Why it matters.         Repeat appointments, by themselves, are common in the arbitration market and are generally disclosable but not disqualifying (IBA Orange List). What is unusual here is the             concentration and direction    : a single firm — counsel for the Respondent — accounts for roughly half of the arbitrator’s recent income and two-thirds of her pending fee base, its clients prevail at a rate of roughly 78–89%, and the Respondent itself ranked the arbitrator first. That pattern creates an economic-dependence concern: the arbitrator has a continuing financial interest in maintaining the Barrow & Thistlewood referral relationship, and that interest is aligned with the Respondent’s side. Standing alone this is a moderate ground; as an aggravator of Issues 3, 4, 5, 6, and 8 it is powerful, because it supplies the economic motive that makes those undisclosed relationships matter.  
          Severity: MEDIUM as a standalone ground; HIGH as an aggravator.             Favors a challenge by the Claimant.  
          Recommendation.         Disclose and argue in aggregate. The challenging party should present the concentration data (with the discrepancies noted) and tie it to the specific undisclosed relationships (CLE with lead counsel; Whitmore’s trustee role; the omitted pending matter; the indirect fund interest). The IBA Orange List 3.3.3 threshold is exceeded many times over and should have been disclosed in terms of the concentration, not merely the individual appointments.

The public record does not indicate whether the affiliation is employment, independent contractor, or equity/member-owner, and the financial terms (whether Lakeview takes a percentage of the arbitrator’s fees for referred matters, receives separate administrative fees, or depends on maintaining referral relationships with law firms) are not publicly available. Lakeview also hosted the October 14, 2023 CLE at which the arbitrator and Mr. Hargrave co-paneled (Issue 3).

The arbitrator disclosed the affiliation, identified the Managing Director, stated that Lakeview receives case referrals from both Barrow & Thistlewood and Fielding Marsh, noted that this case was administered by the AAA (not Lakeview), and stated her view that the referrals do not create a conflict.
