            The three most urgent priorities, each of which is detailed in the body of this memorandum, are: (1) immediate registration with the Massachusetts and Colorado paid leave programs; (2) a structural decision on the self-funded paid family leave plan and the paid medical leave gap; and (3) revision of the paid sick leave policy to cure the deficiencies at issue in the Muñoz complaint and to satisfy the requirements of all seven states before the revised Handbook target date of July 1, 2025.  
                II.  Scope, Methodology, and Limitations  
            Scope. This engagement encompasses a compliance review of Vantage’s paid leave policies and practices across the seven jurisdictions identified in the engagement letter, covering paid sick leave, paid family leave, paid medical leave, employer obligations (registration, contributions, withholding, recordkeeping, posting), and PTO/vacation payout on separation. The review addresses both current-state gaps (NJ, NY, CT) and prospective gaps for the four expansion states (MA, CO, OR, WA).  
            Methodology. We compared the Company’s written policies (Employee Handbook, Rev. January 15, 2022, Section 6: Leave Policies) and documented practices against the statutes, regulations, and agency guidance of each jurisdiction. We also reviewed the PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. payroll withholding summary (data as of March 31, 2025), the HR expansion plan memorandum (March 28, 2025), the internal HR email thread (March 10–17, 2025), the NJ DOL complaint correspondence (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117), and a representative sample of the Company’s leave-tracking spreadsheets. Where practice diverges from written policy, we have assessed the practice as well.  
         New Jersey FLI/TDI benefit parameters (duration, maximum weekly benefit) are not detailed in the PinnacleHR withholding report, which addresses NJ contribution rates; those parameters are drawn from the applicable statutes (N.J.S.A. 43:21-25 et seq.) per the engagement's legal-research scope and should be confirmed against current NJDOL guidance.  
            Limitations. Our analysis is based on the laws, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of the date of this memorandum and on the accuracy and completeness of the information and representations provided by the Company. State paid leave law is a rapidly evolving area; legislative or regulatory changes may occur after delivery. This memorandum (i) is outside scope with respect to representation in the pending NJ DOL complaint, which continues under the separate engagement dated February 15, 2025; (ii) does not constitute a federal FMLA compliance review; (iii) does not address local or municipal paid leave ordinances; and (iv) does not include drafting of the revised Handbook, which would be a separate engagement. This memorandum is a compliance diagnostic and does not constitute legal advice as to any individual employee claim.  
                III.  Current-State Gap Analysis (New Jersey, New York, Connecticut)  
             A.  Paid Sick Leave — Cross-Cutting Deficiencies (NJ, NY, CT)  
            The Company’s paid sick leave policy (Handbook §6.2) is non-compliant in all three current states on multiple dimensions. These deficiencies are not state-specific drafting errors; they reflect a policy designed to a single, simplified standard that falls below the requirements of every jurisdiction in which the Company operates.  
              Accrual rate.           The policy accrues sick leave at one hour per 40 hours worked. New Jersey and New York require one hour per 30 hours worked; Connecticut requires one hour per 40 hours (compliant on rate only). The under-accrual is systemic and quantifiable; the leave-tracking sample confirms it (e.g., the complainant in the Muñoz matter accrued 13.0 hours on 520 hours worked, a 1/40 rate).  
              Annual cap.           The policy caps full-time employees at 40 hours and part-time employees at 24 hours per year. New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut each require 40 hours (New York requires 56 hours for employers with 100 or more employees, a threshold Vantage exceeds with 680 New York employees) and none permits a reduced part-time cap. The part-time cap directly underpays part-time employees and is confirmed in the tracking sample.  
              Waiting period.           The policy imposes a uniform 120-day waiting period before accrued sick leave may be used. New Jersey permits a 120-day waiting period (compliant). New York permits no waiting period—employees may use leave as it accrues—so the 120-day rule is non-compliant there. Connecticut’s eligibility is hours-based rather than a flat calendar waiting period, making the 120-day rule at risk. The tracking sample shows employees denied use during the waiting period in both New York and New Jersey.  
              Carryover.           The policy provides no carryover of unused sick leave (balances reset to zero on January 1) and no year-end payout. All three states require carryover (New Jersey permits carryover of up to 40 hours or, alternatively, year-end payout plus front-loading). The tracking sample confirms year-end forfeitures (e.g., 12 hours and 8 hours forfeited for New Jersey employees).  
              Covered uses.           The policy limits permitted uses to the employee’s own illness, injury, or medical appointment. All three states additionally require leave for care of a family member, absences related to domestic or sexual violence (“safe leave”), public-health-emergency and school/place-of-care closures, and (in New Jersey) school-related meetings and functions. The omission of these uses is a central allegation in the Muñoz complaint.  
            Bereavement (as a sick leave use).        Bereavement is not a required paid sick leave use in any of the seven jurisdictions, so the policy's omission of bereavement from the sick leave policy is not a sick-leave compliance gap.  
              Family-member definition.           The policy defines “family member” as spouse, child, or parent only. Each state’s definition is materially broader. New Jersey’s definition (N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1) expressly includes a child of a domestic partner, domestic and civil-union partners, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, any blood relative, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship. The narrow definition is the direct cause of the denial at issue in the Muñoz complaint and is non-compliant in all three states.  
              Documentation threshold.           The policy requires a doctor’s note after two consecutive scheduled workdays. Each state permits documentation to be required only after three or more consecutive days (or, in New York, on reasonable suspicion of abuse). The two-day threshold is more stringent than the statutes permit and creates a denial risk if applied rigidly.  
              Posting and notice.           The Handbook is silent on the state-specific workplace postings and individual hire notices that each state requires. We could not confirm that the required postings are displayed at the Company’s facilities. Posting violations are independently citable.  
             B.  New Jersey — Specific Considerations  
            The New Jersey deficiencies are compounded by the pending NJ DOL complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117). The complaint alleges that the Company denied Carla Muñoz, a warehouse associate at the Edison facility, the right to use accrued earned sick leave to care for her domestic partner’s child—a covered family member under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1—and that the policy’s enumeration of covered uses omits categories required by N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3. The Company’s February 21, 2025 response acknowledged that the policy language “raises legitimate questions” and committed to a policy review by July 1, 2025. The NJ DOL has reserved the right to conduct a broader investigation of the Company’s sick leave practices, policies, and records. Because the deficiencies identified in the complaint (family-member definition and covered uses) are present in the policy as applied to all New Jersey employees, the exposure is not limited to the individual complainant; a broader investigation could surface the accrual-rate, cap, and carryover deficiencies as well. Penalties are available under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-7.  
             C.  New York — Specific Considerations  
            New York presents the most acute paid sick leave exposure among the current states because the Company exceeds the 100-employee threshold, triggering the 56-hour annual entitlement (versus the policy’s 40-hour cap), and because New York permits no waiting period before use of accrued leave (versus the policy’s 120-day waiting period). The tracking sample shows New York employees denied use during the waiting period and balances forfeited at year-end. The combination of a sub-statutory cap, an impermissible waiting period, and no carryover produces a cumulative shortfall for New York employees that is readily quantifiable from payroll and time records.  
             D.  Connecticut — Specific Considerations  
            Connecticut is the only current state in which the Company’s sick leave accrual rate (1/40) matches the statute. However, the part-time cap (24 hours) is non-compliant (Connecticut applies 40 hours regardless of full-time/part-time status), there is no carryover, and the covered-uses and family-member definitions are narrower than required. Connecticut is also the focal point of the paid medical leave gap discussed in Section V.B below: at least four Bridgeport employees have inquired about paid leave for their own serious health conditions and were directed to the employee-paid voluntary short-term disability plan rather than the Connecticut Paid Leave program they are funding through payroll withholdings.  
                IV.  Prospective Gap Analysis (Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, Washington)  
            The four expansion states each impose paid sick leave and paid family/medical leave requirements that the current Handbook does not address. In addition, the Company has not registered with the paid leave insurance program in any of the four states, and no payroll withholding has been configured. The following summarizes the principal prospective gaps; the Compliance Matrix contains the dimension-by-dimension comparison.  
             A.  Massachusetts (facility opens August 1, 2025)  
            Massachusetts is on the critical path. The Massachusetts Paid Family and Medical Leave (MA PFML) program requires employer registration and contribution remittance from the first day any covered employee works in the state. PinnacleHR has advised that it cannot configure withholding until it receives the Company’s registration confirmation, and that its configuration lead time is approximately six to eight weeks. To have withholding operational by the August 1, 2025 opening, registration with the Massachusetts Department of Family and Medical Leave (DFML) should be completed no later than approximately June 1, 2025. As of the date of this memorandum, the Company has not registered.  
            Separately, the paid sick leave policy is non-compliant in Massachusetts: the accrual rate (1/40 versus the required 1/30), the part-time cap (24 hours versus 40 hours), the waiting period (120 days versus the 90-day statutory threshold), the absence of carryover, and the narrow covered-uses and family-member definitions all require correction before the opening. Massachusetts also requires payout of accrued unused vacation upon separation under the Wage Act, M.G.L. c. 149 § 148; the Company’s no-payout policy is non-compliant and exposes the Company to treble damages and attorney’s fees. The 5-day PTO carryover cap is high-risk in Massachusetts for the same reason. The revised Handbook (target July 1, 2025) must address all of these items before the first Massachusetts employee is hired.  
             B.  Colorado (facility opens November 1, 2025; 3 remote hires April 2025)  
            Colorado is the most urgent registration item. Three Colorado-based salaried employees are being hired in April 2025 to work remotely in advance of the Aurora facility opening. If the Colorado FAMLI registration obligation is triggered by the first date a Colorado-based employee performs work—a position the Colorado Division of Family and Medical Leave Insurance generally takes—then the Company’s registration may already be overdue, or will be within days. Even under the more favorable interpretation that registration is tied to the November 1, 2025 facility opening, the six-to-eight-week PinnacleHR lead time requires registration no later than approximately mid-August 2025. We recommend immediate registration and a conservative reading of the trigger date.  
            Colorado is also the only expansion state in which the Company cannot self-fund in lieu of state program participation: the FAMLI private-plan opt-out is limited to local-government employers. The Company must register with and participate in CO FAMLI. The paid sick leave policy is non-compliant in Colorado on the accrual rate (1/40 versus 1/30), the cap (40/24 hours versus 48 hours), the waiting period (120 days versus immediate use—Colorado permits no waiting period), the absence of carryover, and the narrow covered-uses and family-member definitions. Colorado also requires supplemental public-health-emergency leave of up to 80 hours under the Healthy Families and Workplaces Act. Finally, Colorado requires payout of all accrued unused vacation upon separation (C.R.S. § 8-4-101 et seq.); the no-payout policy and the 5-day carryover cap are both non-compliant. A Colorado candidate has already raised the payout issue with the Company’s recruiter.  
             C.  Oregon (facility opens February 1, 2026)  
            Oregon provides more runway but remains on the critical path. Registration with Paid Leave Oregon (administered by the Oregon Employment Department) should be completed no later than approximately December 1, 2025 to allow PinnacleHR configuration before the February 1, 2026 opening. Paid Leave Oregon covers family leave, medical leave (the employee’s own serious health condition), and safe leave (domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, and harassment); the Company’s self-funded plan covers none of the medical or safe-leave components and is below the statutory benefit on rate, cap, and duration. A private-plan option is available in Oregon but only if the private plan exceeds Paid Leave Oregon benefits. The paid sick leave policy is non-compliant in Oregon on the accrual rate, the part-time cap, the waiting period (120 days versus 90 days), the absence of carryover, and the covered-uses and family-member definitions. Oregon also requires payout of accrued unused vacation upon separation (ORS 652.140), with penalty wages of up to 30 days available under ORS 652.150 for late or withheld payment; the no-payout policy and the 5-day carryover cap are non-compliant.  
             D.  Washington (facility opens May 1, 2026)  
            Washington is the furthest out but requires the same advance planning. Registration with the Washington Paid Family and Medical Leave program (administered by the Washington Employment Security Department) should be completed no later than approximately March 1, 2026. Washington PFML covers family leave, medical leave (the employee’s own serious health condition), and qualifying military exigency; the Company’s self-funded plan omits the medical and military-exigency components and is below the statutory benefit on rate, cap, and duration. A voluntary-plan option is available but only if the voluntary plan meets or exceeds Washington PFML benefits. The paid sick leave policy is non-compliant in Washington on the accrual cap (Washington prohibits accrual caps, though a use cap may be permissible), the waiting period (120 days versus 90 days), the absence of carryover (Washington requires full year-to-year carryover), and the covered-uses and family-member definitions. The accrual rate (1/40) does match Washington’s requirement. Washington, like New Jersey and New York, does not require payout of unused vacation if a clear written forfeit policy is in place, so the no-payout policy is permissible there subject to consistent application.  
                V.  Structural Assessment: Self-Funded Paid Family Leave Plan and the Paid Medical Leave Gap  
             A.  Self-Funded Paid Family Leave Plan  
            The Company self-funds a paid family leave benefit providing 50% of the employee’s average weekly wage, capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks per qualifying event (Handbook §6.3). The Company does not participate as the contributing employer in any state family leave insurance program; PinnacleHR processes only the mandatory employee-side withholdings in New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut. The Company has not filed a private-plan application in any jurisdiction. Our assessment is that the self-funded plan is below the statutory benefit level in all seven states and does not qualify as a compliant private-plan substitute in any of them.

            The self-funded plan is below the statutory benefit on the replacement rate, the weekly cap, and the duration in every state. In Colorado, a private-plan substitute is not available to private employers at all. In the remaining six states, a private plan is permitted only if it meets or exceeds (in Oregon, exceeds) the state program in all respects; the current plan fails that test in every state, and no application has been filed. The practical consequence is that employees in New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut are paying state program contributions through payroll withholdings while receiving an inferior self-funded benefit—a dual-coverage structure that creates both overpayment/underpayment confusion and a basis for employee complaints. The Muñoz complaint demonstrates that the Company’s employees are willing to escalate to state agencies when they perceive a gap between what they are paying into and what they are receiving.  
            Recommended structural options. The Company should select one of the following approaches, in consultation with H&C, before the July 1, 2025 handbook revision:  
             Option 1 —            participate in the state-administered program in each jurisdiction and treat the self-funded benefit as a supplemental, top-up benefit (or discontinue it to avoid employee confusion and dual-coverage issues);  
             Option 2 —            file private-plan (or voluntary-plan) approvals in the six states that permit them (NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR, WA), with enhanced benefits that meet or exceed each state’s statutory minimum, and participate in CO FAMLI (no opt-out available); or  
             Option 3 —            discontinue the self-funded plan and rely entirely on the state programs, redirecting employees to file claims through the state programs (with the Company administering job protection and any supplemental top-up).  
            H&C’s preliminary recommendation is Option 1 or Option 3. A private-plan approval (Option 2) is administratively burdensome across six states, requires the Company to assume the insurance risk, and must be re-approved as benefits change; it is generally attractive only where an employer can offer benefits meaningfully above the state floor at lower cost, which is not the case here. Whichever option is selected, the Company must register with and begin remitting contributions to the four expansion-state programs regardless, because registration is a prerequisite to either participation or a private-plan filing.  
             B.  Paid Medical Leave and the Voluntary STD Misdirection  
            The Company provides no employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee’s own serious health condition beyond accrued sick leave (capped at 40 hours) and PTO (Handbook §6.4). The voluntary short-term disability plan through Keystone Benefit Administrators is employee-paid and optional; fewer than 30% of Connecticut hourly employees are enrolled. Every state in which the Company operates or will operate provides a state-mandated benefit for the employee’s own serious health condition: New Jersey TDI (up to 26 weeks), New York DBL (up to 26 weeks), Connecticut Paid Leave (up to 12 weeks), and the MA PFML, CO FAMLI, OR PLO, and WA PFML medical-leave components.  
            The more serious concern is operational. The internal email thread (March 10–17, 2025) establishes that HR staff in Connecticut and New Jersey have been directing employees who inquire about paid leave for their own conditions to the employee-paid voluntary STD plan rather than to the state programs (NJ TDI, NY DBL, CT PL) that those employees are funding through payroll withholdings. At least seven such inquiries have been identified across the current footprint (four in Bridgeport, two in Edison, one in Queens). This pattern of misdirection creates two distinct exposures: (i) employees may have retroactive claims for state benefits they were discouraged from filing; and (ii) the pattern itself is evidence of a systemic administration failure that a state agency investigating a complaint (such as the Muñoz matter) could expand to examine. The General Counsel’s March 17, 2025 interim guidance—directing HR staff not to turn away employees who ask about paid medical leave for their own conditions without checking whether a state program applies, and to escalate uncertain cases—is an appropriate stopgap but must be formalized in the revised Handbook and reinforced with training.  
                VI.  Operational Risk Assessment: Manual Leave Tracking  
            The Company tracks sick leave accruals, usage, and balances through manual Microsoft Excel workbooks maintained by two HR coordinators, one workbook per state. Paid family leave is tracked through a separate paper-based process; PTO is tracked within WorkBridge Pro but is not connected to the sick leave spreadsheets. The spreadsheets are not integrated with WorkBridge Pro (HRIS) or ClockSync Enterprise (time and attendance). This approach has been adequate for three states but presents material compliance and recordkeeping risk as the Company scales to seven states and approximately 2,560 employees.  
            Specific deficiencies identified in the HR expansion plan memorandum and confirmed by the tracking sample include:  
              No state-specific rule differentiation.            The spreadsheets apply a single company-wide policy and do not differentiate state-specific accrual rates, caps, waiting periods, or carryover rules. As documented above, the states differ significantly on each of these dimensions.  
              No automated alerts.            There are no automated notifications for accrual caps, waiting-period eligibility, or carryover deadlines. Coordinators must manually review each employee record. At 2,560 employees this is not sustainable.  
              No audit trail.            Changes to the spreadsheets are not logged. There is no record of original entries, correction dates, or reasons for adjustments. In an audit or complaint proceeding, the Company could not demonstrate how a particular balance was determined.  
              No system integration.            Hours-worked data is manually extracted from ClockSync and re-entered; demographic data is manually cross-referenced against WorkBridge Pro. This creates transcription-error risk and data inconsistency between systems.  
              Limited scope.            The spreadsheets track only earned sick leave. Paid family leave, PTO, and state program claims are tracked elsewhere or not at all. There is no single view of an employee’s total leave entitlements and usage.  
            Recent errors. Internal audits have identified a part-time New Jersey employee whose sick leave balance was incorrectly capped at 24 hours due to application of the wrong accrual rate; a full-time New York employee who was told he could not use accrued sick leave until 120 days after hire (a waiting period whose legality HR could not confirm); and negative balances in the tracking sample (a New Jersey employee at –2.5 hours and a New York employee at –0.75 hours), indicating data-entry or calculation errors. Several states impose specific recordkeeping requirements regarding leave accruals and usage; the manual system’s lack of an audit trail and state-specific logic creates a recordkeeping deficiency independent of the substantive policy gaps.  
            Recommendation. We concur with the Director of Human Resources’ recommendation that the Company invest in a dedicated leave management solution that integrates with WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync Enterprise and can be configured with state-specific rules for all seven jurisdictions. The estimated cost ($40,000–$75,000) falls within the $175,000 compliance remediation budget but would consume a material portion of it; the Company should confirm budget allocation priorities with Finance. The system should be selected by July 1, 2025 and live by Q1 2026 to support the Oregon and Washington openings. In the interim, the manual process must be supplemented with documented state-specific calculation rules, a change log, and a reconciliation procedure against WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync data.  
                VII.  Severity-Ranked Findings  
            The following consolidates the material deficiencies by severity. The Compliance Matrix (Deliverable 1) and the “Gap Summary by Severity” sheet therein contain the complete dimension-by-dimension listing with recommended owners and deadlines.  
             A.  Critical  
             C-1. Massachusetts PFML registration.           The Company has not registered with the MA DFML. With the Worcester facility opening August 1, 2025 and PinnacleHR’s 6–8 week configuration lead time, registration must be completed by approximately June 1, 2025. Any delay cascades into operating in Massachusetts without proper withholding on day one. Estimated annual contributions approximately $68,640.  
             C-2. Colorado FAMLI registration and early remote hires.           The Company has not registered with the CO FAMLI Division. Three Colorado-based employees were hired in April 2025; if registration is triggered by the first Colorado-based hire, registration may already be overdue. Colorado permits no private-employer opt-out, so the Company must participate. Registration no later than approximately September 1, 2025 under the facility-opening interpretation; immediate under the first-hire interpretation. Estimated annual contributions approximately $45,900.  
             C-3. Self-funded paid family leave plan fails equivalency in all seven states; no private-plan application filed.           The plan (50% AWW / $850 cap / 8 weeks) is below the statutory benefit on rate, cap, and duration in every state and does not qualify as a compliant private-plan substitute. Employees in NJ, NY, and CT are paying state contributions while receiving an inferior benefit. A structural decision is required before the July 1, 2025 handbook revision.  
             C-4. No employer-funded paid medical leave; HR misdirection to voluntary STD.           The Company provides no paid medical leave for an employee’s own serious health condition, and HR staff have directed employees to the employee-paid voluntary STD plan instead of the state TDI/DBL/PL programs those employees are funding. At least seven inquiries identified. Creates retroactive-claim and expanded-investigation exposure. Immediate interim guidance in place; structural fix required by July 1, 2025.  
             C-5. PTO/vacation payout non-compliant in MA, CO, and OR.           Massachusetts (Wage Act, treble damages), Colorado, and Oregon each require payout of accrued unused vacation at separation. The no-payout policy and the 5-day carryover cap are non-compliant in these states. Must be corrected before the first MA/CO/OR employees are hired; a Colorado candidate has already flagged the issue.  
             B.  High  
             H-1. Sick leave accrual rate below statutory minimum.           1/40 accrual is below the 1/30 minimum in NJ, NY, MA, CO, and OR. Systemic under-accrual. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-2. Sick leave caps below statutory requirements.           Part-time 24-hour cap non-compliant in NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR (40 hours required regardless of FT/PT); full-time 40-hour cap below NY’s 56 hours and CO’s 48 hours. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-3. 120-day sick leave waiting period exceeds statutory thresholds.           Non-compliant in NY (no waiting period permitted), MA, OR, WA (90 days), and CO (immediate use). Remediate by July 1, 2025 and before each opening.  
             H-4. No sick leave carryover.           All seven states require carryover (or, in NJ, carryover or year-end payout). Policy provides neither. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-5. Sick leave covered uses omit family care, safe leave, PHE, and school-related uses.           Required by all seven states; central allegation in the Muñoz complaint. CO also requires supplemental PHE leave up to 80 hours. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-6. Sick leave family-member definition narrower than all seven states.           Direct cause of the Muñoz denial. Must be expanded to each state’s statutory definition. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-7. Paid family leave qualifying reasons omit military exigency and safe leave.           Six states cover military exigency; three cover safe leave. Policy covers only bonding and caregiving. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-8. Paid family leave eligibility more restrictive than state programs.           12-month/1,000-hour test may exclude employees the state programs cover. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-9. Paid family leave notice requirement does not address unforeseeable leave.           30-day flat notice creates denial risk; HR has inconsistently applied interim guidance, creating discrimination exposure. Remediate by July 1, 2025.  
             H-10. Oregon and Washington PFML registration.           OR by approximately December 1, 2025; WA by approximately March 1, 2026. Less urgent than MA/CO but on the critical path given PinnacleHR lead time.  
             C.  Moderate  
             M-1. Sick leave documentation threshold more stringent than statute.           2-day threshold versus 3-day statutory threshold in all seven states. Align to 3-day threshold by July 1, 2025.  
             M-2. State-specific postings and individual notices not verified/implemented.           Posting violations are independently citable. Verify current states immediately; implement expansion states before each opening.  
             M-3. Manual leave-tracking system does not scale to seven states.           No state-specific logic, alerts, audit trail, or integration. Recent errors confirmed. Select system by July 1, 2025; live by Q1 2026.  
             M-4. Connecticut PTO carryover cap at risk.           5-day cap with year-end forfeiture may forfeit “earned” wages in CT. Review by July 1, 2025.  
             M-5. HRIS and time-and-attendance not configured for expansion states.           WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync require configuration/deployment per state, sequenced to openings. Initiate MA procurement immediately.  
             M-6. Connecticut sick leave waiting-period interaction requires verification.           CT PSL eligibility is hours-based; the 120-day calendar rule may be more or less restrictive. Verify against current CT PSL regulations by July 1, 2025.  
                VIII.  Prioritized Remediation Plan  
            The following plan sequences remediation to the Company’s facility opening schedule and the July 1, 2025 revised Handbook target. Owners are recommended; deadlines are calibrated to the opening dates and PinnacleHR’s 6–8 week configuration lead time.  
                                      Deadline  
                         Action Item  
                         Owner  
                         Linked To  
                          IMMEDIATE (May 2025)  
                        Register with MA DFML; provide registration confirmation to PinnacleHR to begin 6–8 week withholding configuration.  
                        HR (Delgado) + Legal; PinnacleHR (Samuelson)  
                        MA opening 8/1/25 (C-1)  
                          IMMEDIATE (May 2025)  
                        Clarify CO FAMLI registration trigger date for the 3 April 2025 remote hires; register with CO FAMLI Division on a conservative (first-hire) reading.  
                        Legal (Thorsen) + HR  
                        CO remote hires (C-2)  
                          IMMEDIATE (May 2025)  
                        Issue written interim guidance to all HR staff: (i) do not deny/delay leave for unforeseeable situations based on the 30-day notice rule; (ii) do not turn away employees who ask about paid medical leave for their own conditions without checking whether a state program applies; escalate uncertain cases.  
                        HR (Delgado)  
                        C-4, H-9  
                          By June 1, 2025  
                        Complete MA PFML registration (hard deadline to allow PinnacleHR configuration before 8/1/25 opening).  
                        HR + Legal  
                        MA opening (C-1)  
                          By July 1, 2025  
                        Publish revised Employee Handbook with multi-state leave policies for all seven jurisdictions: corrected sick leave (accrual 1/30 where required, 40/48/56-hr caps, 90-day or no waiting period, carryover, expanded covered uses and family-member definitions, 3-day documentation threshold); revised PFL (qualifying reasons incl. military exigency/safe leave, foreseeable/unforeseeable notice, eligibility aligned to state programs); PTO payout policy for MA/CO/OR; liberalized carryover cap where required.  
                        HR (Delgado) + Legal (Ng/Ikeda)  
                        Handbook target; C-3, C-5, H-1–H-9, M-1, M-4, M-6  
                          By July 1, 2025  
                        Make and document the structural decision on the self-funded PFL plan (participate in state programs / file private plans / discontinue) and the paid medical leave approach.  
                        Legal (Ng) + GC (Thorsen) + HR  
                        C-3, C-4  
                          By July 1, 2025  
                        Issue RFP / select dedicated leave management software; define state-specific rule configuration for all seven states.  
                        HR (Delgado) + IT  
                        M-3  
                          By July 1, 2025  
                        Verify and document state-specific workplace postings and individual hire notices in NJ, NY, CT; procure postings for MA, CO, OR, WA.  
                        HR  
                        M-2  
                          By Aug. 1, 2025  
                        MA facility go-live: MA PFML withholding operational; MA sick leave, PTO payout, and posting policies in effect; WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync configured for MA.  
                        HR + IT + PinnacleHR  
                        MA opening (C-1, C-5)  
                          By Sept. 1, 2025  
                        Complete CO FAMLI registration (if not already completed for remote hires); ensure PinnacleHR CO configuration complete.  
                        HR + Legal  
                        CO opening 11/1/25 (C-2)  
                          By Nov. 1, 2025  
                        CO facility go-live: CO FAMLI withholding operational; CO sick leave (48 hrs, no waiting period, PHE supplemental leave), PTO payout, and posting policies in effect.  
                        HR + IT + PinnacleHR  
                        CO opening (C-2, C-5)  
                          By Dec. 1, 2025  
                        Complete Paid Leave Oregon registration; provide confirmation to PinnacleHR.  
                        HR + Legal  
                        OR opening 2/1/26 (H-10)  
                          By Dec. 2025–Jan. 2026  
                        Implement dedicated leave management software; configure for all seven states; migrate data; train HR staff.  
                        HR (Delgado) + IT + vendor  
                        M-3; OR/WA openings  
                          By Feb. 1, 2026  
                        OR facility go-live: PLO withholding operational; OR sick leave (40 hrs, 90-day use, carryover, safe leave), PTO payout, and posting policies in effect.  
                        HR + IT + PinnacleHR  
                        OR opening (H-10, C-5)  
                          By March 1, 2026  
                        Complete WA PFML registration; provide confirmation to PinnacleHR.  
                        HR + Legal  
                        WA opening 5/1/26 (H-10)  
                          By May 1, 2026  
                        WA facility go-live: WA PFML withholding operational; WA sick leave (no accrual cap, 90-day use, full carryover), PTO payout, and posting policies in effect.  
                        HR + IT + PinnacleHR  
                        WA opening (H-10, C-5)  
                          Within 30 days of WA opening  
                        Post-launch audit of all systems and compliance processes across all seven states.  
                        HR + Legal  
                        All  
                          Ongoing (2025–2026)  
                        Train all supervisors and HR staff on the revised policies, state-specific family-member definitions, covered uses, unforeseeable-leave notice, and the paid medical leave / state-program claims process.  
                        HR (Delgado)  
                        All; supports NJ DOL response  
       
                IX.  NJ DOL Complaint — Resolution Strategy Framework  
            Representation in the pending NJ DOL complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117, Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.) is outside the scope of this engagement and continues under the separate engagement dated February 15, 2025. To the extent the compliance review findings inform the Company’s response posture in that proceeding, we offer the following framework for the Company’s consideration with the litigation team:  
              Remediation as good faith.            The Company’s February 21, 2025 response acknowledged that the policy language “raises legitimate questions” and committed to a policy review by July 1, 2025. The compliance review confirms that the family-member definition and covered-uses deficiencies identified in the complaint are present in the policy as applied to all New Jersey employees. A revised policy adopted before the July 1, 2025 target will support the Company’s good-faith remediation posture.  
              Individual make-whole.            The complaint seeks payment for the unpaid time off or restoration of PTO hours used by Ms. Muñoz. Making the complainant whole for the specific incident is a low-cost, high-value step that demonstrates good faith and may support a cooperative resolution with the Department.  
              Anticipate expanded scope.            The Department has reserved the right to conduct a broader investigation. Because the accrual-rate, cap, and carryover deficiencies affect all New Jersey employees, the Company should be prepared for the possibility that the investigation expands beyond the individual complainant. Completing the policy revision and supervisor training before any expanded inquiry materially improves the Company’s position.  
              Cooperative resolution.            The Company should request that the Department allow a reasonable period—through the July 1, 2025 handbook revision—for completion of the review, policy update, and training before any enforcement action, consistent with the position taken in the February 21 response.  
              Record preservation.            The Company should preserve all leave records, payroll records, and communications relevant to the complaint and to the broader sick leave practices, given the Department’s reserved right to investigate and the recordkeeping deficiencies identified in Section VI.  
                X.  Budget Considerations  
            The Company has allocated a compliance remediation budget of $175,000 encompassing legal fees, system updates, and policy redrafting. H&C estimates professional fees for this engagement in the range of $28,000–$42,000. The principal competing budget demand is the dedicated leave management software ($40,000–$75,000) recommended in Section VI. Additional HR headcount (two generalists, estimated $120,000–$140,000 annualized) is recommended by the Director of Human Resources but would be pursued through the normal budgeting process rather than the remediation budget. We recommend that the Company confirm budget allocation priorities with Finance, and consider whether the software investment should be pursued through a separate capital expenditure request so that the remediation budget can be reserved primarily for legal fees and policy work. The registration and policy-revision workstreams are time-critical and should not be delayed by budget deliberation; the software selection can proceed on the July 1, 2025 RFP timeline with implementation funding confirmed thereafter.  
                XI.  Conclusion  
            Vantage’s paid leave policies and practices require substantial remediation before the multi-state expansion proceeds and to cure existing deficiencies in the current three-state footprint. The good news is that the great majority of the deficiencies are curable through a single, comprehensive revision of the Employee Handbook (target July 1, 2025) combined with timely state program registrations and a structural decision on the self-funded plan. The most time-critical items—Massachusetts and Colorado registration—require immediate action independent of the handbook revision. We are available to discuss these findings with you and the HR team at your convenience, to assist in preparing non-privileged communications for PinnacleHR and Keystone Benefit Administrators where appropriate, and to scope any additional engagement the Company may wish to pursue, including assistance with the state program registrations and drafting of the revised Handbook.  
            We will provide an interim status update on or about May 2, 2025. Given the registration urgency, the Company should anticipate that we may provide an early-action notification regarding Massachusetts and Colorado registration requirements in advance of that update.  
            Respectfully submitted,  
    
             HARTWELL & CABOT LLP  
            Catherine Ng, Partner  
            Daniel Ikeda, Senior Associate  
                This memorandum is privileged and confidential, constitutes attorney work product, and is provided for the purpose of obtaining and implementing legal advice. It is a compliance diagnostic based on the laws, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of May 19, 2025, and on the information and representations provided by the Company; it is not legal advice as to any individual claim and does not guarantee any particular outcome. State paid leave law is rapidly evolving and periodic re-review is recommended. Distribution should be limited to authorized Vantage personnel; sharing with third-party vendors should be limited to non-privileged summaries or operational directives.

### FILE: buy-side-cim-analysis-memo.docx
          CONFIDENTIAL — INTERNAL USE ONLY  
        Buy-Side CIM Analysis Memorandum  
       Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. (    “    CES    ”         or the         “    Company    ”    ) — Project Cascade  
                          To:  
          Marcus Yuen, Partner (Deal Lead); Diane Holbrook, Managing Partner  
                From:  
          David Koh, Associate  
                CC:  
          Sarah Lindgren, Vice President  
                Date:  
          June 25, 2025  
                Re:  
          Buy-side analysis of the CES Confidential Information Memorandum and supporting materials — financial inconsistencies, risk factors, valuation, and IOI recommendations  
             Prepared in connection with the Confidential Information Memorandum and financial exhibits distributed by Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC on June 9, 2025. IOI deadline: July 14, 2025. This memorandum is intended solely for internal use by Thornfield Capital Partners investment professionals and incorporates the sector benchmarks developed in Ms. Lindgren’s June 12, 2025 sector memo. It should not be shared with any external party.  
      
        1. Executive Summary  
          Recommendation: Proceed to a disciplined Indication of Interest, but anchor valuation on independently adjusted trailing EBITDA and use a PFAS-linked earnout to bridge the gap between the seller’s growth narrative and a defensible underwriting case.         CES is a genuinely attractive lower-middle-market environmental services platform that fits Fund III’s sector and geographic focus, and the core business (excluding the disputed PFAS ramp) exhibits real, durable growth. However, the CIM and supporting exhibits contain a pattern of financial inconsistencies, aggressive PFAS projections, and EBITDA adjustments that are likely overstated — issues that are consistent with the pattern observed in our GreenWorks Remediation and Allied Waste Systems investments and that materially affect both earnings quality and valuation.  
          Key findings:  
             The financial exhibits do not reconcile to the CIM, and contain internal inconsistencies.         The balance sheet in the financial exhibits shows total assets of $50.0M versus $46.1M in the CIM (a $3.9M variance), equity of $18.8M versus $17.1M, and cash of $4.1M versus $2.3M. The income statement exhibits report operating income of $11.1M that does not tie to the stated Reported EBITDA of $12.1M (a $3.2M internal gap). Segment splits, the customer list, and geographic mix also differ between documents. These are not rounding items; they must be reconciled in QoE before any number in the CIM can be relied upon.  
             The stated DSO of 52 days does not reconcile to the balance sheet.         Year-end accounts receivable of $16.9M against FY2024 revenue of $87.3M implies DSO of approximately 71 days (82 days if unbilled receivables are included) — roughly 19–30 days longer than represented. This suggests working capital is heavier than presented and that the NWC peg and cash-conversion profile need independent verification.  
             PFAS projections are aggressive and underpin the growth story.         The CIM projects PFAS revenue growing 125.8% in FY2025E (to $14.0M) and 57.1% in FY2026E (to $22.0M), with PFAS driving ~64% of incremental revenue through FY2026. Sector data and our GreenWorks experience indicate 30–50% annual growth is the realistic ceiling once a practice exceeds $5M. The August 2024 departure of one of three Professional Engineers (PE headcount now two) creates a near-term capacity bottleneck for the PE-oversight-intensive PFAS work.  
             Maintenance capex is materially understated versus sector norms.         CIM maintenance capex of $3.0M (3.4% of revenue) is 150–200 bps below the 5–7% sector range ($4.4M–$6.1M). Our GreenWorks experience — where pre-close maintenance capex was understated at ~3.5% and ran at 6.2% post-close — is directly on point. A $2.5M annual understatement reduces free cash flow and the implied return.  
             The $1.2M regulatory/legal add-back is likely overstated.         The August 2024 Oregon DEQ enforcement action (improper storage of contaminated soils at CES’s own Portland facility) is presented as fully non-recurring. Sector run-rates of $200K–$500K and our portfolio experience (GreenWorks $275K/yr; Allied $310K/yr) indicate only the amount above a normalized run-rate is defensible — a $300K–$500K haircut, or $2.4M–$5.0M of enterprise value at the guided multiple.  
             Customer concentration is high and the largest contract has already expired.         PNR represents 21.4% of revenue; the top 5 represent 52.5%; the top 10 represent 71.0%. The PNR master service agreement expired March 31, 2025 — before the CIM was distributed — and renewal status is not disclosed. This is the single most important commercial diligence item.  
             Real estate and related-party arrangements require structural resolution.         The Portland HQ is owned personally by the founder/CEO and leased to CES at $1.4M/yr versus an estimated $0.6M market rate. The $0.8M normalization is defensible only if the buyer either acquires the property at fair market value or executes a new arm’s-length lease; the structure of this resolution affects both EBITDA quality and transaction mechanics.  
          Preliminary valuation view.         On a buyer-adjusted trailing basis, FY2024 EBITDA is approximately $16.4M (versus the CIM’s $17.1M), implying an enterprise value of $131M–$164M at 8.0x–10.0x. On a forward basis with a haircut PFAS case, FY2025E EBITDA is approximately $17.2M (versus the CIM’s $19.7M), implying $138M–$172M. We recommend an IOI enterprise value range of             $145M–$165M    , structured as approximately             $140M–$150M of cash consideration at close         plus a             contingent earnout of up to ~$15M–$20M (total consideration ~$140M–$170M depending on earnout achievement)         tied to FY2025–FY2026 PFAS revenue and adjusted EBITDA thresholds. This bridges the seller’s ~$171M+ expectation to a defensible underwriting case while preserving upside if the PFAS ramp is realized.  
      
        2. Transaction Overview and Process  
      Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC (Managing Director Philip Tran) is running a competitive sale of 100% of the equity of CES on behalf of founder/CEO Randall Oakes (68% holder) and the other equity holders. Counsel to the Company is Wexford, Mallory & Chen LLP; auditors are Ridgeline Accounting Group, PC. The process is three-phased:  
              Phase I — IOIs due 5:00 PM PT, Monday, July 14, 2025.         The process letter requires each IOI to specify (i) an enterprise value range on a cash-free, debt-free basis with normalized NWC, identifying trailing vs. projected metrics and the implied EBITDA multiple; (ii) transaction structure, including treatment of existing indebtedness, the founder-owned HQ real estate, and any rollover/earnout; (iii) sources of financing; (iv) remaining diligence items; (v) post-closing management structure and accommodation of Mr. Oakes’s 18–24 month transition; (vi) approvals and timeline to close; (vii) relevant sector experience; and (viii) contacts.  
              Phase II — Management presentations (August 2025) and confirmatory diligence         for selected bidders, with VDR access and site visits to Portland (and Boise/Seattle if requested).  
              Phase III — Final bids and definitive documentation    , with a draft stock purchase agreement distributed for markup.             Target signing/closing: Q4 2025.  
      Existing indebtedness with Cascadia Commercial Bank (senior term loan SOFR + 3.75%, maturing August 31, 2026, plus a $8.0M revolver) is expected to be refinanced or repaid at closing. The seller seeks a partner to support continued growth (particularly PFAS) while providing liquidity; Mr. Oakes intends to exit day-to-day operations within 18–24 months post-close and is willing to serve in a transitional advisory capacity.  
      
        3. Company Overview and Investment Thesis  
      CES is a Delaware C-corporation founded in 2009 and headquartered in Portland, Oregon, with additional facilities in Seattle, WA and Boise, ID. The Company operates an integrated environmental services platform across a four-state Pacific Northwest territory (Oregon, Washington, Idaho, Northern California) through three segments: Environmental Remediation (FY2024 revenue $48.2M, 55.2%), Industrial Cleaning ($27.8M, 31.8%), and Emergency Response ($11.3M, 12.9%). Total FY2024 revenue was $87.3M (12.5% CAGR FY2021–FY2024). The Company employs 412 people, holds hazardous waste transporter permits in all four states plus an Oregon DEQ contractor license, and reports an EMR of 0.87.  
          Genuinely attractive attributes (independently assessed):  
              Integrated, regulated platform with a real moat.         Four-state hazmat transporter permits, the Oregon DEQ contractor license, and PE-stamped remediation capabilities take years to replicate and create meaningful barriers to entry. The integrated remediation/cleaning/emergency-response model drives cross-selling and share-of-wallet expansion.  
              Durable core growth, even excluding PFAS.         Traditional remediation grew from $34.8M (FY2021) to $42.0M (FY2024) and industrial cleaning from $19.2M to $27.8M over the same period — respectable organic growth in a market that generally grows 5–10% annually.  
              Regulatory tailwinds are real.         EPA’s 2024 PFAS drinking water rule, CERCLA designation activity, and active Oregon/Washington state enforcement underpin multi-year demand for investigation, remediation, and compliance work.  
              Revenue visibility.         $42.6M contracted backlog plus $31.4M pipeline ($74.0M total) as of 12/31/2024; ~77.5% of backlog expected to convert within twelve months. Reported 90% MSA renewal rate and no top-10 customer losses in five years.  
              Recurring revenue characteristics.         MSA-based industrial cleaning on scheduled turnaround cycles, multi-year remediation lifecycles, and standby emergency-response retainers provide a predictable base.  
              Platform for consolidation.         A fragmented Pacific Northwest market with 5–7 identified tuck-in targets supports a buy-and-build thesis consistent with Fund III’s strategy.  
              Strong safety record.         EMR of 0.87 is a meaningful commercial qualifier for access to refining and industrial facilities.  
      These attributes support proceeding. The remainder of this memo focuses on the issues that affect price, structure, and diligence — not on whether the asset is worth pursuing.  
      
        4. Financial Analysis  
       4.1 Historical Performance  
      Revenue grew from $61.4M (FY2021) to $87.3M (FY2024), a 12.5% CAGR, with growth accelerating to 14.7% in FY2024. Reported EBITDA grew from $7.4M to $12.1M (12.1% → 13.9% margin), and Adjusted EBITDA from $9.7M to $17.1M (15.8% → 19.6% margin). The reported EBITDA margin of 13.9% is within the 10–15% sector range for companies of this size and service mix, which is reassuring and suggests the core operation is reasonably well managed. The Adjusted EBITDA margin of 19.6% is at the upper boundary of plausibility for the sector (sector adjusted margins typically 15–18%) and is driven entirely by the $5.0M of add-backs analyzed in Section 4.3.  
        4.2 Segment Analysis — PFAS Is the Story  
      The financial exhibits break out PFAS within Environmental Remediation. The trajectory and the projections:  
                              PFAS Revenue ($M)  
          FY2022A  
          FY2023A  
          FY2024A  
          FY2025E  
          FY2026E  
          FY2027E  
            PFAS Remediation  
          1.1  
          3.4  
          6.2  
          14.0  
          22.0  
          27.0  
            YoY growth  
          —  
          209.1%  
          82.4%  
          125.8%  
          57.1%  
          22.7%  
            PFAS % of total revenue  
          1.6%  
          4.5%  
          7.1%  
          14.2%  
          19.6%  
          21.8%  
         The CIM states PFAS will drive ~64% of incremental revenue from FY2024 to FY2026 ($15.8M of $24.7M). Traditional remediation is projected roughly flat ($42.0M → $44.0M), meaning the entire growth narrative — and the projected margin expansion from 19.6% to 21.0%+ — rests on a service line that is two years old and generated $6.2M last year. This concentration of the growth thesis in a single, nascent service line is the central risk in the deal and is addressed in detail in Section 6.1.  
        4.3 Adjusted EBITDA Bridge — Line-by-Line Critique  
      The CIM adds back $5.0M to bridge from $12.1M reported EBITDA to $17.1M Adjusted EBITDA. Each adjustment is assessed below.  
                           Adjustment  
          CIM ($M)  
          Assessment  
          Buyer view  
            Owner compensation normalization  
          1.9  
          Oakes total comp $2.4M less $0.5M replacement. The $2.4M explicitly includes         “    personal expenses allocated to the Company.    ”         The personal-expense component is not quantified and may include legitimate business costs a replacement CEO would also incur.  
          Largely defensible, but haircut ~$0.3M pending verification of the personal-expense component.  
            Related-party lease normalization  
          0.8  
          HQ leased from Oakes at $1.4M vs. $0.6M market. Defensible as a normalization             only if         the buyer pays market rent going forward (via new arm’s-length lease or property acquisition).  
          Defensible, conditional on real-estate resolution (Section 6.6).  
            One-time legal/regulatory costs  
          1.2  
          DEQ enforcement settlement ($875K) + legal fees ($325K) for improper storage of contaminated soils at CES’s             own         Portland facility. Sector run-rate $200K–$500K; our portfolio experience (GreenWorks $275K/yr; Allied $310K/yr) indicates a baseline of regulatory cost is inherent.  
          Overstated; defensible add-back ~$0.7M–$0.9M (haircut $0.3M–$0.5M).  
            Non-recurring equipment relocation  
          0.4  
          Boise depot relocation/expansion, completed FY2024.  
          Defensible as one-time.  
            Stock-based comp (phantom units)  
          0.3  
          Non-cash. All phantom units settle in             cash at closing         — a separate use of funds, not captured here.  
          Defensible as EBITDA add-back, but the cash settlement liability must be quantified for sources & uses.  
            One-time IT system implementation  
          0.1  
          ERP upgrade, completed FY2024.  
          Defensible as one-time.  
             Buyer-adjusted FY2024 EBITDA: $17.1M − $0.4M (regulatory) − $0.3M (owner comp) ≈ $16.4M         (range $16.1M–$16.8M). The related-party lease, relocation, IT, and phantom add-backs are retained as defensible. The two principal haircuts (regulatory and owner comp) reduce Adjusted EBITDA by ~$0.7M, which at 8.0x–10.0x equates to ~$5.6M–$7.0M of enterprise value before considering the larger PFAS and capex issues.  
        4.4 Free Cash Flow and Capital Intensity  
      Reported free cash flow is thin relative to EBITDA and is the area where the capex and working-capital issues compound:  
                           ($M)  
          FY2021  
          FY2022  
          FY2023  
          FY2024  
            Cash from operations (CIM)  
          5.8  
          6.4  
          7.8  
          9.2  
            Capital expenditures  
          (3.6)  
          (4.1)  
          (5.8)  
          (7.2)  
            Free cash flow (CIM)  
          2.2  
          2.3  
          2.0  
          2.0  
            Reported EBITDA  
          7.4  
          8.6  
          10.3  
          12.1  
            FCF / Reported EBITDA  
          30%  
          27%  
          19%  
          17%  
         FCF conversion has declined from ~30% to ~17% of reported EBITDA as capex (notably growth capex for PFAS equipment and the Boise expansion) and working-capital build (accounts receivable grew from $10.8M to $16.9M) have absorbed cash. Two implications: (i) the business is more capital- and working-capital-intensive than the EBITDA headline suggests, which constrains leverage capacity and the equity check; and (ii) if maintenance capex is understated by ~$2.5M (Section 6.2), true FCF is even lower — roughly breakeven on a normalized basis in FY2024 — which is a material underwriting concern that must be stress-tested.  
        4.5 Balance Sheet and Net Debt  
      The CIM reports total debt of $17.3M (term loan $14.2M, of which $2.3M current; revolver $3.1M drawn against an $8.0M commitment) and cash of $2.3M, implying net debt of ~$15.0M. The term loan matures August 31, 2026 — near-term and to be refinanced at closing per the process letter. Net working capital of $11.8M (13.5% of revenue) is within the sector range of 10–14%, though the DSO discrepancy discussed in Section 5.2 suggests the quality of that working capital warrants scrutiny. The financial exhibits’ balance sheet differs materially from the CIM’s (Section 5.1), including $2.5M of         “    Goodwill & Intangible Assets    ”         that is not mentioned or explained anywhere in the CIM despite no acquisitions being described — an item that requires explanation.  
      
         5. Data Integrity and Reconciliation Issues  
      A buy-side analysis must first establish whether the numbers can be relied upon. Across the four documents (CIM, financial exhibits, customer/backlog report, and process letter), there are numerous non-reconciling figures. The most material are summarized below; a full reconciliation table is in the Appendix.  
       5.1 CIM vs. Financial Exhibits — Balance Sheet (FY2024)  
                          Line item ($M)  
          CIM  
          Exhibits  
          Variance  
            Cash & equivalents  
          2.3  
          4.1  
          (1.8)  
            Accounts receivable, net  
          16.9  
          16.9  
          0.0  
            Other current assets  
          4.8  
          4.1  
          0.7  
            Total current assets  
          24.0  
          25.1  
          (1.1)  
            PP&E, net  
          18.7  
          21.3  
          (2.6)  
            Other non-current assets  
          3.4  
          3.6  
          (0.2)  
                Total assets  
              46.1  
              50.0  
              (3.9)  
            Accounts payable  
          5.4  
          6.1  
          (0.7)  
            Total current liabilities  
          12.2  
          13.3  
          (1.1)  
            Total liabilities  
          29.0  
          31.2  
          (2.2)  
                Stockholders’ equity  
              17.1  
              18.8  
              (1.7)  
         The exhibits also carry $2.5M of         “    Goodwill & Intangible Assets    ”         with no corresponding disclosure in the CIM (no acquisitions are described). Total assets differ by $3.9M and equity by $1.7M — these are not rounding items.  
        5.2 CIM vs. Financial Exhibits — Income Statement (FY2024) and Internal Inconsistency  
                          Line item ($M)  
          CIM  
          Exhibits  
          Variance  
            Revenue  
          87.3  
          87.3  
          0.0  
            Gross profit  
          29.5  
          28.8  
          0.7  
            Gross margin

         6. Key Risk Factors and Diligence Priorities  
      The following are prioritized by impact on valuation and deal certainty. Each maps to a specific diligence request in Section 10.  
       6.1 PFAS Revenue Projections (HIGH)  
      The CIM projects PFAS revenue of $14.0M in FY2025E (125.8% growth) and $22.0M in FY2026E (57.1% growth), with PFAS providing ~64% of incremental revenue and underpinning the projected margin expansion to 21.0%+. Sector analysts project 25–35% growth for the overall PFAS market, but company-level growth is constrained by (a) licensed-engineer and trained-technician availability, (b) specialized treatment-equipment capital, (c) competitive entry by larger national firms, and (d) the pace of enforcement that actually triggers             funded         remediation (as opposed to investigation/assessment).  
      Our GreenWorks experience is directly analogous: an emerging service line (vapor intrusion mitigation) projected at 80%+ growth in years two and three moderated to ~35% once pent-up demand was worked through. Applying a 30–50% company-level growth assumption to CES’s $6.2M FY2024 PFAS base yields FY2025E PFAS of ~$8.1M–$9.3M — roughly $5M–$6M below the CIM’s $14.0M. If PFAS carries above-average margins (as the CIM implies), this shortfall could reduce FY2025E EBITDA by ~$1.5M–$2.0M and undermine the projected margin expansion.  
          PE capacity constraint.         PE headcount fell from three to two in January 2025 (one retired, unreplaced). PFAS site assessments and remediation designs require PE oversight. Sustaining the projected ramp likely requires hiring two to three additional PEs; environmental PE recruitment typically runs six to twelve months. Management’s PE hiring plan — and whether the loss of one-third of PE capacity creates a near-term execution bottleneck — is a critical diligence question.  
        6.2 Maintenance Capex (HIGH)  
      CIM maintenance capex of $3.0M (3.4% of revenue) is 150–200 bps below the 5–7% sector range ($4.4M–$6.1M for CES’s revenue). Our portfolio data confirms the range: GreenWorks ran at 6.2% post-close (after a pre-close understatement to ~3.5%), and Allied at 5.8%. If true maintenance capex is ~$5.5M (sector midpoint), the ~$2.5M annual difference directly reduces free cash flow — and at CES’s already-thin FCF (Section 4.4), this could push normalized FCF near breakeven. A detailed fleet age and condition assessment (the fleet is ~4.5 years old, replacement value >$22M) is essential early diligence. This is the single largest potential swing factor in the underwriting model after PFAS.  
        6.3 Regulatory Cost Add-Back (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
      The $1.2M add-back for the August 2024 DEQ enforcement action is presented as fully non-recurring. Two concerns: (i) the action concerned             improper storage of contaminated soils at CES’s own Portland facility         — an operational compliance failure at the Company’s primary site, not a client-site issue — which raises the question of whether it reflects systemic internal compliance weakness rather than an isolated event; and (ii) sector and portfolio data show a baseline of $200K–$500K/yr in regulatory and compliance cost is inherent to the business model. Best-practice QoE normalizes to an ongoing run-rate and adds back only the excess. At a $300K run-rate, the defensible add-back is ~$0.9M (a $0.3M haircut); at $500K, ~$0.7M (a $0.5M haircut). The full 10-year regulatory enforcement history must be reviewed to assess whether prior actions suggest a pattern.  
        6.4 Customer Concentration and PNR Contract Renewal (HIGH)  
      Top-customer concentration (PNR 21.4%; top 5 52.5%; top 10 71.0%) is typical for project-based environmental services but is a real risk.             The PNR master service agreement expired March 31, 2025 — before the CIM was distributed on June 9, 2025.         The CIM and backlog report describe the PNR relationship as         “    deeply embedded    ”         with on-site personnel and preferred-provider status, but neither document confirms renewal. Whether PNR has renewed (and on what terms), the renewal cadence of the other top-10 contracts (several expire in 2025–2026), and the realistic revenue-replacement capacity if PNR scope is reduced are the most important commercial diligence items. The concentration trend is favorable (top-10 share declining from 74.3% to 71.0% over FY2021–FY2024), but the absolute level remains high and the largest contract is in renewal at the moment of sale.  
        6.5 Founder Transition and Key-Person Risk (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
      Mr. Oakes (age 61, 68% owner, 30+ years’ experience) intends to exit within 18–24 months post-close. He is the central strategic, customer, and regulatory-relationship figure. The GreenWorks experience — where a key project manager departed within six months of close — is a cautionary precedent. Janet Prewitt (VP Operations, 18-year tenure, holds key remediation client relationships) is the critical retention target and should be covered by a retention agreement as a closing condition. The broader management bench (VP Sales since 2014; Controller since 2016; Safety Director since 2012) is reasonably tenured but has not operated the company without Mr. Oakes. A credible CEO succession plan — including the $0.5M replacement-compensation assumption underlying the owner-comp add-back — must be validated.  
        6.6 Real Estate and Related-Party Lease (MEDIUM)  
      The Portland HQ (8.5 acres, purpose-built environmental operations center) is owned personally by Mr. Oakes and leased to CES at $1.4M/yr through 2029, versus an estimated $0.6M market rate. The $0.8M EBITDA normalization is defensible only if the buyer will in fact pay market rent going forward. The buyer must either (a) acquire the property from Mr. Oakes at independently appraised fair market value (adding to uses of funds but eliminating rent and the related-party conflict), or (b) execute a new arm’s-length lease at market rate (preserving the normalization but leaving a related-party landlord in place for the lease term). The CIM notes industrial yard space in Portland leases at $8–$12/SF and that         “    specialized environmental staging facilities    ”         can command $1.0M–$1.2M/yr — which, if accurate, suggests the $0.6M         “    market    ”         estimate may itself be low and that the property’s specialized improvements carry premium value. The real-estate resolution is both a valuation and a transaction-structure issue and should be addressed explicitly in the IOI.  
        6.7 Adjusted EBITDA Adjustment Defensibility (MEDIUM)  
      Beyond the regulatory and owner-comp items already discussed, the phantom equity plan warrants attention. The $0.3M annual non-cash add-back is appropriate for EBITDA, but             all outstanding phantom units settle in cash at closing    . The total cash settlement liability is not quantified in the CIM and must be estimated for sources & uses; based on the annual expense run-rate it could be a low-single-digit-million obligation. This is a use of funds at closing, not an EBITDA item, and reduces net equity proceeds to the seller.  
        6.8 Fund Fit (CONFIRM)  
      The sector memo characterizes CES as fitting         “    squarely within    ”         Fund III’s parameters. Fund III ($425M, closed 2023) is described as targeting platforms in the $30M–$100M enterprise value range. CES’s implied enterprise value of $137M–$171M (CIM) — and our recommended $145M–$165M IOI range — is             above         that stated ceiling. The deal team should confirm Fund III’s actual EV/equity-check limits, concentration limits, and whether a platform of this size requires LPAC approval or a fund-limit exception. At a ~$150M EV with ~40% leverage, the equity check is ~$90M (~21% of fund), which is a meaningful but supportable platform concentration if within mandate.  
      
         7. Valuation Analysis  
       7.1 CIM Implied Valuation  
      The CIM cites comparable environmental services transactions at 8.0x–10.0x Adjusted EBITDA and implies:  
          FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA $17.1M × 8.0x–10.0x =             $136.8M–$171.0M         enterprise value  
          FY2025E Adjusted EBITDA $19.7M × 8.0x–10.0x =             $157.6M–$197.0M         enterprise value  
      The CIM argues CES’s growth, PFAS opportunity, and platform characteristics support the higher end and the forward basis.  
        7.2 Buyer-Adjusted EBITDA  
      Applying the haircuts in Section 4.3:  
              FY2024 buyer-adjusted EBITDA ≈ $16.4M         ($17.1M less ~$0.4M regulatory and ~$0.3M owner-comp; range $16.1M–$16.8M).  
              FY2025E buyer-adjusted EBITDA (PFAS haircut case) ≈ $17.2M         ($19.7M less ~$1.8M PFAS shortfall impact, ~$0.4M regulatory, ~$0.3M owner-comp; range approximately $17.0M–$17.5M). This assumes PFAS grows ~35% to ~$8.4M rather than 125.8% to $14.0M, with other segments at CIM/exhibit projections.
