         8. IOI Recommendations  
       8.1 Indication of Value  
      Submit an enterprise value range of             $145M–$165M         on a cash-free, debt-free basis with normalized net working capital, structured as:  
              ~$140M–$150M cash at close         (implying ~8.5x–9.1x buyer-adjusted FY2024 EBITDA of ~$16.4M), plus  
              A contingent earnout of up to ~$15M–$20M (total consideration ~$140M–$170M depending on earnout achievement)    , payable based on FY2025 and FY2026 PFAS revenue and/or consolidated Adjusted EBITDA thresholds (e.g., PFAS revenue ≥ $12M in FY2025 and ≥ $18M in FY2026; or Adjusted EBITDA ≥ $19M in FY2025 and ≥ $22M in FY2026).  
      This structure pays the seller for the growth story if it materializes, protects our downside if it does not, and directly addresses the principal valuation dispute (PFAS) without requiring us to underwrite the full ramp at close. State the valuation on both a trailing (FY2024 buyer-adjusted) and forward (FY2025E) basis, and identify the implied multiple range (approximately 8.5x–9.1x trailing buyer-adjusted EBITDA on the cash-at-close component, scaling to ~10x trailing if the full earnout is earned).  
        8.2 Transaction Structure  
              Stock purchase         (consistent with the process letter’s expected structure).  
              Existing indebtedness         refinanced/repaid at closing; transaction valued cash-free/debt-free with a normalized NWC peg to be set on verified (QoE) metrics, not the CIM’s stated DSO.  
              HQ real estate:         propose to acquire the Portland property from Mr. Oakes at independently appraised fair market value (preferred — eliminates related-party conflict and rent), or alternatively execute a new long-term arm’s-length lease at market rate. Either way, the $0.8M lease normalization is preserved in EBITDA only against market-rate occupancy.  
              Rollover equity:         given Mr. Oakes’s stated desire to exit, a meaningful rollover is unlikely to be required; consider a modest rollover (e.g., 5–10%) for key management (e.g., Ms. Prewitt) as a retention/alignment mechanism rather than for the founder.  
              Earnout         as described above, with clear, auditable metrics and a 12–24 month measurement period.  
        8.3 Sources of Financing  
              Equity:         committed from Fund III. Indicate the equity check (~$85M–$95M at ~40% leverage) and that Fund III has capacity.  
              Debt:         ~40–45% of EV, anticipated as a senior debt / unitranche facility from a lender with environmental-services experience; name candidate lenders if appropriate. Note the capital-intensive, working-capital-heavy profile supports moderate (not aggressive) leverage.  
          Indicate financing is subject only to customary conditions (definitive docs, QoE, final diligence).  
        8.4 Diligence Priorities (Summary)  
      The prioritized diligence list is in Section 10. In the IOI, summarize the key remaining items: (i) PFAS backlog breakout (signed vs. pipeline) and PE hiring plan; (ii) PNR renewal status and top-10 contract renewals; (iii) QoE and reconciliation of the CIM/exhibits discrepancies; (iv) fleet age/condition and true maintenance capex; (v) 10-year regulatory enforcement history; (vi) real-estate resolution; (vii) phantom equity liability; (viii) management retention. Indicate a 6–8 week confirmatory diligence timeline consistent with an August management presentation and Q4 close.  
        8.5 Management and Operations  
          Accommodate Mr. Oakes’s 18–24 month transition in an advisory/transition role.  
          Commit to retaining the existing management team and field workforce; propose retention agreements for Ms. Prewitt (VP Operations) and other key personnel as a closing condition.  
          Plan to recruit a replacement CEO at the ~$0.5M compensation level assumed in the owner-comp normalization, with environmental-services operating experience.  
          No anticipated near-term operational changes beyond continued investment in PFAS capacity and fleet.  
        8.6 Approvals, Timeline, and Relevant Experience  
          Internal approvals: Thornfield investment committee; confirm LPAC requirements given EV size relative to fund (Section 6.8).  
          Regulatory: customary HSR if applicable; state environmental permit transfers (hazardous waste transporter permits in four states; Oregon DEQ contractor license) — confirm transferability and timing.  
          Timeline: target definitive agreement Q4 2025, closing 30–60 days thereafter subject to permit transfers.  
          Relevant experience: cite GreenWorks Remediation (Fund II, 2019) and Allied Waste Systems (Fund II, 2021) as directly comparable environmental-services platform investments, with operational and regulatory-compliance lessons applicable to CES.  
      
         9. Recommendation and Next Steps  
          Recommendation: Proceed to IOI at $145M–$165M enterprise value, structured as ~$140M–$150M cash at close plus a PFAS/EBITDA-linked earnout of up to ~$15M–$20M.         The asset is attractive and strategically on-target for Fund III; the risks are identifiable, quantifiable, and largely addressable through structure (earnout, retention conditions, real-estate resolution) and diligence (QoE, fleet assessment, regulatory history, PNR renewal). The pattern of financial inconsistencies and aggressive adjustments is consistent with seller-CIM behavior we have observed in this sector and does not, on present information, warrant passing — but it does warrant a disciplined valuation anchored on independently adjusted trailing EBITDA rather than management’s forward projections.  
          Immediate next steps (target completion ahead of July 14 IOI):  
          Resolve the customer-identity discrepancy (CIM vs. backlog report) with Greenleaf so we know whose contracts we are underwriting; confirm PNR renewal status.  
          Engage a QoE provider (per the sector memo, to be selected by the deal team) and transmit the reconciliation items in the Appendix as the opening QoE request.  
          Request the PFAS backlog breakout (signed contracts vs. pipeline) and management’s PE hiring plan.  
          Confirm Fund III mandate/LPAC requirements for a platform of this EV size.  
          Circulate this memo to the deal team for discussion; schedule an IC pre-read ahead of IOI submission.  
          Prepare the IOI letter per the Section 8 framework, with buyer’s counsel (Calloway, Barrett & Sims LLP) reviewing structure and conditions language.  
      
        10. Diligence Request List (Prioritized)  
          A. Commercial / PFAS  
          PFAS backlog breakout as of 6/30/2025: signed contracts vs. awarded vs. pipeline, by customer and expected conversion period.  
          PFAS pipeline detail: opportunity names, scope, expected award dates, win-rate assumptions supporting $14.0M FY2025E.  
          Management’s PE hiring plan: open requisitions, recruitment timeline, compensation, and impact on PFAS capacity.  
          PFAS unit economics: gross margin, project economics, equipment utilization, and competitive positioning vs. national entrants.  
          B. Customers / Contracts  
          PNR: current MSA status (post March 31, 2025), renewal terms, scope, pricing, and any change-order or volume trends; on-site personnel arrangement.  
          Reconciliation of the top-10 customer list between the CIM and the backlog report — confirm correct customer identities, segments served, and contract expirations.  
          Top-10 contract renewals: status of all 2025–2026 expirations (Meridian 12/31/25; Cascade Terminals/Clearwater 6/30/26; Puget Sound/Northshore 9/30/26; Tidewater/Harborview 3/31/27; etc.).  
          Customer retention and win/loss history for the past five years; any lost top-10 customers.  
          C. Financial / QoE  
          Reconciliation of all discrepancies between the CIM, financial exhibits, and backlog report (see Appendix) — balance sheet, income statement, segment splits, cash flow, geographic mix.  
          Explanation of the $2.5M         “    Goodwill & Intangible Assets    ”         on the exhibits’ balance sheet (no acquisitions described in the CIM).  
          DSO reconciliation: stated 52 days vs. ~71 days implied by AR/revenue; receivables aging and unbilled-revenue detail.  
          Quality of EBITDA adjustments: owner-compensation detail (salary, bonus, benefits, and the unquantified         “    personal expenses allocated to the Company    ”    ); related-party lease documentation and market-rate support; phantom equity plan terms and total cash settlement liability at closing.  
          Maintenance vs. growth capex detail for FY2022–FY2024; fleet age, condition, and replacement schedule; deferred-maintenance assessment.  
          D. Regulatory / Compliance  
          Complete 10-year regulatory enforcement history (all agencies, all facilities); details of the August 2024 DEQ action and corrective measures.  
          Current status and transferability of all permits (four-state hazmat transporter permits; Oregon DEQ contractor license; EPA Generator ID); renewal calendar.  
          Environmental compliance status of CES’s own facilities (Portland HQ wash rack, soil stockpile/staging areas) post-DEQ action.  
          E. Real Estate / Facilities  
          HQ property: appraisal, environmental site assessment, title, and Mr. Oakes’s willingness to sell vs. lease at market.  
          Boise and Seattle lease assignments and any change-of-control provisions.  
          F. Management / Employees  
          Management retention risk assessment; willingness of Ms. Prewitt and key PMs to commit to retention agreements.  
          Phantom equity plan: participant list, vesting, and total cash settlement obligation at closing.  
          CEO succession plan and replacement-compensation benchmarking supporting the $0.5M assumption.

           E. Other Cross-Document Items  
                           Item  
          CIM  
          Other source

            DSO  
          52 days  
          ~71 days (AR $16.9M / rev $87.3M)  
          ~19 days  
            Geographic mix (OR+WA)  
          ~75%

          ~7 pp

          Prepared by David Koh, Associate, Thornfield Capital Partners. This memorandum is based solely on the Confidential Information Memorandum, financial exhibits, customer/backlog report, and process letter distributed by Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC, together with the internal sector memo dated June 12, 2025. No figures herein have been independently verified. All adjustments and projections are preliminary and subject to confirmatory due diligence and quality-of-earnings analysis. For internal use only; do not distribute externally.

### FILE: closing-issues-memo.docx
             Privileged & Confidential — Attorney-Client Communication / Attorney Work Product. Prepared by Bridgeford Hollis LLP in its capacity as Fund Counsel to Whitmore Capital Partners VII, LP and Whitmore Capital Management LLC.  
       MEMORANDUM  
          TO:         Marcus J. Whitmore, Managing Member & Chief Investment Officer, Whitmore Capital Management LLC (General Partner); Sandra K. Liu, Chief Compliance Officer & General Counsel, Whitmore Capital Management LLC  
          FROM:         David R. Kasten, Partner, Bridgeford Hollis LLP (Fund Counsel); Emily T. Naraghi, Associate  
          DATE:         January 31, 2025  
          RE:         Discrepancies and Unresolved Issues Identified in the Source Documents in Connection with the Final Closing Certificate — Whitmore Capital Partners VII, LP (Final Closing: January 31, 2025)  
      
       I. Introduction and Scope  
      In connection with our preparation of the Final Closing Certificate for Whitmore Capital Partners VII, LP (the         “    Fund    ”    ), we have reviewed the following source documents (collectively, the         “    Source Documents    ”    ):  
          Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership, dated as of September 15, 2024 (the         “    LPA    ”    );  
          Amendment No. 1 to the LPA, dated as of November 30, 2024 (    “    Amendment No. 1    ”    );  
          Form of Subscription Agreement for use in connection with the Final Closing (the         “    Subscription Agreement    ”    );  
          Capital Call Notice No. 1, dated October 10, 2024 (    “    CC #1    ”    );  
          Capital Call Notice No. 2, dated December 15, 2024 (    “    CC #2    ”    );  
          GP Internal Closing Memorandum, dated January 28, 2025 (the         “    GP Memo    ”    );  
          AML/KYC completion email from Jonathan C. Prewitt (Oakmere Fund Services LLC), dated January 24, 2025 (the         “    AML/KYC Email    ”    ); and  
          Administrator’s closing checklist workbook, including the Closing Checklist, Commitment Schedule, and Equalization Calc tabs (the         “    Admin Checklist    ”    ).  
      This memorandum identifies discrepancies among, and unresolved issues within, the Source Documents that bear on the General Partner’s ability to certify the conditions precedent to the Final Closing under Section 11.2 of the LPA. Each issue is classified by severity:  
              CRITICAL         — bears on a non-waivable condition precedent (Section 11.2(3), (4), or (5)) or otherwise could prevent the Final Closing from proceeding; must be resolved before the certificate is executed.  
              SIGNIFICANT         — a material inconsistency or cross-reference error that should be corrected or reconciled before or promptly after closing.  
              MINOR         — a clerical, drafting, or post-closing item that does not impede closing but should be noted.  
      A summary table appears in Section II. The detailed discussion follows in Sections III–V. Section VI sets out our recommended actions.  
        II. Summary of Issues  
                            #  
          Issue  
          LPA Condition / Section  
          Severity  
          Status  
            1  
          ERISA/Benefit Plan Investor percentage is inconsistent across documents; under one reading the 24.99% cap is exceeded  
          11.2(5) (non-waivable); §6.8  
              CRITICAL  
          Unresolved  
            2  
          Three conflicting equalization-interest / total-due calculations  
          11.2(2); §3.2(c)  
              CRITICAL  
          Unresolved  
            3  
          Capital contributions not yet confirmed received as of the last checklist update  
          11.2(2)  
              CRITICAL  
          Pending  
            4  
          Legal opinions not yet delivered; limited-liability-of-LPs opinion not confirmed  
          11.2(7)  
              CRITICAL  
          Pending  
            5  
          Individual initial-closing LP commitment amounts conflict (CC #2 Exhibit A vs. Admin Checklist)  
          11.2(8); Schedule A  
          SIGNIFICANT  
          Unresolved  
            6  
          No identifiable         “    GP representations and warranties    ”         article in the LPA; mis-cited in Amendment No. 1 and GP Memo  
          11.2(10)  
          SIGNIFICANT  
          Unresolved  
            7  
          Default interest rate conflict: LPA 12% vs. Subscription Agreement 18%  
          §3.3(b)  
          SIGNIFICANT  
          Unresolved  
            8  
          Target Fund Size Advisory Committee notification omitted from GP Memo action items  
          §3.1(d)  
          SIGNIFICANT  
          Action required  
            9  
          Numerous incorrect LPA cross-references across the Source Documents  
          various  
          SIGNIFICANT  
          To be corrected  
            10  
          CC #1 funding-date discrepancy (Oct 24 vs. Oct 25)  
          —  
          MINOR  
          Note  
            11  
          “    Investment Manager    ”         undefined term in Amendment No. 1  
          —  
          MINOR  
          Note  
            12  
          Equalization day-count convention (LPA         “    through and including    ”         vs. 113/47 vs. 113/46)  
          §3.2(c)  
          MINOR  
          Confirm  
            13  
          Governmental plans treated as Benefit Plan Investors — definitional tension with LPA  
          §6.8; Art. I  
          MINOR  
          Confirm  
            14  
          Advisory Committee fifth seat vacant (post-closing obligation)  
          §9.1(b)  
          MINOR  
          Post-closing  
            15  
          Miscellaneous clerical items (memo date, QP section cite, etc.)  
          —  
          MINOR  
          Note  
           III. Critical Issues  
       Issue 1 — ERISA / Benefit Plan Investor percentage is internally inconsistent; under one reading the 24.99% cap is exceeded (Section 11.2(5); Section 6.8)  
          Why it matters.         Section 11.2(5) requires the General Partner to determine that, after giving effect to the admission of all incoming Limited Partners at the Final Closing, the aggregate Capital Commitments of Benefit Plan Investors (    “    BPIs    ”    ) do not exceed 24.99% of total Capital Commitments. Pursuant to Section 11.3, this condition             may not be waived under any circumstances    . If the true BPI percentage exceeds 24.99%, the Final Closing cannot proceed.  
          What the documents say.         Three different figures appear:  
             GP Memo (Section V.C):         States that BPI commitments are         “    approximately $225,300,000    ”         and that         “    total Fund commitments will be approximately $1,140,000,000,    ”         yielding         “    approximately 19.8%.    ”         This calculation is wrong on two inputs: (a) the $225,300,000 numerator reflects only the             prior-closings         BPI commitments and             omits the $130,000,000 of Final Closing BPIs         (Meridian State Teachers’ Retirement System ($100,000,000) and Sentry Municipal Employees’ Pension Trust ($30,000,000)); and (b) the $1,140,000,000 denominator is inconsistent with the $1,615,000,000 total Fund commitments stated in the GP Memo’s own reconciliation table (Section III) and in every other Source Document. We could not derive $1,140,000,000 from any combination of the stated commitment figures.  
             Subscription Agreement (Section 2.3 and Schedule I) and Admin Checklist (ERISA Analysis tab and Closing Checklist item 19):         State total BPI commitments of $355,300,000 (= $225,300,000 prior + $130,000,000 Final Closing) against total Fund commitments of $1,615,000,000, yielding             22.00%    . This is below the 24.99% cap.  
             Admin Checklist — Commitment Schedule tab:         Marks             nine (9) Limited Partners         as         “    Y    ”         (ERISA Plan Investor). Summing those nine         “    Y    ”         designations yields             $465,300,000    , which is             28.81%         of $1,615,000,000 —             above         the 24.99% cap. The nine         “    Y    ”         LPs are:  
                           Limited Partner  
          Closing  
          Commitment  
          Type  
            Commonwealth Public Employees’ Retirement Fund  
          Initial  
          $85,000,000  
          State pension (IL)  
            Lakewood Municipal Pension Fund  
          Initial  
          $80,000,000  
          State pension (CA)  
            Westfield County Employees’ Retirement System  
          Initial  
          $40,000,000  
          State pension (NY)  
            Ashford Healthcare System Retirement Plan  
          Initial  
          $25,000,000  
          ERISA plan (TX)  
            Pemberton Teachers’ Pension Trust  
          Initial  
          $35,000,000  
          State pension (PA)  
            Dalton Firefighters’ Pension Fund  
          Second  
          $45,300,000  
          Municipal pension (MI)  
            Oakdale Police & Fire Retirement Trust  
          Second  
          $25,000,000  
          Municipal pension (OH)  
            Meridian State Teachers’ Retirement System  
          Final  
          $100,000,000  
          State pension (IN)  
            Sentry Municipal Employees’ Pension Trust  
          Final  
          $30,000,000  
          Municipal pension (OH)  
                Sum of all                 “        Y        ”                 designations  
       
              $465,300,000  
              28.81%  
         The $355,300,000 figure used in the ERISA Analysis (and in the Subscription Agreement) is             $110,000,000 lower         than the sum of the         “    Y    ”         designations. That $110,000,000 corresponds to Commonwealth Public Employees’ Retirement Fund ($85,000,000) plus one of either Oakdale ($25,000,000) or Ashford ($25,000,000) — both of which are marked         “    Y    ”         in the Commitment Schedule but are not reflected in the ERISA Analysis total. There is no consistent rationale for the exclusion: the ERISA Analysis counts other governmental plans (Lakewood, Westfield, Pemberton, Dalton, Meridian, Sentry) as BPIs, so excluding Commonwealth (also a governmental plan marked         “    Y    ”    ) is internally inconsistent.  
          Recommendation.         Before the certificate is executed, the General Partner and Fund Administrator must (a) reconcile the Commitment Schedule’s         “    Y    ”         designations to the ERISA Analysis total, confirming which Limited Partners are Benefit Plan Investors for purposes of Section 6.8; (b) recompute the BPI percentage using a single, documented methodology; and (c) confirm in writing that the resulting percentage does not exceed 24.99%. If the nine         “    Y    ”         designations are correct, the BPI percentage is 28.81% and the non-waivable condition in Section 11.2(5) is             not satisfied    , which would require reducing or declining one or more BPI commitments before the Final Closing can be consummated. The certificate’s Condition 5 certification (stated at 22.00%) must not be executed until this is resolved.  
        Issue 2 — Three conflicting equalization-interest and total-due calculations (Section 11.2(2); Section 3.2(c))  
          Why it matters.         Section 11.2(2) requires that all Capital Contributions due at the Final Closing — including Equalization Contributions and Equalization Interest — be received in immediately available funds, with the Fund Administrator confirming receipt. The exact amount due from each incoming Limited Partner must be settled before funds are collected and before the certificate certifies receipt.  
          What the documents say.         The catch-up (equalization) Capital Contribution is undisputed at             $42,750,000         (15% of $285,000,000 = 10% for CC #1 + 5% for CC #2), and the equalization-interest rate (8% per annum, simple interest, Actual/365) is consistent across documents. However, the             day count for Capital Call #2         and the resulting             equalization interest         differ across the Source Documents, producing three different         “    total amount due    ”         figures:  
                            Source  
          CC #1 days  
          CC #2 days  
          Total Equalization Interest  
          Total Amount Due  
            Subscription Agreement, Schedule I (per-LP)  
          113  
          47  
          $852,657.53  
          $43,602,657.53  
            Subscription Agreement, Schedule I (stated         “    aggregate    ”    )  
          113  
          47  
          $852,661.64  
          $43,602,661.64  
            Admin Checklist, Equalization Calc tab  
          113  
          46  
          $849,534.24  
          $43,599,534.24  
            LPA literal (    “    from and including … through and including    ”    )  
          114  
          48  
          $862,027.40  
          $43,612,027.40  
         We independently recomputed each figure and confirmed the arithmetic:  
              Subscription Agreement per-LP total ($852,657.53)         is correct for a 113-day / 47-day count and equals the mathematically precise aggregate (because simple interest is linear, the sum of per-LP interest equals the interest on the aggregate $285,000,000).  
              The Subscription Agreement’s stated                 “        aggregate        ”                 of $852,661.64 does not reconcile         to its own per-LP calculations. The Schedule I note attributes the $4.11 difference to         “    rounding in the individual per-LP calculations,    ”         but the precise aggregate equals the per-LP sum to the cent ($852,657.53); the $4.11 difference is therefore unexplained, and the Schedule I designation of $852,661.64 as the         “    controlling amount    ”         appears to be in error.  
              The Admin Checklist uses 46 days for CC #2         (vs. 47 in the Subscription Agreement), which understates the CC #2 interest by one day and produces a total that is $3,127.29 lower than the Subscription Agreement’s per-LP total. The standard date difference between December 15, 2024 and January 31, 2025 is 47 days; the 46-day figure appears to be an off-by-one error.  
              The LPA’s                 “        from and including the date of each prior Capital Call through and including the date of the applicable Subsequent Closing        ”                 language         (Section 3.2(c) and the Equalization Interest definition) supports a both-inclusive count of 114 days (CC #1) and 48 days (CC #2), which neither the Subscription Agreement (113/47) nor the Admin Checklist (113/46) uses.  
          Recommendation.         The Fund Administrator should issue a single, final closing statement for each incoming Limited Partner using one documented day-count convention, and the General Partner should confirm that the wires received match that statement. The day-count convention should be confirmed against the LPA’s         “    through and including    ”         language (we recommend 114/48 unless the parties have consistently applied an exclusive-start convention, in which case 113/47). The certificate’s Condition 2 certification should reference the Fund Administrator’s final closing statement rather than any of the conflicting preliminary figures.  
        Issue 3 — Capital contributions not yet confirmed received (Section 11.2(2))  
          What the documents say.         The Admin Checklist (Closing Checklist item 21) marks escrow funding as             “        Pending — Awaiting bank confirmation of wire receipts as of January 30, 2025.        ”         The GP Memo (Section VI, Condition 2) likewise marks this condition             “        Pending.        ”         Wire instructions were distributed on January 20, 2025, and funds were expected in the Pinnacle National Bank escrow account (ending 9213) on or before January 31, 2025.  
          Recommendation.         The certificate’s Condition 2 certification (receipt of all Capital Contributions in immediately available funds, confirmed by the Fund Administrator) must not be executed until the Fund Administrator has confirmed in writing that all six incoming Limited Partners’ wires — catch-up capital plus equalization interest — have been received and reconciled to the final closing statement (see Issue 2).  
        Issue 4 — Legal opinions not yet delivered; limited-liability opinion not confirmed (Section 11.2(7))  
          What the documents say.         The Admin Checklist (item 23) and the GP Memo (Section VI, Condition 7) both mark the legal opinions as             “        In Process        ”                 /                 “        Draft circulated — awaiting execution        ”         as of January 28–30, 2025. Section 11.2(7) requires Fund Counsel to deliver opinions addressing: (i) due formation and valid existence of the Partnership as a Delaware limited partnership; (ii) enforceability of the LPA against the Partnership and the General Partner; and (iii)         “    such other matters as are customary for private equity fund closings,             including the limited liability of the Limited Partners    .    ”  
      The GP Memo describes the opinions being prepared as (a) a formation opinion, (b) an enforceability opinion, and (c)         “    a securities law opinion regarding the availability of the private placement exemption under Regulation D.    ”         The GP Memo does             not         mention an opinion on the             limited liability of the Limited Partners    , which Section 11.2(7)(iii) specifically calls out.  
          Recommendation.         Confirm that the executed opinion set expressly addresses the limited liability of the Limited Partners (in addition to formation, enforceability, and any Reg D/securities-law opinion). The certificate’s Condition 7 certification should not be executed until the fully executed opinions, dated as of the Final Closing, have been delivered.  
         IV. Significant Issues  
       Issue 5 — Individual initial-closing LP commitment amounts conflict between CC #2 Exhibit A and the Admin Checklist (Section 11.2(8); Schedule A)  
          What the documents say.         CC #2 Exhibit A lists three initial-closing Limited Partners (who are also Advisory Committee members) with commitment amounts that differ from the Admin Checklist Commitment Schedule:  
                           Limited Partner  
          CC #2 Exhibit A  
          Admin Checklist  
          Difference  
            Silvermark Capital Advisors  
          $125,000,000  
          $75,000,000  
          $50,000,000  
            Commonwealth Public Employees’ Retirement Fund  
          $100,000,000  
          $85,000,000  
          $15,000,000  
            Graystone Foundation  
          $75,000,000  
          $50,000,000  
          $25,000,000  
            15 remaining initial-closing LPs (subtotal)  
          $640,000,000  
          $730,000,000  
          ($90,000,000)  
                Total initial-closing LPs  
              $940,000,000  
              $940,000,000  
              $0  
         Both sources total $940,000,000, but the split between the three named LPs and the remaining fifteen differs by $90,000,000. This matters because (a) it affects the per-LP capital-call amounts actually assessed (e.g., CC #2 assessed Silvermark $6,250,000, which is 5% of $125,000,000 per CC #2 Exhibit A but would be $3,750,000 if Silvermark’s commitment is $75,000,000 per the Admin Checklist); (b) these three LPs are Advisory Committee members, so their commitments are relevant to the Committee’s composition; and (c) the updated Schedule A (Condition 11.2(8)) must reflect the correct individual amounts.  
          Recommendation.         Reconcile the individual initial-closing commitment amounts against the executed Subscription Agreements and the Fund Administrator’s records before finalizing the updated Schedule A. The certificate’s Exhibit A (Schedule A) currently reflects the Admin Checklist figures; confirm those are correct.  
        Issue 6 — No identifiable         “    General Partner representations and warranties    ”         article in the LPA; mis-cited in Amendment No. 1 and the GP Memo (Section 11.2(10))  
          What the documents say.         Section 11.2(10) requires the General Partner to certify that         “    all representations and warranties of the General Partner contained in this Agreement are true and correct in all material respects.    ”         However, the LPA does not contain a dedicated article setting forth the General Partner’s representations and warranties. Article VI is titled         “    Representations, Warranties, and Covenants of             Limited Partners    ”         (i.e., LP representations, not GP representations). The General Partner’s statements in the LPA (e.g., the SEC-registration statement in Section 9.2) are scattered and are not framed as a discrete set of GP representations and warranties.  
      Two Source Documents purport to locate the GP representations, and they cite             different, and both incorrect, articles    :  
              Amendment No. 1, Section 2.2(a):         “    All representations and warranties of the General Partner set forth in             Article XII         of the Original Agreement are true and correct…    ”         Article XII of the LPA is         “    Confidentiality; Side Letters; MFN    ”         — not GP representations.  
              GP Memo, Section VI (Condition 10):         “    All representations and warranties of the General Partner set forth in             Article VII         of the LPA remain true and correct…    ”         Article VII of the LPA is         “    Management Fees and Expenses    ”         — not GP representations.  
          Recommendation.         Identify precisely which LPA provisions constitute         “    representations and warranties of the General Partner    ”         for purposes of Section 11.2(10) (and the parallel certification in Amendment No. 1, Section 2.2(a)), and correct the article cross-references in any future amendment or closing deliverable. The certificate’s Condition 10 certification should be tied to the specific provisions the General Partner intends to certify, rather than to a non-existent article.  
        Issue 7 — Default interest rate conflict: LPA 12% vs. Subscription Agreement 18% (Section 3.3(b))  
          What the documents say.         The LPA, Section 3.3(b), provides that a Defaulting Partner curing within the Cure Period must pay interest         “    at a rate of             twelve percent (12%) per annum         from the original Contribution Date through the date of payment.    ”         The Subscription Agreement, Section 1.2, by contrast, states that a defaulting subscriber may be subject to         “    default interest at a rate of the             lesser of 18% per annum         and the maximum rate permitted by applicable law.    ”         CC #1 (Section V) and CC #2 (Section V) both cite 12% (consistent with the LPA), although CC #2 adds         “    compounding quarterly,    ”         which the LPA does not specify (the LPA’s interest framework is simple, non-compounded).  
          Recommendation.         The Subscription Agreement’s 18% default rate is inconsistent with the LPA’s 12% and should be conformed to the LPA (the LPA controls under the Subscription Agreement’s own terms and the capital-call notices). The         “    compounding quarterly    ”         language in CC #2 should also be conformed to the LPA (simple interest). Confirm the controlling rate with the General Partner and correct the Subscription Agreement form for future use.  
        Issue 8 — Target Fund Size Advisory Committee notification omitted from the GP Memo action items (Section 3.1(d))  
          What the documents say.         After the Final Closing, aggregate Capital Commitments of $1,615,000,000 exceed the Target Fund Size of $1,500,000,000 by $115,000,000. The Final Closing is the closing at which the Target Fund Size is             first exceeded         (after the Second Closing, total commitments were $1,330,000,000, below the Target). Section 3.1(d) requires the General Partner to notify the Advisory Committee in writing of such excess, including the amount of the excess and the identities and commitments of the Partners admitted at the applicable Closing,             not later than five (5) Business Days following the Closing         at which the Target Fund Size is first exceeded. The Subscription Agreement (Sections 2.3 and 5.1) flags this notification as a condition to accepting commitments above the Fund Target. The GP Memo’s Section XII action-item list (items 1–8), however, does             not         include the Section 3.1(d) Advisory Committee notification.  
          Recommendation.         Add the Section 3.1(d) Advisory Committee notification to the closing action items. The notification must be delivered within five Business Days of the Final Closing (i.e., by approximately February 7, 2025, accounting for weekends). The certificate includes a certification regarding this notification; ensure the notification is in fact delivered within the required window.  
        Issue 9 — Numerous incorrect LPA cross-references across the Source Documents  
      Several Source Documents cite incorrect LPA sections or articles. These should be corrected (and, where they appear in executed documents such as Amendment No. 1 and the capital-call notices, noted for the record):  
                           Document  
          Stated reference  
          Correct LPA provision  
          Subject  
            Amendment No. 1 (recitals)  
          Section 11.1(b)  
          Section 3.1(b)  
          Subsequent closings authority  
            Amendment No. 1 (recitals & §2.1)  
          Section 14.3 / 14.3(b)  
          Section 14.1  
          GP amendment authority  
            Amendment No. 1 (recitals)  
          Section 3.1(a)  
          Section 3.1(c) / 3.1(d)  
          Hard Cap / Target Fund Size  
            Amendment No. 1 (§2.2(a))  
          Article XII  
          (no GP-reps article exists)  
          GP representations  
            Amendment No. 1 (§2.2(c))  
          “    the Investment Manager    ”  
          (undefined in LPA)  
          See Issue 11  
            Subscription Agreement (§1.2)  
          Section 3.5  
          Section 3.3  
          Default remedies  
            Subscription Agreement (§2.4(c), §8.8)  
          Article X  
          Article XIII  
          Transfer restrictions  
            Subscription Agreement (§8.2)  
          Section 14.6  
          Section 14.4  
          Dispute resolution / arbitration  
            Subscription Agreement (§8.9)  
          Section 14.2  
          Section 14.9  
          Power of attorney  
            Subscription Agreement (§3.4(b))  
          Articles IV and V  
          Article IV (allocations) and Article VIII (distributions)  
          Allocations and distributions  
            Subscription Agreement (§2.8)  
          Section 12.3  
          Section 12.2 (side letters); 12.3 is MFN  
          Side letters  
            CC #1 (§V, §VII)  
          Section 3.5 / Section 14.1  
          Section 3.3 / Section 14.2  
          Default remedies / Notices  
            CC #2 (§III, §V, §VIII)  
          Section 6.1 / Section 3.5 / Section 13.1  
          Section 7.1 / Section 3.3 / Section 14.2  
          Management fee / default / notices  
            GP Memo (§VIII)  
          Section 8.1  
          Section 2.7  
          Investment Period  
            GP Memo (§VI, Cond. 10)  
          Article VII  
          (no GP-reps article exists)  
          GP representations  
             Recommendation.         Correct the cross-references in the Subscription Agreement form and in any future amendments/closing deliverables. For the already-executed Amendment No. 1 and capital-call notices, consider a ministerial correction under Section 14.1 of the LPA or note the errors for the record. The LPA’s actual section numbering (e.g., Hard Cap at Section 3.1(c), Target Fund Size at Section 3.1(d), Notices at Section 14.2, Power of Attorney at Section 14.9, Arbitration at Section 14.4, Transfers at Article XIII, Investment Period at Section 2.7, Management Fee at Section 7.1, Default at Section 3.3) should govern.  
         V. Minor Issues  
       Issue 10 — CC #1 funding-date discrepancy (October 24 vs. October 25, 2024)  
      CC #1 states that the Drawdown Date is             October 25, 2024    , described as         “    ten (10) business days from the date of this Notice.    ”         CC #2 (Section I) states that         “    The Funding Date for Capital Call Notice No. 1 was             October 24, 2024    .    ”         Ten business days after October 10, 2024 is October 24, 2024 (Thursday). The one-day discrepancy does not affect equalization interest (which runs from the capital-call date, October 10, not the funding date), but the two notices should be consistent. Confirm the actual funding date for the records.  
        Issue 11 —         “    Investment Manager    ”         undefined term in Amendment No. 1  
      Amendment No. 1, Section 2.2(c), refers to         “    the General Partner,             the Investment Manager    , or their respective key persons.    ”         The LPA does not define a separate         “    Investment Manager    ”    ; Whitmore Capital Management LLC serves as both the General Partner and the investment adviser. The term is undefined and should be deleted or defined (e.g., as a reference to the General Partner in its capacity as investment adviser).  
        Issue 12 — Equalization day-count convention should be confirmed  
      As detailed in Issue 2, the LPA’s         “    from and including … through and including    ”         language supports a both-inclusive day count (114 days for CC #1; 48 days for CC #2), while the Subscription Agreement uses 113/47 and the Admin Checklist uses 113/46. The parties should confirm and document the intended convention. The dollar impact of the convention ranges across the figures in the Issue 2 table (a spread of approximately $12,493 in the aggregate total due).  
        Issue 13 — Governmental plans treated as Benefit Plan Investors — definitional tension  
      The LPA’s defined term         “    Benefit Plan Investor    ”         (Article I) is limited to (a) employee benefit plans subject to Title I of ERISA, (b) plans subject to Section 4975 of the Code, and (c) entities whose assets include         “    plan assets    ”         by reason of investment by the foregoing. Governmental plans (Section 3(32) of ERISA) and church plans (Section 3(33)) are generally excluded from that definition. The Subscription Agreement (Box C), however, states that governmental plans and church plans are         “    treated as benefit plan investors for purposes of the LPA Section 6.8 threshold calculation,    ”         and the Admin Checklist marks several governmental plans as         “    Y.    ”         This conservative overlay is not expressly supported by the LPA’s definition and contributes to the inconsistency in Issue 1. The treatment should be documented and applied consistently.  
        Issue 14 — Advisory Committee fifth seat vacant (post-closing obligation)  
      Pursuant to Section 9.1(b) of the LPA, one Advisory Committee seat remains vacant and is to be filled from among the Limited Partners admitted at the Final Closing. The GP Memo (Section XI) recommends approaching Meridian State Teachers’ Retirement System. This is a post-closing obligation (the General Partner must use commercially reasonable efforts to fill the vacancy within 30 days of the Final Closing) and is not a condition precedent under Section 11.2, but it should be tracked as a post-closing action item.  
        Issue 15 — Miscellaneous clerical items  
              GP Memo date.         The GP Memo is dated January 28, 2025, but the Admin Checklist (item 24) references a         “    GP Internal Closing Memorandum dated January 30, 2025.    ”         Confirm whether a revised memo was issued.  
              Qualified Purchaser section reference.         The Admin Checklist (items 12–17) cites         “    Subscription Agreement, Section 4(f)    ”         for the qualified-purchaser representations; the qualified-purchaser representation is in Section 2.2 of the Subscription Agreement (there is no Section 4(f)).  
              Subscription Agreement §3.4(a) / §1.3.         The Subscription Agreement references         “    Section 3.2(c)(iii) of the LPA    ”         for the equalization-interest distribution; Section 3.2(c) of the LPA is not subdivided into numbered subsections.  
              AML/KYC Email as the                 “        written completion certificate.        ”         Section 11.2(3) requires the Fund Administrator to         “    deliver a written AML/KYC completion certificate to the General Partner prior to each Closing.    ”         The AML/KYC Email from Mr. Prewitt (January 24, 2025) confirms completion and states Oakmere is         “    prepared to confirm this in any closing certificate or deliverable as required,    ”         but it is an email rather than a separately executed certificate. We recommend obtaining a short signed AML/KYC completion certificate from Oakmere to place in the closing binder, in addition to the email.  
         VI. Recommended Actions Before Executing the Final Closing Certificate  
              Resolve Issue 1 (ERISA).         Reconcile the Commitment Schedule         “    Y    ”         designations to the ERISA Analysis; recompute the BPI percentage under a single documented methodology; confirm in writing that it does not exceed 24.99%. If the nine         “    Y    ”         designations stand, the percentage is 28.81% and the non-waivable condition in Section 11.2(5) is not satisfied — do not execute the certificate.  
              Resolve Issue 2 (equalization interest).         Have the Fund Administrator issue a single final closing statement per incoming LP using one confirmed day-count convention; reconcile the wires to that statement (Issue 3).  
              Confirm Issue 3 (funds received).         Obtain the Fund Administrator’s written confirmation that all six Final Closing wires (catch-up capital + equalization interest) have been received in the escrow account.  
              Confirm Issue 4 (legal opinions).         Obtain the fully executed opinion set, confirming it expressly addresses the limited liability of the Limited Partners.  
              Resolve Issue 5 (Schedule A amounts).         Reconcile the individual initial-closing commitment amounts (Silvermark, Commonwealth, Graystone) against executed Subscription Agreements before finalizing the updated Schedule A.  
              Address Issue 6 (GP reps).         Identify the specific LPA provisions being certified under Section 11.2(10) and correct the article cross-references.  
              Conform Issue 7 (default rate).         Confirm the controlling default interest rate (12% per the LPA) and conform the Subscription Agreement form.  
              Add Issue 8 (Target notification).         Deliver the Section 3.1(d) Advisory Committee notification within five Business Days of the Final Closing.  
              Correct Issue 9 (cross-references)         in the Subscription Agreement form and future deliverables; note the errors in executed documents for the record.  
              Obtain a signed AML/KYC completion certificate         from Oakmere (Issue 15) for the closing binder.  
        VII. Conclusion  
      The Fund is, on the whole, positioned to proceed with the Final Closing, and most of the Section 11.2 conditions are documented as satisfied. However, four             critical         items — the ERISA/Benefit Plan Investor calculation (Issue 1), the equalization-interest reconciliation (Issue 2), confirmation of receipt of capital contributions (Issue 3), and delivery of the legal opinions including the limited-liability opinion (Issue 4) — must be resolved before the General Partner executes the Final Closing Certificate. Issue 1 is the most serious: under a straightforward reading of the Admin Checklist’s own designations, the non-waivable 24.99% ERISA cap would be exceeded, which would preclude the Final Closing. We recommend that the General Partner not execute the certificate until Items 1–4 above are confirmed resolved and the remaining significant items are addressed or noted.  
      We are available to assist with each of the foregoing items and to finalize the certificate upon resolution.  
      Respectfully submitted,  
          Bridgeford Hollis LLP  
      David R. Kasten, Partner  
      Emily T. Naraghi, Associate  
      77 West Monroe Street, Suite 4400, Chicago, IL 60603  
          CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGED — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT. This memorandum is prepared by Fund Counsel for Whitmore Capital Management LLC in its capacity as General Partner of Whitmore Capital Partners VII, LP, and for the Fund. It is intended solely for the use of the addressees and should not be distributed, reproduced, or disclosed to any third party without the prior written consent of Bridgeford Hollis LLP.  
     

### FILE: final-closing-certificate.docx
             Drafter’s note (not part of the executed certificate). This certificate is a draft prepared by Bridgeford Hollis LLP (Fund Counsel) for execution by the General Partner at the Final Closing on January 31, 2025. It certifies each condition precedent to the Final Closing under Section 11.2 of the Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership (the                 “        LPA        ”        ). Certain figures reflected below — in particular the ERISA/benefit plan investor percentage (Condition 5) and the equalization interest component of the capital contributions (Condition 2) — are drawn from the deal team’s stated calculations and remain subject to the reconciliation items identified in the accompanying memorandum,                 “        Closing Issues Memo.        ”                 The General Partner should not execute this certificate until those items are resolved, because Conditions 11.2(3), 11.2(4), and 11.2(5) may not be waived under Section 11.3 of the LPA.  
       FINAL CLOSING CERTIFICATE  
       WHITMORE CAPITAL PARTNERS VII, LP  
          Delivered pursuant to Section 11.2 and Section 9.4(d) of the Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership  
          Date of Final Closing: January 31, 2025  
      
      This Final Closing Certificate (this         “    Certificate    ”    ) is executed and delivered by             Whitmore Capital Management LLC    , a Delaware limited liability company, in its capacity as general partner (the         “    General Partner    ”         or         “    GP    ”    ) of             Whitmore Capital Partners VII, LP    , a Delaware limited partnership (the         “    Partnership    ”         or the         “    Fund    ”    ), in connection with the final closing of the Fund (the         “    Final Closing    ”    ) held on January 31, 2025. Capitalized terms used but not defined in this Certificate have the meanings ascribed to them in the Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership of the Partnership, dated as of September 15, 2024, as amended by Amendment No. 1, dated as of November 30, 2024 (as so amended, the         “    LPA    ”         or the         “    Partnership Agreement    ”    ).  
        RECITALS  
      WHEREAS, the Partnership was formed as a Delaware limited partnership on August 1, 2024, pursuant to a Certificate of Limited Partnership filed with the Secretary of State of the State of Delaware (File No. 7842319), and Whitmore Capital Management LLC serves as the General Partner of the Partnership;  
      WHEREAS, the Partnership held its Initial Closing on September 15, 2024, at which eighteen (18) Limited Partners were admitted with aggregate Limited Partner Capital Commitments of $940,000,000, together with the General Partner’s Capital Commitment of $15,000,000, for aggregate Capital Commitments of $955,000,000;  
      WHEREAS, the Partnership held a Subsequent Closing (the         “    Second Closing    ”    ) on November 30, 2024, at which nine (9) additional Limited Partners were admitted with aggregate Limited Partner Capital Commitments of $375,000,000, bringing aggregate Capital Commitments to $1,330,000,000;  
      WHEREAS, the Partnership is now holding the Final Closing on January 31, 2025, at which six (6) additional Limited Partners are being admitted with aggregate Limited Partner Capital Commitments of $285,000,000, as set forth below:  
                         #  
          Incoming Limited Partner  
          Capital Commitment  
            1  
          Meridian State Teachers’ Retirement System  
          $100,000,000  
            2  
          Haverfield Family Office LLC  
          $40,000,000  
            3  
          Northbridge Endowment Fund  
          $60,000,000  
            4  
          Cascade Re Insurance Ltd.  
          $35,000,000  
            5  
          Sentry Municipal Employees’ Pension Trust  
          $30,000,000  
            6  
          Kingsgate Ventures Fund-of-Funds III, LP  
          $20,000,000  
         
              Total Final Closing LP Commitments  
              $285,000,000  
         WHEREAS, after giving effect to the Final Closing, the aggregate Capital Commitments of all Partners (including the GP Commitment) will be $1,615,000,000, comprised of $1,600,000,000 in aggregate Limited Partner Capital Commitments and the $15,000,000 GP Commitment;  
      WHEREAS, Section 11.2 of the LPA sets forth ten (10) conditions precedent to each Closing, which must be satisfied or waived by the General Partner (to the extent waivable) prior to the consummation of the Final Closing, and Section 11.3 of the LPA provides that the conditions set forth in Section 11.2(3) (AML/KYC Compliance), Section 11.2(4) (Qualified Purchaser Status), and Section 11.2(5) (ERISA Compliance) may not be waived under any circumstances;  
      WHEREAS, Section 9.4(d) of the LPA requires the General Partner to certify at each Closing that each Key Person continues to devote substantially all of his or her business time and efforts to the Partnership and that no Key Person Event has occurred or is reasonably expected to occur as of the date of such Closing; and  
      WHEREAS, the General Partner is delivering this Certificate to the Limited Partners, the Fund Administrator (Oakmere Fund Services LLC), and Fund Counsel (Bridgeford Hollis LLP) as a closing deliverable in connection with the Final Closing.  
      NOW, THEREFORE, the undersigned, being the Managing Member and Chief Investment Officer of the General Partner and duly authorized to execute this Certificate on behalf of the General Partner, hereby certifies as follows as of the date of the Final Closing:  
        CERTIFICATIONS  
          1. Condition Precedent — Subscription Agreements and LPA Signature Pages (Section 11.2(1)).         Each of the six (6) incoming Limited Partners being admitted at the Final Closing has duly executed and delivered (i) a Subscription Agreement, in the form provided by the General Partner, containing all of the representations, warranties, and covenants required by Article VI of the LPA, and (ii) a counterpart signature page to the LPA or a joinder agreement in the form attached to the LPA as Exhibit B. Executed originals of each such Subscription Agreement and signature page/joinder are on file with the General Partner and Fund Counsel. This condition is satisfied.  
          2. Condition Precedent — Capital Contributions (Section 11.2(2)).         All Capital Contributions due at the Final Closing — including, with respect to the incoming Limited Partners, the catch-up (equalization) Capital Contributions and Equalization Interest required under Section 3.2(c) of the LPA in respect of Capital Call Notice No. 1 (dated October 10, 2024) and Capital Call Notice No. 2 (dated December 15, 2024) — have been received in immediately available funds in the escrow account of the Partnership at Pinnacle National Bank (account ending in 9213) designated by the General Partner. The aggregate catch-up Capital Contribution of the incoming Limited Partners is $42,750,000 (equal to 15% of the aggregate Final Closing Capital Commitments of $285,000,000), together with Equalization Interest calculated by the Fund Administrator at the Equalization Interest Rate of 8% per annum (simple interest) and set forth in the Fund Administrator’s closing statement delivered to each incoming Limited Partner. The Fund Administrator has confirmed in writing receipt of all required funds prior to the consummation of the Final Closing. This condition is satisfied.  
          3. Condition Precedent — AML/KYC Compliance (Section 11.2(3)).         The Fund Administrator (Oakmere Fund Services LLC) has delivered a written AML/KYC completion certificate to the General Partner confirming that satisfactory anti-money laundering and         “    know your customer    ”         documentation has been received and verified for each incoming Limited Partner, including (without limitation) OFAC/SDN and sanctions screening results, government-issued identification or corporate formation documents (as applicable), and, where applicable, beneficial ownership certifications under the Corporate Transparency Act and the Customer Due Diligence Rule (31 C.F.R. § 1010.230). No adverse findings were identified for any incoming Limited Partner. This condition is satisfied and, pursuant to Section 11.3 of the LPA, was not waived.  
          4. Condition Precedent — Qualified Purchaser Status (Section 11.2(4)).         Each incoming Limited Partner has represented and warranted in its Subscription Agreement that it is a         “    qualified purchaser    ”         as defined in Section 2(a)(51) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, as amended. The General Partner has reviewed each such representation and the supporting documentation provided by each incoming Limited Partner and has no reason to believe that any such representation is untrue. Following the Final Closing, the Fund will have 34 beneficial owners (33 Limited Partners plus the General Partner), well within the 2,000-qualified-purchaser limit under Section 3(c)(7) of the Investment Company Act. This condition is satisfied and, pursuant to Section 11.3 of the LPA, was not waived.  
          5. Condition Precedent — ERISA Compliance (Section 11.2(5)).         The General Partner has determined that, after giving effect to the admission of all incoming Limited Partners at the Final Closing, the aggregate Capital Commitments of all Benefit Plan Investors are $355,300,000, which represents 22.00% of the total Capital Commitments of all Partners (including the GP Commitment) of $1,615,000,000, and therefore does not exceed the limitation of 24.99% set forth in Section 6.8 of the LPA. The General Partner has documented such determination in writing, and such calculation is included in the closing deliverables provided to the Fund Administrator and Fund Counsel and reflected on the updated Schedule A attached hereto as Exhibit A. This condition is satisfied and, pursuant to Section 11.3 of the LPA, was not waived.  
          6. Condition Precedent — No Material Adverse Change; Key Persons (Section 11.2(6)).         No material adverse change has occurred with respect to the General Partner, the Partnership, or the General Partner’s ability to manage the Partnership since the Second Closing on November 30, 2024. Each Key Person (as defined in Section 9.4 of the LPA) continues to devote substantially all of his or her business time and efforts to the Partnership, and no Key Person Event has occurred or is reasonably expected to occur as of the date of the Final Closing. This condition is satisfied.  
          7. Condition Precedent — Legal Opinions (Section 11.2(7)).         Fund Counsel (Bridgeford Hollis LLP) has delivered to the General Partner and the Fund Administrator legal opinions, dated as of the date of the Final Closing, in customary form and substance for transactions of this type, addressing: (i) the due formation and valid existence of the Partnership as a limited partnership under the laws of the State of Delaware; (ii) the enforceability of the LPA against the Partnership and the General Partner; and (iii) such other matters as are customary for private equity fund closings, including the limited liability of the Limited Partners. This condition is satisfied.  
          8. Condition Precedent — Updated Schedule A (Section 11.2(8)).         The General Partner has caused the Fund Administrator to prepare an updated Schedule A to the LPA reflecting the Capital Commitments of all Partners after giving effect to the Final Closing, including the names, Capital Commitments, dates of admission, Benefit Plan Investor status, and qualified purchaser confirmations of all Partners. Such updated Schedule A is attached hereto as Exhibit A and has been delivered to all Partners and the Fund Administrator concurrently with the Final Closing. This condition is satisfied.  
          9. Condition Precedent — Hard Cap Compliance (Section 11.2(9)).         The General Partner has confirmed that, after giving effect to the admission of all incoming Limited Partners at the Final Closing, the aggregate Capital Commitments of all Partners (including the GP Commitment) are $1,615,000,000, which does not exceed the Hard Cap of $1,750,000,000 set forth in Section 3.1(c) of the LPA. The General Partner has documented such confirmation in writing. This condition is satisfied.  
          10. Condition Precedent — Representations and Warranties of the General Partner (Section 11.2(10)).         The General Partner hereby certifies that all representations and warranties of the General Partner contained in the LPA are true and correct in all material respects as of the date of the Final Closing as though made on and as of such date, except for representations and warranties that by their terms relate to a specific date, which are true and correct in all material respects as of such specified date. This condition is satisfied.  
        KEY PERSON CERTIFICATION (Section 9.4(d))  
      In addition to the certification set forth in paragraph 6 above, and pursuant to Section 9.4(d) of the LPA, the General Partner hereby certifies that each Key Person — Marcus J. Whitmore (Founder and Chief Investment Officer of the General Partner) and Priya S. Mehta (Managing Director of the General Partner) — continues to devote substantially all of his or her business time and efforts to the affairs of the Partnership, and that no Key Person Event (as defined in Section 9.4(b) of the LPA) has occurred or is reasonably expected to occur as of the date of this Certificate. The General Partner acknowledges that the Limited Partners are relying on the continued involvement of each Key Person in making their investment decisions and funding their Capital Commitments.  
        ADDITIONAL CERTIFICATIONS  
          Target Fund Size Notification (Section 3.1(d)).         The General Partner certifies that, after giving effect to the Final Closing, aggregate Capital Commitments of $1,615,000,000 exceed the Target Fund Size of $1,500,000,000 by $115,000,000, and the General Partner will promptly (and in any event not later than five (5) Business Days following the Final Closing) notify the Advisory Committee in writing of such excess, the amount by which the Target Fund Size has been exceeded, and the identities and Capital Commitments of the Partners admitted at the Final Closing, as required by Section 3.1(d) of the LPA.  
          Advisory Committee.         The General Partner certifies that the Advisory Committee is constituted in accordance with Section 9.1 of the LPA and currently consists of four (4) members, with one (1) seat remaining vacant to be filled from among the Limited Partners admitted at the Final Closing in accordance with Section 9.1(b) of the LPA.  
          Service Providers.         The General Partner certifies that the Fund’s service providers — Oakmere Fund Services LLC (Fund Administrator), Bridgeford Hollis LLP (Fund Counsel), Clearstone Trust Company (Custodian), and Pinnacle National Bank (depository) — remain engaged and in good standing as of the date of the Final Closing.  
          No Defaulting Partners.         The General Partner certifies that no Partner is in default of any obligation to fund Capital Contributions under the LPA as of the date of the Final Closing.  
        RELIANCE AND LIMITATIONS  
      This Certificate is delivered solely in connection with the Final Closing and is based upon the General Partner’s review of the closing deliverables, the records of the Fund Administrator, and the information provided by the incoming Limited Partners in their respective Subscription Agreements and supporting documentation. The certifications set forth herein are made as of the date of the Final Closing only and speak only as of such date. Except as expressly permitted by Section 11.3 of the LPA, no condition precedent set forth in Section 11.2 of the LPA has been waived in connection with the Final Closing, and the non-waivable conditions set forth in Section 11.2(3), 11.2(4), and 11.2(5) have been satisfied in accordance with the requirements of the LPA.  
      IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the undersigned has executed this Final Closing Certificate on behalf of the General Partner as of the date first written above.  
          WHITMORE CAPITAL MANAGEMENT LLC    , in its capacity as General Partner of Whitmore Capital Partners VII, LP  
      By: _______________________________  
      Name: Marcus J. Whitmore  
      Title: Managing Member and Chief Investment Officer  
      Date: January 31, 2025

### FILE: h1b-issue-memo.docx
          MEMORANDUM  
          TO:         File / Supervising Attorney             FROM:         Immigration Paralegal Review Team             DATE:         April 2025             RE:         Issue Identification — H-1B Petition on Behalf of Rajesh Venkataraman (Brightfield Analytics Inc., Senior Machine Learning Engineer)  
      
       I. Executive Summary  
      This memorandum identifies issues arising from a cross-reference of the ten documents in the H-1B petition package for             Rajesh Venkataraman    , to be employed by             Brightfield Analytics Inc.         as a             Senior Machine Learning Engineer         (SOC 15-2051, Data Scientists) at $128,000/year, with an employment period of October 1, 2025 through September 30, 2028. The review identified             twenty-four (24) issues    , ranging from potential petition-denial risks to minor data inconsistencies.  
      The most serious issues are:  
              Field-of-study mismatch         — the beneficiary’s degree is in Electronics and Communication Engineering (ECE), while the position requires Computer Science, Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, or a         “    closely related    ”         field, and the credential evaluation expressly declines to opine on field-of-study fit. Only ~6.5–7.5% of the coursework is computer-related under the evaluator’s own narrow count.  
              Wage-level misclassification risk         — a senior, supervisory position exercising         “    independent judgment    ”         was classified at DOL Wage Level 2 (    “    limited judgment,    ”         “    moderately complex tasks    ”    ). The duties more closely match Level 3 ($137,946) or Level 4 ($162,157); at Level 3 the offered $128,000 wage would fall             $9,946 below         the prevailing wage, falsifying the LCA wage attestation.  
              Undocumented experience         — three years of claimed independent machine-learning consulting (August 2020–August 2023), the very period in which the required TensorFlow/PyTorch/AWS/LLM skills were allegedly acquired, is supported by no experience letter, client letter, or 1099 evidence.  
              LCA posting deficiencies         — the worksite posting ran only             8 business days         (March 10–19, 2025) versus the 10-consecutive-business-day requirement, and only             one         posting location is documented versus the two required.  
              Cross-document date and data inconsistencies         — the resume overstates the Kiran and NovaStar end dates (concealing a ~3-month employment gap), the support letter and org chart disagree on the number of direct reports (3 vs. 2), and the posting notice lists the beneficiary’s personal cell number as the employer telephone.  
      A consolidated issue table appears at Section VII.  
      
        II. Documents Reviewed  
                          #  
          Document  
          Date / Reference  
            1  
          Beneficiary Resume (Rajesh Venkataraman)  
          undated  
            2  
          Certified Labor Condition Application (LCA)  
          Case No. I-200-25031-543219; certified March 10, 2025  
            3  
          Foreign Credential Evaluation (Westbridge Credential Services)  
          Report No. WCS-2025-03187; February 18, 2025  
            4  
          Employer Support Letter (Brightfield Analytics Inc.)  
          April 7, 2025  
            5  
          JNTU Hyderabad Official Transcript  
          Ref. JNTUH/ECE/2014/08347; issued June 12, 2014  
            6  
          Kiran Software Solutions Experience Letter  
          February 18, 2025  
            7  
          LCA Posting Notice (Brightfield Analytics Inc.)  
          posted March 10–19, 2025  
            8  
          NovaStar Data Systems Experience Letter  
          March 5, 2025  
            9  
          Organizational Chart (Data Science & ML Group)  
          March 2025  
            10  
          Prevailing Wage Determination (NPWC/DOL)  
          PWD No. P-300-25012-183456; January 15, 2025  
         
        III. Foundational Facts  
          Parties.  
              Petitioner / Employer:         Brightfield Analytics Inc. — Delaware C-Corporation, founded 2017, headquartered at 4200 Congress Avenue, Suite 1100, Austin, TX 78701; NAICS 541511 (Custom Computer Programming Services); 187 full-time employees; gross annual revenue $41,200,000 (FY2023); FEIN 84-3291756; CEO Margaret         “    Maggie    ”         Torrance; HR point of contact Patricia Kwon, HR Director.  
              Beneficiary:         Rajesh Venkataraman — DOB March 14, 1992; citizen of India; currently maintaining F-1 Optional Practical Training (OPT) status; residence 1807 Barton Springs Rd, Apt 4B, Austin, TX 78704.  
              Counsel of Record:         Daniel Okafor, Holloway Sterne LLP, 700 Lavaca Street, Suite 2400, Austin, TX 78701; TX Bar No. 24098172.  
          Proffered Position.  
              Title:         Senior Machine Learning Engineer.  
          **SOC / O*NET:** 15-2051.00 — Data Scientists.  
              Worksite:         4200 Congress Avenue, Suite 1100, Austin, TX 78701 (Travis County, TX) — single worksite; no additional worksites stated.  
              Employment period:         October 1, 2025 – September 30, 2028 (3 years).  
              Basis:         Full-time, 40 hours/week.  
              Wage:         $128,000/year (bi-weekly).  
              Stated minimum education:         Bachelor’s degree in Computer Science, Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, or a closely related field (LCA § G.6–G.7; Support Letter § 3). Support Letter § 3 also offers an         “    alternative    ”         of a bachelor’s in         “    any engineering discipline    ”         plus 5 years of progressive ML experience.  
              Stated minimum experience:         3 years of progressive experience in machine learning engineering (Support Letter § 3; PWD § 3 —         “    36 months (3 years)    ”    ).  
          Labor Condition Application.  
              LCA Case No.:         I-200-25031-543219;             Status:         CERTIFIED;             Certification Date:         March 10, 2025;             Validity:         October 1, 2025 – September 30, 2028.
