        IV. Issues Identified  
      Issues are grouped by subject matter. Each issue states the finding, the source, why it matters, and a recommended fix. Severity is marked             [HIGH]    ,             [MEDIUM]    , or             [LOW]    .  
       A. Beneficiary Qualifications & Specialty Occupation  
          Issue 1 — Field-of-study mismatch between the beneficiary’s degree and the required major. [HIGH]  
              Finding:         The position requires a bachelor’s degree in         “    Computer Science, Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, or a closely related field    ”         (LCA § G.7; Support Letter § 3). The beneficiary’s degree is a B.Tech in             Electronics and Communication Engineering         (Resume; Credential Evaluation § 1; Transcript; Support Letter § 4). The credential evaluation (WCS-2025-03187, § 5) equates the degree to a U.S. B.S. in             Electronics and Communication Engineering         — not in CS/ML/AI — and expressly states in § 7 that         “    [t]his evaluation does not opine on whether the applicant’s field of study satisfies the requirements of any particular position or petition.    ”         The evaluation’s own program analysis (§ 4) concludes that         “    computer science and information technology-related coursework constitutes approximately 12 credit hours out of the total approximately 160 credit hours … or approximately 7.5% of the overall curriculum.    ”  
              Why it matters:         Whether ECE is         “    closely related    ”         to CS/ML/AI is the central qualification question. The record contains no expert opinion bridging that gap; the only support is the Support Letter’s conclusory statement (§ 4) that coursework in         “    programming, data structures, and artificial intelligence    ”         provides         “    a strong technical foundation.    ”         With only ~6.5–7.5% of the curriculum being computer-related (and the evaluator declining to opine on fit), USCIS may issue an RFE or deny on the basis that the beneficiary does not hold a degree in the required specialty. See 8 CFR 214.2(h)(4)(iii)(A) (degree must be in the specialty or a closely related field).  
              Recommendation:         Obtain a supplemental, position-specific expert opinion (preferably from a university professor or a NACES evaluator willing to opine on fit) establishing that the ECE coursework — including Computer Architecture (EC602), Microprocessors (EC503), Digital Logic Design (EC402), Digital Image Processing (EC604), Probability & Stochastic Processes (MT401), and the Intro to AI elective (EC704) — provides the theoretical foundation for machine learning engineering and that ECE is         “    closely related    ”         to the required field. Alternatively, rely squarely on the experience-equivalency path (Issue 12) and document it fully.  
          Issue 2 — Wage-level classification appears understated for the duties described. [HIGH]  
              Finding:         The LCA (§ F.4) and PWD (§ 4) classify the position at             Wage Level 2         ($113,734), described by the PWD as applying to workers who         “    perform moderately complex tasks requiring limited judgment.    ”         Yet the Support Letter (§ 2) describes a             “        Senior        ”         role that         “    exercises independent judgment in the design and implementation of machine learning solutions,    ”         and whose Duty 4 is         “    Leading and mentoring a team of 3 junior data scientists … providing technical guidance on model development, code review, and best practices.    ”         The PWD’s own level definitions place         “    independent judgment    ”         and         “    supervisory responsibilities    ”         at             Level 3         ($137,946) and         “    supervise and train other workers    ”         at             Level 4         ($162,157).  
              Why it matters:         The offered wage of $128,000             exceeds         the Level 2 prevailing wage ($113,734) by $14,266 but             falls below         Level 3 ($137,946) by $9,946 and below Level 4 ($162,157) by $34,157. If the correct level is 3 or 4, the wage offered is below the prevailing wage, the LCA wage attestation (INA § 212(n)(1)(A); LCA § K.1) would be false, and the petition is vulnerable to denial (and the employer to back-wage liability). The         “    senior    ”         title, the independent-judgment language, and the supervisory/mentoring duties are inconsistent with Level 2’s         “    limited judgment.    ”  
              Recommendation:         Re-assess the wage level against the DOL criteria. If the duties genuinely involve independent judgment and supervision, request a new PWD at Level 3 (or 4) and, if necessary, increase the proffered wage to at least $137,946 (Level 3) before filing, or re-file the LCA at the correct level. If Level 2 is defended, revise the Support Letter to remove or soften the         “    independent judgment    ”         and         “    lead/mentor a team    ”         language so the duties are consistent with Level 2’s         “    limited judgment    ”         — but note this creates tension with the specialty-occupation and         “    senior    ”         framing (see Issue 6).  
          Issue 3 — Three years of claimed progressive experience are undocumented. [HIGH]  
              Finding:         The Resume and Support Letter (§ 4) claim         “    Independent Machine Learning Consultant    ”         experience from August 2020 to August 2023 — three years during which the beneficiary allegedly         “    Designed and deployed end-to-end machine learning systems using TensorFlow, PyTorch, and AWS SageMaker    ”         and         “    Implemented large language model (LLM) fine-tuning workflows.    ”         No experience letter, client letter, 1099, contract, or business registration is included for this period. By contrast, the Kiran and NovaStar periods are each supported by a signed experience letter.  
              Why it matters:         This three-year window is the only documented period in which the specific tools required by the position — TensorFlow, PyTorch, AWS SageMaker, and LLM fine-tuning (Support Letter § 3) — were used (the NovaStar letter references only Python, scikit-learn, Tableau, SQL, and A/B testing). Without corroboration, USCIS may decline to credit this experience, undermining both the primary qualification showing and the alternative         “    5 years of progressive ML experience    ”         path (Issue 12). Self-employment is creditable only if documented.  
              Recommendation:         Obtain letters from at least two consulting clients (on client letterhead, signed by a supervisor/engagement lead, detailing projects, dates, tools, and the beneficiary’s role), plus 1099s or invoices and any business-registration evidence. If client contact is no longer possible, supplement with a detailed sworn declaration from the beneficiary describing each engagement, deliverables, and technologies, supported by artifacts (project repositories, model documentation, deployment records).  
          Issue 4 — Current title (        “        Data Scientist        ”        ) differs from the petitioned title (        “        Senior Machine Learning Engineer        ”        ). [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The Resume lists the beneficiary’s current Brightfield role as             “        Data Scientist        ”         (September 2023 – present). The petitioned position is             “        Senior Machine Learning Engineer.        ”         The Support Letter (§ 4) lists the Brightfield tenure but does not state the current title or explain the relationship between the current role and the petitioned role.  
              Why it matters:         Because the beneficiary is already employed by the petitioner (on F-1 OPT), USCIS may question whether the         “    Senior Machine Learning Engineer    ”         position is a bona fide new/senior role or merely a reclassification of the existing Data Scientist duties. An unexplained title change can also complicate the specialty-occupation and wage-level analysis (a         “    senior    ”         supervisory role vs. an individual-contributor Data Scientist role).  
              Recommendation:         Add a paragraph to the Support Letter explaining the relationship: whether the Senior ML Engineer role is a promotion/new position with materially different duties (supervision, infrastructure ownership, client-facing leadership) versus the current Data Scientist role, and confirm the beneficiary will move into the petitioned role on October 1, 2025. Consider including a current Brightfield experience/role letter.  
          Issue 5 — SOC classification (15-2051 Data Scientists) vs.         “        Engineer        ”                 title and software-engineering duties. [LOW/MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The position is titled         “    Senior Machine Learning             Engineer    ”         and includes substantial software-engineering and cloud-infrastructure duties — Duty 2 (    “    Architecting and managing cloud-based ML infrastructure on AWS … SageMaker, EC2, S3, and Lambda    ”    ) and Duty 3 (    “    production Python codebases … TensorFlow, PyTorch, and scikit-learn    ”    ) — yet it is mapped to SOC 15-2051 (Data Scientists) rather than, e.g., 15-1252 (Software Developers) or 15-1221 (Computer and Information Research Scientists).  
              Why it matters:         15-2051 is defensible for an ML role, but the engineering/infrastructure emphasis creates a mismatch between the SOC and a portion of the duties. USCIS occasionally questions SOC selection where the title and a material portion of duties point elsewhere; the wage level and specialty-occupation analysis both depend on the SOC.  
              Recommendation:         Confirm 15-2051 is the best fit (it likely is, given the modeling/NLP/LLM core). Be prepared to defend the SOC against an RFE by emphasizing that the majority of duties (model design/deployment, NLP/LLM, analytics presentation) fall within Data Scientists, and that the infrastructure duties are in service of the ML function.  
        B. Wage & LCA Compliance  
          Issue 6 — Internal inconsistency between the                 “        senior/supervisory        ”                 duties and the Level 2 wage level. [HIGH]  
              Finding:         See Issue 2. The Support Letter’s         “    senior,    ”         “    independent judgment,    ”         and         “    lead and mentor a team    ”         framing is internally inconsistent with the Level 2 wage level the employer requested and the LCA certified. The employer cannot simultaneously assert Level 2 (    “    limited judgment,    ”         “    moderately complex tasks    ”    ) and a senior supervisory role with independent judgment.  
              Why it matters:         This inconsistency is the mechanism by which USCIS or DOL could challenge the wage level. It also cuts against the specialty-occupation narrative if the duties are later softened to defend Level 2.  
              Recommendation:         Reconcile the duties and the wage level as a single, coherent position description (see Issue 2). Do not leave the         “    senior/supervisory    ”         and         “    Level 2/limited judgment    ”         characterizations side by side.  
          Issue 7 — LCA posting ran only 8 business days; 10 consecutive business days required. [MEDIUM/HIGH]  
              Finding:         The LCA (§ J.2) and Posting Notice state the notice was posted March 10, 2025 and removed March 19, 2025. March 10, 2025 is a Monday; the inclusive period March 10–19, 2025 contains only             8 business days         (Mar 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 17, 18, 19). 20 CFR § 655.734(a)(1) requires posting         “    for a total of 10 consecutive business days.    ”         To satisfy the 10-business-day requirement beginning March 10, the notice would have had to remain posted through             Friday, March 21, 2025    .  
              Why it matters:         A posting shorter than 10 consecutive business days is a regulatory violation of the LCA notice attestation (INA § 212(n)(1)(A)(iv); 20 CFR § 655.734). It exposes the employer to DOL enforcement (back wages, civil money penalties) and undermines the LCA on which the petition depends.  
              Recommendation:         Re-post the notice for a full 10 consecutive business days in two conspicuous locations, document the dates and locations, and retain evidence (photos, signed posting log) in the Public Access File. If the LCA must be re-filed to support the petition, ensure the new posting precedes or coincides with the new filing and runs the full 10 business days.  
          Issue 8 — Only one posting location documented; two conspicuous locations required. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The LCA (§ J.3) documents a single posting location —         “    Employee common area / break room, first floor.    ”         The Posting Notice itself states the notice         “    is being posted in two conspicuous locations at the place of employment,    ”         but only one location is identified anywhere in the package. 20 CFR § 655.734(a)(1) requires posting         “    in at least two conspicuous locations.    ”  
              Why it matters:         Documenting only one location is inconsistent with the regulation and with the Posting Notice’s own representation, and is a notice-attestation deficiency.  
              Recommendation:         Identify and document a second conspicuous posting location (e.g., a second break area, the main entrance bulletin board, or the HR office), and retain evidence of both postings in the Public Access File.  
          Issue 9 — Consulting business model vs. single-worksite LCA representation; possible itinerary need. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         Brightfield is a consulting firm (NAICS 541511,         “    Custom Computer Programming Services    ”    ) that         “    provides advanced data analytics, machine learning consulting, and AI-driven product development services to enterprise clients across healthcare, financial services, and technology sectors    ”         (Support Letter § 1). The LCA (§ H.2) nevertheless states         “    Additional Worksites: None (single worksite only),    ”         and the Support Letter fixes the work location at the Austin HQ. The company also maintains         “    a satellite office in Denver, Colorado, and a small distributed remote team    ”         (Support Letter § 1).  
              Why it matters:         If the beneficiary will perform any work at client sites, at the Denver office, or remotely (including from home), the LCA’s single-worksite representation is incomplete and additional LCA worksite coverage and an itinerary are required (8 CFR § 214.2(h)(2)(i)(B), (h)(11)(i)(A); 20 CFR § 655.733). An inaccurate worksite representation is an LCA violation and a petition vulnerability.  
              Recommendation:         Confirm in writing that the beneficiary will perform all work exclusively at the Austin HQ. If any off-site, client-site, Denver, or remote work is contemplated, file an additional LCA covering each worksite and include an itinerary with the petition.  
        C. Experience Documentation & Cross-Document Consistency  
          Issue 10 — Resume overstates Kiran and NovaStar end dates and conceals a ~3-month gap. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:  
              Kiran:         Resume says         “    June 2014 – March 2017    ”    ; the Kiran experience letter says employment         “    commenced on June 16, 2014 and concluded on December 9, 2016    ”    ; the Support Letter says         “    June 2014 – December 2016.    ”         The resume extends Kiran by ~3 months.  
              NovaStar:         Resume says         “    April 2017 – August 2020    ”    ; the NovaStar experience letter and the Support Letter say         “    April 2017 – July 2020.    ”         The resume extends NovaStar by ~1 month.  
          Per the experience letters, there is a             ~3-month gap         (December 9, 2016 – April 1, 2017 = 113 days) between Kiran and NovaStar that the resume conceals by extending Kiran to March 2017.  
              Why it matters:         Inconsistencies between the resume and the signed experience letters/support letter undermine the beneficiary’s credibility and the reliability of the experience record. The concealed gap may prompt USCIS to question the beneficiary’s activities and status during January–March 2017.  
              Recommendation:         Correct the resume to match the experience letters (Kiran ending December 2016; NovaStar April 2017 – July 2020) and be prepared to account for the January–March 2017 period (e.g., job search, return to India, etc.). Ensure every date in the petition package is reconciled to the experience letters.  
          Issue 11 — Support letter and org chart disagree on the number of direct reports (3 vs. 2). [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         Support Letter § 2, Duty 4 states the role involves         “    Leading and mentoring a team of             3         junior data scientists.    ”         The Organizational Chart shows the Senior Machine Learning Engineer with only             2         direct reports (two         “    Data Scientist (Filled)    ”         positions at Level 4). The LCA job duties (§ G.5) say         “    lead and mentor a team of data scientists    ”         without a number.  
              Why it matters:         The discrepancy is an internal inconsistency USCIS may cite. It also bears directly on the wage-level analysis (Issue 2/6): the supervisory scope is smaller than represented, which both weakens the         “    Level 3/4    ”         argument and shows the Support Letter overstates the position.  
              Recommendation:         Reconcile the number. If the team is two, correct the Support Letter to         “    2 junior data scientists    ”         and update the org chart/support letter to match. If a third report is planned, document it (e.g., an approved requisition) and make all documents consistent.  
          Issue 12 —                 “        Alternative qualification        ”                 formulation is inconsistent with the primary requirement and the LCA. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         Support Letter § 3 states the minimum education as         “    Computer Science, Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, or a closely related field,    ”         then offers an         “    Alternative Qualification    ”         of         “    a Bachelor’s degree in             any engineering discipline         combined with 5 years of progressive experience in machine learning.    ”         The LCA (§ G.7) lists only         “    Computer Science, Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, or closely related field    ”         as the major field of study — it does not reflect the         “    any engineering + 5 years    ”         alternative.  
              Why it matters:         (a) The         “    any engineering discipline    ”         alternative is broader than the stated minimum and is not reflected in the LCA, creating an inconsistency between the petition and the LCA. (b) Offering the alternative tacitly concedes that ECE may not be         “    closely related    ”         to CS/ML/AI — i.e., it is an implicit acknowledgment of the field-of-study vulnerability in Issue 1. (c) If the true minimum is         “    any engineering + 5 years,    ”         the primary         “    CS/ML/AI/closely related    ”         statement overstates the requirement, which can weaken the specialty-occupation showing (the position must require a degree in a specific specialty, not merely         “    any engineering    ”         degree).  
              Recommendation:         Decide on a single, coherent minimum requirement. If relying on the experience-equivalency path, state it precisely per 8 CFR § 214.2(h)(4)(iii)(A) (a degree in the specialty or a closely related field, or an equivalent established through a combination of education and progressive experience in the specialty), align the LCA’s major-field entry, and document the 5 years fully (Issue 3). Avoid the loose         “    any engineering discipline    ”         phrasing.  
          Issue 13 — Required tools (TensorFlow, PyTorch, AWS SageMaker) are not confirmed by any experience letter. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The position requires         “    demonstrated proficiency with Python, TensorFlow, PyTorch, and cloud-based ML deployment platforms such as AWS SageMaker    ”         and         “    Experience with natural language processing (NLP) and large language model fine-tuning    ”         (Support Letter § 3). The NovaStar experience letter confirms Python, scikit-learn, Tableau, SQL, and A/B testing — but             not         TensorFlow, PyTorch, AWS, NLP, or LLMs. The Kiran letter confirms Java, Python, SQL, and database work. The only source for TensorFlow/PyTorch/AWS/LLM experience is the undocumented independent-consulting period (Issue 3) and the current Brightfield role (no separate experience letter).  
              Why it matters:         The verifiable experience letters do not corroborate the specific advanced tools the position requires. This compounds the documentation gap in Issue 3.  
              Recommendation:         Obtain a current Brightfield role letter confirming the beneficiary’s hands-on use of TensorFlow/PyTorch/AWS SageMaker/NLP/LLM in the present position, and client letters for the consulting period (Issue 3) confirming the same tools.  
        D. Document Completeness & Status  
          Issue 14 — No evidence of current nonimmigrant status or employment authorization. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The Support Letter (§ 4) states the beneficiary         “    is currently maintaining valid F-1 Optional Practical Training (OPT) status and will transition to H-1B nonimmigrant status effective October 1, 2025.    ”         The package contains no passport copy, F-1 visa, I-94, EAD (OPT or STEM OPT), or Form I-20. Given the beneficiary has been employed at Brightfield since September 5, 2023, post-completion OPT (12 months) would have lapsed around September 2024; continuation of work authorization through October 1, 2025 depends on a STEM OPT extension and/or the cap-gap provision.  
              Why it matters:         Without documentation of valid status and employment authorization through September 30, 2025, there is a risk of a gap in authorization and a status-maintenance problem that could affect both the beneficiary’s ability to remain employed and the change-of-status component of the H-1B petition.  
              Recommendation:         Include copies of the passport biographic page, F-1 visa, most recent I-94, all EAD cards (OPT and STEM OPT), and the current Form I-20 (with STEM OPT recommendation and any cap-gap endorsement). Confirm the STEM OPT and/or cap-gap coverage runs through September 30, 2025.  
          Issue 15 — Actual B.Tech degree certificate is not in the package. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The credential evaluation (§ 2) states it reviewed the         “    Original B.Tech degree certificate (Diploma) issued by JNTU Hyderabad, dated May 2014.    ”         The package includes the transcript and the evaluation, but not a copy of the degree certificate itself.  
              Why it matters:         USCIS routinely expects a copy of the actual degree certificate (in addition to the transcript and evaluation) to establish the degree was conferred.  
              Recommendation:         Include a clear copy of the B.Tech degree certificate (front and back, with seal/hologram), and the evaluation’s cover letter/transmittal.  
          Issue 16 — Form I-129 and core petition forms are not in the package. [LOW]  
              Finding:         The package consists of supporting documents only; Form I-129 (including the H Classification Supplement and the H-1B Data Collection Supplement), the filing fee evidence, and any cap-selection notice are not present.  
              Why it matters:         These are the petition’s core instruments; their absence from the review set means the cross-reference could not verify consistency of the I-129 entries (e.g., the SOC code, wage, worksite, and beneficiary data) against the supporting documents.  
              Recommendation:         Confirm the I-129 and supplements are completed and that every data field (beneficiary name/DOB, SOC, wage, worksite, LCA case number, employment dates) matches the supporting documents exactly. Include the cap-selection notice if cap-subject.  
          Issue 17 — Financial ability to pay is asserted but not independently documented. [LOW]  
              Finding:         The Support Letter (§§ 1, 5) asserts Brightfield         “    has the financial ability to pay the proffered annual wage of $128,000,    ”         citing $41.2M FY2023 revenue and 187 employees. No tax return, audited financial statement, or annual report is included.  
              Why it matters:         8 CFR § 214.2(h)(4)(ii)(B) requires the petitioner to establish ability to pay the proffered wage. The revenue/employee figures (≈$220k revenue per employee) suggest ability to pay is likely satisfied, but USCIS may request supporting financials.  
              Recommendation:         Include the most recent federal tax return and/or audited financials, or be prepared to produce them on RFE.  
        E. Internal / Data Inconsistencies  
          Issue 18 — Multiple inconsistent employer telephone numbers; posting notice uses the beneficiary’s personal cell number. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The employer’s telephone number differs across documents:  
          LCA § C.4 / § D.4: (512) 555-        0142  
          PWD § 2: (512) 555-        0187  
          Support Letter letterhead/contact: (512) 555-        0194  
          Posting Notice         “    Employer Telephone    ”    : (512) 555-        0174         — which is the             beneficiary’s personal cell number         as listed on the Resume.  
              Why it matters:         Four different phone numbers for the same employer across four documents is a data-integrity problem. The posting notice error (using the beneficiary’s personal number as the employer contact) is particularly problematic because the posting notice is a public-facing LCA compliance document.  
              Recommendation:         Standardize one employer telephone number across the LCA, PWD request, support letter, and posting notice. Correct the posting notice to the employer’s main line and re-post if necessary (coordinate with Issue 7/8).  
          Issue 19 — Transcript GPAs do not reconcile with the course grades under the stated grading scale. [MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The transcript states SGPAs of 8.26, 8.64, 8.22, 8.52, 8.59, 8.50, 8.65, and 8.80, and a CGPA of 8.52/10.00. Under the transcript’s own 10-point scale (O=10, A+=9, A=8, B+=7, B=6, C=5), a credit-weighted computation from the listed course grades yields SGPAs of 7.89, 8.24, 7.87, 8.12, 8.14, 8.00, 8.00, and 8.30, and a CGPA of             8.07         — none of which match the stated figures. The stated CGPA (8.52) equals the simple average of the stated SGPAs (8.5225), not a credit-weighted calculation. No alternative grade-point mapping reproduces the stated SGPAs.  
              Why it matters:         The grades shown are arithmetically inconsistent with the GPAs stated on the same document. This raises a transcript-integrity concern that USCIS or a credential evaluator could flag, and it undermines the reliability of the academic record on which the qualification showing depends.  
              Recommendation:         Obtain a clarification or re-issued transcript from JNTU Hyderabad reconciling the grades to the stated GPAs (or confirming the correct GPAs), and have the credential evaluator address any discrepancy. Do not rely on the stated 8.52 CGPA until reconciled.  
          Issue 20 — Credential evaluation’s credit-hour breakdown does not foot to the transcript. [LOW/MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The evaluation (§ 4) states the program requires         “    approximately 160 credit hours,    ”         with         “    Laboratory Components (~12 credit hours)    ”         and         “    Computer-Related Coursework (~12 credit hours).    ”         The transcript shows             184 total credits    , of which             28 are laboratory credits         (not 12) and ~156 are theory credits. The evaluation’s category subtotals (24 + 80 + 32 + 12 + 12 = 160, plus project/seminar ≈ 170) do not foot to the transcript’s 184.  
              Why it matters:         The evaluation appears to conflate theory credits (~160) with total credits (184) and substantially understates laboratory credits (12 vs. 28). Inaccuracies in the evaluation weaken its reliability, particularly where the evaluation is central to the field-of-study analysis (Issue 1).  
              Recommendation:         Request a corrected or clarified evaluation that reconciles its credit figures to the transcript (184 total; 28 lab; ~156 theory) and foots internally.  
          Issue 21 — Credential evaluation’s narrow                 “        computer-related        ”                 count understates relevant coursework. [LOW/MEDIUM]  
              Finding:         The evaluation (§ 4) counts only ~12 credit hours (7.5%) as         “    computer-related    ”         — C Programming (×2), Data Structures, and Intro to AI — excluding plainly relevant courses such as Computer Architecture and Organization (EC602, 4 cr, A+), Microprocessors and Microcontrollers (EC503, 4 cr, A), Digital Logic Design (EC402, 4 cr, A), Digital Image Processing (EC604, 3 cr, A), Probability Theory and Stochastic Processes (MT401, 4 cr, A), and the Computer Programming Lab (CS101L, 2 cr). A broader, defensible count is ~29 credit hours (~15.8% of 184).  
              Why it matters:         The narrow count works against the beneficiary by making the field-of-study gap (Issue 1) appear larger than it is. A more complete course-by-course analysis would better support the         “    closely related    ”         argument.  
              Recommendation:         Obtain a course-by-course supplement from the evaluator (or a qualified professor) that maps the ECE coursework — including the courses above — to the knowledge areas underlying machine learning engineering (algorithms, computer architecture, probability/statistics, signal/image processing, AI), to support the         “    closely related    ”         showing.  
        F. Other / Verification Items  
          Issue 22 — Evaluator’s credentialing institution should be verified. [LOW]  
              Finding:         The evaluation is signed by         “    Dr. Linda Farnsworth, Ph.D., Senior Credential Analyst,    ”         with the notation         “    Ph.D., Education — Halford University.    ”         Westbridge Credential Services is represented as a NACES and AICE member.  
              Why it matters:         The credibility of the evaluation (central to Issue 1) depends in part on the evaluator’s qualifications.         “    Halford University    ”         is not a widely recognized institution; its accreditation status and the evaluator’s expertise to opine on an engineering credential should be confirmable.  
              Recommendation:         Verify Halford University’s accreditation and Dr. Farnsworth’s qualifications. If concerns arise, consider engaging a different NACES evaluator or a university professor for the supplemental opinion (Issues 1 and 21).  
          Issue 23 — Years-of-experience characterization is inconsistent (resume                 “        7+        ”                 vs. support letter                 “        over 8        ”        ). [LOW]  
              Finding:         The Resume’s Professional Summary states         “    7+ years of experience.    ”         The Support Letter (§ 4) states         “    over 8 years of progressive experience.    ”         Measured from the NovaStar start (April 2017) to the Support Letter date (April 7, 2025), ML-specific experience is ~8.0 years; measured from the Kiran start (June 2014), total experience is ~10.8 years.  
              Why it matters:         A minor inconsistency, but one more place where the resume and the petition disagree. The         “    7+    ”         figure also understates the experience the Support Letter relies on.  
              Recommendation:         Align the resume’s summary with the Support Letter (e.g.,         “    8+ years of experience in machine learning and software development    ”    ) and ensure the figure is defensible against the documented record.  
          Issue 24 — H-1B-dependency and willful-violator attestations cannot be verified from the package. [LOW]  
              Finding:         The LCA (§§ C.10–C.11) attests the employer is not H-1B-dependent and not a willful violator. The package does not state the total number of H-1B workers employed, so dependency (triggered at >15% of 187 employees, i.e., 29+ H-1B workers) cannot be independently confirmed.  
              Why it matters:         Minor; the attestation is the employer’s responsibility, but the reviewer cannot confirm it from the documents provided.  
              Recommendation:         Internally confirm the H-1B headcount supports the         “    not dependent    ”         attestation and retain the supporting calculation in the Public Access File.  
      
         V. Cross-Document Consistency Check (Summary of Agreements)  
      The following key data points are             consistent         across all documents in which they appear and require no action:  
          Beneficiary name (Rajesh Venkataraman), DOB (March 14, 1992), and citizenship (India).  
          Employer legal name, address (4200 Congress Avenue, Suite 1100, Austin, TX 78701), FEIN (84-3291756), NAICS (541511), employee count (187), and revenue ($41.2M).  
          Position title (Senior Machine Learning Engineer), SOC (15-2051), worksite, employment period (Oct 1, 2025 – Sept 30, 2028), and wage ($128,000).  
          LCA case number (I-200-25031-543219), PWD number (P-300-25012-183456), prevailing wage ($113,734), and wage level (Level 2).  
          Degree (B.Tech, ECE, JNTU Hyderabad, Aug 2010 – May 2014) and counsel of record (Daniel Okafor, Holloway Sterne LLP).  
          Support Letter duty percentages sum to 100% (15 + 20 + 20 + 15 + 15 + 10 + 5).  
      
        VI. Recommended Priority Actions  
              Resolve the field-of-study issue (Issue 1).         Obtain a position-specific expert opinion that ECE is         “    closely related    ”         to CS/ML/AI, supported by a course-by-course mapping (Issue 21), or commit to and fully document the experience-equivalency path (Issues 3, 12, 13).  
              Re-assess and correct the wage level (Issues 2, 6).         Determine whether Level 3/4 is required; if so, raise the wage and re-file the LCA at the correct level. If Level 2 is defended, make the duties consistent with         “    limited judgment.    ”  
              Cure the LCA posting deficiencies (Issues 7, 8).         Re-post for 10 consecutive business days in two documented locations; retain evidence in the Public Access File.  
              Document the independent-consulting experience (Issue 3) and the required tools (Issue 13).         Obtain client letters and 1099/invoice evidence; obtain a current Brightfield role letter.  
              Reconcile all dates and data (Issues 10, 11, 18, 19, 20, 23).         Correct the resume to match the experience letters; reconcile the direct-report count; standardize the employer telephone; reconcile the transcript GPAs and the evaluation’s credit figures.  
              Assemble the missing status and identity documents (Issues 14, 15, 16, 17).         Passport, visa, I-94, EAD(s), I-20, degree certificate, I-129/supplements, and financials.  
              Confirm the worksite representation (Issue 9).         Confirm exclusive Austin-HQ work, or add LCA worksite coverage and an itinerary for any off-site/client/remote work.  
              Verify the evaluator (Issue 22) and the H-1B-dependency attestation (Issue 24).  
      
        VII. Consolidated Issue Table  
                           #

          Prepared on the basis of the ten documents listed in Section II. This memorandum identifies issues for further investigation and correction; it is not a determination of petition outcome. All quantitative findings (posting business-day count, wage-level comparisons, GPA reconciliation, credit-hour reconciliation, and experience-timeline gaps) were computed and verified.

### FILE: invoice-review-memo.docx
          INVOICE REVIEW MEMORANDUM  
          PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF BILLING DISPUTE  
          TO:         Rachel Dominguez, Associate General Counsel (Designated In-House Counsel); Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel             FROM:         Legal Billing / Invoice Review             DATE:         June 13, 2025             RE:         Review of Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN — Hargrove & Linden LLP —             Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC    , Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.), Hon. Richard W. Stanton — Billing Period May 1–31, 2025  
      
       I. Executive Summary  
      Hargrove & Linden LLP (    “    H&L    ”         or the         “    Firm    ”    ) submitted Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN (dated June 5, 2025) for legal services rendered May 1–31, 2025, in the captioned matter. The invoice             demands $201,822.50         ($193,372.50 in attorney fees plus $8,450.00 in expenses).  
      This review was conducted against (a) the Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan dated February 10, 2025; (b) Pinnacle’s Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (January 2023) (the         “    Guidelines    ”    ); (c) the April 28 / May 1, 2025 staffing-addition email correspondence regarding Nina Barros; and (d) H&L’s May 2025 Monthly Case Status Report.  
          Principal conclusions:  
             The invoice does not foot.         The Time Detail (the auditable line-item record) totals             483.5 hours / $215,680.00         in attorney fees, but the Summary page demands only             422.5 hours / $193,372.50         — a             $22,307.50 / 61.0-hour variance         that the Firm has not explained. The invoice is internally inconsistent and cannot be reliably audited as submitted.  
             Two timekeepers were never approved.         Nina Barros (mid-level associate, $23,275.00) and Priya Sengupta (a second junior associate, $10,125.00) billed the matter with no written approval, in violation of Guidelines §4.2 and the express terms of the Approved Staffing Plan. Their combined             $33,400.00         is subject to rejection in full.  
             The contract attorney was overcharged.         Marcus Tate was billed at $210/hr — $25/hr above the $185/hr pass-through rate approved in the Staffing Plan and $10/hr above the $200/hr Guidelines cap — producing an overcharge of             $3,862.50    .  
             Block billing is pervasive.         Forty-three (43) entries by approved timekeepers combine two or more discrete tasks in a single line, triggering the automatic 25% reduction under §5.4 —             $26,693.75    .  
             Task-leveling, over-staffing, and expense violations         add a further             $10,755.00         in reductions (partner-level document review and cite-checking, a junior associate doing routine review, a purely administrative entry, an over-staffed strategy call and deposition, and non-reimbursable local travel).  
             Budget overage without pre-approval.         Attorney fees exceed the §7.3 ceiling ($165,000 × 1.15 = $189,750) on both the summary basis ($193,372.50) and the detail basis ($215,680.00), with no pre-approval obtained.  
          Bottom line:         Line-by-line review of the Time Detail identifies             $74,711.25 in non-compliant charges         ($72,611.25 in fees; $2,100.00 in expenses). Applied to the Time Detail, the compliant amount is             $149,418.75    . We recommend             returning the invoice for correction         of the footing error and applying the reductions below; the amount payable should             not exceed $149,418.75    , and will likely be lower once the $22,307.50 variance is reconciled.  
      
        II. Documents Reviewed  
                          Document  
          Date  
          Relevance  
            Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN (Summary, Time Detail, Expense Detail, Rate Schedule)  
          June 5, 2025  
          Subject of review  
            Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan  
          Feb. 10, 2025 (approved)  
          Authorized timekeepers, rates, hour ranges, budget  
            Pinnacle Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (Jan. 2023)  
          Jan. 1, 2023  
          Governing billing rules  
            Staffing-addition email thread (K. Cho / R. Dominguez)  
          Apr. 28 & May 1, 2025  
          Status of Nina Barros request  
            H&L Monthly Case Status Report — May 2025  
          June 1, 2025  
          Narrative of work performed and staffing  
         
        III. Invoice Overview  
              Firm / Vendor:         Hargrove & Linden LLP  
              Invoice No. / Date:         HL-2025-05-PIN / June 5, 2025  
              Billing Period:         May 1 – May 31, 2025  
              Matter:             Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC    , No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.)  
              Payment Terms:         Net 30 (due July 5, 2025)  
          Timekeeper summary (as stated on the invoice Summary page):  
                           Timekeeper  
          Role  
          Rate  
          Hours (summary)  
          Fees (summary)  
            David Hargrove  
          Lead Partner  
          $985  
          38.5  
          $37,922.50  
            Karen Cho  
          Senior Associate  
          $625

          $46,250.00  
            James Pettersson  
          Mid-Level Associate  
          $475  
          82.5  
          $39,187.50  
            Nina Barros  
          Mid-Level Associate  
          $475  
          45.0  
          $21,375.00  
            Tyler Webb  
          Junior Associate  
          $375  
          35.0  
          $13,125.00  
            Priya Sengupta  
          Junior Associate  
          $375

          $10,312.50  
            Marcus Tate  
          Contract Attorney

                Total  
       
       
              422.5  
              $193,372.50  
             Expenses (as stated):         Court reporting $3,200.00; travel $2,100.00; e-discovery hosting $2,650.00; courier $500.00 —             $8,450.00    .  
          Total demanded: $201,822.50.  
      
        IV. Critical Finding — The Invoice Does Not Foot (Billing-Integrity Error)  
      The Summary page and the Time Detail page do not reconcile. Re-adding every line in the Time Detail produces a materially larger total than the Summary demands:  
                              Timekeeper  
          Detail hours  
          Summary hours  
          Δ Hours  
          Detail fees  
          Summary fees  
          Δ Fees  
            David Hargrove  
          38.5

          0.0  
          $37,922.50  
          $37,922.50  
          $0.00  
            Karen Cho

          +15.5

          +4.0  
          $23,275.00  
          $21,375.00  
          +$1,900.00  
            Tyler Webb  
          34.0

          (1.0)  
          $12,750.00  
          $13,125.00  
          ($375.00)  
            Priya Sengupta  
          27.0  
          27.5  
          (0.5)  
          $10,125.00  
          $10,312.50  
          ($187.50)  
            Marcus Tate  
          154.5  
          120.0  
          +34.5  
          $32,445.00  
          $25,200.00  
          +$7,245.00  
                Total  
              483.5  
              422.5  
              +61.0  
              $215,680.00  
              $193,372.50  
              +$22,307.50  
             Why this matters.         Guidelines §9.1 requires both a summary page (total fees by timekeeper) and a time-detail page, and they must reconcile. §10.2 reserves Pinnacle’s audit rights and provides that failure to maintain adequate records         “    may result in the disallowance of the fees or expenses in question.    ”         An invoice whose supporting detail exceeds its own demand by $22,307.50 cannot be reliably audited: Pinnacle cannot determine which 61 hours / $22,307.50 of line items the Firm actually intends to collect, and therefore cannot verify that the demanded figure is correct or that the entries supporting it comply with the Guidelines. The variance is not uniform (the Summary             overstates         Webb and Sengupta while             understating         Cho, Pettersson, Tate, and Barros), indicating the Summary was prepared independently of, and not derived from, the Time Detail.  
          Disposition.         This is, standing alone, grounds to return the invoice for correction under §9.1 and §10.2 before payment. The substantive reductions below are computed against the             Time Detail         (the auditable line-item record). Because the Summary already reflects $22,307.50 less than the Detail, the final payable after reconciliation may be lower than the figure shown here; the figure shown is a             ceiling    .  
      
        V. Substantive Findings and Reductions  
       A. Unapproved Timekeepers — Guidelines §4.2 (and Approved Staffing Plan §§2.3–2.4)  
          Governing rule.         §4.2 requires written pre-approval from the Designated In-House Counsel             before         any new timekeeper performs billable work, and provides that staffing changes         “    submitted after the fact, or where the new timekeeper has already commenced work prior to receiving written approval, are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle’s sole discretion.    ”         The Approved Staffing Plan (§§2.3, 2.4) makes the roster         “    complete and exclusive,    ”         states that         “    James Pettersson is the only mid-level associate assigned to this matter,    ”         and states that         “    only one junior associate will be assigned to and authorized to bill on this matter at any given time,    ”         with any second junior associate requiring prior written approval.  
          Finding 1 — Nina Barros (mid-level associate): $23,275.00 (49.0 hrs).         On April 28, 2025, Karen Cho emailed Rachel Dominguez requesting approval to add Nina Barros as a second mid-level associate at $475/hr, estimating 40–50 hrs/month. On May 1, 2025, Ms. Dominguez replied:             “        Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you… I’ll try to circle back with you early next week.        ”         That is a deferral, not an approval. No subsequent written approval appears in the record. The invoice’s own Rate Schedule confirms the status:             “        Pending Approval — Added May 2025 … client response pending.        ”         Ms. Barros nonetheless billed 49.0 hours beginning May 1 — the same day Ms. Dominguez deferred the request. Under §4.2, all fees attributable to this unapproved timekeeper are subject to rejection.             Reduction: $23,275.00 (full rejection).  
          Finding 2 — Priya Sengupta (second junior associate): $10,125.00 (27.0 hrs).         The Approved Staffing Plan authorizes a single junior-associate slot, filled by Tyler Webb (2nd year). It expressly states that assigning more than one junior associate concurrently         “    would constitute a staffing change requiring prior written approval under Section 4.2.    ”         Priya Sengupta (1st year, $375/hr) billed 27.0 hours concurrently with Mr. Webb throughout May. No written request or approval for her addition exists; the Rate Schedule lists her only as         “    Added May 2025    ”         with no approval status. She is therefore an unapproved timekeeper under §4.2 and an unauthorized second junior associate under the Staffing Plan.             Reduction: $10,125.00 (full rejection).  
          Combined §4.2 reduction: $33,400.00.         (Note: these figures are taken from the Time Detail. The Summary understates Ms. Barros by $1,900.00 and overstates Ms. Sengupta by $187.50; reconciliation is required, but no portion of either timekeeper’s time is payable absent written approval.)  
        B. Contract Attorney Rate Exceeds Approved Rate and Cap — Guidelines §5.1; Staffing Plan §§2.5, 6  
          Governing rule.         §5.1 caps contract attorneys at $200/hr. The Approved Staffing Plan (§§2.5, 6) and the engagement terms fix Marcus Tate’s rate at             $185/hr    , billed as a pass-through at the Redwood Document Solutions vendor rate         “    with no markup.    ”         §5.1 provides that rates in excess of the caps         “    will be automatically reduced to the applicable cap upon invoice review.    ”  
          Finding.         The invoice bills Mr. Tate at             $210/hr         — $25/hr above the approved $185/hr pass-through rate and $10/hr above the $200/hr Guidelines cap. Across 154.5 hours (per the Time Detail), this is an overcharge of 154.5 × $25 =             $3,862.50    . (Even measured only to the $200 cap, the overcharge would be $1,545.00; the approved rate of $185/hr controls.)             Reduction: $3,862.50         (re-rate to $185/hr).  
        C. Block Billing — Guidelines §5.4  
          Governing rule.         §5.4 prohibits combining         “    two or more discrete tasks into a single line item    ”         and mandates         “    an automatic twenty-five percent (25%) reduction to the total fees of each block-billed entry,    ”         cumulative with all other reductions. The Guidelines’ own examples treat entries that combine drafting with research, or deposition attendance with a post-deposition summary, as impermissible block billing.  
          Finding.         Forty-three (43) entries by approved timekeepers combine discrete tasks (e.g., drafting + research; review/revise + client or team conference; deposition attendance + post-deposition summary; strategy session + drafting + reviewing separate work product). The affected entries and the 25% reduction are summarized below; the full entry list follows.

          Draft summary judgment outline; research SJ standards  
            101  
          05/28  
          Cho  
          4.5  
          $2,812.50  
          Review SJ outline; confer with partner; identify gaps  
            107  
          05/29  
          Pettersson  
          4.5  
          $2,137.50  
          Continue drafting outline; prepare factual chronology  
            108  
          05/29  
          Cho  
          4.0  
          $2,500.00  
          Review/revise chronology; confer with associate; prepare timeline  
            111  
          05/30  
          Hargrove  
          1.5  
          $1,477.50  
          Review billing summary/status report; prepare invoice cover memo  
            113  
          05/30  
          Pettersson  
          4.0  
          $1,900.00  
          Prepare case timeline; draft witness list; coordinate scheduling  
            114  
          05/30  
          Cho  
          4.0  
          $2,500.00  
          Prepare status report; review/compile billing; conference with partner  
         Entries that combine a single research task with preparation of the resulting memorandum, or a single document-review task with coding/flagging within the review platform, were treated as one integrated task and not penalized. Entries by the unapproved timekeepers (Barros, Sengupta) are not listed separately because their time is rejected in full under §4.2. Entry #62 (Pettersson) is block-billed but is addressed under §4.3 below (full disallowance), so it is excluded here to avoid double-counting.             Reduction: $26,693.75.  
        D. Timekeeper-to-Task Appropriateness — Guidelines §6.1  
          Governing rule.         §6.1 requires each task to be staffed to the lowest-cost competent timekeeper and makes document review and cite-checking by partners/senior associates presumptively inappropriate, reducing such time to the contract-attorney or junior-associate rate; routine first-level review by a junior associate is reduced to the contract-attorney rate; and purely administrative tasks (scheduling, coordinating with court reporters, confirming locations) are overhead and are written off or reduced to the paralegal rate.  
                               #  
          Date  
          Timekeeper  
          Hrs  
          Billed  
          Issue  
          Corrected  
          Reduction  
            9  
          05/05  
          Hargrove (Partner)  
          4.5  
          $4,432.50  
          Document review (L040) by lead partner  
          $375/hr (jr. assoc.) = $1,687.50  
          $2,745.00  
            43  
          05/14  
          Hargrove (Partner)  
          2.0  
          $1,970.00  
          Cite-check/citation finalization by partner  
          $375/hr (jr. assoc.) = $750.00  
          $1,220.00  
            79  
          05/22  
          Webb (Jr. Assoc.)  
          3.5  
          $1,312.50  
          Routine first-level document review by junior associate  
          $185/hr (contract) = $647.50  
          $665.00  
            15  
          05/06  
          Pettersson (Mid-level)  
          1.5  
          $712.50  
          Purely administrative: schedule depositions, coordinate court reporter, confirm locations  
          Write-off (overhead)  
          $712.50  
                 Entry #9.         Mr. Hargrove personally reviewed third-production documents and prepared a findings memorandum. The Case Status Report frames this as strategic calibration of the review protocol, but the entry is coded L040 (document review) and describes review/analysis of produced documents — presumptively partner-inappropriate under §6.1(a). Because the review involved legal judgment (trade-secret relevance), it is reduced to the junior-associate rate ($375/hr) rather than the contract-attorney rate.  
              Entry #43.         Mr. Hargrove         “    review[ed] and revise[d] [the] cite-check memorandum prepared by T. Webb    ”         and         “    finalize[d] citations    ”         — cite-checking work presumptively inappropriate for a partner under §6.1(b), reduced to the junior-associate rate.  
              Entry #79.         Mr. Webb performed routine first-level review         “    for relevance and privilege    ”         — the contract attorney’s core function (the Case Status Report confirms he filled in when Mr. Tate was at capacity). Reduced to the $185/hr contract-attorney rate Pinnacle actually pays for that work.  
              Entry #15.         Scheduling depositions, coordinating court-reporter availability, and confirming locations are Administrative Tasks under §2 and §6.1(g)–(h) — non-compensable overhead. Written off in full.  
          Reduction: $5,342.50         ($4,630.00 in rate reductions + $712.50 administrative write-off).  
        E. Over-Staffed Internal Meeting and Deposition — Guidelines §4.3  
          Governing rule.         §4.3 caps billing timekeepers for any single internal meeting/conference call at three (3), even for Substantive Case-Strategy Sessions; timekeepers beyond the cap are reduced         “    in their entirety    ”         absent advance designation. For depositions (which are             not         Court Appearances), two billing timekeepers are permitted without approval; more than two requires advance approval requested no later than five business days before the deposition, failing which the additional timekeepers’ time is reduced in its entirety.  
          Finding 1 — May 8 team strategy call: $937.50.         Five timekeepers billed the same May 8 discovery-strategy call (Hargrove #22, Cho #23, Pettersson #24, Barros #25, Webb #26); the Case Status Report confirms         “    the full litigation team participated.    ”         This is a Substantive Case-Strategy Session, so multiple timekeepers may bill, but the three-timekeeper cap applies. No advance designation was made. Ms. Barros’s time is already rejected under §4.2; of the remaining four approved timekeepers, one exceeds the cap. The three most senior/essential (Hargrove, Cho, Pettersson) are retained; the fourth — Tyler Webb, a junior associate whose role was observational — is disallowed.             Reduction: $937.50 (Webb, entry #26, 2.5 hrs).  
          Finding 2 — Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition (May 19): $2,375.00.         Three attorneys billed the deposition (Hargrove #60, Cho #61, Pettersson #62); the Case Status Report confirms         “    all three attorneys were present for the full duration.    ”         No advance approval for a third deposition timekeeper was requested (none appears in the record, and the five-business-day requirement could not have been met for a May 19 deposition). Two timekeepers are permitted: Ms. Cho (who conducted the direct examination) and Mr. Hargrove (lead partner, observing/strategizing) are retained. Mr. Pettersson — the third attorney, whose role was exhibit management, note-taking, and summary preparation — is the         “    additional timekeeper    ”         whose time §4.3 permits Pinnacle to reduce in its entirety. His entry (#62) is also block-billed (deposition + summary), so the compensable summary-preparation portion cannot be isolated; full disallowance is therefore appropriate, and the entry is excluded from the block-billing calculation to avoid double-counting.             Reduction: $2,375.00 (Pettersson, entry #62, 5.0 hrs).  
          Reduction: $3,312.50.  
        F. Monthly Budget Overage Without Pre-Approval — Guidelines §7.3  
          Governing rule.         The Approved Staffing Plan (§3) sets a monthly attorney-fee range of $95,000–$165,000. §7.3 requires written pre-approval before monthly fees exceed $165,000 × 1.15 =             $189,750.00    ; absent pre-approval, Pinnacle may reduce the invoice to that ceiling.  
          Finding.         Attorney fees exceed the $189,750 ceiling on both bases: the Summary reports $193,372.50 (over by $3,622.50) and the Time Detail totals $215,680.00 (over by $25,930.00). No §7.3 pre-approval was requested or granted (the Case Status Report notes only a May 29 discussion of         “    budget and staffing for June–July,    ”         not a pre-approval of the May overage). This is a §7.3 violation.  
          Disposition.         The §7.3 ceiling is a cap on the amount payable, not an additive deduction. Because the substantive reductions above (principally the §4.2 rejections) bring allowable fees to $143,068.75 — well below $189,750 — the ceiling is not the binding constraint and no separate budget-cap deduction is applied. The overage is nonetheless a compliance violation that should be cited to the Firm and that supports returning the invoice for a compliant resubmission. The Firm must also provide the §7.2/§9.1(g) budget-to-actual reconciliation, which is missing from the invoice (see §V.H).  
        G. Non-Reimbursable Travel Expense — Guidelines §§6.4, 8.2, 8.3  
          Finding — Travel, $2,100.00 (Expense #2).         The entry reimburses         “    mileage, parking, meals    ”         for Hargrove and Cho to attend the Liu deposition. The Case Status Report states the deposition was taken         “    at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia    ”         — i.e., the Firm’s own office. Travel to one’s own office is local commuting, not reimbursable travel: §6.4 provides that         “    [l]ocal travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable under any circumstances,    ”         and §8.2 makes meals non-reimbursable except during approved out-of-town travel (none occurred here). Separately, §8.3 requires pre-approval for any travel exceeding $1,000 in the aggregate; this $2,100 expense exceeds that threshold and was not pre-approved.             Reduction: $2,100.00 (full disallowance).  
          Expenses allowed (with notes):  
              Court reporting, $3,200.00 (Expense #1, Grayson — approved vendor).         One transcript copy is reimbursable under §8.1.             Note:         the description includes         “    expedited delivery    ”    ; Pinnacle should confirm the charge reflects standard rates and that expedited handling was necessary, but absent a breakdown the expense is allowed.  
              E-discovery hosting, $2,650.00 (Expense #3, Tripoint — approved vendor).         Reasonable and necessary for an ~85,000-document production.             Note:         §8.1 makes e-discovery vendor charges         “    subject to pre-approval    ”    ; the Staffing Plan contemplated Tripoint hosting but no specific monthly pre-approval is documented. Recommend obtaining retroactive written confirmation; expense allowed.  
              Courier, $500.00 (Expense #4,                 “        Various        ”        ).         Courier service for time-sensitive filing/service is reimbursable under §8.1.             Note:         §8.1 requires receipts/vendor invoices; the         “    Various    ”         vendor designation is insufficient documentation. Require receipts before payment; expense allowed in principle.  
          Expense reduction: $2,100.00. Allowable expenses: $6,350.00.  
        H. Additional Compliance Issues (No Separate Monetary Reduction)  
      These violations do not support a discrete dollar reduction but should be cited to the Firm and corrected:  
              Missing budget-to-actual reconciliation (§§7.2, 9.1(g)).         The invoice contains no budget-to-actual reconciliation showing the approved range, actual and cumulative fees/expenses, percentage of phase budget consumed, or narrative explanation of the >10% variance (which is plainly present). This is a required invoice component and compounds the §7.3 overage problem.  
              Half-hour billing increments (§5.5).         Every entry is recorded in 0.5-hour increments, coarser than the 0.25-hour increments §5.5 already rejects and far coarser than the 0.1-hour maximum required. The §5.5 rounding remedy does not reduce 0.5-hour values, so no deduction is applied, but the practice is non-compliant and suggests rounding that may inflate hours; require 0.1-hour increments going forward.  
              Hour-range exceedances (Staffing Plan §2).         On a Time-Detail basis, four approved timekeepers exceeded their approved monthly hour ranges: Hargrove 38.5 hrs (cap 25; +54%), Cho 89.5 hrs (cap 80; +12%), Pettersson 91.0 hrs (cap 90; +1%), and Tate 154.5 hrs (cap 150; +3%). The Case Status Report anticipates that partner hours will         “    return to more typical levels    ”         in June; the exceedances should be monitored and are relevant context for the §7.3 overage.  
              Invoice narrative sufficiency (§9.2).         Most narratives are adequately specific. Entry #79 (    “    Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege    ”    ) is generic but identifies the production batch and purpose and is addressed under §6.1; no separate §9.2 rejection is recommended.  
              E-billing submission (§9.4).         Confirm the invoice was submitted through Pinnacle’s e-billing platform as required.  
      
         VI. Reduction Calculation Summary  
          A. Attorney fees (computed against the Time Detail, the auditable record):  
                           #  
          Issue  
          Guideline  
          Reduction  
            1  
          Unapproved timekeepers — Nina Barros (49.0 hrs)  
          §4.2  
          $23,275.00  
            2  
          Unapproved timekeeper / 2nd junior associate — Priya Sengupta (27.0 hrs)  
          §4.2 / Plan §2.4  
          $10,125.00  
            3  
          Contract attorney over-rate (154.5 hrs × $25)  
          §5.1 / Plan §2.5  
          $3,862.50  
            4  
          Block billing — 43 entries, 25% of $106,775.00  
          §5.4  
          $26,693.75  
            5  
          Partner document review (entry #9)  
          §6.1(a)  
          $2,745.00  
            6  
          Partner cite-checking (entry #43)  
          §6.1(b)  
          $1,220.00  
            7  
          Junior associate routine review (entry #79)  
          §6.1  
          $665.00  
            8  
          Administrative task — write-off (entry #15)  
          §6.1(g)–(h)  
          $712.50  
            9  
          Over-staffed strategy call — Webb (entry #26)  
          §4.3  
          $937.50  
            10  
          Over-staffed deposition — Pettersson (entry #62)  
          §4.3  
          $2,375.00  
         
              Total fee reductions  
       
              $72,611.25  
             B. Expenses:  
                           #  
          Issue  
          Guideline  
          Reduction  
            11  
          Non-reimbursable local travel (Expense #2)  
          §§6.4, 8.2, 8.3  
          $2,100.00  
         
              Total expense reductions  
       
              $2,100.00  
             C. Reconciliation:  
                     
          Amount  
            Time Detail — attorney fees  
          $215,680.00  
            Less: fee reductions  
          ($72,611.25)  
                Allowable attorney fees (detail basis)  
              $143,068.75  
            Expenses billed  
          $8,450.00  
            Less: expense reductions  
          ($2,100.00)  
                Allowable expenses  
              $6,350.00  
                Maximum amount payable (detail basis)  
              $149,418.75  
         
       
            Memo: §7.3 ceiling ($165,000 × 1.15)  
          $189,750.00  
            Allowable fees vs. ceiling  
          below ceiling — cap not binding  
            Memo: amount demanded (Summary)  
          $201,822.50  
            Memo: Time Detail total (fees + expenses)  
          $224,130.00  
            Memo: footing variance (Detail − Summary demand)  
          $22,307.50  
         
        VII. Recommended Payable and Disposition  
             Return the invoice for correction.         The $22,307.50 / 61-hour footing variance between the Time Detail and the Summary is a material billing-integrity defect under §§9.1 and 10.2. The Firm must reconcile the two, identify which line items it intends to collect, and resubmit a compliant invoice (including the missing §7.2/§9.1(g) budget-to-actual reconciliation).  
             Apply the reductions identified above.         Total identified non-compliance:             $74,711.25         ($72,611.25 fees + $2,100.00 expenses).  
             Maximum payable: $149,418.75.         This is the compliant amount on a Time-Detail basis and operates as a             ceiling    . Because the Summary already reflects $22,307.50 less than the Detail, the final payable after reconciliation will depend on which line items the Firm confirms:  
          If the Firm confirms the             Time Detail         as accurate (and corrects the Summary upward), the payable is             $149,418.75    .  
          If the Firm confirms the             Summary         ($193,372.50 in fees) and removes the excess Detail entries, the payable will be             lower         — between approximately $127,111.25 and $149,418.75 — depending on whether the removed entries are among those reduced above.  
             Withhold payment         pending reconciliation and resubmission, consistent with §9.3. Payment of any amount is without prejudice to Pinnacle’s audit and adjustment rights under §10.2.  
      
        VIII. Recommended Actions  
              Issue a written reduction notice         to Karen Cho / David Hargrove citing each finding above (§4.2 rejections of Barros and Sengupta; §5.1 Tate re-rate; §5.4 block billing; §6.1 task-leveling; §4.3 over-staffing; §§6.4/8.2/8.3 travel; §7.3 overage; §9.1 footing/reconciliation defects), with the $149,418.75 ceiling and a request to reconcile and resubmit within 15 days (consistent with the §5.4/§9.2 cure windows).  
              Resolve the Barros staffing request.         If Pinnacle wishes to add Ms. Barros prospectively, issue a written approval (or denial) now; until then, no Barros time is payable. Note that her addition as a second mid-level associate also warrants revisiting the budget under §7.4.  
              Clarify the junior-associate slot.         Confirm whether Tyler Webb or Priya Sengupta is the authorized junior associate going forward; only one may bill concurrently absent written approval.  
              Correct the contract-attorney rate         to the $185/hr pass-through and explain the $210/hr charge (including whether any markup was applied, which would violate the engagement terms and §5.1).  
              Require itemized time entries         (no block billing) and 0.1-hour increments on all future invoices, and require the §7.2 budget-to-actual reconciliation with each invoice.  
              Obtain pre-approval         before any future month is projected to exceed the $189,750 §7.3 threshold, and pre-approve any deposition expected to require more than two billing timekeepers (§4.3, five business days in advance).  
              Require receipts         for the courier expense and confirm standard-rate billing for the court-reporting expense.  
      
          Prepared by Invoice Review. This memorandum is privileged and confidential, prepared in anticipation of a billing dispute, and intended solely for Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. and its in-house legal department.
