                3.  DISCREPANCIES FLAGGED  
             The following inconsistencies were identified among the closing documents. None is believed to affect the monetary reconciliation, but each should be reconciled or corrected on or before Closing.  
            Title commitment number and date.         PSA §5.1 references a title commitment "dated April 30, 2025, Commitment No. PCT-2025-08814." The title commitment actually delivered is Commitment No. TC-2025-07182, effective June 18, 2025 (issued by Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC for Continental Fidelity/Hartleigh Title Insurance Company). The file number PCT-2025-08814 is used on the settlement statement as the file reference. Confirm that the June 18 commitment (TC-2025-07182) is the operative commitment and that the PSA reference is simply an earlier-filed or placeholder number.  
            Title underwriter name.         The title commitment refers to the underwriter as both "Continental Fidelity Title Insurance Company" (header) and "Continental Hartleigh Title Insurance Company" (body and Conditions & Stipulations). These appear to be two different names for the same entity. Confirm the correct legal name of the underwriter so that the owner's and lender's policies are issued in the proper name.  
            Closing-agent address.         The PSA and title commitment list Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC at 88 Field Point Road, Greenwich, CT 06830. The lien payoff cover letter is addressed to the closing officer at 3100 Main Street, Suite 204, Bridgeport, CT 06604, and the tax-lien statement is addressed to 1200 Post Road East, Suite 214, Westport, CT 06880. Confirm the correct office and, critically, verify all wire instructions by independent verbal callback before disbursing (wire-fraud best practice).  
            Letters Testamentary date.         The Fiduciary's Certificate states Letters Testamentary were issued on October 11, 2024; the title commitment states "on or about October 14, 2024." Reconcile the exact issuance date. The certificate is dated June 25, 2025, which is within the Lender's 60-day and the Title Company's 60-day recency requirements for the July 15, 2025 Closing.  
            Commercial lease remaining term.         PSA Exhibit C states the remaining term as of Closing (7/15/2025) is "approximately 5 years, 5 months, and 16 days" (lease expires 12/31/2030). The Lender Commitment and the rent-roll Notes column both state "approximately four and one-half years remaining." From July 15, 2025 to December 31, 2030 is approximately 5 years, 5.5 months — not 4.5 years. The "4.5 years" figures appear to be errors. Confirm the correct remaining term, as it is relevant to the Lender's underwriting and the required Subordination, Non-Disturbance, and Attornment Agreement (SNDA).  
            Lender loan-to-value (LTV) figure.         The Lender Commitment states LTV is "approximately 67.7%" and then computes $2,640,000 ÷ $3,920,000 (appraised value) = 67.35%, "rounded to approximately 67.7%." However, 67.35% does not round to 67.7%; the 67.7% figure corresponds to $2,640,000 ÷ $3,900,000 (purchase price) = 67.69%. The commitment conflates the purchase-price-based and appraised-value-based LTV. Cosmetic, but confirm the Lender's intended LTV basis.  
            Payoff-letter date references.         Title Commitment Requirements 4 and 5 reference Harborstone payoff statements "dated June 10, 2025." The payoff letters actually enclosed (and used on the settlement statement) are dated July 8, 2025 and supersede any earlier statement. Confirm the July 8 figures ($687,412.33 and $148,219.56, good through 7/20/2025) are the operative payoffs.  
            Financing-contingency cross-reference.         The Lender Commitment states the financing contingency was waived "pursuant to Section 5.2 of the Purchase Agreement." In the PSA, §5.2 is "Permitted Exceptions"; the financing waiver is addressed in PSA §3.3 and §10.2. Minor cross-reference error in the commitment letter; the waiver itself is documented and effective as of June 10, 2025.  
            Occupied vs. collected rent distinction.         The rent roll reports "Total Monthly Rent (Occupied Units) $25,200.00" (which includes Unit 2E, occupied but delinquent) and "Total Collected July 2025 Rent $23,600.00" (which excludes 2E). The $1,600.00 difference equals Unit 2E's delinquent July rent. The settlement statement prorates only the $23,600.00 collected, consistent with PSA §7.3 (no credit for uncollected rents). No error, but the distinction should be understood by all parties.  
                4.  ASSUMPTIONS MADE  
             The following assumptions were used where figures were unavailable, not yet determined, or required estimation. Each is subject to adjustment, reproration, or confirmation as noted.  
            Earnest-money accrued interest (estimated $730.42).         No interest figure was provided in the delivered documents. The settlement statement estimates $730.42 assuming a 2.00% APY escrow rate: First Deposit $100,000 × 82 days (4/24/2025–7/15/2025) = $449.32; Second Deposit $95,000 × 54 days (5/22/2025–7/15/2025) = $281.10. The actual interest credit will be per the Escrow Agent's final accounting and will adjust Buyer's cash-to-close (and the sources/uses) by any difference. PSA §3.2(e) requires the interest be credited to Buyer.  
            Connecticut Real Estate Conveyance Tax (estimated $44,750.00 total; $22,375.00 per party).         Taken from the Title Commitment Schedule C estimate, split 50/50 per PSA §12.1 and §6.4. The Title Commitment notes this is an estimate using "the tiered rate structure applicable to the City of Bridgeport" and that the actual amount "will be confirmed upon preparation and filing of Form OP-236." The mixed-use character of the property (commercial ground floor + residential upper floors) may affect the rate computation. Subject to adjustment at Closing.  
            Next-fiscal-year (2025–2026) property tax (estimated at the current-year levy of $52,480.00).         The new mill rate has not been set and no bill has been issued (Tax Lien Statement §3). Per PSA §7.1(b), the current-year amount is used as the estimate. Seller is charged for 14 days (7/1–7/14) = $2,012.93; Buyer is credited and will pay the bill when issued. Subject to reproration under PSA §7.7 within 90 days after Closing.  
            Security-deposit accrued interest (excluded).         The $32,400.00 transferred reflects principal only (per the security-deposit schedule). Connecticut General Statutes §47a-21 may require interest on residential security deposits held in escrow. Any accrued interest is not quantified in the delivered records and is excluded from the settlement statement. To be confirmed with Bayshore Management Co. and added to the credit to Buyer if applicable.  
            Gas and electric utilities (no proration reflected).         No final bills, meter readings, or account balances were provided for gas or electric service. Per PSA §7.5(d), utility deposits are retained by Seller and Buyer establishes new accounts as of Closing. The settlement statement reflects no gas/electric proration. If final landlord-paid gas/electric charges for Seller's period are later determined, they will be handled by reproration under PSA §7.7. (The commercial space is served by a separate HVAC system; residential/common areas use the oil-fired boiler; residential electric is likely tenant-metered.)  
            Water/sewer treatment — PSA §7.5(a) interpretation.         PSA §7.5(a) states that where a utility billing period ends on or before the Closing Date, "the full unpaid balance shall be credited to Buyer on the settlement statement." The WPCA account statement and Tax Lien Statement, however, require that the $1,847.60 water/sewer balance "be satisfied at or before closing." The settlement statement treats the $1,847.60 as a charge to Seller paid from Seller's proceeds directly to the City of Bridgeport at Closing (consistent with the WPCA's closing requirement and the Tax Lien Statement payoff summary). This is economically equivalent to the PSA's allocation (Seller bears the full cost); the difference is only mechanical (direct disbursement vs. credit to Buyer). If the parties prefer to follow the PSA's "credit to Buyer" language literally, the entry would be adjusted to Debit Seller / Credit Buyer $1,847.60, with Buyer remitting payment to the WPCA by the 8/1/2025 due date — but this would conflict with the WPCA's closing-satisfaction requirement.  
            Repair Escrow treatment.         Per PSA §8.4(c), the $45,000.00 Repair Escrow is a third-party holdback funded solely from Seller's proceeds. It is NOT a credit to Buyer and NOT part of the Purchase Price. It appears as a charge to Seller (disbursement to the Escrow Agent) and is held in a segregated, interest-bearing account. The pre-closing inspection (7/14/2025) confirms the roof deficiencies remain unrepaired; the escrow is therefore required at Closing. Any unused balance plus interest reverts to Seller after 12 months (PSA §8.4(e)).  
            Estate tax / FIRPTA.         The Estate of Gerald T. Whitford is below the Connecticut estate-tax threshold; CT estate tax due is $0.00, and the CT DRS estate-tax lien requires only a recorded release (no payoff). The settlement statement reflects $0.00 for the DRS lien; the $73.00 release-recording fee is a Seller charge. The PSA requires a FIRPTA affidavit or estate-equivalent certification; no federal or state withholding is anticipated.  
            Appraisal fee — Paid Outside of Closing (POC).         The $4,500.00 appraisal fee was paid by Buyer directly to Ashford Valuation Associates LLC on 5/5/2025, outside of Closing. Per the Lender Commitment's explicit and repeated instructions, it is disclosed on the settlement statement as POC with $0.00 impact on Buyer's cash-to-close and is NOT included in any debit/credit computation.  
            Payoff good-through dates.         All lien payoffs (Harborstone first mortgage $687,412.33; HELOC $148,219.56) and the delinquent-tax payoff ($28,602.80) are good through July 20, 2025. Closing on July 15, 2025 falls within the good-through period, so no per-diem interest adjustment is required. Assumption: Closing occurs on or before July 20, 2025. If delayed, per-diem interest applies ($112.67/day first mortgage; ~$27.93/day HELOC; $13.12/day delinquent tax) and updated payoff statements must be obtained.  
                5.  ITEMS REQUIRING CONFIRMATION OR COMPLETION BEFORE CLOSING  
             The following should be confirmed or delivered on or before the Closing Date. Monetary items may adjust the final cash-to-close and net-proceeds figures.  
           Confirm exact earnest-money accrued interest with the Escrow Agent (Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC).  
           Confirm final Connecticut Conveyance Tax on Form OP-236 (filed with the Bridgeport Town Clerk at recording).  
           Confirm with Bayshore Management Co. whether any interest has accrued on residential escrow deposits under CGS §47a-21; add to the security-deposit credit if applicable.  
           Obtain final gas/electric readings or bills for any landlord-paid accounts; otherwise confirm accounts are tenant-metered or to be established by Buyer.  
           Reconcile the title-commitment number/date (PSA: PCT-2025-08814, 4/30/2025 vs. delivered: TC-2025-07182, 6/18/2025); confirm the operative commitment.  
           Confirm the correct legal name of the title underwriter (Continental Fidelity vs. Continental Hartleigh) for policy issuance.  
           Verify all wire instructions by independent verbal callback to a known number before disbursing (wire-fraud prevention).  
           Confirm the correct remaining term of the Commercial Lease (~5.5 years to 12/31/2030) for the SNDA and Lender records.  
           Execute the Subordination, Non-Disturbance, and Attornment Agreement (SNDA) among Lender, Buyer, and Coastal Provisions Market LLC (Lender Condition 7).  
           Deliver evidence of property/casualty insurance ($3,900,000 replacement cost) naming Tidewater Savings Bank as mortgagee/loss payee (Lender Condition 5).  
           Deliver the ALTA/NSPS land title survey or survey endorsement (Title Requirement 12; Lender Condition 4).  
           Deliver the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment (ASTM E1527-21) (Lender Condition 6).  
           Execute the personal guaranty by Elaine R. Matsuda (Lender Condition 10).  
           Deliver Buyer entity documentation: Certificate of Good Standing, Operating Agreement, and member resolution (dated within 30 days of Closing) (Lender Condition 11; Title Requirement 11).  
           Deliver the certified Fiduciary's Certificate (dated within 60 days of Closing; issued 6/25/2025 — within requirement) (Title Requirement 2; Lender Condition 12).  
           Deliver the CT DRS estate-tax lien release (Release Certificate No. ETL-2025-08834) for recording (Title Requirement 7).  
           Deliver the Northbridge Construction Co. mechanic's-lien release in recordable form, in exchange for the $31,000.00 settlement disbursement (Title Requirement 6).  
           Deliver the Assignment and Assumption of Leases (commercial and residential) executed by both parties (Title Requirement 9; PSA §6.2(c), §6.3(f)).  
           Deliver the Executor's Warranty Deed and the Connecticut Conveyance Tax Return (Form OP-236) for recording (Title Requirement 1, 3).  
           Confirm the Repair Escrow Agreement is executed and names Tidewater Savings Bank as an interested party (Lender Condition 13; PSA §8.4(c)).  
                6.  SURVIVAL, REPRORATION, AND POST-CLOSING ADJUSTMENTS  
             Pursuant to PSA §7.7 and §15.1, the proration and adjustment obligations survive the Closing for 90 days. Any proration that could not be finally determined at Closing — most notably the next-fiscal-year property tax (estimated at the current-year levy), any gas/electric utility charges, and any security-deposit interest — is to be readjusted between the parties when final figures become available. Any amount owed as a result of readjustment is payable within 10 business days following demand, accompanied by reasonable supporting documentation. The Repair Escrow survives for 12 months (PSA §8.4(d)–(e)); any unused balance plus accrued interest reverts to the Estate.  
                7.  SOURCES REVIEWED  
             This memo and the accompanying settlement statement were prepared from the following closing documents:  
           Purchase and Sale Agreement dated April 22, 2025 (with Exhibits A–D)  
           Rent Roll and Security Deposit Schedule (Bayshore Management Co., dated July 1, 2025)  
           Title Commitment No. TC-2025-07182 (Pinnacle Abstract & Title LLC, effective June 18, 2025)  
           Lender Commitment Letter, Loan No. TSB-2025-CRE-04183 (Tidewater Savings Bank, dated June 24, 2025)  
           Existing Lien Payoff Letters (Ashford, Clement & Paige LLP compilation, dated July 10, 2025): Harborstone FCU first mortgage, Harborstone FCU HELOC, Northbridge Construction Co. mechanic's lien  
           Municipal Tax Status Certificate and Payoff Statement No. TLC-2025-04892 (City of Bridgeport Tax Collector, dated July 10, 2025)  
           WPCA Account Statement / Final Read (City of Bridgeport Water Pollution Control Authority, dated July 7, 2025)  
           Pre-Closing Inspection Report and Heating Oil Tank Reading (Soundview Property Inspections LLC, dated July 14, 2025)  
           Property Management Agreement and Notice of Termination (Estate and Bayshore Management Co., dated October 15, 2024 / June 13, 2025)  
           Fiduciary's Certificate and Estate Summary (Fairfield County Probate Court, Docket No. 2024-PR-04417; Ashford, Clement & Paige LLP, dated July 10, 2025)  
             
              Prepared by the Closing Agent for the transaction. This memorandum is provided for the convenience of the parties and their counsel and is not legal advice. In the event of any conflict between this memo and the PSA, the PSA controls.  
              Distribution:  Seller's Counsel (Ashford, Clement & Paige LLP)  •  Buyer's Counsel (Ridgeline Law Group PLLC)  •  Lender (Tidewater Savings Bank)  •  Closing File

### FILE: exhibit-summary-memo.docx
          INTERNAL MEMORANDUM  
          PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT  
                          TO:  
          Sarah Whitfield, Esq.; Jordan Reeves  
                FROM:  
          Case Team, Calloway & Birch LLP  
                DATE:  
          October 7, 2024  
                RE:  
          Review of Respondent Darren Kwan’s Document Production —             Torres-Kwan v. Kwan    , Case No. 2024-DR-01847 (Cir. Ct. Fairview County, Columbia; Hon. Patricia Yoon-Callahan)  
                PRODUCTION REVIEWED:  
          Respondent’s production of documents in response to Petitioner’s First Request for Production, Nos. 1–22, transmitted by cover letter from Raymond Fossett, Esq. (Hargrove Fossett & Yates LLP)  
         
       I. Executive Summary  
      Respondent Darren Kwan produced seven (7) document sets (PROD-001 through PROD-007) in response to Petitioner’s First Request for Production, Nos. 1–22, together with a cover letter identifying four (4) documents withheld on privilege (PRIV-001 through PRIV-004). The production is facially responsive but, on cross-referencing the exhibits against one another and against the Sworn Financial Declaration (PROD-001), reveals             material omissions and internal inconsistencies         that, in our preliminary assessment, materially understate Respondent’s income, assets, and liabilities, and that raise concerns about post-separation dissipation and incomplete disclosure.  
      The most significant findings are:  
             Undisclosed real property.         The 2023 tax return (PROD-002) and the produced mortgage statements (PROD-005) both reflect a rental condominium at             1035 Elm Street, Unit C    , titled in Respondent’s name alone, carrying a mortgage of approximately             $241,281         and generating             $2,100/month         in rent. This property, its mortgage, and its rental income are             entirely omitted         from the Sworn Financial Declaration, which affirmatively states that Respondent         “    does not hold any ownership interest … in any other real property    ”         and that he has         “    no … rental income.    ”  
             Income understatement.         The Declaration reports 2023         “    K-1 income    ”         of             $174,000    , which is the             cash distribution         figure. The K-1 (PROD-002) reports             ordinary business income         of             $367,200         — a difference of             $193,200    . The Declaration also omits interest, dividend, and capital-gain income reflected on the joint return, and omits the rental income noted above.  
             $85,000 post-separation wire to an undisclosed account.         On             June 15, 2024         — two weeks after the Declaration’s stated separation date of June 1, 2024 — Respondent wired             $85,000         from the Crestline brokerage account (PROD-003) to             Harborstone Financial Group, account ending 2291    , an account that appears             nowhere         in the Declaration.  
             Recurring transfers to an unidentified third party.         The bank statements (PROD-004) show             $14,600         in Venmo transfers to         “    Kiera M.    ”         over March–July 2024 (~$2,920/month), none of which is disclosed or explained.  
             Separation-date conflict.         The Declaration fixes separation at             June 1, 2024    , while Respondent’s own cover-letter privilege log repeatedly references a separation date of             August 1, 2024    . The date of separation is dispositive to the marital-estate cutoff and to characterization of the June 15 wire and other transactions.  
             Valuation-date misrepresentation.         The Declaration represents all balances as of the June 1, 2024 separation date, but the figures reported for the individual checking, savings, and brokerage accounts in fact match the             August 1, 2024         statement values — obscuring the post-separation decline in the brokerage account (approximately             $81,245    , driven largely by the $85,000 wire).  
      A detailed exhibit catalog, figure summary, itemized inconsistency log, and recommended follow-up discovery follow.  
      
        II. Exhibit Catalog  
      The production consists of the cover letter and seven document sets. Bates numbering is inconsistent across sets (see Part IV.M).  
                             Prod. No.  
          Document  
          Source / Custodian  
          Bates Range  
          Period Covered  
          Responsive RFP Nos.  
            Cover Ltr.  
          Transmittal letter & privilege log (PRIV-001–004)  
          R. Fossett / Hargrove Fossett & Yates LLP  
          n/a  
          undated (transmittal)  
          1–22 (index); 13, 14, 15, 18 (withheld)  
            PROD-001  
          Respondent’s Sworn Financial Declaration  
          Darren Kwan (prep. by counsel)  
          n/a  
          filed Sept. 20, 2024 (per declaration)  
          1, 2, 3  
            PROD-002  
          2023 Form 1040 excerpts (Sched. E, K-1, W-2s, entity summary)  
          Archer & Linden CPAs; joint return  
          DK-PROD-0045 – 0058  
          Tax Year 2023  
          4, 5, 8  
            PROD-003  
          Crestline Wealth Advisors brokerage statements (acct. 9956)  
          Crestline / Nathan Greer, CFP  
          KWAN_PROD_00142 – 00158  
          Jan. 1 – Aug. 1, 2024  
          6, 7  
            PROD-004  
          Fairview Community Bank individual statements (acct. 7703, 7704)  
          Fairview Community Bank  
          KWAN        PROD    0401 – 0424  
          Mar. 1 – Aug. 1, 2024  
          6, 7  
            PROD-005  
          Meridian Home Lending mortgage statements (1035 Elm St., Unit C)  
          Meridian Home Lending  
          n/a (loan 7842-0163-ELM)  
          June–Aug. 2024  
          9, 10  
            PROD-006  
          Platinum Visa credit card statements (acct. ending 8316)  
          Fairview Community Bank  
          none stamped  
          May–Aug. 2024  
          6, 7, 11  
            PROD-007  
          NovaBridge Solutions LLC expense reimbursement reports (D. Kwan)  
          NovaBridge Solutions LLC  
          KWAN        PROD    00187 – 00193  
          Q1–Q2 2024  
          5, 8, 12  
             Documents withheld (privilege log):         PRIV-001 (email, Kwan→Fossett, Aug. 5, 2024, re: financial obligations post-separation); PRIV-002 (email chain, Aug. 12–14, 2024, re: document preservation/disclosure post-separation); PRIV-003 (memorandum, Fossett→Kwan, Aug. 20, 2024, re:         “    Preliminary analysis of marital estate    ”    ); PRIV-004 (email, Kwan→Fossett, Sept. 8, 2024, re:         “    Response to petition    ”    ). A formal privilege log is promised within ten (10) business days.  
          Documents not yet produced (noted in cover letter):         Entity-level NovaBridge records, the Operating Agreement, and documents responsive to RFP Nos. 16–22 are being         “    coordinated    ”         with NovaBridge and co-member Jared Ostrowski and will be         “    supplemented.    ”         Respondent’s own First RFP to Petitioner (served Sept. 18, 2024) remains outstanding.  
      
        III. Summary of Key Figures (As Declared vs. As Documented)  
       A. Income (Calendar Year 2023)  
                           Component  
          Declared (PROD-001)  
          Per Tax Return / K-1 (PROD-002)  
          Variance  
            W-2 salary (NovaBridge)  
          $185,000  
          $185,000 (W-2 Box 1)  
          none  
            K-1 pass-through income  
          $174,000 (labeled         “    K-1 Income / Distributions    ”    )  
          $367,200 (K-1 Box 1, ordinary business income)  
              +$193,200  
            K-1 cash distributions  
          (reported as income, above)  
          $174,000 (K-1 Box 19a)  
          n/a — distribution, not income  
            Net rental income (1035 Elm St.)  
          $0 (declared         “    None    ”    )  
          $2,940 (Sched. E)  
              +$2,940  
            Interest income (joint)  
          $0 (declared         “    None    ”    )  
          $1,840 (Form 1040, Line 2b)  
              +$1,840  
            Ordinary dividends (joint)  
          $0 (declared         “    None    ”    )  
          $6,310 (Line 3b)  
              +$6,310  
            Capital gains (joint)  
          $0 (declared         “    None    ”    )  
          $8,450 (Line 7)  
              +$8,450  
            Vehicle allowance (W-2 Box 14)  
          not disclosed  
          $9,600 ($800/mo.)  
              +$9,600  
                Respondent-attributable total  
              $359,000  
              ≥ $555,140         (W-2 + K-1 income + rental) plus share of joint investment income  
              understated by ≥ $196,140  
         The Declaration’s ¶19 affirmatively states that Respondent has         “    no other sources of income, including but not limited to interest income, dividend income, rental income, royalty income, trust income, or income from any other business venture or investment.    ”         The tax return contradicts this on four of those categories. The $193,200 gap between K-1             income         ($367,200) and the             distribution         ($174,000) equals Respondent’s 60% share of NovaBridge’s 2023 entity-level retained earnings ($322,000 × 60% = $193,200), i.e., marital equity retained inside the business and not captured by the distribution figure.  
        B. Assets (As Declared)  
                          Asset  
          Declared Value  
          Notes / Cross-Reference  
            Marital residence — 418 Birchwood Lane  
          $715,000 (value); $218,400 mortgage; $496,600 equity  
          Online estimate; no appraisal; no mortgage statement produced for this property  
            Joint checking (FCB 4821)  
          $12,340  
          No statement produced  
            Joint savings (FCB 4822)  
          $28,750  
          No statement produced  
            Individual checking (FCB 7703)  
          $8,215  
          Matches             Aug. 1, 2024         balance, not June 1  
            Individual savings (FCB 7704)  
          $42,680  
          Matches             Aug. 1, 2024         balance, not June 1  
            Brokerage (Crestline 9956)  
          $387,450  
          Matches             Aug. 1, 2024         valuation; was ~$468,695 on June 1  
            401(k) (Pinnacle 3310)  
          $412,800  
          No statement produced  
            403(b) — Petitioner (Pinnacle 5587)  
          $89,200  
          Petitioner’s; not in Respondent’s control  
            2022 BMW X5  
          $48,500 value; $22,100 loan; $26,400 equity  
          Titled to Respondent  
            2020 Honda CR-V  
          $24,800 value; $0 loan; $24,800 equity  
          Titled to Petitioner  
            Household/personal property  
          $30,000  
          Estimate  
            NovaBridge 60% membership interest  
              unvalued  
          K-1 ending capital account $675,800 (tax basis); no appraisal  
                Total financial accounts (declared)  
              $981,435  
          includes Petitioner’s 403(b)  
           C. Liabilities (As Declared)  
                          Liability  
          Declared Balance  
          Notes  
            Mortgage — 418 Birchwood Lane (Meridian)  
          $218,400  
          No statement produced  
            Auto loan — 2022 BMW X5 (FCB Auto Finance)  
          $22,100  
          $425/mo.; payment not visible in produced checking records  
            Credit card — Platinum Visa  
          $8,500  
          Creditor misstated; see Part IV.G  
                Total declared liabilities  
              $249,000  
       
                Undisclosed: mortgage — 1035 Elm St. (Meridian)  
              ~$241,281  
          Per PROD-005 (Aug. 1, 2024)  
                Adjusted total liabilities  
              ~$490,281  
          understated by ~$241,281  
           D. Monthly Expenses (As Declared, PROD-001 §V)  
      Declared total personal monthly expenses:             $8,885    ; plus declared voluntary contribution of             $3,500         to the marital household =             $12,385/month         total outlay. The bank records show materially higher actual monthly outflows (see Part IV.I).  
        E. Business Interest — NovaBridge Solutions LLC (60%)  
                          Metric  
          2023  
          2022  
            Gross revenue  
          $4,280,000  
          $3,915,000  
            Ordinary business income (Form 1065)  
          $612,000  
          $548,000  
            Total partner distributions  
          $290,000 (Kwan $174,000 / Ostrowski $116,000)  
          $260,000  
            Entity-level retained earnings  
          $322,000  
          n/a  
            Respondent’s K-1 ordinary income  
          $367,200 (60%)  
          n/a  
            Respondent’s ending capital account  
          $675,800  
          n/a  
            Respondent’s share of recourse liabilities  
          $36,400  
          n/a  
         No business valuation has been performed. The Operating Agreement (containing transfer restrictions, buyout provisions, and the self-approval provision discussed in Part IV.J) has not been produced.  
      
         IV. Flagged Inconsistencies and Red Flags  
       A. Undisclosed Rental Property — 1035 Elm Street, Unit C (HIGH)  
      The Sworn Financial Declaration (¶20–23) lists only the marital residence at 418 Birchwood Lane and states that Respondent         “    does not hold any ownership interest, whether sole or joint, in any other real property.    ”         Three other exhibits contradict this:  
              PROD-002 (tax return), Schedule E, Part I:         reports a rental condominium at 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, Fairview, Columbia 20147,         “    Property titled in the name of Darren Kwan,    ”         rented 365 days, generating             $25,200         in 2023 rent ($2,100/month) and             $2,940         net rental income, with             $11,280         in mortgage interest paid to Meridian Home Lending.  
              PROD-005 (mortgage statements):         three monthly statements for loan 7842-0163-ELM, borrower Darren Kwan (sole, no co-borrower), property 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, original loan $279,000 dated Oct. 8, 2019, current principal balance             $241,281.19         as of Aug. 1, 2024, PITI             $1,680/month    .  
              PROD-004 (bank statements):         recurring         “    ACH Deposit — Tenant Rent Payment    ”         of             $2,100/month         (April 5, May 3, June 5, July 3, 2024) and a recurring         “    ACH Payment — Meridian Home Lending    ”         of             $1,680/month         (the Elm Street mortgage), distinct from the $1,842 Birchwood mortgage payment.  
          Impact:         The Declaration omits (i) a marital asset (the Elm Street condominium, acquired and financed during the marriage in October 2019), (ii) a liability of approximately             $241,281    , and (iii) rental income of             $2,100/month    . The property was acquired during the marriage and is presumptively marital notwithstanding sole title. Notably, Respondent produced mortgage statements for the             undisclosed         Elm Street property but produced             no         mortgage statements for the             disclosed         Birchwood marital residence.  
        B. Income Understatement (HIGH)  
      As detailed in Part III.A, the Declaration reports $174,000 of         “    K-1 Income / Distributions,    ”         conflating the cash distribution (K-1 Box 19a) with taxable pass-through income (K-1 Box 1 = $367,200). It also affirmatively denies interest, dividend, rental, and capital-gain income that the joint return reports. The understatement of Respondent-attributable 2023 income is at least             $196,140         and is directly material to child-support and alimony calculations.  
        C. $85,000 Wire Transfer to Undisclosed Account (HIGH — Dissipation Concern)  
      PROD-003 (Crestline, June 2024 statement) shows an outgoing wire transfer of             $85,000         on             June 15, 2024    , from account 9956 to             Harborstone Financial Group, account ending 2291         (reference WT-2024-061524-0087), initiated by the account holder with verbal telephone authorization. The Harborstone account is not listed anywhere in the Declaration’s account schedule (¶24–33). The wire occurred two weeks after the Declaration’s stated June 1, 2024 separation date. The Crestline year-to-date summary confirms total withdrawals of $97,300 through Aug. 1, 2024, of which $85,000 is this single wire (the remainder being advisory fees and a $10,550 IRS estimated-tax payment). This transfer is a prime candidate for a dissipation-of-marital-assets claim and for a tracing/subpoena of the Harborstone account.  
        D. Recurring Venmo Transfers to         “    Kiera M.    ”         (HIGH)  
      PROD-004 (checking 7703) shows recurring Venmo payments to         “    Kiera M.    ”    :  
                        Date  
          Amount  
            Mar. 15, 2024  
          $2,800  
            Apr. 22, 2024  
          $3,100  
            May 10, 2024  
          $2,500  
            Jun. 18, 2024  
          $3,200  
            Jul. 14, 2024  
          $3,000  
                Total (5 months)  
              $14,600  
         These transfers (~$2,920/month) are not disclosed or explained anywhere in the production. The identity of         “    Kiera M.    ”         and the purpose of the payments are unknown. The pattern is consistent with support of a third party and should be explored by interrogatory and at deposition.  
        E. Separation-Date Conflict (HIGH)  
              PROD-001 (Declaration, ¶5):         “    The parties effectively separated on or about             June 1, 2024    .    ”  
              Cover letter, privilege log:         PRIV-001 references         “    the parties’ separation on             August 1, 2024    ”    ; PRIV-002 is captioned         “    post-separation August 1, 2024.    ”  
      A two-month discrepancy in the date of separation is material: it determines the marital-estate cutoff and the characterization of the June 15, 2024 $85,000 wire, the June–July Venmo transfers, the brokerage decline, and the commencement of separate living expenses. If separation was August 1 (as Respondent’s counsel’s own privilege log states), then the June 15 wire and related transactions occurred             during         the marriage and the June 1         “    separation-date    ”         valuations in the Declaration are premature. This conflict must be pinned down.  
        F. Valuation-Date Misrepresentation (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
      The Declaration (¶56) represents that         “    [a]ll asset values and account balances reflected herein are approximate as of the date of separation, which Respondent identifies as on or about June 1, 2024.    ”         The produced statements show the reported balances actually match             August 1, 2024         values:  
                            Account  
          Declared (claimed 6/1/24)  
          Actual 6/1/24 value  
          Actual 8/1/24 value  
          Match  
            Checking 7703  
          $8,215  
          ~$26,870  
          $8,215.00  
          Aug. 1  
            Savings 7704  
          $42,680  
          ~$40,548  
          $42,680.00  
          Aug. 1  
            Brokerage 9956  
          $387,450  
          ~$468,695  
          $387,450.00  
          Aug. 1  
         The brokerage account declined approximately             $81,245         between June 1 and August 1, 2024, driven principally by the $85,000 wire (Part IV.C). Reporting the post-transfer August 1 value while labeling it a June 1 separation-date value obscures the transfer and the account’s higher marital value.  
        G. Credit-Card Discrepancies (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
              Creditor misidentified.         The Declaration (¶46) states the card is held with         “    First National Card Services.    ”         The produced statements (PROD-006) and the bank ACH payee (PROD-004) both identify the creditor as             Fairview Community Bank         (account ending 8316, $50,000 credit limit).  
              Balance/payment characterization.         The Declaration describes an         “    approximate revolving balance    ”         of $8,500 and states Respondent         “    typically pays between $500 and $1,000 per month.    ”         The statements show the card is             paid in full each month         (payment equals prior balance), with monthly charges of $7,038.45 / $8,124.30 / $12,486.75 / $6,418.50 (May–Aug. 2024), totaling             $34,068         in four months (~$8,517/month average). No statement reflects an $8,500 revolving balance, and actual payments far exceed $500–$1,000/month.  
              Possible second credit account.         The checking account shows monthly ACH payments to         “    Fairview Community Bank Visa    ”         of $1,450 / $2,230 / $3,875 / $4,120 / $5,480 (March–July 2024). These amounts             do not reconcile         with the pay-in-full payments shown on the Platinum Visa statements (ending 8316), which post on the 1st of each month in different amounts. This suggests either a second card/line of credit at Fairview Community Bank or misapplied payments — either way, an obligation not fully disclosed.  
        H. Mischaracterization of         “    Voluntary    ”         Support (MEDIUM-HIGH)  
      The Declaration (¶51) states Respondent         “    voluntarily    ”         contributes             $3,500/month         “    toward the marital home mortgage payment and household expenses for the benefit of Petitioner and the minor children.    ”         The bank records (PROD-004) show two monthly Meridian Home Lending ACH payments totaling $3,522:             $1,842         (the Birchwood marital mortgage) and             $1,680         (the Elm Street rental mortgage). The $1,680 payment services Respondent’s own undisclosed investment property and confers no benefit on Petitioner or the children. Characterizing the combined $3,522 as support         “    for the benefit of Petitioner and the minor children    ”         overstates the support provided to the household by approximately             $1,680/month         and is relevant to any temporary-support motion or credit claim.  
        I. Expense Declaration Understates Actual Spending (MEDIUM)  
      The declared monthly budget ($8,885 personal; $12,385 with voluntary contribution) omits several substantial recurring outflows visible in the bank records, including: the Venmo transfers to         “    Kiera M.    ”         (~$2,920/month), the Elm Street mortgage ($1,680/month), the full credit-card payments ($1,450–$5,480/month), and a recurring $1,437.50/month ACH to Pinnacle Retirement Services 401(k) (see Part IV.L). As an illustration, June 2024 checking outflows totaled             $15,450.08         — exceeding the declared $12,385 total outlay even before accounting for the $85,000 brokerage wire. Specific line-item variances include auto insurance (declared $210/month vs. actual Progressive ACH $312/month) and clothing/dining (declared $200 + $600 = $800/month vs. credit-card charges routinely exceeding $3,000/month in those categories alone, e.g., Monarch Fine Clothiers $1,420–$2,340/month; country-club dues $425/month).  
        J. NovaBridge Expense Reimbursements — Self-Approval and Personal-as-Business (MEDIUM)  
      PROD-007 discloses that, pursuant to             Section 5.3 of the NovaBridge Operating Agreement    , Respondent — as the 60% managing member —             self-approves his own expense reimbursements         without any co-member review (Ostrowski’s review is marked         “    N/A — not required    ”    ). Reimbursements totaled             $12,662         in H1 2024 (Q1 $3,373; Q2 $9,289) and were deposited directly into Respondent’s personal checking (PROD-004: $3,400 on Apr. 10; $2,150 on May 20). Two concerns:  
              Governance.         With no independent oversight of the majority member’s reimbursements, the reimbursement channel can function as disguised, discretionary compensation — relevant to both income and business-valuation analyses.  
              Personal items reimbursed as business.         A Q2 entry reimburses         “    TechVault Solutions — Technology Equipment —             Personal Devices         — Tablet, smartphone, and accessories — $2,150    ”         (05/17/2024); the credit-card statements show a same-day         “    Pinnacle Electronics — Personal Devices — $2,149.50    ”         charge (05/17/2024). The         “    Personal Devices    ”         labeling on both is a red flag that personal electronics may have been charged to the company. The Q2 report also includes a $3,400         “    Sapphire Sky Travel — client appreciation event planning    ”         charge, while the credit card shows a separate $6,200         “    Sapphire Sky Travel    ”         charge in July 2024 (Q3, not produced).  
      The Operating Agreement itself has             not         been produced (RFP Nos. 16–22 outstanding).  
        K. Vehicle Allowance Not Disclosed as Income (LOW-MEDIUM)  
      The 2023 W-2 (PROD-002) Box 14 reports a             $9,600 vehicle allowance         ($800/month) paid by NovaBridge. This benefit is not reflected anywhere in the Declaration’s income section (¶12–19) or expense section (where auto costs are listed gross, with no offsetting allowance). Separately, the declared $425/month BMW auto-loan payment is not visible as a distinct ACH in the produced checking records — its payment source is unclear (possibly the vehicle allowance or a separate account).  
        L. 401(k) ACH Anomaly — Possible Undisclosed Loan (MEDIUM)  
      PROD-004 shows a recurring             $1,437.50/month         ACH to         “    Pinnacle Retirement Services 401(k)    ”         (March 28, April 28, May 28, June 28, July 28, 2024),             in addition to         the payroll-deferred 401(k) contributions already reflected in the net direct deposit. On             August 1, 2024    , a single ACH of             $14,644.60         to Pinnacle 401(k) (    “    Early Month    ”    ) appears. A separate monthly ACH to the 401(k) custodian, on top of payroll deferrals, is consistent with a             401(k) loan repayment         — which would be an undisclosed liability. No 401(k) statements have been produced to confirm the nature of these transfers or the $412,800 declared balance.  
        M. Production Metadata / Authentication Inconsistencies (LOW-MEDIUM)  
              Case number.         Most documents bear Case No.         2024-DR-01847    . The Fairview Community Bank production legend (PROD-004) cites             2024-DR-00847         and captions the matter         “        In re Marriage of Kwan    ”         (vs.     “        Torres-Kwan v. Kwan    ”    ) in the         “    Circuit Court of Columbia, Fairview County    ”         (vs.     “    Circuit Court of Fairview County, Columbia    ”    ). These clerical discrepancies should be corrected to avoid authentication issues.  
              Bates numbering.         Prefixes and formats are inconsistent:         “    DK-PROD-    ”         (PROD-002),         “    KWAN_PROD_    ”         (PROD-003),         “    KWAN        PROD    ”         (PROD-004, PROD-007). PROD-006 (credit card) bears             no Bates stamp at all    . The ranges also overlap oddly (Crestline 00142–00158; expense reports 00187–00193; FCB 0401–0424). A unified, consecutive Bates scheme should be demanded.  
              Crestline account tax-ID anomaly.         The Crestline statements list the account Tax ID as             ●●●-●●-8341    , whose last four digits match the SSN of the parties’ minor child             Ethan Kwan (XXX-XX-8341)         rather than Respondent’s (XXX-XX-4718). This may be a redaction artifact, but it warrants verification of the account’s registered owner/tax ID (via the account application or W-9), as it could bear on whether the account is properly Respondent’s individual asset.  
        N. Filing-Date and Counsel-Contact Discrepancies (LOW)  
              Filing date.         The cover letter describes PROD-001 as         “    filed with the Court on or about             October 4, 2024    ,    ”         while the Declaration itself states         “    Filed:             September 20, 2024    ”         and bears a September 20, 2024 notarization and certificate of service.  
              Counsel contact.         The cover letter lists Respondent’s counsel as tel. (571) 555-4200, email rfossett@hfy-law.com. The Declaration’s prepared-by and certificate-of-service blocks list tel. (571) 555-0184, email rfossett@hfylaw.com (different number and email domain).  
      
         V. Recommended Follow-Up Discovery  
      The following follow-up is recommended, organized by objective. Priorities are marked             [P1]         (highest) through             [P3]    .  
       A. Real Property and the Marital Residence  
              [P1]         Serve a targeted interrogatory demanding that Respondent identify             every         real property interest held at any time during the marriage, including 1035 Elm Street, Unit C, and to explain its omission from the Declaration.  
              [P1]         Request/produce mortgage statements and the note for             418 Birchwood Lane         (the disclosed marital residence), which were             not         produced, to verify the declared $218,400 balance and $1,842 PITI.  
              [P1]         Request the deed, settlement statement, and current mortgage statement for             1035 Elm Street, Unit C    , plus all lease/rental records and tenant information, and an appraisal or market analysis of its fair market value.  
              [P2]         Request Schedule E and rental records for tax years 2020–2022 to establish the Elm Street acquisition and rental history.  
        B. Income and Tax  
              [P1]         Request the             complete         2023 Form 1040 (all 47 pages), including Schedule A, Schedule B, Schedule D, Schedule SE, and Form 8995, plus the complete 2021 and 2022 returns.  
              [P1]         Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to reconcile the $174,000         “    K-1 income    ”         figure in the Declaration with the $367,200 K-1 ordinary business income on the tax return, and to state his total 2023 gross income per the return.  
              [P1]         Request             2024 pay stubs         (year-to-date) to reconcile the $7,115.38 bi-monthly net direct deposit ($170,769 annualized) against the declared $185,000 gross salary — the implied total withholding (~7.7%) is inconsistent with the 2023 W-2 withholding pattern and suggests either higher 2024 gross pay or sharply reduced withholding/deferral.  
              [P2]         Request all K-1s and distribution records for 2021–2024 and any 2024 estimated-tax payment records.  
        C. The $85,000 Wire and the Harborstone Account  
              [P1]         Serve a             subpoena duces tecum on Harborstone Financial Group         for all records of account ending 2291 (and any other accounts in Respondent’s name), including opening documents, signature cards, statements, and wire/transfer records from January 2024 to present.  
              [P1]         Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to identify the Harborstone account, its purpose, all deposits and withdrawals, and the disposition of the $85,000 wired on June 15, 2024.  
              [P1]         Request the Crestline wire-transfer authorization file, including the recorded telephone authorization referenced in the June 2024 statement.  
        D.         “    Kiera M.    ”         and Other Unexplained Transfers  
              [P1]         Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to identify         “    Kiera M.,    ”         the nature of the relationship, and the purpose of each Venmo transfer (totaling $14,600 March–July 2024), and to produce any related correspondence or agreements.  
              [P2]         Request Venmo/PayPal/Zelle and other P2P payment records for Respondent for 2022–2024, and bank records for any account used to fund or receive such transfers.  
        E. Separation Date and Valuation  
              [P1]         Serve an interrogatory requiring Respondent to state under oath the precise date of separation and to reconcile the Declaration’s June 1, 2024 date with the August 1, 2024 date in his counsel’s privilege log.  
              [P1]         Request that Respondent re-state all account balances and asset values as of the agreed/court-determined valuation date, with monthly statement balances for each account from January 2024 to present, to expose post-separation changes (especially the brokerage decline).  
        F. Credit Cards and Other Debts  
              [P1]         Request             all         credit-card, charge-card, and line-of-credit statements for Respondent (and joint) for 2022–2024 from Fairview Community Bank and any other issuer, to resolve the reconciliation gap between the checking-account         “    FCB Visa    ”         ACH payments and the Platinum Visa (8316) statements, and to confirm whether a second credit account exists.  
              [P2]         Request the auto-loan account statements for the 2022 BMW X5 and confirmation of the payment source (the $425/month payment is not visible in the produced checking records).  
        G. Retirement Accounts  
              [P1]         Request             401(k) statements         (Pinnacle, account 3310) for 2022–2024, including any loan account records, to determine whether the $1,437.50/month ACH and the $14,644.60 August 1 transfer constitute loan repayments (an undisclosed liability) and to verify the $412,800 balance.  
              [P2]         Request the 403(b) records for Petitioner’s account (5587) through Petitioner’s own production.  
        H. NovaBridge and the Business Interest  
              [P1]         Press for production of the             NovaBridge Operating Agreement         (all versions) and all entity-level financial records responsive to RFP Nos. 16–22, which Respondent has deferred.  
              [P1]         Request Form 1065 and financial statements for NovaBridge for 2021–2023, and year-to-date 2024, plus the K-1 package and capital-account ledgers.  
              [P1]         Request the             underlying receipts         for the PROD-007 expense reimbursements, with particular attention to the         “    Personal Devices    ”         entries and the Sapphire Sky Travel charges, and an interrogatory on whether any personal expenses were reimbursed as business expenses.  
              [P2]         Retain a             business valuator         to value Respondent’s 60% membership interest, accounting for the self-approval/compensation discretion in Section 5.3, transfer restrictions, buyout provisions, and the $36,400 recourse-liability exposure.  
              [P2]         Consider a deposition of co-member             Jared Ostrowski         (or a 30(b)(6)/entity deposition of NovaBridge) on compensation, distributions, reimbursements, and any post-separation changes.  
        I. Joint Accounts and Petitioner’s Reciprocal Production  
              [P1]         Request statements for the             joint checking (4821) and joint savings (4822)         accounts, which were not produced despite being listed in the Declaration; Respondent’s objection to producing         “    Petitioner’s solely-held accounts    ”         does not extend to joint accounts.  
              [P2]         Pursue Petitioner’s             reciprocal production         in response to Respondent’s First RFP (served Sept. 18, 2024), and gather Petitioner’s 403(b), pay, and bank records to complete the estate picture.  
        J. Privilege and Withheld Documents  
              [P2]         Hold Respondent to the promised             formal privilege log         within 10 business days. Scrutinize             PRIV-003         (the Aug. 20, 2024         “    Preliminary analysis of marital estate    ”         memorandum) — to the extent it contains factual financial information or communications that are not legal advice, consider a privilege challenge. The privilege-log entries referencing         “    financial obligations and asset disclosures    ”         (PRIV-001) and         “    document preservation and disclosure obligations    ”         (PRIV-002) may also contain non-privileged factual content.  
              [P3]         Consider a motion to compel if the Operating Agreement and entity-level records (RFP Nos. 16–22) are not produced promptly.  
        K. Authentication / Production Hygiene  
              [P3]         Request that Respondent correct the case-number and caption discrepancies in the FCB production (2024-DR-00847 vs. 01847) and adopt a single, consecutive Bates-numbering scheme, and that PROD-006 (credit card) be properly Bates-stamped.  
      
         VI. Preliminary Assessment and Next Steps  
      The production, while voluminous, is incomplete and internally inconsistent in ways that uniformly favor Respondent: income is understated (K-1 distribution reported instead of pass-through income; investment and rental income denied); an entire real-property asset and its mortgage are omitted; a large post-separation transfer to an undisclosed account is not explained; recurring transfers to an unidentified third party are not disclosed; and the declared account balances use a later valuation date than represented, masking the transfer. The separation date itself is contradicted by Respondent’s own privilege log.  
          Recommended immediate actions:  
          Calendar the 10-business-day deadline for the formal privilege log and the supplementation deadline for RFP Nos. 16–22.  
          Prepare and serve the             targeted interrogatories         (Part V.A.1, V.B.6, V.C.10, V.D.12, V.E.14) and the             subpoena to Harborstone Financial Group         (Part V.C.9) — these are the highest-value, fastest-moving items.  
          Draft a             meet-and-confer letter         to opposing counsel identifying the Elm Street omission, the income understatement, the $85,000 wire, the         “    Kiera M.    ”         transfers, and the separation-date conflict, and demanding supplementation of the Declaration and production of the missing mortgage, joint-account, 401(k), and Operating Agreement records.  
          Begin scoping a             forensic accounting/tracing         engagement focused on (i) the $85,000 wire and the Harborstone account, (ii) the         “    Kiera M.    ”         transfers, and (iii) the Elm Street rental cash flow.  
          Evaluate a             motion to compel         and/or a             dissipation claim         contingent on the supplementation and subpoena returns.  
      We are available to draft the interrogatories, subpoena, and meet-and-confer letter upon your direction.  
      
          Prepared by the case team for internal use. This memorandum constitutes attorney work product and is privileged and confidential. All figures are drawn from the produced exhibits cited; final figures should be confirmed against source documents and any supplementation.

children.name: set_arm.sh
agent_loop.BARE.py
children.body:
"""The agent loop — model calls tools until it finishes or hits max turns.

This is the core of the harness. It's deliberately simple: the model does
the thinking, the loop just shuttles messages back and forth.

The agent finishes when it stops making tool calls (no explicit `finish`
tool). The agent loop ends on:
  1. No tool calls returned — the model has nothing more to do
  2. Max turns reached
"""

import time
import json
from pathlib import Path

from harness.adapters.base import ModelAdapter, ModelResponse
from harness.tools import ToolExecutor, get_all_tool_definitions


def run_agent(
    adapter: ModelAdapter,
    system_prompt: str,
    user_prompt: str,
    tool_executor: ToolExecutor,
    tools: list[dict] | None = None,
    max_turns: int = 200,
    transcript_path: str | None = None,
) -> dict:
    """Run the agent loop to completion.

    Args:
        adapter: The model adapter (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, xAI).
        system_prompt: Capabilities and conventions (preamble + skill manuals).
        user_prompt: The first user message — the task assignment.
        tool_executor: Configured tool executor with documents and output dirs.
        tools: Tool definitions to use. Defaults to standard 6 tools if not provided.
        max_turns: Maximum number of loop iterations.
        transcript_path: Optional path to write transcript JSONL.

    Returns:
        Dict with run results: messages, metrics, timing.
    """
    messages = [
        adapter.make_system_message(system_prompt),
        adapter.make_user_message(user_prompt),
    ]
    if tools is None:
        tools = get_all_tool_definitions()

    total_input_tokens = 0
    total_output_tokens = 0
    total_cache_read = 0
    total_cache_creation = 0
    turn_count = 0
    start_time = time.time()

    transcript_file = None
    if transcript_path:
        Path(transcript_path).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        transcript_file = open(transcript_path, "w")
        print(json.dumps({"turn": 0, "role": "system", "text": system_prompt}), file=transcript_file)
        print(json.dumps({"turn": 0, "role": "user", "text": user_prompt}), file=transcript_file)
        transcript_file.flush()

    context_overflow = False
    try:
        for turn in range(max_turns):
            turn_count = turn + 1

            # Call the model
            try:
                response = adapter.chat(messages, tools)
            except Exception as e:
                err_msg = str(e)
                if "prompt is too long" in err_msg or "context_length_exceeded" in err_msg:
                    context_overflow = True
                    print(f"Context window exceeded on turn {turn_count}: {err_msg}")
                    break
                raise

            messages.append(response.message)
            total_input_tokens += response.input_tokens
            total_output_tokens += response.output_tokens
            total_cache_read += response.cache_read_input_tokens
            total_cache_creation += response.cache_creation_input_tokens

            # Log to transcript
            if transcript_file:
                _log_turn(transcript_file, turn_count, "assistant", response)

            # If no tool calls, the agent is done
            if not response.tool_calls:
                break

            # Execute each tool call and feed results back
            tool_results = []
            for tc in response.tool_calls:
                result = tool_executor.execute(tc.name, tc.arguments)

                if transcript_file:
                    _log_tool(transcript_file, turn_count, tc.name, tc.arguments, result)

                tool_results.append((tc, result))

            # Add tool results to message history via the adapter
            result_messages = adapter.make_tool_result_messages(
                [(tc.id, result) for tc, result in tool_results]
            )
            messages.extend(result_messages)

    finally:
        if transcript_file:
            transcript_file.close()

    elapsed = time.time() - start_time

    return {
        "messages": messages,
        "turn_count": turn_count,
        "input_tokens": total_input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": total_output_tokens,
        "cache_read_input_tokens": total_cache_read,
        "cache_creation_input_tokens": total_cache_creation,
        "wall_clock_seconds": round(elapsed, 2),
        "finished_cleanly": (not context_overflow and
                             (not response.tool_calls if turn_count > 0 else False)),
        "context_overflow": context_overflow,
        "tool_metrics": tool_executor.get_metrics(),
        "finish_summary": None,
    }


def _log_turn(f, turn: int, role: str, response: ModelResponse):
    """Log a turn to the transcript JSONL."""
    entry = {
        "turn": turn,
        "role": role,
        "text": response.text if response.text else None,
        "tool_calls": [
            {"name": tc.name, "arguments": tc.arguments}
            for tc in response.tool_calls
        ] if response.tool_calls else None,
        "input_tokens": response.input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": response.output_tokens,
        "cache_read_input_tokens": response.cache_read_input_tokens,
        "cache_creation_input_tokens": response.cache_creation_input_tokens,
    }
    f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
    f.flush()


def _log_tool(f, turn: int, name: str, arguments: str, result: str):
    """Log a tool execution to the transcript JSONL."""
    entry = {
        "turn": turn,
        "role": "tool",
        "tool_name": name,
        "arguments": arguments if isinstance(arguments, str) else str(arguments),
        "result_preview": result[:1000],
        "result": result,
    }
    f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
    f.flush()

children.name: agent_loop.LOOP.py
children.body:
"""The agent loop — model calls tools until it finishes or hits max turns.

This is the core of the harness. The model does the thinking; the loop shuttles
messages back and forth. One enforced step is layered on top of the bare loop:
a single, hard-capped **finalize verification pass** (see FINALIZE_PROMPT). When
the agent first signals it is done (stops making tool calls), the loop injects
one verification turn that makes the agent check its deliverable for required
outputs, numeric correctness, and grounding, then finish. It fires AT MOST ONCE
(`finalize_fired`), so it cannot run away — after the pass the agent stops
normally on its next no-tool-call turn.

The agent finishes when it stops making tool calls (no explicit `finish` tool).
The agent loop ends on:
  1. No tool calls returned AND the finalize pass has already fired
  2. Max turns reached
"""

import time
import json
from pathlib import Path

from harness.adapters.base import ModelAdapter, ModelResponse
from harness.tools import ToolExecutor, get_all_tool_definitions


# One bounded verification pass, injected once when the agent first stops making
# tool calls. It is deliberately a CORRECTNESS check (required outputs present,
# figures computed and footing, claims grounded) and NOT a "find more issues"
# pass — broadening coverage here displaces required facts and inflates length
# without adding graded credit.
FINALIZE_PROMPT = (
    "Before you finish, run ONE verification pass over the deliverable(s) you have "
    "written. Do not start new analysis or add new issues — only verify and fix. "
    "Check, in order:\n\n"
    "1. REQUIRED OUTPUTS. Re-read each file you wrote in the output directory. "
    "Is every deliverable the task asked for present, in the right format, and does "
    "each contain every section, table, column, or dimension the task named? Add any "
    "required element that is missing.\n\n"
    "2. FIGURES. Is every figure the task needs present and correct? For each DERIVED "
    "figure (a proration, interest accrual, equity = value - debt, a sum, subtotal, "
    "total, per-share or per-unit amount, percentage, or a bridge that must foot), "
    "RECOMPUTE it now with python via `bash` — not mental math — and confirm every "
    "total foots against its components. For each figure STATED in a document, confirm "
    "you quoted it exactly (same number, units, defined term). Fix any number that is "
    "wrong, missing, or does not foot.\n\n"
    "3. GROUNDING. Remove or correct any statement, issue, or figure not supported by a "
    "document; do not invent anything to fill a template.\n\n"
    "If everything is already correct and complete, simply stop without further tool "
    "calls. Otherwise make the fixes with `edit`/`bash`/the skill scripts, then stop."
)


def run_agent(
    adapter: ModelAdapter,
    system_prompt: str,
    user_prompt: str,
    tool_executor: ToolExecutor,
    tools: list[dict] | None = None,
    max_turns: int = 200,
    transcript_path: str | None = None,
) -> dict:
    """Run the agent loop to completion.

    Args:
        adapter: The model adapter (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, xAI).
        system_prompt: Capabilities and conventions (preamble + skill manuals).
        user_prompt: The first user message — the task assignment.
        tool_executor: Configured tool executor with documents and output dirs.
        tools: Tool definitions to use. Defaults to standard 6 tools if not provided.
        max_turns: Maximum number of loop iterations.
        transcript_path: Optional path to write transcript JSONL.

    Returns:
        Dict with run results: messages, metrics, timing.
    """
    messages = [
        adapter.make_system_message(system_prompt),
        adapter.make_user_message(user_prompt),
    ]
    if tools is None:
        tools = get_all_tool_definitions()

    total_input_tokens = 0
    total_output_tokens = 0
    total_cache_read = 0
    total_cache_creation = 0
    turn_count = 0
    start_time = time.time()

    transcript_file = None
    if transcript_path:
        Path(transcript_path).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        transcript_file = open(transcript_path, "w")
        print(json.dumps({"turn": 0, "role": "system", "text": system_prompt}), file=transcript_file)
        print(json.dumps({"turn": 0, "role": "user", "text": user_prompt}), file=transcript_file)
        transcript_file.flush()

    # The finalize verification pass fires at most once — this guard is the
    # runaway bound (it converts "redo until perfect" into exactly one extra
    # check-and-fix cycle).
    finalize_fired = False

    context_overflow = False
    try:
        for turn in range(max_turns):
            turn_count = turn + 1

            # Call the model
            try:
                response = adapter.chat(messages, tools)
            except Exception as e:
                err_msg = str(e)
                if "prompt is too long" in err_msg or "context_length_exceeded" in err_msg:
                    context_overflow = True
                    print(f"Context window exceeded on turn {turn_count}: {err_msg}")
                    break
                raise

            messages.append(response.message)
            total_input_tokens += response.input_tokens
            total_output_tokens += response.output_tokens
            total_cache_read += response.cache_read_input_tokens
            total_cache_creation += response.cache_creation_input_tokens

            # Log to transcript
            if transcript_file:
                _log_turn(transcript_file, turn_count, "assistant", response)

            # If no tool calls, the agent thinks it is done.
            if not response.tool_calls:
                # Enforce exactly one finalize verification pass before exiting,
                # provided there is turn headroom to act on it.
                if not finalize_fired and turn_count < max_turns - 1:
                    finalize_fired = True
                    messages.append(adapter.make_user_message(FINALIZE_PROMPT))
                    if transcript_file:
                        print(
                            json.dumps({"turn": turn_count, "role": "user",
                                        "text": FINALIZE_PROMPT, "injected": "finalize"}),
                            file=transcript_file,
                        )
                        transcript_file.flush()
                    continue
                break

            # Execute each tool call and feed results back
            tool_results = []
            for tc in response.tool_calls:
                result = tool_executor.execute(tc.name, tc.arguments)

                if transcript_file:
                    _log_tool(transcript_file, turn_count, tc.name, tc.arguments, result)

                tool_results.append((tc, result))

            # Add tool results to message history via the adapter
            result_messages = adapter.make_tool_result_messages(
                [(tc.id, result) for tc, result in tool_results]
            )
            messages.extend(result_messages)

    finally:
        if transcript_file:
            transcript_file.close()

    elapsed = time.time() - start_time

    return {
        "messages": messages,
        "turn_count": turn_count,
        "input_tokens": total_input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": total_output_tokens,
        "cache_read_input_tokens": total_cache_read,
        "cache_creation_input_tokens": total_cache_creation,
        "wall_clock_seconds": round(elapsed, 2),
        "finished_cleanly": (not context_overflow and
                             (not response.tool_calls if turn_count > 0 else False)),
        "context_overflow": context_overflow,
        "tool_metrics": tool_executor.get_metrics(),
        "finish_summary": None,
    }


def _log_turn(f, turn: int, role: str, response: ModelResponse):
    """Log a turn to the transcript JSONL."""
    entry = {
        "turn": turn,
        "role": role,
        "text": response.text if response.text else None,
        "tool_calls": [
            {"name": tc.name, "arguments": tc.arguments}
            for tc in response.tool_calls
        ] if response.tool_calls else None,
        "input_tokens": response.input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": response.output_tokens,
        "cache_read_input_tokens": response.cache_read_input_tokens,
        "cache_creation_input_tokens": response.cache_creation_input_tokens,
    }
    f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
    f.flush()


def _log_tool(f, turn: int, name: str, arguments: str, result: str):
    """Log a tool execution to the transcript JSONL."""
    entry = {
        "turn": turn,
        "role": "tool",
        "tool_name": name,
        "arguments": arguments if isinstance(arguments, str) else str(arguments),
        "result_preview": result[:1000],
        "result": result,
    }
    f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
    f.flush()

children.name: system_prompt.BARE.md
children.body:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## File formats

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.

children.name: system_prompt.PROSE.md
children.body:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Approach to the work

These steps apply to any legal deliverable; adapt the emphasis to the task.

1. **Read every document fully, then fix the scope.** `read` each file in
   `$DOCUMENTS_DIR` completely — decisive facts hide in late schedules,
   exhibits, defined-term sections, and worked tables. Then determine which
   matter, party, or property the task concerns and which documents bear on
   it; do not discard a relevant document as "out of scope" unless you are
   sure.

2. **Produce every required output, and state the foundational facts plainly.**
   Identify exactly what the task asks you to deliver (each file, section,
   table, column, or dimension it names) and produce all of it. The easiest
   credit is in the baseline facts the task turns on — parties, dates,
   defined terms, headline figures, the full capitalization or amount table,
   aggregate totals — so state these **explicitly and exactly**, up front.
   Do not let analysis crowd them out: a deliverable that is long on
   discussion but silently omits a required number, table, or fact loses that
   credit even when the analysis is good.

3. **Treat every figure with discipline — compute, do not eyeball.** For any
   figure the task needs:
   - If it is **stated in a document**, quote it exactly (same number, units,
     and defined term) — never paraphrase or round silently.
   - If it is **derived** (a proration, interest accrual, an `equity = value −
     debt`, a sum/subtotal/total, a per-share or per-unit amount, a percentage,
     a bridge that must foot), **compute it with `bash` using python** — never
     mental math — and **verify that every subtotal and total foots** against
     its components. Re-derive any figure two documents disagree on, and report
     the discrepancy. A wrong or missing number is a failed item regardless of
     surrounding prose.

4. **For review / "identify issues" / audit / diligence tasks, cover the
   issues the record supports and develop each fully** — (a) identify and cite
   it to the source, (b) say why it matters under the controlling standard,
   (c) give a concrete fix/recommendation, and (d) any required
   severity/classification. Prefer **precise, grounded specifics over breadth**:
   surface the distinct issues actually present, but do not manufacture issues
   to fill a template, flag a non-issue, soften a sharp conclusion the record
   warrants, or reorganize the deliverable in a way that buries a required
   fact. Completeness on the specific graded points beats catalog size.

5. **Stay grounded — do not invent.** Raise only what the record supports;
   never fabricate an issue, citation, or figure. If something required cannot
   be found, flag it as a data gap rather than inventing it.

## File formats

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.
